results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: The Coalition campaigns on national security, Kevin Rudd advises MPs to pull their heads in
fields.bodyText: I didn't quite manage a lunchtime summary today. Let's rectify that this evening. Today in federal politics: The Coalition established the terms of the political face-off early in the day, signalling its intention to pursue the case of an Egyptian man held in a low security immigration detention facility in the Adelaide Hills despite being flagged by Interpol. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor said first that neither he, nor his predcessor Chris Bowen, were aware of the circumstances of this case until April this year. O'Connor later had to amend this statement when he learned that Bowen had been briefed while still in the portfolio. The Coalition grabbed the contradictions and ran with them, insisting this constituted a major national security failing on the part of the Gillard Government. The Prime Minister tried to circumvent the political attack by announcing in Question Time that the Director-General of Intelligence would hold an inquiry into this case, (and others involving complex security issues for would-be asylum seekers.) Not good enough thundered the Coalition - (and crossbenchers Andrew Wilkie and Rob Oakeshott) - who thought the multipartisan parliamentary committee on intelligence should conduct the probe. This proposal lacked the required parliamentary majority to proceed. In other issues, Julia GIllard got her landmark Gonski reforms through the House of Representatives. Despite the legislative victory, Labor continued to have a public dialogue with itself about its failings, with various people suggesting the government couldn't sell it's policies. Kevin Rudd went to the doors of parliament and advised people in the caucus to "pull their heads in." Treasurer Wayne Swan advised people to "put a sock in it." Labor Senator Mark Bishop, meanwhile, banged the table during a committee hearing, producing vision that became emblematic of Labor's internal ill-discipline and frustration. New national acocunts data confirmed the Australian economy grew by 0.6% in the March quarter. Climate Minister Greg Combet told the National Press Club Tony Abbott would not repeal the carbon price regardless of what he's saying now. That's essentially today, stripped of fog and moonstruck madness. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers for brilliant pictures. See you all tomorrow. Shadow Minister for Border Protection Michael Keenan is still going. David Speers, fresh from luring Liberal Greg Hunt away from a critical division, is now quizzing Keenan on Sky TV. Speers is putting a rebuttal from refugee advocates about this Egyptian - that he wasn't convicted in a legitimate court - to Keenan. (Are you confident that this man has been convicted in a legitimate court of these serious offences Speers inquires?) Keenan insists the Opposition is absolutely confident about the facts it is asserted about this case. After the chaos of the day, let's move in more tranquil fashion towards the evening. Can I point readers to Simon Jackman's new blog post? (Simon, Stanford Professor, is our resident, learned, utterly delightful, political numbers guy). He's written today on last week's political controversy around public funding for elections. You can read the full blog post here. Simon would cop more public funding for elections if the Australian system forced the political parties to actually tell us who is signing the cheques. Lots of meaty morsels in this piece. Here's an excerpt: We have very little information about the major parties’ expenditures, despite the parties taking considerable sums of public money via the “per vote” funding models. We know more about the relatively tiny expenditures of independents than the expenditures of the major parties: Tony Windsor spent $94,249.93 in the 2010 election, Rob Oakeshott $78,139.16 and Andrew Wilkie $35,924. Tony Abbott reported receiving nothing and spending nothing on his 2010 re-election in Warringah, the same for Julia Gillard in Lalor, and Adam Bandt in Melbourne – all true as a formal, legal matter, but quite the giggle otherwise. We only get a sense of where party money goes when the parties are giving money to entities required to report receipts to the AEC: for example, the parties transferring funds between federal and state branches, to various supporting foundations and thinktanks (some might say “front organisations”). This is all the more galling when the cross-partisan “deal” that was scuttled last week included public money to cover the parties’ “administrative costs”. This isn’t good enough. And this is why the politics of last week were so distressing. Morrison says this person should be deported. Doesn't matter if Egypt has the death penalty. Morrison: We shouldn't get distracted by (that) issue. Ever indefatigable. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has called a press conference. This inquiry the Prime Minister has announced doesn't go nearly far enough. Morrison declares there's a don't ask, don't tell policy on national security. The vote on whether to recommit the vote failed to secure the absolute majority required. Wrong way. Go back. Anthony Albanese tries to convince the Independents to drop their motion. The ABC's Melissa Clarke puts this well. Speernapped. A brand new concept has entered the political discourse. Liberal Greg Hunt is now addressing the matter of his absence during the vote. He says he was trapped in a sound proof booth with the Sky political editor David Speers. Hunt: I will learn to run faster. Albanese: National security cannot be a political plaything. I say to the crossbenchers and everyone else, don't play politics. Think of the consequences of this motion. The Wilkie motion failed to secure the required absolute majority. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne is now trying to recommit the vote. Pyne: There was great confusion. (Katharine: You don't say Christopher.) Pyne says Liberal MP Greg Hunt was trapped outside the glass doors. The Member for Fisher did not appear either. People didn't know this Wilkie motion was coming. They went on with other business, only to be called back urgently. He's pleading misadventure. Let's vote again, Pyne says. Anthony Albanese is not impressed. What a short memory the Member for Sturt has got. Albanese says the Coalition can't have procedural issues both ways. The fact is the Opposition was two votes short of a majority. He says if two genuine votes can present themselves, then let's talk. Otherwise this is a stunt. Albanese adds that the intelligence committee is not empowered to inquire into individuals. The standing orders forbid it. Albanese is urging the Independents to withdraw their motion. It should not be proceeded with. The motion moved by the Member for Denison would have been clearly out of order. Quick check of the standing orders. While we wait for this vote, enjoy the day in a video. A day of frantic racing around after bits and pieces, fragments of a rolling debate. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison just wants to land his lines about the failure of national security. Unfortunately for him, Labor's Joel Fitzgibbon strolls by. The herd with microphones thunder after Fitzgibbon in hot pursuit. I think we can all laugh at each other's absurdities for a moment here. Fellow cross bencher Rob Oakeshott is seconding the Wilkie motion. The House is dividing. The Acting Speaker, Bruce Scott, seemed a little uncertain whether he should call one. The disposition of the chamber was unclear. It's that kind of day. Actually not quite, here is another suspension of the standing orders. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie this time. He wants a parliamentary inquiry by the joint committee on intelligence into the facts of this case involving the Egyptian man. This incident, Wilkie says, is a cause for very serious concern It's important that someone, sombody get to the bottom of this. This is a matter the parliament should consider. The vote on the suspension of the standing orders was negated. Over, and out. Perfect chamber shot from Mike Bowers. 101 days to poll day. Strategy mode. After Albanese's analysis of the Coalition's dull predictability on the launching of suspensions of the standing orders, Speaker Anna Burke asks if the motion should be agreed to. The House is dividing. What's Albanese talking about? "Wheat for weapons" was a scandal concerning kickbacks that the Australian Wheat Board paid to the Iraqi Governmment in contravention of UN rules. This happened under the Howard Government. And Haneef? Thanks to the Law Council for this quick summary: Dr Mohamed Haneef, an Indian national, was arrested at Brisbane airport on 2 July 2007 in connection with a failed London bomb plot. He was held for twelve days before being charged with providing support to a terrorist organisation. The charge was unsustainable and was quickly dropped. However, in the interim Dr Haneef's immigration visa was cancelled on character grounds - a decision which on review was found to be unlawful. The gag motion failed. Albanese is on his feet. We will not be lectured on national security by the party who gave us wheat for weapons and the party who gave us the Haneef scandal. A comment from #politicslive reader Alf McMillan. Here's Liberal backbencher Kelly O'Dwyer's comment on the attempt to gag the debate. A little advice needed? Christopher Pyne knows where to seek it. From Peta Credlin, Tony Abbott's chief of staff. The Prime Minister, having announced an inquiry into this case by the Director-General of Intelligence, has attempted to end Question Time. Not so fast is the view of the Opposition. Michael Keenan is back at the dispatch box attempting to suspend the standing orders. This is a serious breach of national security demanding a parliamentary debate. Keenan: This is an urgent matter. We are dealing with a grave breach of national security and nobody will take responsibility for it. Anthony Albanese then moves to shut Keenan down with a gag. I move the member be no longer heard. Bells are ringing. Here is the statement Brendan O'Connor made to parliament just prior to Question Time. He has confirmed that his predecessor in the immigration portfolio, Chris Bowen, was in fact briefed about the Egyptian case. O'Connor has previously said otherwise. O'Connor: I was advised that the former Minister for Immigration was not briefed on the matter of the Egyptian detainee. I was subsequently advised, that the former Minister’s office received a submission relating to this matter on 28 September 2012. The submission was not signed by the former Minister. This submission related to the complex issues surrounding the detainee’s capacity to apply for a protection visa. The Department has advised me that the submission held by the former Minister’s office was not returned to the Department by the then Minister’s office. I can confirm that this submission was not provided to me or my office upon appointment as Minister for Immigration and Citizenship. The Department has also advised that this submission, relating to this detainee’s capacity to apply for a protection visa, has not been resubmitted to me. No matters relating to the detainee were raised with me or my office prior to 17 April 2013, as I advised the Parliament last night. The brief of 17 April 2013 related to the movement of this detainee from the Inverbrackie to Villawood detention facility. I also advise the House that the detainee has always remained in detention since arriving in Australia. Tony Abbott is back with a question to the Prime Minister. Given a convicted jihadist was left behind a pool fence, given everyone in the government was in the dark, given the Prime Minister refused to accept briefings on this matter, how is this not a major national security failing of your government? Julia Gillard is asked by the Speaker to ignore the final inference. The Prime Minister says today I have directed the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Securty to examine this case. The Prime Minister continues her answer. The Leader of the Opposition does not know more about this than the Director-General of ASIO. She says she was briefed on the case after the matter was raised in Senate Estimates. Tony Abbott is back to the Prime Minister. When were you, or your national security adviser, informed of this serious security breach? Julia Gillard: The facts are these. The person he refers to was always in detention. I refer the Leader of the Opposition to the statement by the Immigration Minister just before Question Time. Julie Bishop persists with a supplementary to Dreyfus. What action did you take after the briefing on April 24? Did you brief the national security committee of Cabinet? Albanese wants the question ruled out of order. You can't talk about Cabinet is his argument. That would be breaking the law. Speaker Anna Burke says the question is in order. Dreyfus says he won't be talking about Cabinet. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop has a question for the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Has the Attorney been advised on the case of the Egyptian by ASIO - when, and what action have you taken? Dreyfus: What a disgrace this opposition is. This remarks prompts generalised uproar. Mr Dreyfus says he was briefed on the case on 24 April. He says ASIO is properly resourced to do security assessments. He points to the ASIO triage system. Is the Opposition saying it knows how to do security assessments better than ASIO? If so how, and what would it cost? Michael Keenan has a question for Jason Clare, Minister for Home Affairs. Why didn't the police brief you on the Egyptian sooner than they did? Clare: I won't second guess the Federal Police about what they think it's approrpiate to brief me on. Jason Clare says when he was briefed, he recommended cooperation with other agencies. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is continuing to pursue Brendan O'Connor over the Egyptian. Why weren't you informed about this man for seven months? O'Connor ploughs on. At all times our agencies have worked together. This person has been in detention at all times. Morrison persists. If you thought Inverbrackie was ok to detain this person, why did you move him? O'Connor says officials moved him on advice from the agencies. This was the correct course of action. Suspension of the standing orders in the offing perhaps? Manager of government business Anthony Albanese pays a visit to the cross bench. Meanwhile the Shadow Minister for Border Protection Michael Keenan wants Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor to explain why he told him in a letter in May that the assessment of the Egyptian man had started, but told parliament yesterday that it hadn't. Brendan O'Connor says Keenan referred to three cases in the May letter. His response took that into account. O'Connor says the assessment of the Egyptian man had not commenced at the time the letter was sent. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie comes at the same issue from the left. He's wanting particulars about processing of asylum seekers on Manus Island. Why can't journalists go and check the facility out? The Prime Minister says the government has been open and transparent about the Manus facility. Wilkie begs to differ. Tony Abbott has switched the attack to boats. Given a convicted jihadist terrorist was held in a family detention facility for months due to a clerical error, will the Prime Minister agree her policies have made Australia less safe? Julia Gillard: The Leader of the Opposition is out there trying to create fear. All of this fear raised by an Opposition, that if it was clear and if it was truthful, would admit their policies amount to nothing. The Prime Minister says the Coalition creates fear in the community but won't take legislative action to stop the boats. We make no connection between Treasurer Wayne Swan's answers and Kevin Rudd needing oxygen on the backbench. Hockey is back on the debt cap. Why don't you just fess up, you've made a mess of this haven't you? Why are you leaving it to someone else to clean up your mess? Swan declares that he wants ten questions on this subject. A Dorothy Dixer allowing the Prime MInister to be ebullient about the passage through the House of Representatives of the Gonski package earlier today. Julia GIllard: You would think in this parliament there could be a moment of unity. But unfortunately today, we didn't see that moment of unity today. Speaker Anna Burke. The time for member's statements has expired, in accordance with standing order 43, are there any questions without notice? Here we go for Question Time. Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey opens on the debt cap. It will be breached won't it? Will the Treasurer now apologise for breaking another promise to the Australian people? Treasurer Wayne Swan says Treasury officials have provided face value figures on the debt cap today. Debt in this country is low. Our public finances are in great shape. This (question) just demonstrates how those opposite want to talk down our economy day after day. Having been moved aside by the Prime Minister, Senator Crossin is a big fan of rank-and-file preselection ballots. She's endorsing one now in the Victorian seat of Batman. This must happen. Sky TV journalist David Lipson asks whether she's "forgiven Julia Gillard." Crossin: I'm certainly getting on with what my next challenge in life will be. It's taken a while to get through that. But I know there will be another door. Too much sermonising? Never! Labor's public dialogue with itself concerning the current deficiencies continues. ALP Northern Territory Senator Trish Crossin has bobbed up on a panel on Sky TV. Senator Crossin was recently moved aside somewhat brusquely by the Prime Minister in favour of Nova Peris as Labor's main senate candidate in the NT. Crossin says Labor MPs know what the policies are and what they mean, but voters don't. Crossin: I do think there are some problems with the messaging. We talk about Gonski. People say to me, what's that about? Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey is out now in one of the courtyards responding to the national accounts figures. He's pressed on what the Coalition would do to boost domestic demand and make life better for business. Hockey is asked whether business would thank him for delaying the introduction of the Coalition's paid parental leave scheme (which in its current iteration at least, imposes a levy on business). Hockey: You will see our initiative in that regard prior to the election. I'm not getting into speculation about where we are at. Combet is asked to reflect on what might have been if Kevin Rudd's carbon pollution reduction scheme had passed the parliament. Small laugh from Combet. It's unknowable. Who knows what would have been? My life might have been a bit easier if the CPRS had gone through. Combet is asked whether - like on uranium - the Labor Party will take a stand on environmental grounds, but gradually back track on the carbon pricing mechanism. No, Combet says. This issue has been thrashed out comprehensively within Labor and the labour movement. The carbon price will endure and so will Labor's commitment to it. The Labor Party is committed to tackling climate change. Combet is asked to commit to serving a full term if Labor loses in September. He provides a very elegant non-commital to this question. He's standing, he's committed to fighting, and to Labor winning. I'm still hungry to do more. (That's a bit short of an unequivocal yes.) Combet is asked to substantiate allegations made in his speech that Coalition people have been bullying banks and agencies, like the Clean Energy Finance Corporation. Combet says he doesn't have to reveal private activity. These (interventions) have been quite public. Combet says Tony Abbott in government will not repeal the carbon price. The game has already changed. Combet: Like the floating of the dollar and the opening up of Australia’s economy by the Hawke and Keating Governments, the Clean Energy Future plan is in the great tradition of Labor reforms. It came as no surprise to me that recent polling has seen a turnaround in support for the carbon price. Essential Media found that support for the Government’s carbon pricing policy has increased by six points since January to 43 per cent – at the same time opposition to carbon pricing is down by seven points to also stand at 43 per cent. Another poll found that only 20 per cent of swinging voters wanted the carbon price repealed. The fact that the policy itself is decreasingly contentious, and demonstrably working, is worth careful consideration in the context of politics in future months and years. A scare campaign will only take Tony Abbott so far. He already thinks he has won the next election, but it stretches credulity to contend that a double dissolution election on the issue is already in the bag as well. Tony Abbott knows this. And let’s not forget that he is a first-class political opportunist. His aim is simply to win Government. If he does, my contention is that he cannot and will not repeal the carbon price. Climate Change Minister Greg Combet is now a fair way into his address to the National Press Club. The carbon price is working exactly as intended, Combet says. It creates a powerful incenitve to reduce pollution. As a market mechanism it's ensuring pollution is reduced in efficient ways. The Coalition's policy would pursue higher cost ways of reducing pollution, rather than trusting the efficiency of the market. He suggests the Coalition's policy is a dud. Tony Abbott, Combet says, has been frightening people on low incomes about the impact of the carbon price on the cost of living. Swan is back now to the question he didn't want to engage with earlier - why do you get no credit? He says the government has lost paint politically because it has taken on controversial reforms, like carbon pricing, against substantial political opposition. He's asked does he agree with backbencher Laurie Ferguson that the Prime Minister needs to get more involved in the boats issue? She has been full bore in trying to put together a comprehensive response. We will continue to do everything within our power to get a full suite of policies. Any comment on ill-discipline in caucus? The public who elect us expect us to come here and deal with the big issues. I think its about time that a few people who are out there put a sock in it basically. Swan is making the point in general terms that it would be good for the economy if the Australian dollar started to fall (as it has recently). Swan: I don't speculate about the pathway of the dollar. But he says he always thought the Australian dollar would begin to fall when the US economy started to recover. If you want to get across all the details of the new national accounts data, here's a news wrap. Growth is good. Mike Bowers caught the Treasurer on his way to his media conference. Swan is asked given the strong numbers, why is the government getting no credit? The Treasurer retorts that he'd like to stick to explaining the data for now. He says getting so quickly to a question like that might indicate one of the government's problems. (Presumably he means the fog of media interpretation.) Wayne Swan says there may well be doomsday predictions "from predictable quarters" courtesy of the new national accounts data. But here's his summary. Swan: When you look at today's national accounts I think they are a reminder of what Australia has achieved over the past five years. We have an economy in transition, we have a high dollar which is making that transition harder. But the reality is the economy is in good shape, and the economy is handling these transitons very well. Our economy remains a stand out in terms of the developed would. The Treasurer Wayne Swan is addressing reporters about the national accounts. Growth is good. Friends, one and all. Kevin Rudd, catching up with folks on the sidelines of that Gonski vote, including the man of the moment, Joel Fitzgibbon. And quick as a flash, the Young Liberals have Senator Bishop's "moment" on YouTube. It's fair to say there's a bit of pressure around the traps. Labor senator Mark Bishop went berko in the economics committee late yesterday. Have a listen. It's right at the end of this sound file. (Note to readers: This post has been corrected from the original. I thought initially this committee hearing was today. It was late yesterday. Apologies.) Why did you move the gag motion Mr Oakeshott? His comment to Guardian Australia? This legislation matters to regional students and Chris Pyne and the Opposition were just mucking around with it. A somewhat unusual move: parliamentary independent (who normally doesn't support gag motions) moves gag motion. It's that kind of day. The Oakeshott gag succeeded. Schools Minister Peter Garrett moved straight to the third reading vote, and the Gonski package went through the House of Representatives on the voices. Independent Rob Oakeshott is taking matters into his own hands in the Gonski debate. He's wanting to bring the package to a conclusion. Oakeshott: We have a backlog over the next few weeks of fifty bills. He's moving a gag order to force the package to the necessary set of votes. Lots of invenctive about this in the chamber. Speaker Anna Burke has warned Christopher Pyne. Oakeshott suggests he's not there to be popular. Treasurer Wayne Swan will address reporters on the new national accounts figures at 12.15pm. Coming up at lunchtime, the Climate Change Minister Greg Combet will address the National Press Club. His theme? How carbon pricing is working. Economics correspondent for The West Australian Shane Wright summarises the new data. A couple of transitions. Defence Minister Stephen Smith is holding a press conference with his Indian counterpart, A.K. Antony. New figures for economic growth have just been released. Gross domestic product is up 0.6% in the March quarter. I gather the pursuit of Joel Fitzgibbon that we pointed to a moment ago resulted in a full lap of the core of the parliament. One TV camera man collided with a pot plant. The camera man is fine, the condition of the pot plant unknown. Meanwhile our parliamentarians attempt to make and thwart laws. The Gonski debate continues. A successsion of Liberals are being cross about amendments to the Gonski package, about the package itself, about the ambiguity around the funding for independent schools. From that most special of categories - only in the Labor Party. David Feeney, Labor senator, candidate for the lower house seat of Batman, would like to let 100 flowers bloom. Ladies first, even if they are saying nasty things about me - like why don't you let a woman stand for Labor's safest seat? Here is the Feeney statement: A successful ALP preselection process in the Federal electorate of Batman requires local party members to be able to choose who they want to fight for Labor values on 14 September. For this to be achieved, ALP members must be able to have the candidate of their choice. That is why I am asking the Victorian Branch of the ALP for Mary-Anne Thomas to be given special dispensation to run for preselection in Batman. Despite Ms Thomas’ lack of eligibility to stand for ALP preselection, she has expressed a desire to do so. In the interests of local members, I wholeheartedly agree that Ms Thomas should be able to stand and compete. Only then will members be able to choose the person they consider most qualified for the job. As I have stated previously, this will be an election fought on values. It will be a fight for better education, better disability support and for jobs. I believe I am the best candidate to fight, to win and to advocate in the interests of Batman. I hope the members of Batman will recognise this and choose me to be their advocate and representative. Whoever is chosen, they will need the party united behind them for the fight of a lifetime on 14 September. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon has been the subject of a rolling media pursuit this morning given his contribution to the cause of Labor unity yesterday. Fitzgibbon went to the Beyond Blue event. This, was his inelegant exit. There should be theme music. By close of business, there probably will be. Jeff Kennett is over in the Mural Hall launching an initiative called man therapy. Christopher Pyne, meanwhile, is continuing in the Gonski debate. Why are we debating legislation for a national agreement when there is no national agreement? Pyne says the government will gag the debate and ram this package through despite the fact it has only two jurisdictions on board - New South Wales and the ACT. Shadow Education Minister Christopher Pyne derides the package as a sham. Pyne says it's unclear what additional funds independent schools will get under the new arrangements. He raises Peter Garrett's handling of the insulation roll-out. The pink batts scheme was part of the economic stimulus packages the Rudd Government rolled out during the global financial crisis. (Garrett was the responsible minister for that scheme at the time. He was moved out of the portfolio when the implementation of the scheme was revealed to be a mess.) The inference being - how can you trust this government to implement anything properly? Quick glance in to the House of Representatives. This morning, as we've foreshadowed, a parliamentary debate on the Gonski package. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is arguing the Coalition has no interest in education reform. He's also defending the government's record in education policy. There's one last thing to do. Garrett: We are willing to do the last and most important bit of work which is a needs-based funding system for schools. No school will be worse off. They will only be worse off if Tony Abbott abolishes this package. Speaking of loving this city, the Centenary of Canberra is bringing many small festive joys to the frozen limestone plains this year. One of these was last night - parliament hosted the AFL Hall of Fame induction dinner. With footy players in town, some denizens of the parliament clapped hands and gushed like school kids. I'm not naming names. Opposition leader Tony Abbott is a GWS man, but put aside club loyalty for a chat with Collingwood coach Nathan Buckley. Just because I can. I'm going to post another of Mike Bowers' beautiful stills from around Lake Burley Griffin this morning. Love this city, even when it's a freezer. Here's the Prime Minister, on Gonski, on Twitter, a few minutes ago. Bit curious, this Tweet a few minutes ago from Queensland Premier Campbell Newman. I think the Premier is advising Queensland representatives in Canberra to vote against the Gonski legislation (introduced yesterday) which reduces funding (effectively) for school systems outside the new deal. Of course if Queensland signed up to the Gonski package, they'd get the extra money. Right? Before the day drives us relentlessly forward, let's stand still and work out where we are with this story about the Egyptian man detained at Inverbrackie despite being the subject of an Interpol red notice. The ABC's Sabra Lane did a top notch 'the story so far' package for radio this morning. A transcript of that is here. Let's just set out the known facts before politics weaves its peculiar magic later on. This man arrived in Australia early in 2012, and was detained initially on Christmas Island. He was moved to the Inverbrackie centre in the Adelaide Hills in early May 2012. Interpol had issued a red notice for this person in 2001 following a convition in absentia in Egypt for murder and membership of a terrorist group. ASIO says a clerical error resulted in him being sent to Inverbrackie - a low security detention facility. Federal police say they became aware last November of the conviction in Egypt. Officials from the Immigration Department knew about the Interpol notice in February. But neither the Immigration MInister, Brendan O'Connor, nor his predcessor Chris Bowen, knew anything about the case until April this year, when the first media reports surfaced. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison: We do have this culture of a hands off approach. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor: I've said I want to be told on matters such as this. But it's not for ministers to chase down people. Criticism of the talking points culture within Labor is, of course, entirely legitimate. Both the Rudd and Gillard governments, despite their elaborate communications apparatus, have both been weak in selling their agenda and their policies. I think this statement is uncontentious. It's just a bit funny to hear supporters of Kevin Rudd railing against a culture that Rudd in fact imposed on the party in Opposition, and deepened and systematised in government. I did a fly-on-the-wall piece about Kevin Rudd's office in 2009 that laid out some of these practices. If you are interested, you can read that here. Perhaps their subtle point is Julia Gillard has done nothing at all to dismantle this system, which is entirely true. Clash of the Scots - Doug Cameron versus Julia Gillard's communications director, John McTernan. Labor could sell tickets. One man who always resists calls to pull his head in is Labor backbencher, Senator Doug Cameron. Cameron took his lilting Scottish brogue to ABC News Breakfast this morning. His advice to the Prime Minister is ignore the spin doctors. Cameron: She should do what I do - don't read the nonsense every morning to tell you what your spin lines is. Be (yourself). She's a talented, effective politician, but she's got to be herself. For what it's worth, and whether she'll listen to me or not, that would be my advice to the Prime Minister. (I've created a hashtag: #stopthespindoctors) On the general point of caucus discipline, Cameron reflected thus: What's wrong with a bit of colour and movement? What's wrong with people actually saying what they think? What's wrong with different views in the Labor Party? Readers with us yesterday will know that the Labor caucus had one of the days the Labor caucus has when confronted with the existential nightmare that is September 14. It was a day of outbursts, capers and reflections. Kevin Rudd has this morning sailed up to the doors of parliament to advise colleagues to have a Bex and a lie down. Rudd: Everyone, and I mean everyone, needs to pulls their heads in. Politics live, sadly, lacks an irony font. Good morning and welcome to the day after the Socceroos drew in Japan - and the day before tonight’s State of Origin clash. Mike Bowers' beautiful opening still this morning looking across Lake Burley Griffin to Government House has me thinking about gorillas in the mist. Odd perhaps, but just a reflection on the goings on in this building. The morning has opened with the Coalition pursuing vigorously the case of the Egyptian man held in low security immigration detention for eight months despite being flagged by Interpol. I think we can predict this issue will be front and centre of the Coalition's political attack on the government today. A quick survey of the landscape. (Difficult that, given the clutter.) The Gonski school reform package has joined the other issues which have been running for much of this parliamentary week. The legislation came into parliament yesterday. The Coalition failed last night in an effort to disallow the Gillard Government’s marine park management plans. Our news wrap this morning includes a guest Tweet from Labor MP Andrew Leigh noting this result. I’d also like to congratulate Fairfax Media sketch writer Jacqueline Maley for producing the pithiest lead paragraph of the morning. As the 43rd Parliament lurches towards its nether end, the vibe around the place grows increasingly postmodern. Which is to say - deconstructed, self-referential, culturally relative and, in select cases, more incoherent than the poetry of typewriter-bound monkeys. Let’s run through this morning’s main political stories. 457s, boats and caucus Employers will be legally required to search locally to fill low-skilled job vacancies before taking on temporary foreign workers on 457 visas, in a crackdown Labor strategists had hoped would ease voter concerns about the government’s handling of immigration issues. The immigration minister, Brendan O’Connor, briefed the Labor caucus on Tuesday on the controversial crackdown, revealing the laws would require “market testing” for “nominated non-graduate occupations” and would prevent businesses from hiring 457 workers at arm’s length through labour hire firms. But desperate Labor backbenchers are demanding Julia Gillard address the asylum issue head-on instead of trying to divert the debate to the use of 457 visas. (Guardian Australia) In a meeting of Labor MPs described by some as "surreal, with a feeling of resignation", the Prime Minister was given an ultimatum by a key supporter, western Sydney MP Laurie Ferguson, to turn the public debate on asylum-seekers in Labor's favour or the government would be "dead" in the party's heartland. (The Australian, paywalled) Comeback Kevin .. but you’ll have to actually stand this time Kevin Rudd has been told by his key supporters that they would support another tilt at the leadership but he would have to declare his hand and not leave them hanging. Two senior Labor MPs confirmed discussions had been held over the past two days with the former PM. He was told he could still take the leadership if he wanted it. "But no one is going out on a limb again this time," said one. Another MP confirmed Mr Rudd had again been approached and was told they would still back him. (news.com) Gonski The Gillard government has introduced its centrepiece Gonski education reforms to parliament with a warning that non-participating states will miss out on the cash, while a significant business group announced their backing for the reforms. (Guardian Australia) NBN/Asbestos Union leaders are demanding a halt to the National Broadband Network to ensure an end to asbestos exposure as they put forward a plan to dispatch independent assessors across the project to check on the danger to public health. The union push seeks direct funding from Telstra and the government company building the network, NBN Co, to help federal authorities conduct random safety checks on dozens of subcontractors working on the $37.4 billion project. (The Australian, paywalled) Construction of the wireless section of the national broadband network is running so far behind schedule that less than half the planned homes and offices will be connected by its June 30 target. NBN Co has blamed tall trees blocking signals and a lack of accurate addresses for the slow rollout. But experts said inadequate planning and poor management were also to blame for the delays, which now mean that two of the NBN’s three delivery systems – wireless, fibre and satellite – will miss their original targets. (The Australian Financial Review, paywalled) WorkChoices campaign: the sequel Emotive advertisements depicting families whose lives are being undermined by unjust workplace practices will begin screening tonight, as a part of a major campaign push by unions against insecure work. With 101 days to go to the federal election, the ACTU advertisements will debut during tonight's rugby league State of Origin telecast and will evoke memories of the 2007 Your Rights At Work campaign that was widely credited with helping to bring down the Howard government. (The Australian, paywalled) Marine management plans survive disallowance motion Coalition just voted to scrap Australia's 2.3 million sq km of marine reserves, including one initiated by the Howard Govt. We won 71-70. You can follow the live updates on the blog, and if inclined, join the Twitter conversation by using the hashtag #politicslive Follow me on Twitter @murpharoo

results.webTitle: Australian Politics Weekly podcast: polling day beckons

fields.headline: Australian Politics Weekly podcast: polling day beckons
fields.trailText: Political editor Lenore Taylor and press gallery veterans Michelle Grattan and Malcolm Farr join Katharine Murphy to discuss this week's news from the Australian election campaign
fields.bodyText: Politicians scramble to make a final impression as polling day edges closer. Will Tony Abbott's careful, careful approach get him over the line or could Kevin Rudd create a miracle? Guardian polling punter and Stanford politics professor Simon Jackman explains national and local electorate polls and their relationship with betting odds. Jackman reveals the stats and numbers as he sees them, with surprising predictions. Also a wrap of the election race to date with press gallery journalists; Guardian Australia's Lenore Taylor, The Conversation's Michelle Grattan and Malcolm Farr of news.com.au. It's a wide-ranging chat summing up the campaign on both sides, key differences, policy failures, covering the election in the 'virtual bus' and a whole lot more. Leave your thoughts below.

results.webTitle: Iain Duncan Smith's Commons statement on universal credit:  Politics live blog

fields.headline: Iain Duncan Smith's Commons statement on universal credit:  Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Treasury questions in the Commons.
fields.bodyText: • The National Audit Office has renewed its criticism of the Department for Work and Pensions' handling of the universal credit programme, saying that it did not achieve value for money in its early stages and that the timetable could slip again. (See 3.46pm.) Commenting on the report Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, said: The long delayed DWP accounts confirm what everyone already knew: Iain Duncan Smith is in deep denial about the extent of his failure to deliver universal credit. While IDS accused critics of his programme today of ‘nit-picking’ the view of the auditor general is clear: universal credit has not achieved value for money, and millions of pounds worth of IT will soon be worthless. Will David Cameron now finally step in to get a grip of this failing department? • An investigation into who was to blame for the woeful mismanagement of the TradeMark Southern Africa (TMSA) programme has been launched by the UK government after a scathing report by the aid watchdog that prompted its closure last week. • Downing Street has revealed details of who David Cameron at the national memorial event for Nelson Mandela in South Africa today. This is from the Press Association. Downing Street said that Cameron had the opportunity for brief, informal conversations with a number of world leaders and elder statesmen over the course of the memorial event. Among those he spoke with were Obama and his wife Michelle and three former US presidents - George W Bush, Bill Clinton and Jimmy Carter. Cameron gave his condolences to Hollande over the deaths of two of his country's soldiers in the Central African Republic, and also spoke with his predecessor Sarkozy. He spoke with Mandela's successor as president of South Africa, Thabo Mbeki, as well as the last president of the apartheid era, FW de Klerk, and the former archbishop of Cape Town and prominent anti-apartheid campaigner Desmond Tutu. Cameron was seated next to New Zealand's prime minister John Key during the memorial, and also spoke with the prime minister of Australia, Tony Abbott, the prime minister of Denmark, Helle Thorning-Schmidt and the President of Senegal, Macky Sall. The PM travelled to the FNB Stadium with Nick Clegg and Sir John Major, having been on the same commercial flight as them from the UK, and he also spoke with Tony Blair, said Downing Street. • Cameron has told the Spectator in an interview that he has an increasingly strong belief that the Conservatives could govern better if they did not have to share power with the Lib Dems. Here's the quote. Increasingly, today, I feel very strongly and see very clearly the case for more accountable, more decisive and active government ... [Without the Lib Dems] I think we could go further on welfare reform, to sharpen work incentives and get more people out of poverty, I think that on the European question I can see very clearly now what needs to be done in terms of our relationship with Europe, in terms of the European convention on human rights and the way the human rights act works. I can see when it comes to building a pro-enterprise economy how we go further and faster on backing entrepreneurship, cutting business taxes, getting our economy moving ... Because of what I see as the problems facing Britain — and what I want to do next as prime minister — I feel very passionately that I want single party government. Just to be clear, Cameron was talking about after 2015, not disclosing a secret plan to ditch the Lib Dems before then. In the interview Cameron also did not deny using the phrase "green crap". Instead, when asked about this, he just said he didn't "recall using it". • Low-income households spend an average of £2.10 a person a day on groceries, having cut their daily food budget drastically since the summer, according to the latest instalment of a survey on the impact of welfare reform. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Here's an afternoon reading list. • Iain Martin at the Telegraph says he does not think the Tory strategy for countering Ukip will work. Have you noticed that members of the Tory high command avoid mentioning Ukip in public unless it is completely unavoidable or interviewers bring it up? After dismissing Ukippers as fruitcakes, loonies and closet racists in 2006, David Cameron decided to say nothing on the subject in the hope that Nigel Farage would go away. As of late last year the Prime Minister also started emphasising policies that might appeal to Ukippers, without mentioning Ukip itself. So far, it hasn't worked ... What the Tory leadership seems to be settling on instead is making a direct appeal to Ukip voters in the run-up to the election and during the campaign, explaining that their concerns have been taken seriously but that if they vote for Farage it will let in Ed Miliband. This is, despite claims to the contrary, completely true, although that may not make a difference. Have a pint with a Ukipper and it is not unusual to hear them say that they don't care if they let Miliband in. Supposedly that will reveal how rotten the whole parliamentary edifice is which will bring it crashing down in some ill-defined way. These are Conservatives who are so ultra-conservative that they want, er, a revolution. Will the Tory appeal work? Vote for David Cameron or the country gets it? "It" being a dose of Miliband and policies designed to do what President Hollande has done for France… Of course it will work with some voters, who will recoil when reminded that Ed Miliband loves high taxes, big government and political correctness. But all of them? Or enough to get Ukip back down to 3 per cent? I doubt it very much. • Sarah Lyall in the New York Times interviews David Miliband about his new life in New York. Mr. Miliband is a household name in England. But in New York, where he has installed himself, his wife and their 6- and 8-year-old sons in a rented apartment on the Upper West Side, he faces the opposite phenomenon: Except for super-keen followers of semi-recent Western European diplomatic history, no one has any idea who he is. This includes fellow parents at his sons’ schools, who, initially at least, failed to notice the foreign celebrity in their midst. (Mr. Miliband asked that his sons’ schools names not be published.) “It’s a bit of a different challenge,” he said recently over lunch at a Midtown restaurant, near the organization’s headquarters. “I’m obviously much less a figure of interest for the American press. Here we’re trying to get interest,” he added, alluding to his role at the International Rescue Committee, “whereas there we were trying to shake off interest.” • Gerry Hassan at Newsnet Scotland says the left has a problem with patriotism. Right-wing patriotism has embraced respect for British traditions, history and institutions such as the monarchy. It has articulated the seamless version of the history of the four nations of the UK: one that talks about continuity and conservation and downplays all the ruptures and upheavals. Left-wing patriotism has consistently had a problem and been thrown by the right. They have baulked at the stories and crimes of Empire, imperial wars and militarism ... One reason that the left has had such a problem is the power of the continuity story of Britain. The people’s Britain, with its struggles and radicalism, the G.D.H. Cole account of ‘the common people’ with its English and Scottish variants expressed so eloquently by E.P. Thompson and Tom Johnston respectively, never sidelined the Whig account of British history. There is also the perennial problem the left had with nationalism. British left-wingers have consistently tried to deny that they are British nationalists, witness their age-old cry, ‘I am an internationalist not a nationalist’. So the Michael Foots, Tony Benns, George Galloways and, closer to home, Brian Wilsons of this world, have been comfortable embracing numerous national liberation movements – Vietnamese, Palestinian, Venezuelan – all of which are nationalist, but tied themselves in knots at home. My summary of the NAO report (see 3.46pm) reports that the NAO says that the Department for Work and Pensions is itself admitting there is uncertainty as to whether claimants will be on universal credit (UC) by the end of 2017. I've now seen the DWP accounts (pdf). Here's note 16d from those accounts, the one cited by the NAO as evidence that, internally at least, the DWP is admitting to uncertainty about the timetable. There remains uncertainty regarding the useful economic life of the UC software pending the development of an alternative digital solution. The current plan is for UC claimants to migrate from the current UC software to a replacement Digital system by the end of 2017. The Department will continue to monitor both the use and the useful life of the UC software. Here are the main points from what the NAO report has to say about universal credit. • Amyas Morse, the head of the NAO, says he is even more certain than he was in September that the Department for Work and Pensions has not achieved value for money with UC. In my report “Universal Credit: early progress” (HC 621) published in September 2013, I concluded that at this early stage of the Universal Credit programme the Department had not achieved value for money. I consider this judgement is reinforced by the information in the Department’s 2012-13 Annual Report and Accounts. • The NAO suggests the timetable could slip again. These are considerable sums that the Department is proposing to invest, in a programme where there are significant levels of technical, cost and timetable uncertainty. • And the NAO says the DWP itself says there is "uncertainty" as to whether claimants will be on UC by the end of 2017. Originally the DWP said everyone would be on the benefit by then. Last week it said around 700,000 claimants would not move over in time, but it said that all other claimants would meet this deadline. The NAO report suggests that, internally, the department believes this is questionable. The Department acknowledges in Note 16d to the accounts, that there is uncertainty over the useful economic life of the existing Universal Credit software pending the development of the alternative digital solution and uncertainty over whether Universal Credit claimants will be able to migrate from the current IT infrastructure to the new digital solution by December 2017. • The NAO says that more than £130m invested in UC has been badly spent. At the work and pensions select committee Iain Duncan Smith admitted that the DWP was writing off £40.1m spent on software that was not being. He said that another £91m was being spent on software that would only be used for five years, but he rejected suggestions that this was being wasted. The DWP would get some use out of it, he said. The sum was being written down, not written off. But the NAO says this amounts to a major change. This is a major change in accounting treatment. The Department’s original intention was to use these assets [the ones costing £91m] for at least 15 years and to amortise them over that period. As a result of the decision to migrate to a new digital solution by 2017, the Department now intends to use these assets for only five years and to amortise them over this much shorter period ... While this is the appropriate accounting treatment, it should not detract from the underlying issue that the Department has spent £91.0 million on assets that will only support a limited service for 5 years, with clear consequences for public value. • It says the DWP could have to write off even more money. Without clear and effective management, in the future the Department may also find it needs to impair [ie, write off] some of these new digital assets. • The NAO says there were "considerable weaknesses" in the department's financial controls over UC. Here's a link to the NAO report. And here's a statement from Margaret Hodge, chair of the Commons public accounts committee, on the latest NAO report on universal credit. (See 2.59am.) In 2012-13, the Department for Work and Pensions had to write-off £40.1m for assets that were developed for the implementation of the universal credit system but which they will now never use. They now tell us they will also have to write-off another £91m of assets over the next five years. Whilst these figures are truly shocking, I do not think we have heard the end of this matter and would not be surprised if further write-offs emerge over the coming period. It is deeply depressing that DWP has chosen to pour more money into the existing IT system in what seems like a short-term fix, rather than showing the confidence and foresight to come up with a solution that will truly stand the test of time. Even for those people who transfer to the new benefit, the online system is currently not able to deal with issues like frequent changes of circumstances, claims if a couple splits up, or conditionality. The National Audit Office has put out a report today saying the Department for Work and Pension has not achieved value for money with universal credit. Here's an extract from the news release. In a report today on the 2012-13 accounts of the Department for Work and Pensions, Amyas Morse, the Comptroller and Auditor General, has concluded that the Department has not to date achieved value for money in the development of Universal Credit and to do so in future it will need to learn the lessons of past failures. The C&AG [comptroller and auditor general, the head of the NAO] recommends that the Department properly commission and manage IT development; that it exercise effective financial control over the Universal Credit programme; and that it set realistic expectations for the timescale for delivery. And here's a quote form Amyas Morse, the head of the NAO. I judged in September that, at this early stage of the Universal Credit programme, the Department had not achieved value for money. The underlying issue, highlighted in today’s report, is that the Department has written off £40.1 million on assets it will now never use and spent a further £91 million on assets that will support only a limited service for 5 years, with clear consequences for public value. The report also finds that the amount of money lost through fraud and error in the benefits system is going up. It was £3.5bn in 2012-13 (or 2.1% of the total budget), compared to £3.2bn (2%( the previous year). • Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secreary, has said that Iain Duncan Smith is in denial and failing to admit that universal credit benefit reform is running at least two years late. She made the claim when she tabled an urgent question on the matter in the Commons. Her Labour colleague Dame Anne Begg, who chairs the Commons work and pensions committee, made a similar point in her question to the work and pensions secretary. The secretary of state promised that universal credit would be digital by default. It isn't. He promised that all new claims would be on universal credit by May 2014. They won't. He then promised that 10 areas would be assessing the simplest claims by the end of October. They aren't. So why should anyone believe him when he says that the delivery of universal credit is now on track? In response, Duncan Smith repeatedly said that he had intervened when he realised problems were arising with the project and that he did not want to repeat Labour's mistake of rushing major IT projects. (I've included Begg's quote because it's a textbook example of how to ask a hostile Commons question. It's clear, pithy, factual - and impossible to answer.) • Philip Hammond, the defence secretary, has told MPs in a statement that the government has abandoned its plans to privatise its defence procurement agency because only one group was left bidding for the contract. • The Advisory Council for the Misuse of Drugs has said that the drug ketamine, which is widely used as an anaesthetic for humans and animals, should be reclassified from a class C to a class B drug because of new evidence of the damage it can cause to the bladder. • Maria Miller, the equalities minister, has said that the first same-sex weddings in England and Wales can take place from March 29. • The Office for National Statistics has released figures showing that the cost of providing state pensions to the UK's ageing population is set to grow more than four-fold over the next 50 years if the government does not bring forward increases to the age at which people qualify for payments. • Nick Clegg's Christmas card has been given a festive twist by his three sons, who used the family's iPad to draw a Santa beard and reindeer horns on a photo of their parents. • The Labour MP Andrew Miller has announced that he will stand down at the next election. Iain Duncan Smith v Rachel Reeves - Verdict: Accusing opposition politicians of opportunism is a bit like accusing athletes of being competitive, parents of being protective, models of being vain, or bears of having al fresco toileting habits. It is just what they do. So I hope I'm not being perjorative when I say that today we saw a display of turbo-charged opportunism. A charitable description of what Rachel Reeves and her colleagues were engaged in would be "holding the secretary of state to account", but a more accurate account would be to say that they were engaged in an exercise in trying to make Duncan Smith look weak and useless. And, given the wide gap between his repeated claims that UC is "on time" and reality, it wasn't hard. Rachel Reeves did a pretty effective job, but the single most damaging question probably came from Dame Anne Begg, the Labour chair of work and pensions committee. (See 12.50pm.) I'll post the quote later. In Commons exchanges of this kind you can normally work out who won by seeing who looks most miserable. Today it was clear cut; Duncan Smith looked flustered. Yet, on substance, Duncan Smith had three rather good arguments to deploy that were more robust than anything thrown at him by the other side. First, Labour's record on IT projects of this kind was pretty poor. Second, implementing large IT projects slowly is, on balance, a good idea. And, third, what Labour would actually do about UC remains something of a mystery. All of these are strong points because they are true. So why did Duncan Smith lose? His main problem is that, having stuck so rigidly to the "on time and on budget" mantra, he does not have the grace, humility or dexterity to back down and admit that the timetable has slipped. I suspect it's because he's too thin-skinned and insecure. A minister like Ken Clarke would have pulled this off, dignity intact, quite easily. Guy Opperman, a Conservative MP, says he is at a loss to understand Labour;'s policy. Why is Duncan Smith pursuing his test, learn and implement proposals? Duncan Smith says Opperman is right. He says he has no idea what Labour want. Duncan Smith says in the private sector up to 30-40% is allowed for write-downs and re-writes in IT projects. What is happening within UC is well within this, he says. Labour's Wayne David asks Duncan Smith if he has any credibility. Duncan Smith says he has more credibility than Labour. Duncan Smith accuses Labour of "hypocrisy". They are claiming that all went well under them. But it did not. They wasted billions and billions. Labour's Anne McGuire asks Duncan Smith why he is still in his job, given that he said he was taking such a close interest in the project two years ago. Duncan Smith says that, as result of his intervention, changes were made. Sir Tony Baldry, a Conservative, says Duncan Smith if he heard Rachel Reeves tell the Today programme yesterday that she supported UC. Isn't it strange that she supports it on the airwaves but rubbishes it in the House? Duncan Smith says Reeves also said UC was wasting hundreds of millions of pounds. She has dropped that claim today, he says. If you support a plan, you should support it. But Labour is actually opposed to this, he says. Duncan Smith says he hopes Labour will say sorry by the time of the next election. Labour's Meg Hillier asks what will happen to people on employment and support allowance (a disability benefit). Duncan Smith says this group are known as the "stock". Some Labour MPs complain. Duncan Smith protests, saying this is the term Labour itself used when it was in office. This group will move on to UC in time. Labour's Andy McDonald asks if Duncan Smith or any his ministers tried to influence what the public accounts committee said about UC in its recent report. No, says Duncan Smith. Graham Evans, a Conservative, asks Duncan Smith to agree that UC will save money, and that it is currently handling complicated cases. Duncan Smith says the NAO agrees UC will save money. He does not address the second point. Duncan Smith says Labour is the welfare party because it has opposed every welfare reform measure introduced by the government. Labour's Barbara Keeley asks Duncan Smith if he thinks he has the support of Treasury minister. She quotes the unnamed George Osborne ally quoted in Rachel Sylvester's column in the Times. Duncan Smith says he has the support of the Treasury. David Ruffley, a Conservative, says Duncan Smith has the wholehearted support of MPs on the Conservative benches. Labour wasted £25bn on IT projects. It is right to proceed slowly, he says. Duncan Smith agrees. Labour wasted £13bn on NHS IT, he says. Labour's Glenda Jackson asks when UC will be available for everyone. Duncan Smith says Labour rushed out tax credits too quickly. Some £5bn was lost, and 400,000 people were damaged. Labour's Jenny Chapman asks when UC will be able to deal with people with complicated conditions, like being in a couple. Duncan Smiths says some of the reporting in today's papers is wrong. It was always intended to roll it out in stages. Jacob Rees-Mogg, a Conservative, says Labour should remember what the Bible says about motes and beams. They made plenty of IT mistakes. Duncan Smith says the SNP government in Scotland is in "complete denial" about the cost of welfare. He does not know where they would get the money from under independence for their welfare plans. Duncan Smith says Labour did nothing to improve the quality of life for those trying to find work. Labour want to "nit-pick away about this programme" because they have nothing to say on welfare reform. Duncan Smith says the DWP has carried out an exhaustive impairment review (ie, a review of cost being written off). The write off figure is £40m. The accounts are being published today. Labour's Debbie Abrahams asks Duncan Smith if it is on time, or if Duncan Smith is taking his time. Duncan Smith says Labour MPs do not know what they want. He is taking his time with people on disability benefit because they are vulnerable. Labour want to rush these people onto UC early. Dame Anne Begg, the Labour chair of the work and pensions select committee, says Duncan Smith said originally UC would be digital by default. It isn't. He said it would be in place for all new claimants by May 2014. It won't. Why should anyone believe what he says? Duncan Smith says when problems occurred, the government had to address them. Duncan Smith says UC will roll out without damaging a single person, unlike tax credits. Duncan Smith is responding to Reeves. He says Reeves used to work for a bank. She should know the difference between costs being written off and written down. Does she drive a "motor car"? If she did, she would know that its value goes down every year. Perhaps this "lady" needs a lesson in economics. He says Labour left welfare in a mess. But this government has saved £11bn from the welfare bill already. Child poverty is at its lowest level since the 1990s. And pensioner poverty is at its lowest level for 30 years. Rachel Reeves says Duncan Smith said in September UC would roll out on time and on budget. He made the same point in successive weeks. Yet the goal for getting to a certain level of claimants has been delayed by two years. Some £40m has been written off. And another £90m could be written off.Does this mean a new IT system is being built? When did Duncan Smith decide to delay this for two years? Isn't the problem that that Duncan Smith did not know what he wanted when he started. Will people on UC get free school meals? Duncan Smith is in denial. But doubtless he will deny he is denial. Reeves finishes with the quote from an unnamed minister in today's Rachel Sylvester column: “There are some ministers who improve in office and others, like IDS, who show they are just not up to it." Iain Duncan Smith says this is a major reform that will transform Britain for the better. The government is focusing on its "safe and secure delivery". It is pushing ahead with the culture and business change in job centres. Yesterday he announced his plans for the next stage, and discussed them at length with the work and pensions committee. John Bercow, the Speaker, intervenes. He says the constant heckling is discourteous. Duncan Smith says the DWP will further develop the work started by the Government Digital Service for this project. Next summer the departement will start to deal with universal credit (UC) claims from couples. From the autumn it will start to deal with claims from families. The pilots show 90% of people are claiming online. And 78% say they are confident of their ability to budget on the basis of having monthly payments. Responding to heckling from Labour's front bench, he will not make the mistakes made when tax credits were introduced. The government will test, learn and implement, he says. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, asks for a statement about universal credit. Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, spent more than two hours taking questions from the work and pensions committee yesterday about universal credit (UC). I covered it in detail on my live blog. Today we're getting an encore; he's answering an urgent Commons question tabled by Rachel Reeves, his Labour opposite number. It will start in five minutes. Yesterday's hearing received widespread coverage in today's papers. Broadly, the stories focused on two news lines. • The Guardian and others focused on the amount of UC investment being written off. Here's the start of the Guardian's story. The government has increased its initial write-off of a failed IT system for universal credit by £6m to £40.1m, but acknowledged that a further £90m of software is likely to be written down in its value over the next five years. The precise loss to the taxpayer will depend on how much of the existing IT software is retained after it has been merged with a new IT system being developed by the Cabinet Office's Government Digital Service. Universal credit, which brings together six existing benefits, is seen as potentially the biggest change to welfare since the second world war. Ministers had to concede last week that it had fallen behind schedule and would not be completed by 2017 as originally planned. Seeking to explain the £40m write-off, Mike Driver, finance director general at the Department for Work and Pensions, said: "There is no use for the IT code built to run the computer systems. It has no future value. It is not going to generate any future return for the department." • The Independent and others focused on the revelation that UC cannot yet deal with claims from couples. Here's the start of the Independent's story. The computer system for the flagship welfare reform was not yet ready for couples who make a claim, Iain Duncan Smith, the Work and Pensions Secretary, admitted on Monday. He also announced that the Government had written off more than £40m worth of software designed for the new Universal Credit, while a further £90m of equipment would be worthless in five years’ time. In a bruising session with MPs, Mr Duncan Smith said his reforms remained on track despite a series of delays and set-backs. He denied that the launch of Universal Credit, which is due to replace six benefits and tax credits with a single payment, had turned into a “debacle” and insisted the scheme was “doable”. And here's one more item of background reading. It's a Telegraph blog from John McTernan yesterday. Is Iain Duncan Smith a Stalinist? Not in the Stalingrad way – "there are no prisoners of war, only traitors" – but in the mad, bad 5-Year-Plan way? You know, when production was driven by central planning and workers were exhorted to work harder to turn 5 into 4. I ask because I still can’t quite believe his performance on the Today programme this morning. He stated that Universal Credit is "on time". This is his mantra now. He told the House on October 14 that UC "will be on time and within budget". He repeated his assurance on November 18 when he will have known that, under cover of the Autumn Statement, he was going to slip out an announcement that would exclude claimants of ESA from UC ... Ambitious, but clear. What do we know now? No new claimants will be put onto Universal Credit before the next election. So, instead of 12 million people moving to UC over four years, they will all be moved in two years. In fact, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility the migration to Universal Credit will not start until the summer of 2016, and be concluded by the end of 2017. Four years work will be delivered in 18 months. This is either truly Stakhanovite or utterly delusional. Expect many more delays. The Ministry of Justice has announced plans to enable people who get poor service from claims management companies to get compensation. Here's an extract from the news release. In future complaints about claims firms will be handled by the Legal Ombudsman, which holds powers to force companies to pay compensation or provide other forms of redress. The change planned for next year will mean the Claim Management Regulation (CMR) unit at the Ministry of Justice will also be able to dedicate more resources to tackling bad practice by companies in the sector, which specialise in helping people make claims for compensation for accident injuries and mis-sold financial products like Payment Protection Insurance (PPI). In Treasury questions a Labour MP asked whether the government would let the Office for Budget Responsibility audit Labour's plans before the general election. Responding for the government, David Gauke, the Treasury minister, said the government did not want the OBR to become embroiled in political controversy. Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, is responding to the universal credit UQ. It will be at 12.30pm. I will be covering it in detail. There's an urgent question on universal credit. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. • Tim Shipman in the Daily Mail says ministers are trying to block the publication of a critical report about HS2. Ministers are trying to use emergency powers to ban the publication of a damning report into the £50billion High Speed Rail project. Cabinet Office Minister Francis Maude and Transport Secretary Patrick McLoughlin called on David Cameron to veto releasing a report that labelled HS2 ‘red/amber’ and in danger of failing. They claim it would cause ‘political and presentational difficulties’ and could seriously damage the project. HS2 critics say the gagging attempt is ‘absolutely disgraceful’ and are using Freedom of Information laws to demand that the Project Assessment Review, from November 2011, be published. The Information Commissioner has rejected ministers’ objections and ordered them to release the report. But in a leaked letter to Mr Cameron, circulated to ministers, Mr Maude and Mr McLoughlin urge that an emergency veto be put in place. • Christopher Hope in the Daily Telegraph says David Cameron has angered rightwingers by saying the Foreign Office will take charge of the EU renegotiations. David Cameron has infuriated Conservative right wingers by suggesting he will entrust the renegotiation of Britain's relationship with Europe to the Euro-phile Foreign Office. The Prime Minister made clear he wanted to use the Foreign Office to lead Britain's renegotiation with the European Union describing it as “one of the most expert groups of people I could have”. Eurosceptic Tory MPs said they were dismayed about the comments, with one saying that he had “no faith in the foreign office whatsoever because it is very pro-European”. Asked if he "intended to appoint a negotiator to take forward Britain's renegotiations with the EU", Mr Cameron said: “I have got in William Hague and his team probably one of the most expert groups of people I could have. " • Rachel Sylvester in the Times (paywall) says universal credit is a shambles. There are also tensions within the political and Civil Service tribes. George Osborne, a hard-headed pragmatist, has never understood Mr Duncan Smith’s missionary zeal: he is more interested in saving pounds than souls. Now his allies openly question the Work and Pensions Secretary’s ability to run a major department. “There are some ministers who improve in office and others, like IDS, who show they are just not up to it,” says one minister close to the Chancellor. • Steve Richards in the Independent says that MPs should get their big pay rise - and that we need more MPs able to take a big national leadership role. Another very big part of the problem is that it is completely taboo for leaders to suggest that part of an MP’s job description is to be a figure big enough to devise and implement national policies and become a powerful advocate for them. Instead the qualifications are increasingly related to connections with a constituency and a tireless commitment to the local area. On one level the number of independent-minded Tory MPs elected in the 2010 intake is impressive – backbenchers who care more about speaking for their constituents than they do about becoming a minister. Equally it is a noble ambition to become a social worker, which is how some MPs proudly describe their role to me. Recently I asked a former MP and cabinet minister – now in a much better paid job outside politics – about the main differences. He said that it was almost impossibly demanding to be a cabinet minister and then return to the constituency at weekends to deal with a mountain of local issues, pointing out that in France ministers are spared the additional responsibilities of constituency work. This would never happen here because the connection with the constituency is so highly prized. But the emphasis on the local comes at the expense of the need for MPs to address national responsibilities. Leading, governing, opposing, communicating, advocating, winning elections are increasingly dependent on a small number of MPs on either side who are seen as up to the job. • Janan Ganesh in the Financial Times (subscription) says the ideological differences between left and right are getting wider. And the deepest faultline of all has emerged this year. No longer do the parties merely disagree over how much government should tax and spend the output generated by the market. They also take different views on the market itself. Under Tony Blair and Gordon Brown, New Labour seldom expressed a judgment as to what the private economy “should” look like. As good Fabians, they limited their role to milking capitalism instead of remaking it. Mr Miliband is a different kind of social democrat: happy to intervene in markets, such as household energy, if he suspects they are rigged against the consumer or, in the fuzzier case of gambling and payday lending, if he believes they are not very nice. His proposal for a state-mandated freeze in prices charged by private energy companies would have been laughed out of town by his predecessors, but it does constitute an answer to the existential question that has dogged Labour since the crash became a fiscal crisis: what is the point of the left when there is no money to spend? Mr Miliband believes inequities can be eased through direct market interventions, which cost little or nothing to the exchequer. It is a contentious answer, pregnant with perverse consequences, but it is an answer. And it leaves the ideological space between the two main parties looking wider than at any time since he was a student. • Gideon Rachman in the Financial Times (subscription) says the West is losing faith in its future. What defines the west? American and European politicians like to talk about values and institutions. But for billions of people around the world, the crucial point is simpler and easier to grasp. The west is the part of the world where even ordinary people live comfortably. That is the dream that makes illegal immigrants risk their lives, trying to get into Europe or the US. Yet, even though the lure of the west remains intense, the western world itself is losing faith in its future. Last week Barack Obama gave one of the bleakest speeches of his presidency. In unsparing terms, the US president chronicled the increasing inequality and declining social mobility that, he says, “pose a fundamental threat to the American dream, our way of life and what we stand for around the world” ... If the erosion of living standards continues, how will western voters react? There are already signs of political radicalisation – with the populist right on the rise in both the US and Europe. But, as yet, there is no real sign that the Tea Party in America or nationalist movements in Europe have a realistic shot at controlling the central government in a large nation. The consensus around globalisation also seems to be holding. Indeed this weekend the World Trade Organisation apparently made a breakthrough in the search for a new global trade deal.
