But while new political movements are not yet ready to smash the established parties in the west, mainstream politicians are having to react to the new economic climate. Rising inequality is increasing the pressure for more redistributive taxes and higher minimum wages on both sides of the Atlantic. Another decade of western economic malaise – or, God forbid, another financial crisis – is likely to see more radical solutions and politicians emerging. Here's Nick Clegg's Christmas card. David Cameron has been tweeting from South Africa. There are two polls around this morning. For the record, here are the figures. ICM for the Guardian Labour: 37% (down 1 point from ICM last month) Conservatives: 32% (up 2) Lib Dems: 12% (down 1) Ukip: 9% (down 1) Labour lead: 5 points (down 3) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 64. YouGov for the Sun Labour: 38% (down 1 point from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 33% (down 1) Ukip: 13% (up 2) Lib Dems: 10% (no change) Government approval: -30 (down 5) Labour lead: 5 points (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 56. I'm afraid we're not exactly at the centre of the news cyclone today. You may want to take a look at Matthew Weaver's Guardian live blog of the Mandela memorial service. For those of you loyal enough to stick with Westminster, I can promise you ... well, not much. The diary is empty. But that should leave me plenty of time to go through the papers and scour the internet. And you never know what will turn up. Here's the agenda for the day. 11am: A report from the Commission on Civil Society and Democratic Engagement is launched at an event in the House of Commons. The commission is an alliance of charities, and the report (pdf) explains how the lobbying bill threatens their ability to campaign. 11.30am: Danny Alexander, chief secretary to the Treasury, takes Treasury questions in the Commons. Alexander is leading for the government because George Osborne is abroad. 12.30pm: The advisory council on the misuse of drugs publishes its report on ketamine. It will recommend reclassifying it from a Class C drug to a Class B drug. 2pm: Labour hosts a summit on A&E services. 2.45pm: Mark Sedwill, permanent secretary at the Home Office, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Labour conference - Ed Balls's speech: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Labour conference - Ed Balls's speech: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Balls' speech
fields.bodyText: That's it from us for the night. Andrew Sparrow will be back in charge tomorrow morning. Have a nice evening. Ed Balls seemed notably relaxed and confident during that interview – perhaps suggesting a newfound confidence in the Labour frontbench. Here are the key points: • Balls said “a really hard and tough look” needed to be taken into the High Speed 2 rail scheme. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," he said, although his comments suggested he was sceptical that it was worth the £50bn price tag. • Balls suggested strongly that health spending would remain ringfenced under a future Labour government. • Balls said Labour was no longer advocating a cut in VAT because it no longer suited the economic circumstances: “Clearly since June the recovery has begun.” • He said he agreed wholeheartedly with Ed Miliband’s comments that any shadow minister briefing against a colleague should be sacked, and said he had never done that himself. The Damian McBride era was “a different place – it really feels like quite a long time ago,” he said. • He said the government’s help to buy scheme was set too high, but he could see the case for a smaller scheme. But building houses was more important, he said. In a final question, Richards asks about Balls's piano lessons and whether that was the only time his mind was clear of politics. Yes, he says. It's really hard to do one thing with one hand and one thing with the other, he says, so it takes "absolute total concentration". It's fabulous, he says. Richards tries to get him to pre-announce Ed Miliband's policy announcements planned for his speech tomorrow, but of course he won't. And with that it finishes. Balls is asked to make more of a distinction between Labour and the other parties. The questioner suggests funding Sure Start children's centres. Balls says these are one of Labour's greatest achievement and it's terrible to see them being closed and having services scaled back. "I'm with you. This will be a very important part of what we do in a Labour government." He jokes that he'll tell them if they promise not to tell anyone. (I've never seen him this relaxed.) Balls is asked to name some more policies for people to take home. He says he could do his whole speech from memory. It's over 4,000 words. He says the biggest thing he was worried about was whether his towel joke would work. But Harriet Harman said: "Fabulous towel joke. I wish I'd thought of it." He lists a number of policies he mentioned in the speech. Tomorrow you'll get "certainly two more manifesto commitments which are there and good and strong - but that's off the record!" Richards asks if Labour is going to reverse the rise in VAT. Balls says he made that case in 2011 for 2011 - but it would not be right for 2015 as the recovery begins. "Clearly since June the recovery has begun. We're not advocating a temporary VAT cut now." Balls suggests Nick Clegg got off scot-free when he announced his free school meals policy without announcing how it would be funded. "Nick Clegg got away with it... Well, not really because people didn't take it that seriously because they think, 'It's Nick Clegg ... '" Balls has a good joke himself, about how John Prescott used to sit at the back of his battle bus cold-calling random people to ask if they are going to vote Labour. "Once he asked Gordon to join him ... " Balls is asked to give out a list of the policies he announced today to help members make the case to voters. Balls says he will do something similar by Saturday. The questioner makes a good joke about Balls getting a standing ovation and then returning the mic: "Maybe we've all been to too many rock concerts ... but I was expecting two more policies!" Everyone laughs and the questioner says he has been working on that joke all day. "It was very good," says Balls sweetly. Should the ringfencing of health spending continue? He says he doesn’t want to make a promise now but he says it’s very likely. Balls is asked about Miliband's promises that any shadow ministers who brief against other shadow ministers will be sacked. What does he think and has he ever done that? Balls says he agrees with Miliband wholeheartedly and he has never briefed or sanctioned briefing against colleagues. Asked about the help to buy scheme, Balls says £600,000 is a very high figure for a taxpayer guarantee for mortgages. He can see the case for a smaller scheme. But again he returns to the idea of acting on the supply of housing first. He says Osborne thinks helping first-time buyers is a good thing politically, but he doesn't want to build houses because "he's locked into the fiscal path". Balls is asked whether the green message is a vote winner for Labour. Yes, he says. The government through incoherence and lack of vision has united the CBI and Greenpeace on green issues. Let's have a 2030 renewables goal in law, Balls says - and that is a response to a demand from the CBI and Greenpeace. Cameron has "totally ducked this policy agenda in government" - despite widespread support. "I think it's a big opportunity for us." Balls is asked about land tax. Sometimes there are complexities in the tax system that mean people we don't want to pay have to pay, he says. In the end it is about the supply of homes rather than the price of land, he says. Richards opens it up to questions. "I hope this is interesting for all of you. It feels a bit like In the Psychiatrist's Chair," Balls muses (if only). Balls looks back at the summer of 1996 and recalls the Skinner/Baddiel/Lightning Seeds song Three Lions, with its famous refrain "football's coming home" (and I said there was going to be no culture tonight...). Blair and Brown had independently written their conference speeches to end with the line: "Labour's coming home." A row ensued. Gordon grumpily gave way, even though he liked football more than Blair. How could the two of them get to the day before conference without having seen each other's speeches, asks Balls. Miliband showed him his speech for this year's conference two weeks ago. Richards brings up Damian McBride's revelations and asks what Balls reflects on that era. Balls says he was "horrified" by McBride's revelations. But it allows us to look back on this "different place - it really feels like quite a long time ago," he says. Richards notes that, unusually in recent times, Balls and Miliband are an opposition duo with much experience of power. But he suggests Balls and Miliband do not want to push this because "the recent past became so toxic". Balls says he is happy to have a debate about Labour's time in government and the lessons we have learned. People are more worried about where things are going than where they've come from, he says. He suggests it would be ridiculous if he started every interview about reminiscing about what he did in 2006 "like some pub bore". But he acknowledges Labour's frontbench are a group of 40-somethings who are "veterans". "There's a lot of track record and experience," he says. The 1964 Wilson government was similar, he suggests. A more recent parallel is that Margaret Thatcher had been a cabinet minister before becoming PM, he says. "I don't think a responsible chancellor should be the cheerleader for an infrastructure project on this scale without being hardheaded about value for money," he says. He suggests George Osborne would much rather be a political strategist than a chancellor and he acts like it. "I don't think George Osborne is doing his job properly on HS2." That gets some applause. Richards suggests it sends out an incoherent message to oppose the biggest capital spending project in the country and yet push for more capital spending generally. Balls says Labour has had no discussions about announcing it is opposing HS2. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," Balls says. Money is fungible, he says - every billion you spend on HS2 is not spent on other things. Is it the best project to spend £50bn on, he says. You don't sound very keen on it, says Richards. Balls says he is a Leeds Wakefield MP - you can see some benefits. But journey times alone are not enough, he says. "I'm not meaning to sound un-keen," says Balls. Balls recalls that the Lib Dems changed their policy on the deficit in order to go into coalition with the Tories. We want to build houses, create jobs and keep the economy moving, and we'll be disciplined doing that, he says. Richards asks about High Speed 2. Balls says this is something we have got to really know will work and will give value for money. We supported this, he says, but now costs have gone up and up. There are also questions about the benefits, he says. This is about the use of £50bn of public money, he says. We need to know the costs are properly under control, the benefits are there, and this is the best way to spend £50bn. It needs "a really hard and tough look" - after that they may approve it, he says. Has he already made up his mind? No, says Balls. But the costs have gone up by billions and billions in the last few months, he says. He compares it to the Olympics - which was "fiscally small" compared to HS2. Richards asks if Balls has changed his mind from his 2010 Keynesian views on the economy and the deficit to today's much more conservative approach. Balls suggests Keynes would have approached the 2010 economy by prescribing more spending, but the 2015 case will be very different, because of the mistakes the Coalition has made. "They should have taken our advice in 2010 and 2011, and they didn't." Richards asks if his Keynesian instincts got ahead of his political instincts. Balls says "if you don't make what you think is the right argument, you are letting people down". Balls says he and Ed Miliband, 18 months from the election, have set out more detail of tax and spending manifesto commitments that Cameron did before the 2010 election or Blair did before the 1997 vote. He talks about meeting mothers who said the amount of free childcare they were currently getting was not enough. Richards asks if Balls is trying to take "tax and spend" out of the election campaign. He says it's very hard for an opposition party to win that argument. Balls says if that was his idea he would be drawing the OBR into party politics, which he says he is not. There is a brief outbreak of jargon as Balls hits Richards with a reference to "Barnett consequentials". I'll draw a veil over that. Richards begins by asking Balls about whether the Office for Budget Responsibility will agree to analyse Labour spending policies. Balls says Robert Chote of the OBR supports the idea. He says there is no desire to draw the OBR into party politics. He says the OBR is established enough to take on this new role. He wants exactly the same certification the government gets for its measures. We can change the OBR's charter so it says "governments and prospective governments", but he admits it may need a change in the law. "I've not had a legal opinion on that." Balls says he is surprised that George Osborne has rejected the idea. (Sorry for spelling Steve Richards's name wrong in that tweet below. Very sloppy.) Hi, this is Paul Owen taking over from Andy for the evening. I’m here in a disappointingly bland and windowless conference room in the Metropole hotel – a far cry, unfortunately, from the seedy glamour conjured up by its memorable cameo appearance in The Waste Land. But that’s enough culture. I’m here to see shadow chancellor Ed Balls interviewed by the Independent’s Steve Richards, a perceptive interviewer and commentator who can usually wring some new information out of his victims. This has been Balls’s day at the Labour conference, so fingers crossed Richards will manage to catch him off guard tonight. I’ll live-blog the whole thing here. • Labour delegates have demanded an end to the public sector pay freeze. They applauded Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary,when he put the demand at the heart of his speech to the conference. More than anything, our people need hope. They need to know now that when elected, our party will end the pay cap which is blighting so many lives. It's not rocket science. If this is a cost of living conference, then the pay freeze must end. No ifs, no buts - a clear commitment to end the Tory pay freeze. And they overwhelmingly backed an emergency motion, passed without opposition, saying the next Labour manifesto should include "a strategy to end the public sector pay cap to help support economic growth, improve services and tackle the cost of living crisis". Afterwards a party spokesman played down the suggestion that this meant the party was committed to lifting the pay cap. Of course Labour supports a strategy to return to growth. As public sector pay caps are currently set until 2016, it is right that this motion was amended before it was debated. Labour Party policy is made via the National Policy Forum. In his speech Prentis also called for the value of the national minimum wage to be lifted so it matches that of the living wage. What is so wrong with a clear, unambigous Labour promise to turn a statutory minimum wage into a statutory living wage. • Sajid Javid, a Conservative Treasury minister, has claimed that Ed Balls' speech contains "debt-funded spending commitments". There was nothing new in Ed Balls’ speech – just more debt-funded spending commitments, breaking his own promise to show ‘iron discipline’ on public spending. Coming after this week’s revelation that there is a £27.9bn black hole in Labour’s spending plans, it’s no wonder nobody trusts Ed Balls or Ed Miliband to take the tough decisions needed to secure a recovery for everyone. • Charlie Whelan, Gordon Brown's former spin doctor, has said that Brown would be "shocked" to learn what Damian McBride had been doing on his behalf. This is what he told the Daily Politics. I had no idea what Damian was up to and neither did Gordon Brown. I do genuinely think that Gordon Brown would be more shocked than anybody by some of the allegations that have been made in his book. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour should do more to show that it understands ordinary people, particularly in the south of England, who go to church and like the royal family. (See 4.08pm.) • Vernon Coaker, the shadow Northern Ireland secretary, has said that nationalists must accept that the Good Friday agreement means Northern Ireland remains within the UK until a majority say otherwise. That's all from me for today. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe. On her Reality Check blog Mona Chalabi says she thinks Labour's childcare pledge could cost £2bn, and that the £800m bank levy that is supposed to fund it won't cover the full cost. Here's a short afternoon reading list. • Alastair Campbell on his blog condemns Damian McBride. I honestly believe – and I know that this is a big if given how politics and politicians are – that if the big beasts of New Labour had all more or less stuck together, if Gordon had understood the value of the broader team and not just his own, if some of his darker people had been sacked early – or better still never been near the place – there is a chance we would still be there and this wretched coalition government would not be undoing so much of the good that Tony, Gordon and their colleagues did. That is the real crime of the horrible, nasty, vindictive politics and internal backstabbing that McBride, after all the years of GB denials,now admits to, so forgive me if I refuse to bow down before this attempt at Catholic redemption. • Siobhan Kenny at Comment is free says Damian McBride almost cost her her job. My colleagues tell me the problem with the comments is affecting our whole website. They are on the case at Guardian HQ. At a fringe meeting at lunchtime Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, said Labour must do more to understand people living in the south of England. He said that in Scotland it had become unusual for people to vote Conservative, and he said he hoped that people in the south of England, and affluent voters, never felt the same way about supporting Labour. We have to ensure that it never becomes counter-cultural among optimistic and contented voters to ever consider voting Labour. The Conservatives can win an election without Scotland. It is harder for us to win an election without winning a substantial share of votes in some of the 209 constituencies south .... The Labour Party has sometimes talked in the past about respecting diversity and sometimes that's been, rightly, about the extension of civil rights for those with disabilities and the LGBT (lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender). I think there needs to be a new language about respecting diversity that is about some of those voters who live their lives in a way that is entirely straightforward. They go to church on Sunday and they have an immeasurable sense of patriotism and a real affection for the royal family and much else besides. We have got to have a genuine understanding and attachment to that set of voters. Effectively he was saying Labour should make more of an effort to understand and appreciate those who go to church and like the royal family. The Telegraph's Jon Swaine has found Gordon Brown at the United Nations in New York and asked him about Damian McBride. He did not get an answer. The comments don't seem to be working at the moment. I don't know why, but I'm trying to get that sorted. On her Reality Check blog, Mona Chalabi says that the Conservative costings for Labour's jobs guarantee seem rather selective. And while we're on the subject of Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, he seemed to be threatening some kind of civil disobedience when he was speaking at a fringe meeting at lunchtime. He said this. If this government continues to attack us, and continues to try and reduce our rights even further, let me warn them this. If you push us further outside of the law, then be it on your own heads what the consequences are, because we’ve got no intention in Unite or in this movement of allowing laws introduced by the Bullingdon boys to restrict the legitimate and international rights we have to protect our workers ... Conference, if we’re pushed outside of the law then so be it. McCluskey has made this kind of comment before, talking about the prospect of using civil disobedience to resist any potential attempt to restrict labour rights. Earlier this year Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, suggested that Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, should be sacked. Since then there has been constant speculation about Byrne's future, and so it is hard to avoid seeing his speech to the conference just now in that context. As I said earlier, it sounded uncharacteristically leftish and combative (partly because he was quite shouty), But it went down well. Here are the main points. • Byrne said the Tories should be ashamed of their record. Once upon a time the Tories told us they cared: all those speeches in Easterhouse. And people gave them the benefit of the doubt. We were promised a Tory party that cared about the poor. We were promised a welfare revolution. We were promised we’re all in this together. Three years on I tell you the jury is in. A cost of living crisis. A million young people out of work. Long term unemployment at record highs. Disabled people living in fear. Child poverty rising. Living standards hammered. A promise that started in Easterhouse has ended with the spectacle of a Tory Minister, Michael Gove, blaming the poor for the temerity to turn up at a food bank. He should be ashamed. • He said Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, was incompetent as well as prejudiced. These Tories have let their prejudice destroy their policies. And just as bad as the prejudice is the incompetence. They say to err is human. But if you want someone to really screw it up you send for Iain Duncan Smith. • He said Labour was inviting Jenny Macklin, an Australian politician, to help investigate how to integrate health and social care. But let’s take Andy Burnham’s idea of whole person care and ask why not bring together health, social care - and the back to work system into one comprehensive service. That’s what Labour did in Australia. Let’s see if we can learn from that here. I’m delighted to announce that Jenny Macklin, a fine Labour politician and the architect of the system down under, is going to help us figure out how. In his speech Ed Balls also mentioned the Andy Burnham plan. He said this. Can we improve care and save money, as Andy Burnham has proposed, by pooling health and social care as a single service, with a single budget and joint management? That was significant because at one stage Balls was known to be sceptical about the idea. Taken together, these two speeches suggest that Labour is more committed to the proposal than when Burnham first floated it, slightly tentatively, in a speech. • Byrne said he said he was setting up a committee to look at ways of rescuing universal credit. The Tories’ system may prove dead on arrival. So we need a better way. So, today we announce our Universal Credit Rescue Committee. And I’m grateful to Kieran Quinn, leader of Tameside, the first pathfinder, for his offer to drive our work. • In the conference hall delegates are debating two composite motions on the cost of living. One of them calls for Labour to abandon the public sector pay cap. The session was opened by Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, who delivered an uncharacteristically leftish and combative speech. I'll post some excerpts shortly. Labour has had an opinion poll lead pretty much since the end of 2010, but as Paul Owen has been hearing, that lead is somewhat “fragile”. There were three reasons for this, said Ipsos Mori’s Gideon Skinner at a fringe event at the elegant Hotel du Vin just back from the Brighton seafront: Labour is losing ground on the electorate’s No 1 issue, the economy, with the Tory lead on this issue increasing to 18 points recently and people’s optimism about the economy rising too; Ed Miliband is “still not convincing the public”; and Labour is relying riskily on former Lib Dems switching to their party, when former Tories making the leap will surely be needed too. Skinner mapped Miliband’s approval ratings – which are not impressive – against those of failed Tory leaders Iain Duncan Smith and William Hague. “His highs have been better than Hague’s and IDS’s but his lows are fairly similar,” he said. He pointed out that Margaret Thatcher had worse ratings as a leader on some measures than opposition leaders James Callaghan and Neil Kinnock so these findings were “not the be-all and end-all … but I think there is something there to be concerned about”. Michael Dugher, shadow minister without portfolio, pointed out that a Lord Ashcroft poll recently had found Labour 14 points ahead in the 40 most marginal seats. And he said that in last year’s US election “[Mitt] Romney was ahead of [Barack] Obama on the economy but Obama was ahead of Romney in terms of standing up for ordinary people” – there was a “very similar trend here,” he said. Dugher claimed that “whenever Ed has exposure his ratings go up. We want more Ed Miliband, not less.” As the election approaches, Miliband would get more such exposure, he said. Skinner had shown the crowd a slide showing that 51% of people said they did not know what Miliband stood for, compared to 33% for Cameron, and Sophy Ridge of Sky, also on the panel, said this was “a troubling statistic”. She agreed that it was dangerous for Labour to rely on Lib Dems switching without persuading any Tories to do so – noting that in byelections since 2010 “the Lib Dems have held up pretty well”. Andrew Harrop of the Fabian Society agreed that “in every key marginal seat we need a few hundred Tories to vote Labour for us to be in a winning position”, adding that “on top of that we need to turn out people who didn’t vote last time” – young people, new voters, and people who have been switched off politics”. He was surprisingly confident that Labour “probably will be the largest party” in parliament after the next election, although it might not win a majority – but interestingly he warned that disputes over union funding of the party could be “a car crash this next six months … it could be so distracting”. Here's some reaction to the Ed Balls speech. From Simon Walker, director general of the Institute of Directors Politicians must understand the central role of business in creating wealth, prosperity and employment. I remain concerned by Labour’s proposals to increase the minimum wage in specific sectors, and requiring firms to take on an apprentice for every non-EU worker they hire is a recipe for disaster. Having said that, Ed Balls engaged with several issues vital to business leaders in his speech. We share his concerns about the extended Help to Buy scheme, which risks pushing up house prices while doing little to increase the supply of homes. Planning reform is the vital missing element here. There are still too many flaws in the system that hold up home-building. It was also significant that he questioned whether HS2 was the best way to spend £50bn. The IoD agrees with Labour that infrastructure investment is essential, but governments must be clear that they are getting value for money. Mr Balls’s commitment to discipline in the public finances is welcome, but will require Labour to clamp down on additional spending pledges. Their rhetoric must be matched by actions. He should be very wary of attempting to achieve deficit reduction through tax rises, which would be deeply damaging to the recovery. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Balls identified many issues of concern to business in his conference speech, including infrastructure and growth finance. Yet Labour's policy alternatives on the key long-term economic issues remain embryonic at best. Many policy reviews have been undertaken, but Labour's renewed credibility with business rests on swiftly setting out concrete and clear measures on the key issues facing companies across the UK. And Labour must address worrying hints that they are starting to lose their nerve on transformative investment projects, including HS2, in favour of current spending. From Robin Hillman, a spokesman for the Robin Hood Tax campaign Our public services have been slashed to pay for the financial sector's mistakes, so it is good news that Labour is asking banks to make a greater contribution. But Labour's proposal lacks ambition – the bank levy and one-off bonus tax are little more than small change for the richest sector in Britain. Banks can afford to be paying billions more in tax, money that is desperately needed to shore up public services and create more jobs. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's director of policy and campaigns The shadow chancellor understands that the only long-term plan which works for Britain has low-carbon growth as its cornerstone. Mr Balls’s promise to let the Green Investment Bank borrow to invest and bring in a 2030 target for clean energy is exactly what we need to restore battered investor confidence. • Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, has announced that a Labour government would increase the bank levy by £800m to fund the expansion of free childcare for three- and four-year-olds from 15 hours a week to 25 hours a week. Alison Garnham, chief executive of Child Poverty Action Group, welcomed the announcement. This proposal is great news for hard-pressed families struggling with the cost of childcare. Childcare costs are cripplingly high, and while tax credits give important help, most UK parents are still footing the lion's share of the bill. The more hours of childcare parents can get for free, the more it will help with the squeeze on living standards they are facing. • Balls has questioned the wisdom of spending £50bn on the HS2 high-speed rail project, creating speculation that Labour could abandon its support for the scheme before the 2015 general election. • A Treasury minister, Sajid Javid, has said the government will not change the remit of the Office for Budget Responsibility to allow it to audit Labour's spending plans. He was responding to Balls's call for the OBR to be allowed to assess the accuracy of Labour's claims. But Robert Chote, the head of the OBR, said he thought it would be a good idea to have opposition spending policies independently audited. I think the idea of having independent scrutiny of party manifestos in the runup to an election is generally a good idea. That would provide a more informed public debate, and would, for example, help potentially with coming up with coalition agreements afterwards. Certainly, with my former hat on with the Institute for Fiscal Studies, we were involved in trying to do that ahead of the last two general elections. Chote said that the OBR was taking legal advice to see whether changing its remit would require primary legislation. He also said that in the Netherlands the equivalent of the OBR publishes its assessment of opposition plans 12 weeks before the election. For the same to happen in the UK, parties would have to publish their manifestos much earlier than they do now, he said. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour would make attacking a soldier a specific criminal offence. • Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, has said Labour would hold an inquiry into blacklisting in the construction industry if the government does not order one. (See 11.56am.) • Alan Milburn, the former Labour cabinet minister, has said that Labour needs to find some "beef" to bolster its policies and should not be conniving with Tory attempts to reduce immigration. As Rowena Mason reports, Milburn said he was really worried about the direction of the party on immigration, shortly after Ed Miliband said Labour would cut the number of foreign workers coming to the UK. We seem to be moving towards a nice cosy consensus that I'm really worried about … our future relies on more immigration not less. If we've got an ageing population, with fewer domestically produced workers, the gap can only be closed if are prepared in a sensible, managed and rational way to encourage immigration not close it down. • David Lammy, the former minister, has told the World at One that Ed Miliband has not yet persuaded voters that he is a prime minister in waiting. You’ll get a constituency of people who switch on to the bedroom tax and welfare issues like that, but large segments of the country who own their own home and want to know how you’re going to run the economy because they don’t want interest rates to go up, or they want their kids to be able to get on the housing ladder ... I think Ed Milband has moved to being an effective opposition leader and has taken bold decisions, like on Leveson and his position on Syria. But he now needs to move into being a prime minister in waiting and I don’t think he’s crossed that Rubicon and that needs to happen from this conference outward if we are to be serious about heading to No 10. • Chris Bryant, the shadow immigration minister, has said that Labour would reintroduce the overseas domestic worker visa to prevent a form of virtual slavery in Britain. As Randeep Ramesh reports, Bryant told a fringe meeting that after the government abolished the visa last year, domestic workers brought into the country to work in homes were "completely tied to their employers". My colleague Mona Chalabi is wring a Reality Check blog about some of the claims in Ed Balls' speech. In particular, she is looking at the jobs guarantee and the childcare pledge. Here's the full text of Ed Balls's speech. The Treasury minister Sajid Javid says the government will not let the OBR audit Labour's spending plans. And here's some more general Twitter comment on the speech. From the BBC's Nick Robinson From the Spectator's James Forsyth As usual, Twitter is the place to go for debate on the great issues of the day. (It was rather an odd remark, and it was not entirely clear what Ed Balls meant. Or maybeI'm just innocent.) For the past three years pundits and members of the public have been saying that they want to hear what Labour would do differently. Today, Ed Balls confronted that head-on, and delivered a lengthy, explicit answer. Gordon Brown was fond of using lists in his speeches and Balls used the same device at least twice, when he was reminding the audience what the last Labour government achieved and when he told them, right at the end, what Labour would do in 2015. It was a checklist for activists, ideal for campaigners who need to be able to answer the "why vote Labour" question on the doorstep. There is a lot of interest, too, in what he had to say about HS2. He has been using the no "blank cheque" line for some time, but to put that up in headlights in a conference speech signals, more clearly than before, that Labour is threatening to pull the plug on the project. (If you want to know why, it helps to look at the polls. Public support for HS2 is falling, more people are now opposed than in favour, and Labour supporters are particularly hostile to the idea.) The chances of HS2 ever actually going ahead are looking a bit slimmer than they were. I'll post some more reaction shortly. Balls is winding up now. Conference, we are not the first Labour generation to face a huge deficit and the need for spending restraint and a country crying out for change. We are not the first generation to be awed by the scale of what needs to be done to transform our country. And as we prepare for the 2015 general election, to be held in the 70th anniversary year of the end of the second world war, let us take our inspiration from the great reforming Labour government of 1945. And he finishes with a practical list for delegates. So in the coming weeks and months, when people ask what would a Labour government do, let’s go out and tell them: Jobs for young people guaranteed. Expanding free childcare. A British Investment Bank. Infrastructure delivered. Green investment unlocked. The deficit down fairly. Tax cuts for millions – not millionaires. Reforming our banks. The minimum wage raised. Our NHS save.d Tackling tax avoidance. Rail fares capped. The bedroom tax scrapped. Building the homes we need. This what a Labour government could do. Let us together make it happen. Thank you. Following Sir George Cox’s review on short-termism, we will change takeover rules and corporate incentives and reform our tax system to stop short-term asset-stripping and support long-term investment. And why not use any revenues from the planned increase in the licence fees for the mobile phone spectrum, expected to be over £1bn in the next parliament, to capitalise the British Investment Bank so that, region by region, we can get small and growing businesses the finance they need to grow and create jobs? And, conference, we will set up an independent infrastructure commission, as recommended to us by the chair of the Olympic Delivery Authority, Sir John Armitt, to end dither and delay in infrastructure planning. • Balls says mobile phone spectrum revenues should be used to capitalise the British Investment Bank. Just look at the British car industry in the 1970s and 1980s. Trying to compete on cost. Cutting back on innovation, quality and skills. Plagued by terrible industrial relations. And now look at the renaissance of Jaguar Land Rover – creating thousands more jobs and exporting round the world. Not by cutting corners, but based on world-class, long-term investment in innovation, skills and supply-chains. Chuka Umunna and I are determined to learn from this success. And I can announce that Mike Wright, executive director at Jaguar Land Rover, will now lead a review for us on how we can help strengthen our manufacturing supply-chains and deliver the skills and innovation Britain needs to succeed. Balls says companies look on the government's industrial policy with dismay. The RDAs abolished. The Heseltine growth review neutered. The Business Bank a damp squib. Apprenticeships for young people falling. Energy policy in chaos. Borrowing powers for the Green Investment Bank postponed. And on infrastructure, dither, delay and inaction. Conference, we cannot succeed as a country with this ‘race to the bottom’, deregulation, laissez-faire and old-style ‘trickle-down economics’. It’s a narrow and defeatist vision. Balls turns to childcare. Childcare is a vital part of our economic infrastructure that, alongside family support and flexible working, should give parents the choice to stay at home with their children when they are very small and to balance work and family as they grow older. So to make work pay for families, we must act. Stephen Twigg set out yesterday how we will guarantee childcare available for all primary school children from 8am to 6pm. But we need to do more for families with nursery-age children too. The banks will fund this, he explains. In the last financial year, the banks paid a staggering £2.7bn less in overall tax than they did in 2010. Over the last two years the government’s bank levy has raised £1.6bn less than even they said it would. At a time when resources are tight and families are under pressure that cannot be right. So I can announce today, the next Labour government will increase the bank levy rate to raise an extra £800m a year. And we will use the money, for families where all parents are in work, to increase free childcare places for three- and four-year-olds from 15 hours to 25 hours a week. For the first time, parents will be able to work part-time without having to worry about the cost of childcare. This gets a strong round of applause. Balls says Labour will encourage employers to pay the living wage. And he says Labour would introduce a 10p starting rate of tax, paid for by a mansion tax. So building on the success of Labour’s Future Jobs Fund – so short-sightedly scrapped by this government – we will introduce a Compulsory Jobs Guarantee for young people and the long-term unemployed. We will fund this by a repeat of the tax on bank bonuses and by restricting pension tax relief for the very highest earners to the same rate as the average taxpayer. And we will work with employers to make sure there will be a paid job for all young people out of work for more than 12 months and adults out of work for two years or more, which people will have to take up or lose benefits. That is welfare reform that works. And conference, in tough times it's even more important that all our policies and commitments are properly costed and funded. The British people rightly want to know that the sums add up. So we will go one step further and ask the independent Office for Budget Responsibility – the watchdog set up by this government – to independently audit the costings of every individual spending and tax measure in Labour's manifesto at the next election. This is the first time a shadow chancellor – the first time any political party in Britain – has ever said it wants this kind of independent audit. It’s a radical change from what's gone before, but the right thing to do to help restore trust in politics. Balls turns to HS2. Under this government the High Speed 2 project has been totally mismanaged and the costs have shot up to £50bn. David Cameron and George Osborne have made clear they will go full steam ahead with this project – no matter how much the costs spiral up and up. They seem willing to put their own pride and vanity above best value for money for the taxpayer. Labour will not take this irresponsible approach. So let me be clear, in tough times – when there is less money around and a big deficit to get down – there will be no blank cheque from me as a Labour chancellor for this project or for any project. Because the question is – not just whether a new high-speed line is a good idea or a bad idea, but whether it is the best way to spend £50bn for the future of our country. • Balls reaffirms Labour's decision not to offer a "blank cheque" for HS2. With our zero-based review, we will make different choices. So we will ask: can we improve care and save money, as Andy Burnham has proposed, by pooling health and social care as a single service, with a single budget and joint management? And, conference, we will repeal the damaging and costly Tory privatisation of the NHS. And we will ask: does it really make sense to have separate costly management and bureaucracy for so many separate government departments, agencies, fire services and police forces? And, conference, we won’t pay for new free schools in areas where there are excess school places, while parents in other areas are struggling to get their children into a local school. Balls illustrates some of the tough decisions Labour will have to take. And with our zero-based review – a review of every pound spent by government from the bottom up – Rachel Reeves and my shadow cabinet colleagues have begun the work of identifying savings so that we can switch resources to Labour’s priorities. But we won’t be able to reverse all the spending cuts and tax rises the Tories have pushed through. And we will have to govern with less money around. The next Labour government will have to make cuts too. Because while jobs and growth are vital to getting the deficit down – something this government has never understood – they cannot magic the whole deficit away at a stroke. So delivering our Labour goals will be harder than at any time in living memory. But it can be done – if we get people back to work and strengthen our economy, cut out waste and focus relentlessly on our priorities, and make sure difficult choices are not ducked, but are rooted in our values, in fairness and in common sense. So, conference, at a time when the public services that pensioners rely on are under such pressure, we cannot continue paying the winter fuel allowance to the richest 5% of pensioners. We won’t be able to reverse the government’s cuts to child benefit for the highest earners. We will keep the benefits cap, but make sure it properly reflects local housing costs. We will have a cap on structural social security spending. And yes, over the long-term, as our population ages, there will need to be increases in the retirement age. Labour will inherit a very difficult situation, he says. And we stand to inherit a very difficult situation. After three wasted years of lost growth, far from balancing the books, in 2015 there is now set to be a deficit of over £90bn. David Cameron and George Osborne’s failure on the economy has led to their failure to get the deficit down, and it will be up to the next Labour government to finish the job. And I need to be straight with this vonference and the country about what that means. The government’s day to day spending totals for 2015-16 will have to be our starting point Any changes to the current spending plans for that year will be fully funded and set out in advance in our manifesto. There will be no more borrowing for day-to-day spending. And we will set out tough fiscal rules – to balance the current budget and get the national debt on a downward path. Balls says the government should not cut infrastructure spending. That is why we have consistently said, it is why the IMF have said, bring forward £10bn of infrastructure investment right now, build 400,000 affordable houses over the next two years, create half a million jobs and thousands of apprenticeships. That is the way to secure an economy that works for all and is built to last. This seems to be a "for now" policy, not necessarily an election commitment. In one of his interviews this morning, Balls said Labour would not say whether Labour would increase capital spending until nearer the election. Balls says it is good that the economy is starting to grow. But he issues a warning about Help to Buy. That is why, along with voices from the Bank of England and the IMF, we are right to be concerned that the hovernment is boosting housing demand – with a taxpayer mortgage guarantee on houses of up to £600,000 – while doing nothing about the supply of housing which has fallen to its lowest level since the 1920s. George, it’s basic economics. If you push up housing demand, but don’t act to boost housing supply, all that happens is that you push house prices up and up. And the end result is that the very people your policy should be helping – young first time buyers – will find it even harder to get on the housing ladder. • Balls says the Help to Buy mortgage guarantee scheme could fuel a house price boom. (Vince Cable has expressed exactly the same worry.) The Tories are on the side of privilege, Balls says. And isn’t it now clear whose side David Cameron and George Osborne are really on? Cutting taxes for hedge funds. Trying to bribe working people to give up their rights. Country suppers at Chequers for Tory party fundraising. Protecting the privileges of the few, while the many work hard and don't see the benefit. For all their claims to be modernisers, with Cameron and Osborne, it's not been “Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?” it’s “Who Wants to Help a Millionaire?” Not “phone a friend”, but “cut taxes for your friends.” Not “50-50” but “winner takes all”. Conference, isn’t it about time they “asked the audience”? Because we know the truth. The country knows the truth. David Cameron and George Osborne. For the few, not the many. Balls is still on the failures of coalition's economic policies. Let us remind them: Prices rising faster than wages for 38 out of the 39 months since David Cameron entered Downing Street. Three years of flatlining. The slowest recovery for over 100 years. A million young people out of work. Welfare spending soaring. More borrowing to pay for their economic failure. The coalition's policies are not working for ordinary families, he says. For millions of ordinary families, worried about how to make ends meet when wages are falling, and prices are going up For the young couples struggling to get on the housing ladder because the chronic shortage of homes is forcing up prices For ordinary working families – the aspirational majority – who work hard, pay their taxes, who want to get on and not just get by, but who are working harder for less as the cost of living keeps on rising This Tory plan isn't working. Balls asks if people felt a little sorry for Cameron during the summer. Cameron was photographed on the beach in his shorts. Balls sympathised. He has been photographed like that. But his wife Yvette said Cameron looked slim. Who could she have been comparing him to, he asks. (That's a joke about his own tum.) Balls turns to the economy. The Tories failed by their own terms, he says. They claimed in 2010 that faster tax rises and deeper spending cuts would secure the economic recovery and make it stronger … They said their plan would make people better off and get the deficit down. And on every test they set themselves, this prime minister and chancellor have failed. Balls returns to Syria, and foreign policy. (In the week when MPs were debating this, he said very little, if anything, in public on the subject.) And, conference, when David Cameron and William Hague now have the nerve to go round saying that parliament's refusal to be bounced into military action in Syria has 'diminished' Britain, let us reply: no Labour government will ever stand aside when terrible atrocities are committed and international law is broken. But, conference, we know what has diminished Britain. Flouncing out of a European summit, leaving Britain isolated and without influence. That's what has diminished Britain. Absurdly comparing Britain to Greece and choking off business confidence and our recovery as a result. That's what has diminished Britain. Stigmatising the unemployed and the low paid and calling them shirkers, driving vans round out streets telling immigrants to ‘go home’, attacking our police and teachers and social workers, peddling the lie that 'Britain is broken'. That's what has diminished Britain. Conference, we know who diminishes Britain. David Cameron has diminished Britain. Balls praises Ed Miliband. Miliband has been Leading from the front – on phone-hacking, banking reform, putting the crisis of the ‘squeezed middle’ on the political agenda before anyone else. And who on Syria had the courage to stand up and say that if the case was sound and the United Nations was properly engaged, Labour would support military action, but that Labour would not support a gung-ho prime minister, putting decision before evidence in a reckless dash to conflict. Conference, Ed was right – and he prevailed. My friend, our leader, Britain’s next prime minister, Ed Miliband. Balls turns to Scotland. With Alex Salmond in a state of total confusion on the single most important economic decision a country can take – which currency to have: first he wanted the euro – saying sterling was a “millstone around our neck” and now he wants independence but to keep the pound all the same. Let us win the argument that we are Better Together in next year’s Scottish referendum. Demonstrating, as Carwyn Jones and Welsh Labour have done so brilliantly, that it is our Labour values of co-operation, solidarity and social justice that best secure our union. Balls thanks Margaret Beckett, who has co-chaired the economic policy commission with him. And he says that he is a member of both Unison and Unite. Next year, in the European elections, Labour should kick the BNP out of the European parliament, he says. Balls says Labour also got rid of Section 28, which paved the way for the gay marriage legislation. And he reminds delegates that he will be in the frontline at the general election. As the Labour and Co-operative MP for Morley & Outwood – majority just 1,101 – the seat David Cameron needed to win to get a Tory majority in 2010 and, because of our hard work and determination, the seat he failed to win … conference, I am up for the battle to come. He mentions the achievements of the last Labour government. The national minimum wage. Our schools and hospitals rebuilt. NHS waiting times down from 18 months to 18 weeks. More apprenticeships. Bank of England independence. Not joining the euro. That last, of course, is one he was particularly associated with. He says no party since the 1920s has gone from losing office to a working majority at the next general election. And he sets out Labour's mission. To show we are ready for the great challenges we will face on spending control and the deficit. To set out Labour ‘s alternative plan to create jobs, make work pay and tackle the rising cost of living. And to secure a mandate to build a One Nation Britain: a strong recovery that is built to last. an economy that works for the many and not just a few at the top. Ed Balls is starting his speech now. In his speech to the conference Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, said that Labour would hold a full inquiry into blacklisting in the construction industry if the government did not set one up. Society working with business for the common good. In that spirit we want people to value their work and we want companies to value their people. That’s why we will act to outlaw zero hours contracts where they exploit people. And it’s why if this government won’t launch a full inquiry into the disgraceful blacklisting in the construction industry, we will. You can read all today's Guardian coverage from the conference here. As for the rest of the papers, here are some articles that are particularly interesting. • The Daily Mail, in the latest stage of its serialisation of Damian McBride's memoirs, says that McBride says he and Ed Miliband used to be friends and that Miliband only objected to McBride's negative briefings after he thought he had become a victim of them. Other key revelations in the latest extracts include: How Mr Brown plotted to put Ed Balls in charge of economic policy to undermine Alistair Darling. After calls at the weekend for Darling to return to the shadow cabinet, McBride warns it would be disastrous to recall the ‘catastrophically inept’ former chancellor; He confesses to smearing John Major and Norman Lamont over the Black Wednesday financial crisis while still a supposedly neutral civil servant funded by the taxpayer; He reveals details of Mr Brown’s wild rages and how he attacked BBC political editor Nick Robinson as a ‘f****** Tory’; He admits leaking dozens of budget secrets. • Lord Mandelson in the Financial Times says Ed Miliband needs clearer policies and a better communication strategy. In [Ed Miliband's] speech on Tuesday, the main subject has to be the economy and how higher living standards can be delivered. He must define himself, above all, with workable long-term policies that make sense for business, investment and job creation, while recognising the importance of selective strategic state intervention. We must pump-prime the markets, technologies and innovation we need to succeed. Labour also needs radicalism to improve health, education and other public services. We previously had money plus reform. In 2015 the party must offer reform with no more money. Mr Miliband also needs to signal how he will adapt Britain’s social welfare model to Britain’s changing demography and economy. Mr Miliband’s team like to argue that they “have more policy than any other opposition in the history of modern politics”. The public has not noticed. Mr Miliband needs better communications support. Gone are the days when parliamentary lobby journalists held the key to a party’s image. Mr Miliband’s brand and message need to be recast for the age of social media, and he needs to show boldness and political artistry in grabbing his share of attention. This is best done arguing from clear principles and having the courage to stick by them. • Steve Richards in the Independent says Ed Miliband has great strengths, but that he needs to present them better. [Miliband] has also still to find a compelling public voice, illustrated by his awkward televisiion appearance yesterday with Andrew Marr, and has not yet learnt how to appear commanding – another game in opposition, but a vital one. He should have been on the Today programme at ten past eight the morning after the Syrian vote in the Commons giving the impression he was now in charge of foreign policy. After his clever “One Nation” conference speech a year ago he should have then toured every part of the nation, armed with symbolic policies, to reinforce his point. The political art goes well beyond the important set piece occasions of party conference speeches, Prime Ministers’ Questions and occasional interviews. It is the responsibility of the artist to convey his strengths. In the presidential culture of British politics if voters have not recognised Miliband’s by 2015 Labour will struggle to win a winnable general election. • Boris Johnson in the Daily Telegraph says that Damian McBride is "a small but interesting example of the mental contortions of socialists over the last century, when there is no abuse or tyranny – large or trivial – that has not been justified in the name of the people". Len McCluskey, Unite's general secretary, has just finished a speech to the conference. Delegates are debating employment rights and Unite has tabled an emergency motion calling for stronger collective rights for workers. McCluskey talked about his experiences on the Liverpool docks as he explained why this mattered. When I left school and went to work on the Liverpool docks –– dockers used to crowd into a hall every morning hoping for work. Bosses used to walk amongst them tapping them on the shoulder and handing out brass tallies which were needed to get a day’s work. And when the bosses got tired of that, they would throw the remaining tallies into the air and watch men fight each other –– and I mean fight – because it meant putting food in their kids’ bellies. Zero hours in another age. The only thing that stopped it then, and the only thing that will stop it now, is strong trade unions. He also said that Labour would only have a future if it represented organised Labour. One thing is certain, that if our party is to have a future it must speak for ordinary workers and it must represent the voice of organised Labour. More and more economists recognise – what we’ve always known – that wage depression is a fundamental obstacle to sustainable growth in our economy. That’s why this motion seeks to address the disgraceful treatment, in 21st century Britain, of trade unionists. Trade unionists are the people in this land who create the wealth of our nation and who are responsible for the very fabric of the civilised communities in which we live. And everyone in this party, everyone should be proud of our link with them through their trade unions. In his speech Douglas Alexander, the shadow foreign secretary, said Labour believed in "progressive internationalism". He also said that Ed Miliband spoke for the nation over Syria and stopped a rush to military action. It fell to our leader, Labour's Leader, Ed Miliband, to speak for the nation. He upheld a basic principle: that the evidence should precede the decision, not the decision precede the evidence. And together we set out a 'roadmap for decision': a clear set of tests and conditions by which our nation should reach a decision of such consequence. Conference, it was Labour's leadership that prevented a rush to military action on a timetable set elsewhere, without the necessary steps being taken and without due process being followed. We have learned the lessons of the past. Intervening immediately and asking hard questions later would have ill served our country. As Labour, we are prepared to support force where we must – as we did in Libya two years ago – but we should support diplomacy where we can. The text of the speech is on Labour's website. One thing that was noteworthy about the speech was how little Alexander had to say about Europe. He said Labour wanted to reform Europe, but he had nothing to say about the in/out referendum proposed by David Cameron for after the general election. At a fringe meeting last night he admitted that senior Labour figures were divided over whether the party should match Cameron's offer. I am not for a moment pretending there are not some who have suggested we should countenance a referendum. But I would argue that those people within Labour who argue for a referendum overwhelmingly see it as a means of refreshing consent rather than securing exit. That is pretty different from the Conservative party, where, within the ranks of the cabinet, you have got people like Michael Gove and Philip Hammond falling over themselves on the way to the television studios to be the first to say 'if there was a referendum tomorrow I'd vote no'. Overwhelmingly the Labour party sees Britain's future as within Europe. We are not opposed to a referendum in principle. We have accepted that if there's a significant transfer of sovereignty from Britain to Brussels in future, that would trigger a referendum. But we are not convinced, and we haven't been convinced, that to commit now to an in/out referendum in 2017 – which is now the position of the government – is the right choice for the country. That's why we continue to advocate that stability, growth and jobs should be to priority for the country and reform within Europe rather than exit from Europe should be Labour's approach. Attacking a member of the armed forces would become a specific criminal offence under Labour plans, Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary told the conference. Here's the key quote. Today, too many of our armed forces face disadvantage and discrimination, as do some of our veterans. And it's sad to think that those who fight for our country might need the additional protection of our laws, but some do. That's why I want to tell conference today that next month in the House of Commons Labour will table amendments to the defence reform bill so that for the first time ever it's a specific criminal offence to attack a member of the British armed forces. And at last they will have the protection that their bravery deserves. Glenis Willmott has not been the only speaker attacking Ukip in the Britain's global role debate. In the past Labour hardly bothered to say much about Ukip, but several speakers have had a go at them this morning. Richard Howitt, a Labour MEP, was one. He has just finished speaking, and he mentioned a Ukip councillor who fell asleep after arriving at a council meeting. He was putting the "kip" into Ukip, he said. My colleague Rowena Mason has more. The full text of Glenis Willmott's speech is now on Labour's website. • Willmott said Labour should challenge the idea that Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, is just an ordinary person. On Ukip in particular we need to tell people that Nigel Farage isn’t “just like you”. He isn’t just the normal bloke down the pub with his pint. He is a former Tory. He was a trader in the City, one of the breed who got us into this financial mess in the first place. And a man who doesn’t believe the Conservative party is Conservative enough. We can’t keep letting Ukip and the Tories get away with it. • She said the European Union offered a "beacon of hope" when Britain was under the sway of Thatcherism. Throughout the 80s and early 90s when people were suffering under Thatcherite policies. It was Europe that offered a light, a beacon of hope. A Europe which put social protection at the very top of its agenda. A Europe which helped revitalise and transform our towns and cities. In places like Liverpool, Leicester and Leeds, EU funding gave a lifeline, to places almost abandoned by a callous Tory government. That is what inspired me about Europe then and why I am still determined to fight for it today. On the subject of the Office for Budget Responsibility auditing Labour's spending plans, it is worth remembering what Robert Chote, the head of the OBR, has said about this. I interviewed him last year and asked him if this would be a good idea. He made it clear that in principle he was in favour, but he also said that he did not expect parliament to change the OBR's remit to allow it to audit opposition proposals any time soon. Here's the key quote. It's entirely reasonable for parliament to want us to establish our credibility and trustworthiness with a narrower mandate before you extend it in that sort of way. I think at the end of the day that having independent rigorous analysis of all the political parties' policies is a good thing for them, it's a good thing for public debate, it's a good thing for democracy. Glenis Wilmott, the leader of the Labour MEPs, is winning the soundbite of the day award so far. She has just spoken in the Britain's global role debate and she reminded delegates of David Cameron's comment about going to Brussels ready for an ambush. "In this town you have to be ready for an ambush at any time, and that means lock and load and have one up the spout," he said. After quoting Cameron, she described him as "disturbing cocktail of John Wayne and Alan Partridge". I'll post more from the speech when I've read the text. Justine Thornton, Ed Miliband's wife, is more than just a dress. She said so herself at a Young Labour event last night. I wanted to come along tonight to say a few words because I thought, 'If I don't say anything to you guys tonight probably all you'll know about me this year is the make of the dress I wear for Ed's speech on Tuesday. That's a side of me the media tend to focus on and I though it was quite important to reassure you that I am in fact more than a dress. She talked about how her relationship with Miliband developed. In May 2005, Ed was the prospective parliamentary candidate for Doncaster North. I was his friend. So I went to Doncaster to help him campaign and I was moving chairs around in a hall getting ready for a campaign meeting and I became aware there were three long-standing party members watching me. After a while one of them said to me who was I, so I explained that I was a friend of Ed's and I had come from London to help him campaign. There was a pause and then the lady said to me, 'That's a very long way to come to move chairs, are you sure you're just friends?'. She was clearly very astute and realised before we did I think. And she explained why she backed his decision to stand as for the Labour leadership. The key moment came when she saw him visit a working men's club in Plymouth, she said. As I listened to Ed's answers I realised firstly he knew and understood about the lives of the people in that room and secondly that he cared about creating a society that wouldn't feel like it felt to me that day, which was the people there and the bankers and the lawyers around me in London were living in two separate countries. That was it for me. That was the day when I understood why Ed had decided to stand to be leader of the Labour party, difficult a decision though that was for many reasons, and it was the day when psychologically the day I threw my lot in with Ed and the Labour party. So, there it is, that's why I'll be on the barricades in 2015 wearing whatever dress the media deem necessary to get us through. There was plenty of substance in the various Ed Balls interviews this morning. He wanted to talk about childcare, but the broadcasters wanted to talk about Damian McBride too. Here are the main points. I've taken the quotes from the Press Association and PoliticsHome. • Ed Balls has denied a story in the Times (paywall) saying that Labour is considering a plan to cut child benefit payments to parents who do not ensure their children have the MMR vaccine. He said this: There is no question of a Labour government ever taking child benefit away or punishing parents for choices they make on vaccinations. I’ve no idea where that’s come from. It’s not our policy. It’s on the front of the papers, but it’s just not true. The Times story said the idea was being considered by Jon Cruddas, Labour's policy chief. Here's an excerpt. Under plans being considered by Jon Cruddas, Labour’s policy chief, child benefit would be paid only to parents who could prove that their children’s vaccination records were up to date. The policy is already in place in Australia and Mr Cruddas, MP for Dagenham & Rainham, is championing it as a way of attaching “conditionality” to benefits and services provided by the state. “This is an example of the sort of measure which we want to see that ties public goods to how people behave as citizens,” said a senior source. • Balls has accused the Conservatives of refusing to let the Office for Budget Responsibility audit Labour's spending plans for party political reasons. After the proposal was announced last night, Sajid Javid, a Treasury minister, claimed the idea was "just a stunt" to distract attention from "the fact that Labour have been found out for making unfunded commitments". (Why inviting the OBR to say whether or not the commitments are unfunded should distract attention from this issue is not clear.) But Andrew Tyrie, the Conservative chair of the Commons Treasury committee, said the proposal could "improve the quality of public debate in an election". Balls said that Tyrie was right. I not only want it to happen, I think it will happen. So because the Conservative party will play party politics with trying to give the public reassurance that all parties manifestos add up, to rebuild trust in politics, then it won’t happen? That’s their choice, but I don’t think that’ s what they’ll do. There is currently a consensus about the independent OBR’s role. The chair of the Treasury Select Committee, who is a Conservative, has said he supports this and he doesn’t think it requires legislation. I’ve spoken to the OBR, and I think the OBR are absolutely up for doing this. But it’s parliament’s decision. I hope that without any legislation we can do this. The OBR did this job for the government for months before the law was even passed. If George Osborne can agree with me, the OBR can do this now. • He said that he had never briefed against colleagues in the way that Damian McBride did. This is what he said when he was asked if he could categorically say he had never been involved in a negative briefing against a government colleague. Yes. That’s not something I’ve ever done. Damian McBride has come out and said he did some of those things. It was despicable. • He also denied knowing that McBride was involved in negative briefings. This is what he said when he was asked if he had ever urged Gordon Brown to sack McBride (as Ed Miliband and Douglas Alexander have both said they did). This is what Balls said. To be honest nobody ever came to me and complained about Damian McBride. I didn’t pass on those complaints to Gordon Brown. I didn’t complain about Damian McBride, because I don’t think until we saw the revelations in this book, we didn’t know what was going on. I didn’t know that Damian McBride was doing personal briefings against ministers. The first time I’d found out was when I saw the text of that hideous email in 2009, and I said to Damian ‘what have you done? How could it come to this?’ On the Today programme, Nick Robinson, the BBC's political editor, said that this claim would be greeted with "deep scepticism" in Brighton. He suggested that "collective eyebrows hit the ceiling" when Balls said he did not know what McBride was up to. Here's some examples of journalists – how shall we put this politely – expressing some doubts about this. Miliband said in his interview with Andrew Marr yesterday that he asked Brown to sack McBride. Douglas Alexander told a fringe meeting last night that he did the same. He made the point when asked about McBride's claim that Alexander told Brown to get rid of his sister Wendy as Labour's leader in Scotland. This is what Alexander said. I did urge Gordon to dismiss somebody. But it was Damian McBride. That might explain the way that he briefed against me then and writes against me now. I was always a supporter of my sister and I was never a supporter of Damian. • Balls denied using McBride to undermine his rivals in the Labour leadership contest. If I ever did, it didn't work very well, did it, because I didn't become the party leader. No, it's utter nonsense. There's always going to be people who will in an off-the-record smearing way make allegations, but it's not true and there's no evidence for that at all. It's not something I've ever done, it's not something I will ever do. • Balls said that he did not agree with Tessa Jowell about wanting Gordon Brown to say what he knew about McBride's activities. To be honest, I don’t think the world is helped by Gordon Brown saying anything about this. It’s in the past. It’s irrelevant. • Balls said that Labour had not yet decided whether it would increase capital spending. Labour would match the coalition's plans for day-to-day spending in 2015, while making different choices within the spending envelope. Asked if he would increase spending on capital spending, he said: "On capital, we won't make that decision until we see where we are on the economy in a year-and-a-half's time." • He said that he was happy to describe himself as socialist – but not his economic programme. Of course, I’ve always used the word socialism, I’ve no problem about that. We for decades have talked about socialism, about our values. It’s not a use of the word which would apply to an economic programme, it’s not sort of the kind of German Democratic Socialist Republic kind of socialism. It’s a socialism which is rooted in the struggles of working people in the 19th century to get fairness, proper rights at work, to have a health service which works for them, education available for all. It’s about an economy and a society which is not only for the privileged few. • Balls said he was not worried that some business groups had reacted negatively to Labour's plan to force big firms to hire an apprentice every time they employed a skilled foreign worker. The fact you don't always get a 100% reaction from some businesses or from some banks or from some energy companies to a Labour policy probably tells you that Labour is setting out clear, detailed, tough policies which will work for working families, many of whom will feel they have had a pretty bad deal from banks and energy companies and some businesses. It's Ed Balls' big day at the Labour conference and already we've had two stories from the speech. Last night it was revealed that Balls will use his speech to say that he wants the Office for Budget Responsibility, the government's spending watchdog, to audit Labour's spending plans before the general election. And this morning Labour has released excerpts from the speech saying Balls will propose extending free childcare from 15 to 25 hours a week, saying he can fund the measure by raising the bank levy. This morning Balls has been giving a round of interviews. I'll post the highlights soon. Here's the agenda for the day. 9am: Conference opens. Conference arrangements committee report. 9.35am: Gareth Thomas, chair of the Co-operative party, gives a speech. 9.40am: Debate on Britain's global role, with speeches from Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, Ivan Lewis, the shadow international development secretary, and Douglas Alexander, the shadow foreign secretary. 11am: Debate on work, including a speech from Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, and a vote on an emergency motion saying Labour should set up a commission on employment rights as a matter of urgency. 12pm: Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, delivers his speech. 1pm: Fringe meetings, including Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, on union rights, Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, on Labour's approach to poverty, Chuka Umunna on responsible capitalism and Jim Murphy on how to win the election. 2.15pm: Vernon Coaker, the shadow Northern Ireland secretary, gives a speech. 2.25pm: Debate on work and business, including speeches from Liam Byrne and Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, and a vote on an emergency motion saying a Labour government should end the public sector pay cap. I'll be covering all the news from the Labour conference, including looking at the papers and bringing you the best comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

results.webTitle: Letters: M&S and the gender politics of quilts

fields.headline: M&S and the gender politics of quilts
fields.trailText: Letters: In my experience, infant girls go for pink and frilly, and boys pick gun and dinosaur patterns. Sorry
fields.bodyText: Perhaps Martin Newnham (Letters, 21 September), anxious to dragoon his grandchildren into the right career pathways, should try allowing them to choose their own quilt covers. In my experience, infant girls go for pink and frilly, and boys pick gun and dinosaur patterns. Sorry, but that is generally how it appears to be. Maybe M&S know this. Richard Wilson Oxford • When my dad took me to buy a quilt cover back in the 70s he was asked: "Is it for a boy or a girl?" He answered: "No, it's for a bed." It seems that attitudes haven't changed much. Helen Fowweather Thame, Oxfordshire • Why didn't Mr Newnham just buy a boy's quilt and save himself 60p postage complaining to M&S? He should not worry so much, women are very capable in spite of M&S. Hugh Scullion London • I couldn't help making the connection between the grandfather finding pink quilts labelled for girls and space and dinosaur-themed quilts labelled for boys in Marks & Spencer, and a favourite aunt (A Letter to..., Family, 21 September) buying her nephew Lego, pens and books while her niece gets "pretty scarves, nail varnish and sweet necklaces". Because boys need "food, love and exercise" while girls just want "pretty knick-knacks and girly chats". And we wonder why more girls don't aspire to be scientists and engineers. Janet Hooper Ivybridge, Devon

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: MPs bicker over national security and public funding
fields.bodyText: Sir Humphrey seems the right point at which we part ways. Let's do the evening summary.
