Today, in federal politics: Two issues dominated: national security and a deal between the major parties about election funding and new thresholds for political donations. The Coalition is retaining pressure on the government over proper resourcing for Australia's spy agencies; and it's linking that issue to asylum boats. Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis used a private briefing from ASIO as a means of accusing the Prime Minister of misleading parliament. The Prime Minister had downplayed a Four Corners report on Monday night which suggested ASIO had been the subject of a hack - Senator Brandis suggested Ms Gillard's comment was incorrect based on his information from ASIO. Labor then blasted Senator Brandis for talking out of school. Opposition leader Tony Abbott gathered the cameras to record a meeting he and his team convened with a former ASIO chief, Paul O'Sullivan, on the subject of Australia's security. The Prime Minister then accused Mr Abbott of cheap politics and grubby theatrics. A deal between the major parties on public funding for elections and new thresholds for the disclosure of donations looked to be coming unstuck in the late afternoon as a backlash increased. Liberals were breaking ranks on the package. One MP declared it would be withdrawn before it reached the House of Representatives. The parliamentary independents criticised the package as a major party fix and a breach of faith with the voters. Labor man Martin Ferguson meanwhile announced he would retire from politics at the September poll. It was an emotional valedictory. Mr Abbott choked up as he paid tribute to Mr Ferguson. It was that kind of day - nothing held back. Anywhere. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers, who shot like a dream today in the chamber and elsewhere. Thanks to Sydney colleagues: Madhvi, Mary and Christian. We'll do it all again tomorrow. Vintage Sir Humphrey. Courageous means this will lose you the election. Labor's Joel Fitzgibbon, meanwhile, has gone all Sir Humphrey Appleby on the package. Mr Broadbent says its just untenable for the major parties to give themselves a big lick of taxpayers money. When you can't get money for an infrastructure project for your electorate, you can't go home and tell your voters that you've just voted for a package to give more money to political parties. Back now to this funding and donations deal between the government and the Coalition. It seems to be blowing up, big time. Victorian Liberal MP Russell Broadbent has just told me there is serious disquiet within the Coalition about this proposal. Mr Broadbent: I will not support this bill. There has been no public discussion about this. It's been dumped on us out of the blue. This bill has not been through the shadow cabinet or the party-room. Mr Broadbent won't go as far to say that he will cross the floor. He thinks this proposal is dead in the water. I expect the bill to be withdrawn before it gets to the House. A number of years ago I wrote a feature for The Age on the Fergusons and the uranium debate within the ALP. If you are interested in more particulars about this family, and one of Martin Ferguson's signature policy issues - convincing the ALP to comes to terms with uranium mining and exports - you can have a read of it here. If you click through and look at the page you'll see a great picture of Jack Ferguson and his sons. Here's a excerpt: The story of the Ferguson family tells much about Labor's internal struggles with uranium. Jack Ferguson — father of federal Labor MPs Martin and Laurie, and trade unionist Andrew — spoke passionately against softening Labor's stance at the conference of 1982. And 24 years after that time, his sons remain divided. Martin supports change, Andrew doesn't, and Laurie thinks the current policy is stupid, but this doesn't mean he's comfortable with where the party is going on the issue. A reader has emailed to chide me for failing to tell the story of Martin Ferguson's father Jack. I said father Jack was a NSW politician. My correspondent: Father Jack rose from being a bricklayer to Deputy Premier of NSW. Noted. And of course, that is absolutely true. Here's another section. On Labor values, the achievements since 2007, and the importance of remaining true to the principles of market economics. Mr Ferguson: My friends, we have had 21 years of growth in this country and we should never forget that as a nation. When other countries faced recession during the global financial crisis, Australia continued to grow. In my opinion, this, along with the apology to the Stolen Generation, are major achievements of Labor in government since 2007. I am also delighted that we are now finally moving towards a national disability scheme, a further chance to look after the less privileged in our society. Australia’s recognition as a member of the G20 is also a fitting recognition of the constructive role we play internationally. I think it’s fair to say many try to demonise the resources industry, but we should not forget that it accounts for 60 per cent of our export wealth – wealth that has meant we can take better care of our community, improve the lives of Australians and give opportunities to those who never thought that they could do the things they now do in that sector. As Minister for Energy, I am proud of the microeconomic reforms process I have continued to deliver in the energy sector – competition reforms established under the Keating Government. I am proud that I have stayed true to the market principles underpinning the sector to deliver an efficient and reliable essential service to the community. Let's press on now. I've sought a text of Mr Ferguson's speech. Here are some of the key passages. Mr Ferguson: When I look back on my career, firstly at the Miscellaneous Worker’s Union, then as ACTU President, and finally as a Member of Parliament, my main motivation has been to get Australians into decent, well-paying jobs. This is what the Labor Party means to me: helping those less fortunate in life by providing new jobs and opportunities to achieve a better quality of life. Creating opportunities by working with business is not the same thing as pointless class rhetoric. In essence we need to grow the pie to share it. As I said in my Maiden speech to Parliament on the 2nd of May 1996, “The people of the electorate are working people whose priorities first are their families, better job opportunities, better housing opportunities, better educational opportunities, better health opportunities and support for the aged”. During the 1980s and 1990s, this involved some genuinely tough decisions with people like Simon Crean, Bill Kelty, and of course Bob Hawke and Paul Keating. We knew the economy had to be restructured, which would involve closing down old industries, creating efficiencies and new opportunities. This meant that jobs were lost in many industries with members that I and other unionists had represented for decades. Colleagues, they were hard decisions to make, but they were taken in the knowledge that they would provide the basis for a better Australia for all of us. Let me tell you: standing at the factory gates with people who had just lost their jobs, all for the greater good of reforming our economy was not easy! Mr Dreyfus insists that ASIO is properly resourced. None of the delays in the construction of the ASIO building relate to any breach of security he says. I'm just going to pop round to Sky to talk to David myself about the events of the day. Back with you all in a jiffy. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus is out now to attack Senator George Brandis for revealing details of his private ASIO briefing. He's on Sky News, with political editor David Speers. Mr Dreyfus says the Prime Minister was correct to say there were inaccuracies in the Four Corners report. She was completely right to say so. He then declines to detail what the inaccuracies were. He's back to Senator Brandis being an outrage. While I've been watching Question Time, my colleague Lenore Taylor has been chasing down Coalition people. Just prior to Question Time, it was becoming clear that there is a fair amount of disquiet around the Coalition about the public funding of political parties deal. The Opposition leader's office is saying only that the party will respond after it sees details of the legislative package, which is supposed to be introduced tomorrow. Some MPs are unhappy that the Liberal Party has signed on to this arrangement with the ALP. Mr Abbott composes himself sufficiently to say farewell. Mr Ferguson looks chuffed. Notwithstanding Mr Ferguson's general disaffection - his concerns with Labor's political strategy, his support for Kevin Rudd, his general alienation from things - I think it's amazing he's going. Martin Ferguson is a workaholic. He lives, breathes, eats and sleeps politics, and Labor politics. He was born to, and for, this life. Hard to imagine him moving on. Bit of love too from Kevin Rudd. Some quick context and background for readers who don't follow politics closely. Mr Ferguson was a Cabinet minister up until earlier this year. A supporter of Kevin Rudd, Mr Ferguson went to the backbench after the botched leadership spill in March. Before parliament, Mr Ferguson was a trade union official. The Fergusons are a significant family in Labor history. Father Jack was a NSW politician. Mr Ferguson's brother Laurie is in federal parliament, and brother Andrew is a union leader. Mr Ferguson has been critical of Labor's class war rhetoric in this term. His remarks on that subject in the farewell speech this afternoon were another slap at the Treasurer Wayne Swan. Mr Ferguson's pragmatic political style, and his commitment to free market economics, has won him friends across the aisle. Mr Abbott's emotional reaction to the news is a barometer of that respect. A lot of Coalition MPs like the member for Batman. As Mr Abbott said: I salute an honourable opponent and a great Australian. Mr Ferguson has been a trenchant critic of the Greens. I doubt there will be tears this afternoon from Greens leader Christine Milne. Here was Mr Ferguson, in the chamber, saying farewell, just a few moments ago. The Prime Minister says farewell to Mr Ferguson. Lovely frame from Mike Bowers. Mr Ferguson thanks the parliamentary staff, the parliamentary library and parliamentary committees "where friendships are formed across the political divide." He thanks his former departmental officials, his personal staff. My success has been your success. Mr Ferguson chokes up as he mentions his parents, his wife and his children. Life has never been easy with me due to long hours and lengthy periods away from home. I thank Trisha, Ben and Clare for your encouragement and support. Without that I would never have been part of the achievement of Labor and the labour movement. Parliament breaks into applause. Ferguson is much respected in this place. Now the Prime Minister is seeking to speak on indulgence. It's been a privilege to sit and listen to him. Ms Gillard says in our own Labor way we will honour his commitment. I know personally the sacrifices he has made. I understand the toll that's taken. The Prime Minister says from Labor's point of view, he can leave this parliament knowing hes been a pivotal part of great policy achievements. The Leader of the Opposition contributes. Mr Abbott says its a sad day for the parliament to see the exit of a person of such stature. He is steeped in this parliament. He is steeped in the tradition of service. The Member for Batman is Labor royalty. He respects the party he has served, we too respect the party he has served, the Labor Party at its best has been a nation building party. I regret that he felt unable to remain in the current parliament and in the current government. Now Mr Abbott is crying. Labor's Martin Ferguson has used indulgence to announce he will quit parliament at the next election. Mr Ferguson is clearly emotional. He says he's advised the Prime Minister of his intentions. It's a wide-ranging farewell speech. Mr Ferguson remarks that false class warfare does no-one any credit. He says we have to grow the pie to share the pie. He's paying tribute to colleagues including Simon Crean and Bob Hawke. He says reforms in that era have been vital to Australia prosperity, but explaining that to the workers at the factory gate who had just lost their jobs was not easy. In this parliament, Mr Ferguson reflects on his work as resources minister. Many seek to demonise the resources industry, he says, but we should never forget it provides export wealth. The Prime Minister has ended Question Time. Mr Pyne is unhappy. Ms Gillard has ended proceedings before 3pm. Outrageous. Speaker Anna Burke has just advised the Member for Herbert not to be a dibber dobber. Stephen Jones sets a challenge. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has bowled up two questions to the Prime Minister concerning NSW police seeking information about asylum seekers. Mr Morrison says the government has refused to provide the information being sought by the NSW Police Commissioner. Mr Morrison is quoting evidence to senate estimates. Ms Gillard: If our authorities and agencies get legitimate requests from agencies, then of course they are responded to. That question from Bronwyn Bishop in the making. Liberal frontbencher Bronwyn Bishop to Ms Gillard. Given the Prime Minister's assertion that she takes national security seriously, why then, when she was deputy prime minister, did she send her bodyguard to meetings of the national security committee of Cabinet? The Prime Minister: The Leader of the Opposition in sanctioning that question is showing why he's unfit to lead. Another national security question - asylum seekers released without security checks. The Prime Minister objects again. They (the Opposition) gone down the low road of trying to provoke fear in the community. It is grossly unacceptable. The Prime Minister gets a border protection question. Ms Gillard counsels the Opposition against pursuing questions for base political motives. Hmm. Don't be political in Question Time? There's a new concept. Nobody does eye roll better than Speaker Anna Burke. The Prime Minister is asked whether she stands by comments she made to parliament yesterday concerning the Four Corners report on the hack of the ASIO building. The Prime MInister says she's not endorsing the partial quote from Liberal frontbencher Michael Keenan, but she's standing by her complete answer yesterday. I stand by the statement I made in full. Well done Anthony. Mr Byrne is congratulated by his good friend Kevin Rudd. Labor's Anthony Byrne in the House. The man of the moment. Mr Pyne is back now, asking the Prime Minister about Mr Byrne's answer. Given he's sticking by his criticism, do you still say he's wrong? The Prime Minister remarks that Mr Byrne has provided a very elegant answer to his previous question. Mr Pyne objects. He's now been warned by the Speaker. The Prime Minister sticks by her answer. The facts about ASIO are these. Since coming to government in 2007, the government has increased funding for ASIO by 27 per cent. This is a campaign being pursued for base political reasons, not the national interest. The Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne is posing a question to Labor backbencher Anthony Byrne - the man who kicked off this national security debate by raising concerns about the impact of the efficiency dividend on Australia's spy agencies. This is highly unusual, and House of Representative Speaker Anna Burke rules the question out of order. Mr Pyne objects, at length. Speaker Burke holds her ground. The question is out of order. Mr Pyne points out that Mr Byrne is happy to answer the question. He does look happy enough. Mr Albanese stands up to back the Speaker. Mr Pyne is then asked to withdraw an interjection. He withdraws, but complains that there has been a false statement. Speaker Burke asks Mr Pyne to rephrase his question. Mr Albanese says hang on, why is he getting another go? Speaker Burke ignores Mr Albanese and says re-phrase the question. Mr Pyne re-phrases the question. Mr Albanese objects again. The question is out of order. Speaker Burke says part of it is. Mr Byrne is on his feet. Mr Byrne: I have nothing further to add to the statement I have made. Mr Byrne then criticises the Leader of the Opposition. He says the report from the intelligence committee was a bipartisan report. He blasts Mr Abbott for throwing national security around like a political football. He says Mr Abbott is the least qualified person to comment on national security since Mark Latham. Mark Latham! He would of course be a former Labor leader. I wish readers of #politicslive could have heard the roar of laughter that just echoed up the corridor of the parliamentary press gallery in Canberra. Gold. Here's Question Time. Mr Abbott opens on national security and ASIO. Does the Prime MInister accept evidence given to a senate committee that ASIO has reduced its overseas operations? The Prime Minister: I refer the Leader of the Opposition to my answers yesterday. Over the life of this government funding has increased by 27 per cent. The facts are the facts, and they are exactly as I've outlined them. Mr Abbott comes back with two supplementaries quoting evidence to senate committees. Ms Gillard sticks by her statement that ASIO's funding has increased. Labor MP Stephen Jones, who has lead a backbench push to crack down on live odds being broadcast during sport, has made a quick statement to parliament. He's welcomed the Prime Minister's response unveiled on Sunday. But he's indicating the matter is not yet concluded. Mr Jones wanted stronger measures. It may well not be the last word on the matter. Stirring in Liberal ranks? ABCNews24 is broadcasting an interview with Gary Gray, the former Special Minister of State, on election funding. Political editor Lyndal Curtis is asking Mr Gray can he guarantee that the extra public funding won't go to political advertising for the looming campaign. Mr Gray says the money will go to administrative funding, not to ads. Lyndal doesn't seem convinced. She's also wondering why Labor rolled over on the reporting threshold for political donations. Labor wanted to reduce the threshold to $1,000. The ALP shook hands with the Liberals on $5,000. Why, when you had the numbers to get $1,000 through the parliament with the cross benchers, did you backflip, Lyndal inquires. Mr Gray says its best to reach agreement on such matters between the "parties of government" - not the minors and micros. Not sure how that's become conventional wisdom, but it has over the past 48 hours. The Liberals, it must be said, are noticeably quiet on this deal. I don't think I've heard anyone out defending it. Have I missed something? Question Time coming up at 2pm, stay tuned. Speaking of 100 days to go, election campaigns always mean an assault on the letterboxes of the voters of Australia. This leaflet looks particularly charming. Some video just in of the stoush we pointed you to earlier today between National senator Barnaby Joyce and Independent Tony Windsor - slugging it out in September for the seat of New England. Recapping: Mr Windsor alleged Senator Joyce was the lucky beneficiary of a donation from mining magnate Gina Rinehart. Senator Joyce: To the best of my knowledge, I've received not so much as a dollar. Do you accept Senator Joyce at his word Mr Windsor? No, I don't. Don't worry - only 100 days to go, give or take. Professor Lowe is talking about the downsides of work intensification on well being. (Anyone know what he's talking about? No, didn't think so, el desko readers.) Now he's pondering the strategy of promoting economic growth at all costs. Professor Lowe says we are failing miserably by looking at just one blunt measure. How about sustainability? How about biodiversity? Health, happiness, wisdom. Watching the sunset, listening to the birds. We need to think about what we value and how we get back to improving our quality of life. And we need to be globosapiens (that's wise citizens of the earth). There's only one planet. No plan B. Consumption should not be an end in itself. The future is not somewhere we are going, it's something we are creating. The National Press Club is playing host today to Ian Lowe of the Australian Conservation Foundation. The theme is ecological security, and spending to promote it. A lovely scene from the building from Mike Bowers. Senator Brandis strolls through the Mural Hall with colleague, Wyatt Roy. Now speaking of around the building, I'm informed the Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey and former Prime Minister Kevin Rudd are enjoying a cappucino together in the autumn sunshine. Aussies courtyard. A place of peace love and harmony. Let's take stock. Seven things about federal politics you need to know this lunchtime. National security is still dominating the political news cycle. Shadow-Attorney General Geroge Brandis has accused the Prime Minister of misleading parliament yesterday over comments she made about an "inaccurate" Four Corners story about a hack on the ASIO building. Senator Brandis' comments followed a private security briefing with ASIO late yesterday. Senator Brandis has been given a slap by Transport Minister Anthony Albanese for speaking publicly about an intelligence briefing. Not the done thing, Mr Albanese says. Mr Abbott is keeping the spotlight firmly on this issue, inviting a former ASIO chief, Paul O'Sullivan, in for a chat about security issues in general. Remarked Mr Abbott at said briefing: "These are difficult and testing times for our security agencies. We are determined as a Coalition, should responsibility fall on our shoulders, to make sure that our agencies have the resources to do the job that is necessary." The deal between the ALP and the Liberal Party covering public funding for the election and donation thresholds has prompted all the parliamentary independents to wring their hands and warn of a voter backlash. Senator Xenophon, in continuation. The outrage about the major party agreement on public funding and donation thresholds continues to echo around the parliament today. We've had Rob Oakeshott, and Tony Windsor, and the Greens out bagging this deal this morning. South Australian Independent Nick Xenophon is the latest person to find a microphone. This is about an escalating arms race in terms of advertising, and this helps incumbents. I think there will be a backlash. If so inclined, you can listen-in to Mr Abbott's remarks at the beginning of his meeting with his national security team and Mr O'Sullivan this morning. Speaking of bi-partisanship, rallying for cancer. Health Minister Tanya Plibersek, and her shadow, Peter Dutton. Important to point out for the record that staged photo opportunities is very much multi-partisan practice in this place. Funny isn't it though? It feels very analogue in the digital world. Excuse me. Could we have some privacy? Greens Senator Hanson-Young has ventured out to one of the parliamentary courtyards to comment on a story from the ABC that police in effect inflamed tensions on Christmas Island in the lead-up to a riot by detainees in March. She's not sure if a senate committee was misled by officials giving evidence about this incident. But Senator Hanson-Young wants the Gillard Government to overturn its asylum policy, particularly the no-advantage test ensuring that asylum seekers can't improve their circumstances by paying a people smuggler. It is a recipe for disaster. We need to scrap the no-advantage rule. My colleague Lenore Taylor has kicked off her new analytical blog this morning with a column about the post truth election. Politicians have always tried to paint their opponents in an unflattering way and cast doubt upon their promises and credibility. But increasingly the “narrative”, the story politicians tell about themselves and their opponents, bears scant relationship to the actual policies on offer. Journalism, and the new trend towards "fact checking" units, is supposed to separate the policies from the stories. But as Mitt Romney’s pollster Neil Newhouse famously declared during the US presidential election, “We’re not going to let our campaign be dictated by fact checkers.” The challenge is to make sure that come polling day, voters can still make a decision on the basis of facts. Interested of course in thoughts from readers. You can join in on the blog, or on Twitter, using the hashtag #politicslive I thought yesterday was a blast from 2001. Political tragics might enjoy this wander down memory lane. Here's John Howard's election policy speech from 2001 - the speech that famously declared we decide who comes here, and the circumstances in which they come. The more things change, and all that. Amusingly I gather, Mr Abbott asked the snappers to leave the organised picture opportunity because "we need some privacy." No use pondering manifest ironies in politics. The quickest route to madness - that sort of thing. Meanwhile, Michelle Grattan from The Conversation wrote a piece this morning analysing the return of national security to the political stage. She reasons national security is never a great issue for Labor. Question Time yesterday was dominated by what we once called the "tin hat" themes: appropriate resourcing for security agencies, and boat arrivals. Tony Abbott's political attack was straight out of John Howard's playbook. Here's an excerpt from Michelle: National security issues don’t play well for Labor, so to have two controversies erupting simultaneously is a fresh blow for a government already on the ropes. Four Corners' allegation that Chinese hackers have obtained the blueprint of Canberra’s huge new ASIO building was separate from complaints on Monday from Anthony Byrne, Labor chairman of the parliamentary committee that oversights the intelligence organisations, that agencies are struggling under belt tightening. But the two have been easily conflated by the opposition, which also drew a link between the funding argument and the large numbers of boat people, whom it said are in the community without adequate security checks. And in the background is a heightened awareness of danger, with chief of the Australian Defence Force David Hurley sending ADF members a warning to be vigilant, in the wake of the appalling murder in London of the British soldier, and a high-profile arrest in Sydney this week by officers of the counter-terrorism team of a man who allegedly threatened a public official. Now for some alternative government optics. Photographers were invited down to record a meeting between the leader of the Opposition, Mr Abbott, his national security team, and the former head of ASIO, Paul O'Sullivan. Mr Abbott's spokesman says Mr O'Sullivan was invited in this morning to brief the Opposition on a range of national security issues. Has the budget emergency been overtaken by the national security emergency? And this one. Mr Albanese was asked about party reform because of a story we covered yesterday on the blog: a motion from Victorian backbencher Steve Gibbons to get rid of the practice of the leader appointing the front bench. Kevin Rudd introduced this practice as leader, overturning a longstanding tradition where the factional bosses selected the ministry. Mr Gibbons, just for your background, is no fan of Mr Rudd. Rather amusingly, Mr Rudd was quoted in a newspaper this morning indicating he would support the Gibbons motion. Mr Gibbons took to Twitter for his reaction. If so inclined, you can listen to this short grab from Senator Brandis on Sky Television earlier. He says the Prime Minister in Question Time yesterday dismissed the Four Corners report as inaccurate. That claim, by the Prime Minister, is false. The Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis has been out this morning telling reporters that the Prime Minister misled parliament yesterday when she suggested a Four Corners report about a hack on the ASIO building was "inaccurate." Senator Brandis was briefed by the head of ASIO late yesterday concerning the allegations aired in the Four Corners program. Mr Albanese is asked to comment on whether Ms Gillard misled the parliament. Senator Brandis was briefed by ASIO last night, a reporter begins. Mr Albanese: How do you know that? That was a joke. You want me to comment on an interview between Senator Brandis and ASIO? Get serious. I'm not going to comment on an intelligence briefing. I pretty surprised the Shadow Attorney-General is commenting on a briefing from ASIO. Then he's asked about the electoral funding and donations package. Do these changes have any merit? There are absolute merits to this reform. Such as? Mr Albanese says political donations of $5,000 or more will now be transparent. He says it is always desirable as a point of principle with electoral reform to proceed on a bipartisan basis - to seek major party agreement. Then we are onto ALP reform. Should the caucus select the ALP frontbench, or should the leader select the frontbench? Mr Albanese says the party needs reform. He wants democratisation of the party from the grass roots up. He says party members should have a say in the ALP leadership. Now, is Kevin Rudd causing trouble again? Kevin is continuing to do his job. The Transport Minister Anthony Albanese is out in one of the chilly parliament courtyards addressing reporters. Mr Albanese says he's cranky with the Coalition for voting against legislation last night "standing up for Australian jobs." Mr Albanese then finds himself being asked about Ford. He's sad about Ford leaving Australia, and the job losses at Geelong and Broadmeadows. Then he's asked about the fate of the Parramatta to Epping rail link. (We mentioned this story in our wrap of the political news this morning.) Why has this project been shelved? We didn't defer it, the state government has refused to go ahead with this project. We can't force a state government to build an extension to a state government rail line. On the subject of mining squllionaires, my ever diligent colleague from ABC online, Simon Cullen, has this update about Clive Palmer and his PUP. In addition to the national security theme, MPs are continuing their huffing and puffing over a deal the ALP and the Liberal Party have struck about political donations and public funding for elections. The Independents, Tony Windsor and Rob Oakeshott, think the major parties have done the wrong thing. Mr Oakeshott is suggesting this is a breach of the agreement he reached with the ALP allowing the ALP to form government after the 2010 poll. And just to stir the pot on political donations a bit more, Mr Windsor is out and about declaring that his opponent in the seat of New England, the Nationals' Barnaby Joyce, has been the lucky recipient of help from mining billionaire Gina Rinehart. Senator Joyce is personally close to the reclusive Ms Rinehart. Not so, says Senator Joyce. He's been returning in kind. Mr Windsor, he says, is the multi-millionaire, courtesy of the sale of his property to a coal company. It's nasty up there: New England will be one of the most interesting contests of the 2013 election in my view. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. Our gorgeous opening blog picture from Mike Bowers seems absolutely appropriate given Canberra seems to have swerved briefly into a spy thriller. A testy political debate over spies and terror has carried over from yesterday into the morning in politics. Lets begin this morning with a run through the main political stories in the newspapers. Climate The Clean Energy Finance Corporation is planning to write up to $800 million in green loans before the election, defying the Coalition's call for the agency not to sign contracts before September 14 because Tony Abbott has vowed to scrap it. The CEFC has revealed it is in "active discussions" with 50 projects seeking $2 billion and that an additional 119 project proponents have presented proposals that are seeking finance worth $3.3bn. The figures are contained in an email from the CEFC to the opposition pleading its case not to be scrapped if the Coalition wins the election. (Paywalled) Public funding of elections With unusual bipartisanship, Labor and the Coalition argue their agreement to award political parties another $58m in public funding is good for democracy. But the Greens and independents beg to differ. Despite a fiery debate in Labor’s caucus, in which long-time campaigners for donation reform said they were “ashamed” of the agreement, the government formally announced details of the changes to be rushed through before parliament rises for the September election. The attorney general, Mark Dreyfus, confirmed they would award around $1 per vote in each house (33 cents per vote per year of a parliamentary term) to all parties and independents to help with the costs of party administration and free them from the need to rely entirely on private donations. The payment is on top of the $2.47 per vote the parties are already entitled to. Terror An Australian-born man being investigated for alleged terrorism offences threatened to slit the throat of a commonwealth official during a telephone conversation between the two, a court has heard. Milad Bin Ahmad Shah al-Ahmadzai was arrested at his home in Sydney's west on Monday night by officers from the Joint Counter Terrorism Team, which works closely with ASIO and includes staff from the Australian Federal Police. (Paywalled) Paid parental leave Tony Abbott has sought to silence growing internal dissent over his controversial paid parental leave scheme by asserting the decision to proceed with the policy was set in stone and insisting most women, including farmer’s wives, would benefit. Mr Abbott put his foot down in Tuesday’s joint Coalition party room meeting as the dissent among the Nationals grew louder following the Coalition’s decision to support Labor's budget saving to axe the baby bonus for stay-at-home mothers. Mr Abbott also faced unrest as the party room endorsed the shadow cabinet decision to support another savings measure which would trim the 30 per cent private health insurance rebate by $700 million over four years. Eight MPs spoke out on this. Paid parental leave was discussed at Nationals’ own party room meeting on Monday night. Informally, some Nationals are threatening to vote against the measure when in government; one, who did not want to be identified, told colleagues he had eaten his fair share of “shit sandwiches” over the years but “I’m not going to eat this one”. (Payawlled) Rudd mischief Kevin Rudd backers have again been accused of engaging in a final attempt to destabilise Prime Minister Julia Gillard before the election. With 108 days to go before the poll, tensions in the Labor caucus spilled over yesterday, with the PM coming under attack on a number of fronts from within her own ranks. Rudd supporters have been accused of trying to inflame a sledging match in caucus after Senator John Faulkner condemned new laws which give political parties an extra $58 million in taxpayer-subsidised campaign funds and go soft on disclosure rules. Mr Faulkner called the laws a "disgrace". MPs were also taken by surprise when retiring MP Steve Gibbons, a fan of neither Ms Gillard or Mr Rudd, put a motion to the caucus calling for the PM to be stripped of her powers to choose her Cabinet - powers first granted to Mr Rudd in 2007. Marginal-seat MP Laura Smythe, a Rudd supporter, also weighed in, raising the ongoing debacle of asylum seeker policy. Adding fuel to the fire, Mr Rudd yesterday backed Mr Gibbons' call, claiming he would support the reforms. A spokesman said: "Following his statements of February, 2012, of course Mr Rudd would support this change as part of a package of larger reforms to the Australian Labor Party, including reforms which Mr Rudd has spoken of in the past." Transport Rail commuters and motorists will wait longer for vital infrastructure after the Gillard government quietly shifted $2 billion earmarked for the Parramatta-Epping line into a fund for projects not due to be built until after 2019. The decision puts a gaping hole in Tony Abbott's $6 billion promise to redirect the money to duplicate the Pacific Highway if the Coalition wins government in September. In addition to following my live updates throughout the day, you can also follow me on Twitter @murpharoo and join in the conversation there by using the hashtag #politicslive

results.webTitle: Energy bills plan announced and Cameron in China: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Energy bills plan announced and Cameron in China: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including the full details of the government's plans to cut energy bills, energy firms announcing cuts in response, and David Cameron in China
fields.bodyText: • Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, has complained that energy companies will not have to contribute a penny towards the measures announced today to curb fuel price rises. She was speaking in the Commons, where she dismissed Ed Davey's measures as ineffective. She told the energy secretary: Last month, you appeared to agree with our criticisms of the way energy companies have put up their prices when you said they were treating their customers like, and I quote, 'cash cows'. In light of what you have said, can you confirm that in what you have said today there is not a single measure that will cost the energy companies a single penny? [There are] hundreds of millions of pounds of tax-payers' money being spent, energy companies helping fewer households with their energy efficiency and yet people's bills will still be higher this winter than last, and the energy companies are still allowed to carry on over-charging people. Whatever you say here today, if you genuinely want to get bills down, nothing less than a price freeze, and action to stop the energy companies over-charging, will do. The Green party has also come out against them. This is from Caroline Lucas, the Green MP. It is extraordinarily irresponsible, as well as counterproductive, for the government to claim it is reducing energy bills by slashing the scheme designed to bring them down. Watering down our energy efficiency commitments at precisely the time their most needed would mean more cold homes, more winter deaths, and job losses in the construction industry. Only a massive programme of home insulation and other energy efficiency measures will bring energy bills down for good. • Dominic Grieve, the attorney general, has accused the European Union of over-stepping its legal powers. He is making the argument in a speech in Brussels. This is what he said about the speech in advance. This won't just be the usual Euro bashing by a British minister. I am going to Brussels to speak - partly in French - as a lawyer, to lawyers and spell out what lies at the base of Britain's and other member states' frustrations with the EU. I'm going to talk about some very specific proposals where we think there's been some woolly thinking and try and get across to these key EU legal officials and judges that it's this sort of thing that really riles citizens in Britain and across Europe and needs to be changed if the EU is to meet the challenge of securing prosperity for people across the continent. And here's an extract from the speech released in advance. Overstepping the boundaries under the treaties undermines the very legitimacy of EU action. To put it straightforwardly, the EU should only be doing what member states signed up to. And if this doesn't happen, its legitimacy in the eyes of the public is undermined. As the PM said in January, 'democratic consent for the EU in Britain is now wafer thin'. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Bob Stewart, a Conservative, asks what would happen if the government told the European Union that it was not going to collect VAT on fuel. Davey says that would be an unwise idea. The lawyers would not allow it, he says. Mark Pritchard, a Conservative, asks what the government is doing to help people who use oil to heat their homes. Davey says one of his ministerial colleagues has been doing a lot of work on this. For example, the government has been promoting a "buy early" scheme that can help. Philip Davies, a Conservative, says Davey is reciting "the same kind of claptrap" that Ed Miliband used to produce when he was energy secretary. His constituents want the cheapest energy, not the greenest energy, Davies says. Davey says he does not accept that. Labour's John Healey says people will see a government failing to stand up to the energy companies. Davey says he does not accept that. If they look at Labour, people will see a a government not acting in the national interest, because its plans would cut investment. Labour's David Anderson says the energy companies have got off "scot free". Davey says he does not accept that. Christopher Chope, a Conservative, asks why Davey did not just get rid of VAT on fuel bills. That would have been illegal, Davey says. Labour's David Winnick says an increasing number of people are "terrified" to use their heating. We are returning to the fuel poverty that existed under the last Labour government, he says. As Tory MP s jeer, he corrects himself; under the last Tory government, he says. Ed Davey says the last Labour energy secretary (Ed Miliband) said alarming people about energy bills was not a mature way to conduct politics. He should have kept his word, Davey says. Davey is responding to Flint. He says she did not welcome this price cut. He looked at the energy freeze proposal, he says. But it would not work. Firms would put their prices up before and afterwards. He says the government told the Big Six that it expected them to pass cuts on. That is what will happen. The Big Six were created by Labour, he says. In 2000 there were 17 suppliers in the market. Labour took that down to six. Davey says the government is consulting. And when it publishes its consultation paper, it will publish its impact assessment. He says he is keen to keep people in employment. As for moving the costs on to taxation, that fuel poverty campaign groups campaigned for this, because it is more progressive, he says. Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, is responding. Does Davey accept that the energy firms will still be allowed to raise prices this winter? Can Davey confirm that, if the average increase this winter is £120, his £50 cut will still lead to customers paying £70 more. One supplier has said it will not pass on cuts until March, she says. What will the government do to ensure cuts are passed on immediately. Flint says the Energy Company Obligation is badly designed. How many fewer households will receive energy saving measures? If ECO is running for four years, not two, doesn't that mean half as many people will be helped every year? What will be the impact on jobs? And can Davey confirm the burden is just being shifted to taxation? Davey said a few weeks ago energy firms were treating their customers like cash cows. Can he confirm there is nothing in this that will cost them a penny? Nothing less than a price freeze will do, she says. Davey explains why role the green levies play. But these measures are paid for directly by consumers. So the government should keep them under review. The government has to act responsibly, he says. It has reviewed the "cost profile" of these measures, he says. And that is why it has announced changes. Average bills in 2014 will be lower than they would otherwise have been, he says. And he says the energy companies have said there will be no need for price rises in 2014, unless there is a significant change to wholesale costs. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, is speaking now. The latest price rises announced by the energy companies were particularly unwelcome, he says. Rising wholesale prices were particularly to blame. But the energy firms need to be more transparent about their prices, he says. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, is about to make a statement in the Commons about energy bills. I would be surprised if he has anything much new to say. Full details of the proposals were released this morning. But the reaction could be interesting. More on Hutton-gate. Here's a short energy bills reading list. • YouGov's Peter Kellner says that voters do not trust what politicians are saying about energy prices. Our political leaders, then, are right to detect the political sensitivity of fuel prices. But they should beware of interpreting public opinion too simply. Yes, voters like any help they can get coping with the cost of living. But what they want even more is competent, clear-sighted political leadership. A pound a week off our fuel bills, like the promise of a 17-month post-election freeze, is all very well. But it will attract electoral dividends only if it can be presented as part of a responsible and lasting approach to the problem. Which is why the choice of immediate policy is only part of the challenge. Restoring trust is the bigger task, and the leaderships of all three main parties have a huge amount to do. • Mat Hope at the Carbon Brief blog says today's announcement "looks like it's going to have a small, short-term impact on household energy bills - announced just in time for Christmas". But energy bills will still rise in the New Year, he says. • Trewin Restorick at Huffington Post suggests four measures that could make green levies more palatable to politicians and voters. In China, in a television interview, David Cameron rejected suggestions that there was an inconsistency between his conduct in Sri Lanka, where he made human rights the focus of his visit, and his conduct in China, where he has played down the human rights agenda. I think I am totally consistent. I come to China and I don't believe there is a choice between raising growth and investment issues or raising human rights issues. I raise them both. That's what a policy of engagement is all about. There are some huge opportunities here in China for British jobs, British growth, British investment. I want to make the most of them and that is right for our country to compete and succeed in the global race. But I also raised concerns about human rights and we have agreed on this visit to restart the Britain/China human rights dialogue which will start again next year, so that is an important achievement too. Asked whether he regretted meeting the Dalai Lama (which offended the Chinese, leading to a trip planned for earlier this year being cancelled), Cameron replied: The British policy towards Tibet is unchanged. I have met the Dalai Lama as leader of the opposition, I have met him as prime minister. I don't have plans to meet him again. But my diary is for me to decide. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, is accompanying David Cameron in China. Labour doesn't think he should be there. This is from Andrew Gwynne, a shadow health minister. With A&Es bursting at the seams, the NHS stands on the verge of a winter crisis yet our Health Secretary has decided his priority is jetting off to China. Jeremy Hunt should be focusing on clearing up the mess he and David Cameron have made of the health service not running thousands of miles in the other direction. It proves you can't trust them with the NHS. • The six major energy companies have said gas and electricity bills will rise by around £50 less than expected this winter, after the government published full details of his plans to curb energy bill increases. You can read details of the government's announcements at 8.53am and 9.20am, and the energy companies' response are at 9.39am, at 10.08am and at 11.34am. Ed Miliband has said that, despite what the government has unveiled, bills are still going up. (See 11.02am.) And environmentalists have criticised the move. This is from Sophie Neuburg, a Friends of the Earth energy campaigner. The government has crumbled in response to pressure from the Big Six, leaving the fuel poor and the environment to pick up the bill. The effect of all the measures announced today is that funding for energy efficiency has fallen by over £700 million, condemning thousands of people to shiver in heat-leaking homes. If ministers were serious about tackling fuel bills they would introduce a comprehensive energy efficiency programme and take urgent steps to wean our economy off increasingly costly fossil fuels – the real driving force behind rocketing fuel bills. • Michael Fallon, the energy minister, has confirmed that the government wants to change the definition of fuel poverty. This is what he told the World at One. The old definition wasn’t very helpful – it included relatively well-off people who happened to have very large, drafty houses, and you don’t want taxpayer help for them. And also, the population that we were helping through the old definition moved around a bit, and it was different people all the time. So we’ve been working with the voluntary organisations and we’ve had an independent review under Professor Hills to come up with a better definition of those who not only have higher costs but have less income. We haven’t finalised the definition yet – we’ve simply taken power in the energy bill, which is going to become law shortly, to set this new definition, and we are consulting all the voluntary organisations on it. • David Cameron has said Britain will act as China's strongest advocate in the west. At the start of a three-day trip to the country, he also promised to lead a "dialogue of mutual respect and understanding". • Downing Street has protested to the Chinese authorities about a "completely inappropriate" decision to bar a British journalist from a press conference in Beijing with David Cameron and his Chinese counterpart, Li Keqiang. • Cameron has distanced himself from Boris Johnson after the London mayor mocked people with low IQs and hailed the benefits of greed and inequality in spurring economic growth. • Downing Street has rejected claims that Cameron's visit amounts to a "crony trip" because of the number of close associates he is taking with him. Asked about this claim, a Number 10 spokesman said: "I don't accept that at all." He went on: "The delegation list is drawn together by UKTI. It is determined on the basis of the very best business opportunities. It is all about getting the very best business opportunities for the UK. • The Office for National Statistics has released figures showing Britain's wage-earners have taken a £5,000 pay cut in the past five years. • The Bank of England has admitted that the supply of credit to small businesses is still constrained even though the amount advanced in the third quarter was the highest since the flagship funding for lending (FLS) scheme was introduced in summer 2012. • The TUC has published research claiming that "the government’s General Anti-Abuse Rule (GAAR) is so poorly designed that it will allow 99 per cent of tax avoidance to continue". • A consortium has been formed to save the Gay Hussar, the celebrated political eatery in London's Soho which is due to be sold this week. As the Press Association reports, The Gay Hussar has been the haunt of politicians and journalists for decades and the consortium includes Lord Kinnock, the former Labour leader, and Lord Ashcroft, former deputy chairman of the Conservative Party. Other prominent supporters and backers include former Tory leadership challenger David Davis, Labour MP John Cryer and Neil and Christine Hamilton. There are more details on the consortium's website. My colleague Karl Mathiesen is investigating whether the government is right to say that the energy price measures announced today are really carbon neutral. If you have got any thoughts on this, please post them on his Eco Audit blog. In a new book of essays about foreign policy edited by the shadow foreign secretary, Douglas Alexander, and Ian Kearns, Influencing Tomorrow, Mark Leonard argues that Britain has been rather naive about the prospects of getting China to become more liberal. The hard truth is that Western countries have relied on an overly optimistic view that engaging China and supporting its development would inevitably end up transforming its political system, economy and foreign policy. The experience of the last five years has revealed this as a faith-based approach with nothing to show for it. What happened to Bloomberg's Rob Hutton in China seems to make this point quite well. And so with that in mind (and not just because lobby journalists love writing about themselves - although they do) here's a round up of Twitter comment on Hutton-gate. From Iain Dale, the blogger and publisher From the Daily Mail's Tim Shipman From the Labour blogger Hopi Sen You can read Conquest's three laws here. From the broadcaster Julia Hartley-Brewer From the Telegraph's Peter Foster Hutton has recently published a very good book about journalese called Romps, Tots and Boffins. (On Twitter I called it a mini classic.) This has prompted some predictable jokes. Declaration of interest: Hutton is based in the Guardian's office in the House of Commons, and his desk is next to mine. He's a friend. And, on his China trip, David Cameron has also distanced himself from Boris Johnson's comments suggesting those with a low IQ are destined to fail in life. Here's Nicholas Watt's story on the Bloomberg journalist Rob Hutton being banned from a press conference in China. And here's how it starts. Downing Street has protested to the Chinese authorities over the "completely inappropriate" decision to bar a British journalist from attending a press conference in Beijing with David Cameron and his Chinese counterpart, Li Keqiang. No 10 raised its "deep concerns" on two occasions with Chinese officials after the foreign ministry decided to exclude Robert Hutton, a political journalist with the US wire service Bloomberg, from the event at the Great Hall of the People on Monday. British officials in Beijing informed Hutton, a member of the British parliamentary lobby who is accompanying the prime minister to China, that he would not be admitted to the press conference ... The Bloomberg website is blocked in China after it ran stories on the wealth of families of senior leaders including relatives of the president, Xi Jinping. The US news organisation last month denied killing a similar sensitive story following a New York Times report that said editors had been concerned its ability to report from China would be compromised if it ran the piece. Bloomberg said the piece was still in preparation. "Press conference" is probably not quite the right phrase. As Nick explains in his copy, this was a "no questions" press conference - which means it was not really a press conference at all. Did you know we have a National Fraud Authority? Well, we won't for much longer. Theresa May, the home secretary, is abolishing it. She's announced this in a written statement. Here's an extract. As part of the government’s reforms to policing and the fight against serious and organised crime, I have decided to close the National Fraud Authority and realign its responsibilities to reflect the creation of the National Crime Agency. The National Crime Agency, with its Economic Crime Command, will bring a single national focus to cutting economic crime and will lead and coordinate the national fight against fraud, working with law enforcement agencies, regulators, government and the public, private and voluntary sectors. While the National Fraud Authority has been successful in raising awareness of fraud and improving co-ordination, the focus should now be on cutting economic crime. The National Fraud Authority will close by 31 March. Labour has sharpened its attack on the coalition over energy prices. This looks interesting. Rob Hutton is a Bloomberg political correspondent and a member of the Westminster lobby travelling with David Cameron. I'll post more when I get the details. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are three articles I found particularly interesting. • Andrew Grice in the Independent says George Osborne wants departmental spending cuts to fund the giveaways in the autumn statement. George Osborne is demanding a round of last-minute spending cuts from Whitehall departments to fund more than £2bn of giveaways in his Autumn Statement on Thursday. The Chancellor has angered Conservative and Liberal Democrat ministers by asking for emergency savings on the eve of his statement so that he can balance the books. The departments under pressure include the Home Office, Justice, Defence, Business and Work and Pensions. Mr Osborne’s move has reopened a Cabinet debate over the previous decision to ring-fence the NHS, schools and overseas aid budgets, meaning any extra savings must be found from the non-protected departments that have already suffered cuts announced since 2010. • Elizabeth Rigby and Tanya Powley in the Financial Times (subscription) say industrialists are complaining that green taxes are pushing them to "crisis point". British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. • Tom Bawden in the Independent says the government is changing the definition of fuel poverty. The Government is attempting to manipulate official figures to bring down fuel poverty, it is claimed today. A clause in the Energy Bill will change the definition of the key poverty indicator, reducing the number of English households counted as “fuel-poor” from 3.2 million to 2.4 million overnight. The new definition, which could come into force before Christmas, will instantly reduce the percentage of fuel-poor households in England by nearly a third, from 15 per cent to 11 per cent, according to calculations by MPs on the cross-party Environmental Audit Committee (EAC). “The Government is shifting the goalposts on fuel poverty so that official statistics record far fewer households as fuel-poor,” said the committee’s chair, Joan Walley MP. “The changes to the fuel-poverty definition and target should be stopped unless the Government is prepared to make a public commitment to end fuel poverty altogether.” ScottishPower has now become the last of the big six energy companies to put out a statement responding to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says it hopes to avoid further price rises in 2014. ScottishPower welcomes the Government’s announcement of proposals to reduce the impact of the Energy Company Obligation, an introduction of a universal rebate and other measures. Once implemented early in the New Year, these initiatives will lead to real savings for customers in 2014 and ScottishPower agrees with Government estimates which suggest total savings for households of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. Neil Clitheroe, CEO of Retail and Generation at ScottishPower, said: “We are acutely aware of the burden that higher gas and electricity bills place on households and have been working with Government to ease the strain. We are pleased the Government has recognised the impact of green and social levies on rising consumer energy bills and we have worked collaboratively with them to identify a number of individual savings not only to these programmes, but also other rising input costs. “We look forward to working with Government on the detail of these proposals. We will continue to be as open as possible about consumer bills, explaining the impact of input costs on a line by line basis. As always, we will continue to try and avoid further price rises in 2014, but this will depend on whether there are increases in wholesale energy prices or other costs outside our control." The Office for National Statistics has published a report on what has happened to average household incomes over the last 35 years. Here's the summary and here's the full report (pdf). It says that since the start of the downturn median household income has fallen by 3.8%, allowing for inflation. But there is a big gap between what's happened to pensioner households and non-pensioner households. However, when looking separately at non-retired and retired households, the median income for non-retired households fell by 6.4% between 2007/08 and 2011/12, while the median income for retired households grew by 5.1%. While David Cameron has been tweeting about his meeting with the Chinese president. Here's Ed Miliband on the energy bills announcement. David Cameron has been meeting the Chinese president, Xi Jinping. Here's what the Press Association has filed about how it started. The two men shook hands for the cameras in front of their nations' flags before going into talks with small teams of advisers. Xi thanked Cameron for the congratulations he sent after he was elected general secretary of the Chinese Communist Party and President of China a year ago. And he asked the PM to pass on his "cordial greetings and best wishes to Her Majesty the Queen". Cameron responded: "I will certainly pass your regards to Her Majesty. I know that she too wants to see very strong relations between Britain and China and also looks forward to future potential visits between our two countries." Xi reminded Cameron that they first met in 2007, during his visit to Beijing as leader of the opposition, when he expressed the hope that he would return one day as PM. The Chinese president added: "On several occasions, you have expressed a positive attitude of valuing the role of China and your commitment to enhancing the UK's relations with China. I appreciate your position." And he told Cameron: "We have come to a crucial moment in global economic recovery and change in the international landscape. "Both China and UK are major economies in the world and permanent members of the UN Security Council. The relation between our two countries not only concerns our respective development, but also will exert major global impact." Cameron said that his decision to bring six government ministers and a large business delegation with him was "a signal of the importance I attach to the success of China and, I hope, the success of the China-British relationship." Here is is some more reaction to the announcement. From Paul King, chief executive of the UK Green Building Council Make no mistake, this is bad news for people who cannot afford to heat their homes, especially if they live in solid walled properties, and bad news for thousands of construction industry workers who may well be joining the dole queue this Christmas. It remains perverse that the prime minister is attempting to reduce energy bills by slashing the very scheme that is designed to bring them down for good. We have to recognise that some in the coalition have fought hard for a package of incentives to sweeten the pill. Encouraging households to take up energy efficiency measures when moving into a property through a ‘Stamp Duty rebate’ is something we have repeatedly called for. From Richard Lloyd, executive director at Which?, the consumer organisation It's about time the government started getting the cost of energy under control and this will be a welcome step in the right direction for consumers who are struggling with the increased cost of living. It is right to refocus the Energy Company Obligation so that it gives greater priority to those most in need of help with lower cost measures such as loft and cavity wall insulation. But in return for more time to get this right, the suppliers must now commit to greater transparency and to getting their costs down, fast. From Cathy Debenham at YouGen, a renewable energy advice website While we are pleased to see that the elements of the Energy Companies Obligation aimed at low income and vulnerable households have been maintained, it is disappointing that the Carbon Emissions Reduction Obligation is being reduced. This offers help for people living in homes with solid walls or hard-to-treat cavities. They often have the coldest and most expensive to heat homes, and very few people will be able to insulate without financial help. People are already finding it very difficult to get ECO for solid wall insulation, and we believe that help needs to be strengthened, rather than reduced. Just as the feed-in tariff has helped the solar industry to develop and grow - with more people installing solar panels and massive price reductions as a result - we believe that the solid wall industry needs similar support to grow. This should encourage innovation and economies of scale so that it eventually becomes affordable without subsidy. Here's Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, on the government's energy bills announcement. Even after these changes to levies, energy bills are still rising and the average household will still be paying £70 more for their energy than last winter. Any help is better than none, but you can judge this government by who they’re asking to pick up the tab – the taxpayer. The energy companies have got off scot-free. This shows why nothing less than a price freeze and action to reset the market to stop the energy companies overcharging again in the future will do. Energy UK, the trade body representing energy firms, has welcomed the government's energy bills announcement. Here's its statement. This is a good outcome for consumers. The changes will bring a welcome set of reductions to the policy costs which are a significant part of the household bill, have been rising and were set to rise further. Costs are spread in a fairer manner and household bills are reduced. The industry will continue to insulate homes. The parts of ECO that target fuel poverty have been increased and the complicated solid wall installation has a more practical timescale and is made less complex. Companies will be making their own announcements individually about what it means for their customers. Labour is unimpressed by today's energy bills announcement. Here's Alex Belardinelli, Ed Balls's press spokesman. The Tory Treasury twitter feed (an official account) has hit back. Robert Peston's BBC blog on the energy bills announcement explains the changes very clearly. EDF has now responded to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says the move vindicates its decision to announce a 3.9% price increase (which was based on the assumption that the government was going to cut the cost of green levies). The Carbon Brief blog has posted a graph on Twitter putting the cuts to fuel bills generated by today's announcement in perspective. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews this morning about today's announcement. Here are the main points. • Davey said that the government was saving households £50 a year on average while at the same time "protecting the fuel poor". • He said that energy bills would have been higher if Labour had been in office. For example, under Ed Miliband, they were going to put something called the renewable heat incentive onto energy bills – that would have cost an extra £179. From day one, we took off energy charges from the bill that Labour had wanted, so under Labour energy bills would be much higher than they are now. • He said Labour's plan for a price freeze would hurt the fuel poor because it would lead to a cut in investment. It could also lead to small suppliers going out of business, he said. One of the reasons why Labour’s proposal is economic illiteracy is that if wholesale prices went up during their price freeze, what would happen is the smaller companies - who wouldn’t be able to trade for a loss unlike the bigger companies - would go out of business, and that would reduce competition. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Four of the big six energy companies have now responded to the government's energy bills announcement. Here is what they are saying. Statement from British Gas British Gas is reducing household gas and electricity prices by an average of 3.2%, which is equivalent to £41 on average off the annual dual fuel bill1. An additional £12 customer rebate for the Government’s Warm Home Discount (WHD) scheme brings a total annual saving of £53 to the average dual fuel customer. Today’s announcement follows the Government’s proposed decision to make changes to the Energy Company Obligation and to introduce a rebate to customers to cover the cost of the WHD scheme. It also takes into account the agreement between the Government and electricity network companies to defer an element of electricity distribution costs in the period from April 2014 to March 2015. British Gas is passing on the benefit of all these changes to its customers in full. The price reduction will take effect from 1 January 2014, and will apply to all customers, whether on variable or fixed tariffs. Chris Weston, the Managing Director of British Gas, said: “British Gas is pleased to be cutting energy bills by an average of £53 from 1 January. We’ve been able to do this because the Government has committed to making changes to the environmental and social obligations that are paid for through energy bills. These changes will now allow us to help more people at a lower cost. Two weeks ago British Gas announced it was increasing electricity bills by 10.4%, and gas bills by 8.4%. Statement from SSE Following the announcement by the UK government that it is to consult on proposals to reduce the future costs associated with the Energy Company Obligation and to facilitate other measures to reduce the cost of electricity and gas for customers, SSE can confirm that it will pass any resulting savings on to customers before the end of the current financial year. On the basis of today's announcement and the planned consultation, SSE plans to reduce its household energy prices before the end of its financial year. The overall impact of the proposed changes, including the rebate which is expected to apply in Autumn 2014, should lead to a typical dual fuel customer benefiting by around 4%. This should equate to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. These changes should remove a significant amount of upward pressures from energy bills. SSE plans to extend its current Autumn 2014 price cap at the new lower levels to Spring 2015, subject to there being no marked and sustained increase in wholesale energy costs, network costs or new policy-related costs. William Morris, Managing Director, Retail, said: "We promised our customers that they would benefit from any cost savings and although there is some detail still to be worked out, we are able to commit to lowering our prices. If the proposals set out today are implemented, it will mean energy costs for a typical dual fuel customer can be cut by around 4%. "We continue to believe that policy costs should be paid through general taxation, not energy bills. The Warm Homes Discount changes are therefore a step in the right direction" Statement from Paul Massara, CEO at RWE npower We welcome today's announcement as an important step in cutting energy costs for our domestic customers. As a result of this announcement we will reduce our prices. We are currently calculating how large this reduction will be, and can assure our customers that it will fully reflect the reduction in the costs to our business. "In addition, in order to give our customers greater certainty with their household budgets, we don’t plan to increase energy prices before Spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges. Statement from E.ON. Here's an extract. E.ON has not announced a price rise since December 2012 and once again is the only major company to hold off for so long into the winter. The result of these changes [announced by the government today] should mean our customers will pay less next year than otherwise would have been the case. We are working through the details and will provide an update in due course. E.ON can also provide customers with the assurance that it doesn't expect to have to raise prices as a result of social or environmental obligations in the next 18 months. There remains a risk, however, that increases in network charges or wholesale energy costs for example could force a price increase but the company (E.ON) very much hopes that situation does not occur. The news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change (DECC) about energy bills is unusually detailed. I have mentioned the key points already. (See 8.53am.) Here are nine points from the small print worth flagging up. 1. The £50 a year per household saving is an average. "The value of the benefit will vary between companies," DECC says. 2. The £50 figure applies to England, Scotland and Wales. Separate arrangements apply to Northern Ireland. 3. Mostly the cut is being funded by regulatory changes announced by the government. But firms are also taking "voluntary action" to cut distribution costs, contributing around £5 on average to the value of the cuts. 4. The overall package is carbon neutral. Watering down the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), by giving companies an extra two years to meet its targets (the deadline has been extended from March 2015 to March 2017), actually increases emissions. By the government has announced various energy saving measures, for home buyers, landlords and public sector buildings, to offset this impact. These measures will cost £540m over three years. 5. Around 60,000 homes will benefit from the scheme allowing people to get £1,000 to spend on energy saving measures when they buy a new home. 6. Funding for Green Deal Communities is being extended from £20m to £80m. 7. The Green Deal is being "strengthened, streamlined and reformed". There are more details here, in a separate news release. You could read this as an admission that so far it has not been a glittering success. 8. Energy firms will have become "more transparent" about how much they spend on social and environmental measures. This may require changes to the law. 9. Measures to prevent fraud, particularly relating to loft and cavity wall insulation, are being introduced. Is this an admission that there is a potential problem? This morning the government has announced the full details of its plan to curb energy bill increases. The broad outline of the plans, which will cut bills from what they would otherwise have been by £50 on average, have been known for some days, but this morning all the components of the plan have been set out in a news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change. Here's an extract: Today the Government confirms it will reduce the current cost of levies by: Establishing a rebate saving the average customer £12 on their bill, for the next two years, worth a total of £600 million. The Warm Homes Discount will continue to help millions of vulnerable households receive a £135 rebate off their energy bill. Reducing the cost of the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), an insulation scheme delivered by major energy suppliers. This will result in £30-£35 off bills, on average, next year. The existing dedicated support in ECO for low income and vulnerable households will be maintained and extended from March 2015 until March 2017. In addition, electricity distribution network companies are willing to take voluntary action to reduce network costs in 2014/15. This would allow a further one-off reduction of an average of around £5 on electricity bills, which energy suppliers will be able to pass on to their customers as well. And the energy firms have been reacting. • British Gas is to cut dual fuel energy bills by an average of £53 from January 1. • Npower says it will reduce bills and that "we don't plan to increase energy prices before spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges." • Scottish & Southern Energy said it expects a saving for the typical dual-fuel customer of around 4% before the end of March, equivalent to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual-fuel customer. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews about his announcement this morning. I will post the highlights soon, as well as more detail about the announcement, and more reaction to it, from the energy companies and others. Otherwise, the main event today is David Cameron's "Don't mention the Dalai Lama" tour of China. My colleague Nicholas Watt is travelling with the prime minister. Here's his latest story. The political news diary at home is threadbare. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Crisis talks after reports of exposure to asbestos in the NBN rollout
fields.bodyText: That was quite a day. Let's take stock with the evening summary. The day dawned with asbestos and the NBN roll out dominating the political news cycle. There was a brief diversion into a bipartisan interlude on the republic between Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull; and on a new administrative headquarters for DisabilityCare to be located in Geelong - but within moments, it was back to a brutal day in national affairs. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy gathered a group of stakeholders for a meeting on the asbestos problems in parliament at lunchtime in order to get to the bottom of which contractors or residents could have been exposed to asbestos during the NBN rollout. Telstra chief David Thodey took responsibility, and the group resolved to set up an independent taskforce to examine the issues. The day rolled forward to Question Time. There were short sharp blows on asbestos. The Coalition sought to pressure the government over the management of the whole mess by asking whether it had pursued this issue before media reports surfaced last week of asbestos-related incidents. Shorten said he had, and produced some letters to back the claim up.
