The government then returned fire by raising some unfortunate comments Tony Abbott made about asbestos campaigner Bernie Banton - and also highlighting that deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop had once worked as a lawyer for James Hardie. The Coalition changed course and then went on boat arrivals, and national security, raising the case of an Egyptian jihadist held in a low security detention facility in South Australia. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison used the case to suspend the standing orders - he was seeking an independent inquiry into the case. Morrison said allowing a known jihadist into the country, and allowing him to be held in a low security immigration processing facility, raised profound questions about whether Australia's current border measures and security assessments were adequate, given the strain the whole detention system is under with an upswing in irregular boat arrivals. Morrison only just lost this vote, which was resolved 68-69. Such motions require an absolute majority to proceed. The government ran interference in this attack by highlighting comments from Julie Bishop suggesting the Indonesians would in fact turn people smugglers' boats around, even though they are saying publicly they have no intention of doing so. Here, incidentally, is a tear sheet of one of Shorten's letters to Telstra which we chased up after Question Time. Anyway, that was about the sum of the parts. Thanks very much indeed to Mike Bowers for his fine work today, and to readers of #politicslive Let's do it all again tomorrow. If you've been following this issue and our coverage today, and are interested, I'd encourage you to read Kath Viner's blog post in full. You can do so here. Here's the bottom line: It was a long interview. We journalists and editors need to be careful in making sure selections don't distort the overall intent. But we believe we have represented Bishop's comments accurately. Kath Viner: Since all reports of long interviews necessarily select the parts regarded as newsworthy, we think the key issue was not that a selection had been made but rather whether the selected parts distorted the interview on the point selected, or were otherwise presented in a misleading way. Having double-checked with Bishop on Sunday in light of the ambassador's remarks, and having changed the headline in recognition of the subtle but diplomatically significant distinction between an agreement and an expectation of co-operation, the Guardian believes that it has accurately conveyed the substance of Bishop's comments on this issue in the interview. Our editor-in-chief, Kath Viner, has just posted a blog about Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop today, which has been the subject of controversy around the place today. Kath's blog contains the relevant extract from the transcript of Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop. I'll post that first, then give you another excerpt from Kath's piece. Ms Bishop tells Lenore that based on her conversations with senior officials, she's convinced the Coalition can work with Indonesia to achieve the policy of turning back the boats. The transcript: Lenore Taylor: “On Indonesia, you always seem to say that you have had lots of discussions with Jakarta and you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats. Why do you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats given what Indonesian officials always say publicly? What do you know that we don't know about how about that could be managed?” Julie Bishop: “Well I am not at liberty to tell you. I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim.” Lenore Taylor: “So they would take boats back?” Julie Bishop: “I am confident we would be able to achieve what we did in the past. The fact is they are Indonesian boats with Indonesian crew and I am sure we can work cooperatively with them and Lenore, one thing you understand about diplomacy and others do as well is the professional diplomats are paid to present, aah, particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly, that’s why I am devoting my time to quiet consistent diplomatic messaging and relationships.” Sky TV is just broadcasting some vision from the press conference earlier today from the Telstra CEO David Thodey. I wasn't able to get down there. It is clear that Mr Thodey is taking responsibility for the asbestos issues. Thodey: We take ownership and responsibility. First chance I've had to share Mike Bowers wonderful frame of the chamber today. Your Bear Pit at work. Turnbull continues: They can't just say this is all Telstra's problem. Telstra is doing this work for, and on behalf of the NBN Co. The attempt to push this all off to Telstra is, frankly, a lawyer's argument. If you are the government, Turnbull says, responsible for occupational health and safety, then you have to be all over it. Turnbull says the correspondence Shorten has released raises more questions than it answers. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is out now with the media on asbestos. Turnbull: We welcome the establishment of a register, and we welcome the announcement by Telstra that they will undertake an audit. He says there remains a number of questions about the government's knowledge of this matter. We know the commercial deal was done with NBN Co in 2011. The issue of disturbance of Telstra's infrastructure was therefore going to happen on a grand scale. Shorten's concern demonstrates that he knew asbestos was going to be a big issue. He raised concerns - but why didn't he raise his concerns with the Communications Minister? That's a bit odd isn't it? Want the definition of tough day at the office? Mike Bowers captures it. Is there a rough number of incidents, Shorten is asked? He says there have been thirty reported incidents of asbestos breaches since 1996, the vast majority in the past few weeks. Will the NBN roll out slow down, Conroy is asked? June targets? No. September targets? No. The work is ongoing, he says. If there is a material change, NBN Co will advise of that. Did Telstra just fob you off, The Conversation's Michelle Grattan asks Shorten? They wrote back to me, and said they were on top of it. Minister Conroy is insisting that NBN Co are not responsible for any of this during a mildly combative exchange with David Crowe, journalist from The Australian. In response to a question seeking metrics - how many pits, where are these problems precisely - Shorten suggests there is some information about where the problems currently lie, but the government is still seeking further particulars. We need the best possible information Shorten says. For me, he says, it's an unsatisfactory state of affairs that we can't tell residents the full story. Shorten's questioned about a fund. It was discussed but not resolved definitively. He points to a new register tracking exposure to asbestos. This is a very important advance when it comes to future claims. Shorten is defending his actions based on his contact with Telstra in 2009. He says he was reassured by Telstra that all was safe. Should you have pressed harder, Shorten is asked? When Telstra write back to you not once, not twice, but three times .. Does anyone think the Opposition would be tackling asbestos if it wasn't for the politics of the NBN Co? We contacted Telstra. They gave promises. We are the ones driving this process. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are now reporting back after the talks with Telstra and others earlier today. Shorten says: The group has agreed to support an independent taskforce to examine these issues. Telstra has committed to provide transparent information to people who feel they might have been exposed to asbestos. It will report back to the Commonwealth in a speedy fashion about how it intends to remove asbestos from their pits. Shorten: Everyone has a responsibility in our community to make sure people are safe from the scourge of asbestos. Minister Conroy says NBN Co fully supports the outcomes of the morning. He says there have been a couple of instances where sub contractors to NBN Co have not followed proper procedures. He says there should be no shortcuts when it comes to the handling of asbestos. Conroy is asked whether NBN Co has done enough and whether he should take responsibility. Conroy: I am satisfied that Telstra has put up their hand and taken full responsibility for their work. Should I take responsibility? Well, Telstra has taken full responsibility. Julia Gillard and Craig Emerson, catching up during that division. That's Question Time, over and out. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop seeks leave to make a personal explanation. This concerns the interview with Lenore Taylor that we've referred to throughout today. Bishop: At no stage did I say there was an agreement with the (Indonesian government) Bishop is objecting to a headline on the story, and notes that has been amended. Bishop has been under attack from Labor on this story for most of today - and the static has cut across the Coalition's political attack on the border protection theme. And then it was done. The Morrison motion was not carried by the required absolute majority required in such cases. The House is dividing on the motion now. While we wait, here's Morrison, launching the suspension of the standing orders. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor is on his feet to respond. The Prime Minister has remained in the House. Normally she would leave during such procedural skirmishes. Not today. O'Connor says this person has committed no offences while in Australia. He says this man has never left detention. O'Connor says he is happy to provide a security briefing to the Opposition - but when they did that last week, the details were released publicly. This is not on, O'Connor says. Those briefings have to remain in-camera. The person is now in high security detention. The reason why this motion is being brought on today is (they) are trying to scare the Australian people in a way that is really outrageous. O'Connor says the Coalition is long on rhetoric but short on substance. The Opposition has not cooperated with efforts in parliament to slow down the flow of irregular arrivals. He says Abbott pretends he has an agreement with Indonesia to turn back boats when that clearly isn't the case, but he refuses to deal with the Malaysia Solution. It doesn't make sense. Malaysia wants to assist and Abbott won't allow it. I oppose the motion because the Opposition are disingenuous on this issue. They don't want a compact. They want to get in the way of policies recommended by experts. They want to create fear within our community. Morrison telegraphed his intention late last week. In a statement released on Friday, he demanded that the Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor immediately establish an independent inquiry into the mishandling of the detention of a convicted jihadist terrorist by his department, placed in low security family accommodation for asylum seekers at Inverbrackie in South Australia. The Morrison statement: The Government was grossly negligent in keeping a known and convicted jihadist terrorist in low security asylum seeker family accommodation at Inverbrackie for five months, a facility secured by a perimeter pool fence and located adjacent to a defence force facility. The inquiry must receive full cooperation and access and take evidence from all relevant agencies, ministers and their offices, with usual protocols to apply to sensitive information. The subject of this case is a convicted terrorist whose crimes include premeditated murder, firearm and explosives possession and destruction of property. He was a member of the terrorist organisation Egyptian Islamic Jihad, which merged with Al Qaeda. Morrison is using parliamentary procedure to push for an independent inquiry by a select committee of the parliament into these events. The government, Morrison says, has sat on its hands while a convicted terrorist came into this country. Morrison: That is where the bar has been set. We need an inquiry because this government does not learn from its mistakes. They are light touch on our borders, they are a light touch on national security, and this minister is a light touch. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is pursuing the case of the suspected Egyptian jihadist detained in a low security immigration detention facility in South Australia despite being the subject of an Interpol alert. We covered this case on the live blog last week. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor says the person concerned: has not been out of detention. O'Connor suggests this person has also been monitored by security agencies. He says his department is working with intelligence agencies to ensure the security assessment system works. The agencies did not fail us. Mr Morrison is pressing for more particulars. Mr O'Connor says he has no advice that this person has been through any process leading towards him being granted a protection visa. There are situations where a person could be determined a refugee but not released into the community due to offences that have been previously committed. Mr Morrison is now attempting to suspend the standing orders to discuss the Gillard Government's failings on border protection and national security. Tony Abbott switches the attack to boat arrivals, and the government losing control of the borders. The Prime Minister swivels the attack around using today's Guardian Australia's interview with Julie Bishop. Gillard declares Bishop has embarrassed Indonesia by suggesting there's a deal on turning boats back, when there is no such deal. Trade Minister Craig Emerson is now backing that point in with a Dorothy Dixer. (Much hollering from the Opposition bench.) Emerson says the Indonesians have made it clear they will not tow back boats. They will not. Climate Minister Greg Combet has been wheeled in to support Shorten on asbestos. He's now shouting about Julie Bishop representing James Hardie before she entered politics. National Luke Hartsuyker asks Shorten how many NBN contractors have so far undertaken asbestos removal? What training have they undertaken? Shorten isn't engaging with specifics. He takes the political riposte. Shorten: I've never represented the companies in court who made the absestos; and I've never insulted the victims of asbestos and campaigners. (By this he means Abbott and his deputy, Julie Bishop.) Willing down there today. Manager of Opposition business, Christopher Pyne, directing tactical traffic. Turnbull is back with the contents of that 2009 letter tabled by Shorten. GIven you clearly knew there were risks associated with asbestos, why didn't you raise it with Telstra between March 2009 and this week? Shorten doesn't engage with that allegation directly. A supplementary now from Turnbull to Shorten. Will you confirm you made no contact with Telstra or NBN Co since 2009 despite being aware of the risks? Shorten responds that he wrote to Telstra on August 4, then November 2 - Telstra wrote back with a detailed response. Of Turnbull's pursuit? Shorten: Tawdry. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten. An apt summary by Crikey correspondent Bernard Keane of a Dorothy Dixer Shorten just asked himself to update the House about today's asbestos talks. Independent Rob Oakeshott wants particulars of the Debates Commission - a new body to conduct election debates for September. Does the Prime Minister support this concept? Julia Gillard: We certainly are committed to a leader's debate commission. Turnbull is back at the dispatch box. He'd like the 2009 letter. Given this letter was written well before Telstra's agreement with NBN Co, what was this letter about? Was it about pits and ducts and asbestos? In what capacity was it written? Shorten ignores the specifics of the question. Shorten: Asbestos should not be a political football. The Workplace Minister has now tabled the letter, written in March 2009. Here's Garrett a few moments ago on indulgence. A Dorothy Dixer now allowing Treasurer Wayne Swan to have a shout about Queensland Premier Campbell Newman not signing on to Gonski. Shouting is ensuing. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is asking a question of Bill Shorten. Can he inform the House how often he has met with Telstra in relation to asbestos risks and the NBN before the news reports last week? After all, everyone knew there were risks. Mr Shorten says he wrote first to Telstra in 2009 and Telstra indicated it had the issue under control. Shorten says he's met with a group of stakeholders today. Telstra made it very clear at this meeting that they accept they have to do more. I'm pleased to inform the House ... that they all agreed about the outcomes of the meeting we have just had. We saw Telstra accepted responsibility for end-to-end training throughout the system. Telstra also accepted the need to do much better in accepting the anxieties of the community. Turnbull would like Shorten to table the 2009 letter. House of Representatives Speaker Anna Burke says she can't do that. She can only request that of papers the minister is reading from. Opposition leader Tony Abbott opens on the NBN. Does the Prime Minister agree that millions spent on advertising for the NBN would have been better spent on keeping people safe from asbestos? The Prime Minister returns in kind. I suppose we shouldn't be surprised given this is the same man who insulted Bernie Banton on his death bed. (Mr Banton, as asbestos campaigner, died of an asbestos related illness. Mr Abbott once said of Banton, who was pressuring the Howard Government to take action on asbestos-related diseases: "Just because a person is sick doesn't necessarily mean that he is pure of heart in all things." Abbott later apologised for the remark.) The Prime MInister says Telstra, not the government, is responsible for pits and ducts. It being the scheduled hour of Question Time, it is Question Time. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is on his feet on a matter of indulgence. Garrett is clearly emotional. He speaks of the death of Mr Yunupingu. The music of Yothu Yindi spoke to Australians, Garrett says, with urgent political intent. He dreamed that Australians could be better, and he demanded that Australian politics be better, and so it became. This is a day of sorrow and also a day of pride. For now, he's gone, and like so many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, gone too young. Liberal Ken Wyatt echoes the words of Garrett. Wyatt says Mr Yunupingu was not only a musician but an educator. He brought Australians together, he participated in public debates about education, he had insight into learning which contributed to innovative approaches to education. To me, and so many others who knew him personally, he was an inspiration. Mr Yunupingu, thank you for making our lives so much richer. Here are the two ministers - Bill Shorten and Stephen Conroy - on the way in to the asbestos talks. Through the crack in the doorway. The asbestos talks. Telstra is believed to be outlining the steps it has taken to contain the situation regarding asbestos at the NBN sites. Man in fedora. As discussed. Oh dear. Now the monarchists are cranky. The Australian Monarchist League has fired off this missive to Malcolm Turnbull, (who, as we've noted, was out this morning, fanning those flames of republicanism) - a letter copied to hundreds of their closest friends in the media. 3 June 2013 Hon. Malcolm Turnbull MP PO Box 545 EDGECLIFF NSW 2027 Dear Mr Turnbull, On this morning's ABC News Breakfast show, you said, in reference to comments made in 1999, that the 'monarchists lied'. The Australian Monarchist League is Australia's largest member-based monarchist organisation and both media and the general public refer to us as 'monarchists'. I would point out that the Australian Monarchist League has never intentionally lied to anyone at any time and has always acted in a truthful and open manner. We would therefore appreciate you issuing a public notice that your comments did not refer to the Australian Monarchist League. Thanking you Yours sincerely, Philip Benwell National Chair Australian Monarchist League Crisis talks on asbestos. Mike Bowers records the opening. Perhaps it's Ms Hanson's tendency to refer to herself in the first person? In the ABC footage of Hanson, a chap in a check fedora and sunglasses is watching on with intense interest. And over in Sydney, another lady with red hair, Pauline Hanson, is maintaining her rage: the voice is characteristically querulous, the vibrato outrage undimmed. (And she's announcing she'll have yet another go at trying to get a spot in parliament this September.) Hanson: The cost of living has become unaffordable. Over at The Conversation, political editor Michelle Grattan has been speaking to Brian Howe, the former member for Batman and a former Deputy Prime Minister. The battle for Batman (the Victorian seat vacated by the retiring Labor man Martin Ferguson last week) is the contest everybody is talking about. And I mean everybody. Cats have views on this pre-selection, and they don't mind sharing them. But I digress. Mr Howe has told Michelle Julia Gillard (who is backing a bloke for this seat, factional mover and shaker David Feeney) should just shut up. Here's a bit from The Conversation: Former deputy prime minister Brian Howe, who held Batman from two decades, has strongly backed local woman Mary-Anne Thomas in the bitter preselection battle for the seat. Howe said Labor should keep its promise to deliver women candidates in a fair share of safe seats, but it was important as well to have as candidate a credible and active local resident in an area where the Green vote was increasing. He also said he was “very disappointed to see the Prime Minister involved” in the preselection. “It’s quite counterproductive for her to be involved. This is not about the election – this is about the future.” Let's stand still and work out where politics has touched down this lunchtime. Asbestos and the NBN rollout has been front and centre in the political discussion. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are sitting down as we speak with representatives from Telstra, NBN contractors, unions and asbestos campaigners to work out what to do after recent incidences where workers or residents could have been exposed to asbestos. The two ministers will address the media on the sum of those talks after Question Time today - around 3.30pm. The Prime Minister has confirmed that the national heaquarters of DisabilityCare will be in Geelong - the location of two Labor-held seats in trouble in September. There's been a small outbreak of bipartisanship on the republic - Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull have been out promoting a renewed discussion about constitutional reform, pushing through the defeat of the referendum in 1999. Boats, Indonesia and asylum policy is also kicking around courtesy of the Coalition wanting to keep political focus on this issue; and some comments from Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop which appeared somewhat at odds with what Indonesia is saying publicly about the Coalition's policy of turning back people smuggler's boats. The Fair Work Commission has given the lowest paid a weekly increase of $15. Let's try - all the breaking news notwithstanding - and circle back to turning back people smuggler's boats, and whether the Coalition can achieve an agreement with Indonesia on this front Julie Bishop has put out a statement concerning this morning's Guardian Australia story. There is not an agreement with the Indonesians - that's not possible - Bishop asserts she's simply confident that all will be well. I am confident that, if elected to government, the Coalition will be able to work constructively with the Indonesian government on people smuggling issues. However, it is not possible for the Coalition, from Opposition, to negotiate agreements publicly or privately with foreign governments. This is what Bishop said to Lenore in the interview - she suggested the Indoensians understood the Coalition policy, and had been affirmative about it behind the scenes. Professional diplomats are paid to present particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly. And we have had wide ranging discussions at the highest levels on this issue. The new minimum wage is $622 per week. The weekly increase is just over $15. The president of the FWC Justice Iain Ross notes in this decision that minimum incomes are falling in relative terms in this country. But he argues that increasing the minimum wage is a blunt instrument in addressing the needs of the low paid. He argues he tax-transfer system can apply more targeted assistance to households. Just to add to the briskness of today, the Fair Work Commission is now handing down its minimum wage decision in Melbourne. The minimum wage will be increased by 2.6 per cent. The president of the FWC says inflation is contained, increases in superannuation are on the way, the carbon price impact on households is not as great as first thought, therefore the minimum wage increase is a bit lower than people may have thought. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott was out briefly on the doors of parliament a little while ago. He was asked about how he intended to implement the Coalition's policy of turning back people smugglers' boats when it is safe to do so. (This issue became sticky for the Coalition last Friday when the Indonesian Ambassador to Australia indicated there was no agreement to turn boats back. Defence officials have already queried whether this pledge is workable in practice.) In an interview with Guardian Australia political editor, Lenore Taylor, Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop counsels that all will be well, whatever the Indonesians might be saying to the contrary. 'It's the relationship stupid.' Here's Lenore's piece. This is an excerpt: High-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials have indicated privately that Indonesia would cooperate with a Coalition government to turn back people smuggler boats insists Julie Bishop, the foreign affairs spokeswoman and deputy Liberal leader, despite the country's ambassador claiming publicly that “no such collaboration will happen.” “I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim,” Bishop said in a wide-ranging foreign policy interview with Guardian Australia. Tony Abbott is also on the 'all will be well' theme. The Coalition has a strong and constructive relationship with Indonesia, Abbott says. Here's the relevant sections from his press conference this morning. QUESTION: Mr Abbott are you prepared to defy the Indonesian Government to turn boats back there even though their Ambassador on Friday made it clear that they don’t want to accept them? TONY ABBOTT: Look, we’re very confident that we can have a strong and constructive relationship with the Indonesian Government. I’ve had several meetings with the President. I’m met with Foreign Minister Natalegawa. I have a good and strong relationship with Ambassador Nadjib. Julie Bishop obviously spends an enormous amount of time working with the Indonesians, as does Scott Morrison. So we are very confident that an incoming Coalition government won’t repeat the kind of mistakes that we’ve seen from the current government of which the absolute worst was the suspension of the live cattle trade in a state of panic over a TV programme. QUESTION: You’re talking about the willingness to turn boats around now. Is that different to the reality of turning boats around? Will you actually turn around any boats? TONY ABBOTT: The Howard Government turned boats around. It wasn’t a large number, but it was enough to send an absolutely crystal clear signal to the people smugglers and their clients that the game was up and that the Australian Government and people would no longer be played for mugs. QUESTION: How long do you think it will take to stop the boats? TONY ABBOTT: We will make a difference from day one, from day one we will make a difference and obviously we will be judged on our results – should we win this election – at the subsequent one. Garrett has now switched modes to the Gonski school funding reforms. He (like Swan was earlier) is asked about Premier Campbell Newman, saying no. Or no most likely. Garrett has advised Newman to take a leaf out of New South Wales Premier Barry O'Farrell's book. (NSW has signed on to Gonski). Garrett says he doesn't know whether Newman has been pressured by his Coalition colleagues in Canberra: Tony Abbott and Shadow Schools Minister Christopher Pyne. But he knows he should get on with doing a deal. Garrett: It's time Premier Newman put down the cudgels and picked up the phone to begin negotiations. We are absolutely confident we can reach agreement by the end of the month. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is out now paying tribute to Mr Yunupingu, with his ministerial colleague Warren Snowdon. Garrett: I'm wanting to pay tribute to someone who was a beacon for their people. This is someone we will sorely miss. Mr Garrett (who readers will remember had a rock career with Midnight Oil before entering politics) says he knows he speaks for colleagues in the music industry in this public tribute. He says there are still considerable health challenges facing indigenous communities. Snowdon says Mr Yunupingu taught him to have faith. Not the religious kind of faith, but faith in actions being able to make a difference. Garrett is asked about Treaty. Yothu Yindi sang about it. isn't it time to have one? It was an important song. Garrett treads carefully here. The policy of the Gillard Government is to recognise indigenous people in the constitution - that's one step back from a treaty. Garrett backs government policy and inches forward one jot - we need recognition and any other settlements that might be appropriate. He's asked about his favourite memory of the singer. He nominates sitting backstage sitting with the boys dressed up and painted. Mr Yunupingu spoke about culture and reconciliation. Independent MP Rob Oakeshott is reminding media outlets to show respect in the reporting of Mr Yunupingu's death. He means media outlets should conform with the strictures of traditional culture. (Members of the community are often not named after their death, and images of the deceased person should carry a warning before being broadcast). Asbestos was also on the agenda for the hovering reporters. What will the government do to fix up this terrible mess with the NBN roll-out? Swan: We are the Labor Party. We will do everything we possibly can to make sure this is dealt with correctly. The Treasurer was stopped by reporters on his way out of the republican function. He was asked a range of questions, including about the report in The Australian we pointed you to this morning about the Queensland Premier Campbell Newman signalling he won't sign on to the Gonski package of school reforms before the end of June cut-off. Swan had a go at the Queensland Premier, telling reporters this showed Premier Newman was putting the politics of Tony Abbott and the Coalition ahead of the education of young Queenslanders. Here's a clip of Yothu Yindi singing Treaty at last year's ARIAs. (I think the Schools Minister Peter Garrett might have the microphone at one point.) Tributes are pouring in this morning for Mr Yunupingu, who has died of kidney disease. The Prime Minister: We have lost a uniquely talented musician, a passionate advocate for Aboriginal people and a truly great friend. A former Australian of the Year, his music set out challenges for us all in the road to reconciliation. He will be remembered for his tremendous musical achievements, including being added to the ARIA Hall of Fame last year as the lead singer of Yothu Yindi. He will also be remembered as a great educational leader and a champion for the Yolngu people in East Arnhem Land, in the Northern Territory. He leaves a great body of work to inspire us and we will need all of that inspiration, for so much work remains undone. The Leader of the Opposition: Look, he was a great leader of his people. He was obviously a very significant cultural figure to the wider Australian community as well as amongst Aboriginal people and it’s tragic that he’s gone and I guess it’s very sad that he’s gone at such an early age, but this is one of the real problems in modern Australia – too many Aboriginal people die too young. God save constructive bipartisanship. Lovely frame from Mike Bowers of Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull at the republican do. Malcolm Turnbull is making his contribution now. He's explaining why the Queen - a foreign institution, a much beloved one, but a foreign one - is in the Australian constitution. (To veto local laws Britain didn't aprove of). Turnbull says in some respects modern Australia is more independent than the United Kingdom because Australia is not subject to the strictures of the European Union. It will be extremely hard, he says, to change the status quo. There have been no controversial changes to our constitution since 1946. It is very difficult. You effectively have to have overwhelming support and very little opposition. He rejects arguments that if a model to directly elect the Australian president had been put to Australian voters in 1999 it would have been carried at the referendum. It would have failed, miserably. Direct election would have united the Coalition against the reform proposal and split the ALP, he says. There's no way it would have happened, whatever the direct-elect proponents say. Turnbull: I'm very positive about the republican cause. We are not going to fight the election on this issue .. but we should always be alert to, and interested in, our constitution. Turnbull is of the view a proper, deep, informed discussion won't happen in this country until after Queen Elizabeth departs the throne. He says technology helps this conversation - there are more options to engage the community in a discussion about constitutional change. The pre-requisites (of success) are to prepare the ground very very well. There's got to be the sense that this is the issue of the hour. There's no point in having another referendum that's going to be lost. A little bit of audio for those inclined to listen in. Here's a little bit of audio from Swan's speech. The Treasurer is speaking now at the launch of a new book of republican essays. We pointed you towards this story first thing this morning. Swan: We are all here because we have a deep love of our nation. It's always a time to talk about the merits of having an Australian as our head of state. We do need to put some urgency into a respectful national conversation. How can it be that in a modern democracy that one of our citizens can't aspire to be our head of state? Asbestos and the NBN rollout is a major issue in national politics today. Just a couple of look forward points on that. Legislation will pass the House of Representatives today creating a National Asbestos Eradication Agency. This bill has already been debated, but there will be a vote later. This package does not relate directly to the controversy around the NBN rollout. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten will meet a bunch of stakeholders: Telstra, NBN contractors, unions, asbestos campaigners ay 12.30pm to gather information about incidences of mismanagement, and to work out a way forward. Speaking of Turnbull, we note he's been chatting in the middle of the night with Armidale-based blogger Kate Doak. Kate wrote a blog which examines investments Lucy Turnbull made in companies with associations with the NBN rollout. Turnbull says the interests were disposed off some time back. I gather from his spokesman that they dispute some of the material published in Kate's blog. But it's interesting isn't it - this shift in political communication. Politicians don't only have to grind their teeth over mainstream media coverage, the bloggers are now a significant and growing feature of national affairs coverage - a development I welcome. This morning we pointed you to an opinion piece from Deputy Prime Minister and Treasurer Wayne Swan arguing in favour of a republic. Liberal frontbencher Malcolm Turnbull is out on the subject too, but more cautiously than his Labor counterpart. Swan will speak on this theme in a little while. The Australian Monarchist League has bolted out of the blocks, issuing a statement blasting "the Swan/Turnbull alliance" on the republican issue. Philip Benwell, national chair, Australian Monarchist League: So Wayne Swan is on the republic warpath again. Why does he seem to always get it wrong? The people have no interest whatsoever in constitutional change. They have so many other matters on their mind, which, to them, are far more important. This is particularly so at this election where the fragmenting Labor party is possibly not only fighting for its political survival, but its very existence as a 'party of the people. Nature. We laughed. The pigeons of parliament camp on the right side of the building, but they lean left. That's called having it both ways. They are baaaack! (And luckily for me, so is the lovely Mike Bowers, watching through his lens.) From Gillard's statement about DisabilityCare and Geelong. This will create around 300 new jobs once DisabilityCare is fully rolled out from 2019-20. This is in addition to the 120 jobs already announced for the Agency’s Geelong regional office, which will open in time for the launch of DisabilityCare Australia in the Barwon region next month. The DisabilityCare head office will include staff responsible for managing the scheme’s operation, such as national operations management, the CEO and executive support and corporate services. The Gillard Government has made this decision on the location of the head office on the basis of the Victorian Government’s offer of $25 million toward the costs of relocation and establishment for the head office. Prime Minister Julia Gillard has bobbed up in Geelong to announce that the administrative headquarters of DisabilityCare will be located in the Victorian city. Right here, in Geelong. Thank you to everyone. You deserve it. (DisabilityCare is) starting, it's real, roll on 1st of July. There are a couple of marginal seats in that neck of the woods that Labor will try and defend in September. Ford's decision to pull out of its Australian operations affects workers in Geelong. A jobs announcement, in that context, will likely go down well. Tony Abbott says he supports the HQ being headquartered in Geelong. Obviously I support sensible locating of facilitieas in regional areas. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. It’s a very busy news day already. Parliament resumes today. Readers interested in the legislative program for this week can click on this link and access a very comprehensive summary of all the parliamentary business minus the daily rhetoric. Asbestos dominates the news cycle first up. The Gillard Government will this afternoon meet with representatives from Telstra, trade unions and national broadband network contractors following now widespread reports that asbestos is not being handled properly in the NBN rollout. The Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten has been speaking to affected residents over the weekend - and has hit the airwaves early this morning. We don’t know who has been exposed and who hasn’t been exposed. What this requires is a first class response. Let’s run through the main political stories in the newspapers and digital sites. Asbestos (news.com) The federal government will discuss the management of asbestos sites with Telstra and unions at a crisis meeting. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten, Communications Minister Stephen Conroy and Assistant Treasurer David Bradbury will meet with groups in Canberra on Monday after concerns were raised that Telstra subcontractors weren't properly handling deadly asbestos uncovered during work on NBN sites. On March 20 an open bag marked "asbestos" was found by residents on a footpath near telecommunications works at Bligh Park in Sydney's northwest. Telstra has stopped work preparing pipes and pits to be used for the NBN rollout and has appointed PriceWaterhouseCoopers as independent advisers to help review the process. Disability Care (ABC Local) Less than a fortnight after Ford announced it was closing its Geelong manufacturing plant, the regional Victorian city has been chosen as the headquarters of the new DisabilityCare agency. All states and territories - except Western Australia - have signed up to be part of the scheme, formerly known as the NDIS. Once DisabilityCare is fully rolled out, the national headquarters in Geelong will employ 350 people, in addition to 120 people in the regional office. Turning back people smuggler boats: Indonesia (Guardian Australia) High-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials have indicated privately that Indonesia would cooperate with a Coalition government to turn back people smuggler boats insists Julie Bishop, the foreign affairs spokeswoman and deputy Liberal leader, despite the country's ambassador claiming publicly that “no such collaboration will happen.” “I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim,” Bishop said in a wide-ranging foreign policy interview with Guardian Australia. “Professional diplomats are paid to present particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly," she said. "And we have had wide ranging discussions at the highest levels on this issue.” Cost of asylum seekers (The Daily Telegraph) The cost of supporting more than 40,000 asylum seekers expected to be in Australia next financial year is set to average more than $70,000 each person. And new break-down of costs this year show taxpayers have spent $1.5 billion maintaining the detention network amid record arrivals which are approaching 22,500 this financial year. Supporting almost 13,000 asylum seekers in the community whose asylum claims haven't been processed has cost $265 million for just 10 months. The cost includes $131 million to support asylum seekers on Bridging Visas who are prevented from working and who are paid equivalent welfare payments each fortnight. Renting homes and furnishing them has cost $18 million this financial year. Cost breakdowns given to a parliamentary budget Estimates committee this week also show charities are accepting hundreds of millions of dollars to care for asylum seekers. Campbell Newman and Gonski (The Australian) Campbell Newman has virtually ruled out the Queensland government's agreement to the Gonski school funding reforms by Julia Gillard's deadline of June 30 and warned that agreement at any time is "impossible" without substantial changes. The Queensland Liberal National Party Premier has told the Prime Minister she misunderstands state funding arrangements and is making misleading public statements about the education funding changes. (Paywalled) 457 visas: Simon Crean off script (AFR) Labor elder and former ACTU chief Simon Crean has called on unions to produce evidence of rorts and malpractice in the 457 visa program, as the Gillard government flagged stricter tests for business and more fines. Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor said Labor would impose tougher rules on importing foreign workers, requiring businesses to prove all local labour options had been exhausted. More fines will be imposed for breaches. Mr Crean told The Australian Financial Review he could not understand why employers would not be seeking to use Australian workers, as opposed to bringing them in from overseas, and suggested unions had “gone about it the wrong way” in calling for a crackdown on 457 visa rules. “My point to the unions is give me the evidence to show this is happening. If that is happening, that’s wrong, that’s not the intention of the program,’’ he said. (Paywalled) Batman pre-selection (The Age) Cabinet ministers Jenny Macklin and Penny Wong and minister Catherine King are at odds with Prime Minister Julia Gillard over Martin Ferguson's successor in Batman, with the trio publicly backing the need for Labor to select a woman. Ms Gillard is backing Right powerbroker Senator David Feeney, who is parliamentary secretary for defence. She considers him a strong and loyal ally who has performed well. He helped her rise to the prime ministership and remains favourite for the seat. Republican revival: Wayne Swan opinion piece (National Times) After a recent speech in which I called for a renewed national conversation on an Australian republic, I was met with a curious response: ''Why now? Is this really the right moment?'' The Australian republic, it seems, always raises the crucial question of timing. ''When?'' To monarchists, the answer is always ''never''. To the cautious, the answer is usually ''not now'', or at least ''not until Queen Elizabeth's reign is over''. Others argue that we should raise the issue again only after global recession has been conquered once and for all, and people can concentrate on the future free of practical worries. The time may not indeed be right for an immediate referendum, but it is my belief that it is always the right time to argue for the merits of a republic and prepare the ground for future constitutional change. But before we get carried away, fellow republican Malcolm Turnbull is more cautious (Guardian Australia) Liberal frontbencher Malcolm Turnbull, the former leader of the Australian Republican Movement, who will launch the book alongside Swan in Canberra, urges a more considered and cautious approach to ensure success where the 1999 Republican referendum failed. In his forward to the book of essays Turnbull describes the Republican cause as "simply, purely patriotic." But he says it will be more easily achieved when Queen Elizabeth is no longer on the throne and questions the idea that the "sexy celebrity status" of William and Kate, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, will be enough to maintain the institution. You can follow all the day’s political developments here on the blog, but you can also follow my periodic updates on Twitter @murpharoo If you want to join in the conversation on Twitter use the hashtag politicslive - then I can bring some of that back to the blog.

tags.description: NBN: latest news on the national broadband network from the Guardian

results.webTitle: The archbishop, the duchess and the politics of poverty | Andrew Brown

fields.headline: The archbishop, the duchess and the politics of poverty
fields.trailText: Andrew Brown: By recruiting Camilla to his credit unions cause, Justin Welby is returning to an old idea that the aristocracy should help directly
fields.bodyText: The news that Justin Welby has visited Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall, to discuss credit unions is one of the oddest stories of the week. Are we really back to a society where the archbishop and the future queen have to work on behalf of the poor because no one else is working for charity? I don't think there's any doubt that debt relief of this sort is now seen as the most urgent thing that the church can do to help the poor. It has been one of Welby's personal causes, which he has driven as hard as he could. But it is also an answer to those on the right who argue that there isn't any real poverty in Britain because almost everyone has enough to eat and shelter. By recruiting traditional Anglicans like Camilla, Welby is returning to an older pattern of Christianity, which may never have entirely died out among evangelicals but had certainly receded from the mainstream of British Christianity. The idea that the aristocracy should help the poor directly as part of their Christian duty was completely unfashionable during the heyday of the welfare state. So far as we know, what's going on here is lobbying rather than a simple gift of money. But even that is unusual. In part this reflects Welby's own background and connections. His mother was at the heart of the old establishment and he has just hired as his personal press secretary Ailsa Anderson, who worked for the Queen for 12 years, and had been her personal press secretary since 2010. So it must come naturally to him to suppose these people still have real power, which should be wielded for the common good. It is also consonant with the tradition of Holy Trinity Brompton, the posh evangelical church whose culture shaped him. HTB may not be terribly successful in its prison ministries or in its attempts at church planting in the poorer and more de-Christianised parts of London, but it does take them seriously, and it is about the only part of the Church of England where that tradition is still alive among the congregation. For the other interesting point about this move is that it actually places Welby well to the left of a lot of Anglican sentiment. One of the most fascinating things to emerge from the public opinion surveys conducted for the Westminster Faith Debates earlier this year was the "values gap" which shows that on political and economic questions the ordinary Anglicans, especially irregular attenders, are a long way to the right of the church leadership. This is true both on issues of national policy, like support for asylum-seekers, and on local matters like support for food banks. I don't know a single instance where the initiative for such projects has come from the congregation, rather than the church leaders. It is a very clerical thing to suppose that the church justifies itself by its service to the wider community. So far as the ordinary, intermittent churchgoer is concerned, the church justifies itself by its service to them. Here, as in other areas, there is a huge difficulty in that the policies that attract and energise the core believers are precisely those that put off the peripheral and occasional ones on whom the church relies just as much. But if he can recruit the most notorious adulteress in modern British history to the cause of righteousness he will have done something to move the church's idea of morality from sex to money and power.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Rudd quits as parliament marches on

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Rudd quits as parliament marches on
fields.trailText: Kevin Rudd quits politics drawing warm tributes as parliament carries on as the Treasurer warns of a US-style debt default over Labor's refusal to agree to a $500bn debt limit.
fields.bodyText: So at the end of only the second working day of Parliament, here is where we are up to: Most normal people (as in not we political tragics) woke to the news of Kevin Rudd's resignation. He did one last portrait for photographers in his office. A number of maiden speeches were delivered, the most notable of which were from re-elected Liberal Mal Brough and agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce, who attracted a billionaire mining magnate mate to the public galleries. Veterans affairs minister Michael Ronaldson announced the details of the ballot for the Centenary of Anzac service at Gallipoli on Anzac Day, 2015. The Prime Minister will lead a delegation of only four elected representatives, leaving 8000 tickets for the rest of Australia. The ballot opens on Saturday. Don't wait for tickets on Ebay. The Government's attempt to increase the debt ceiling to half a trillion dollars was stymied in the Senate, with Labor and the Greens amending it to $400bn. Treasurer Joe Hockey refused to accept the amendment and sent it back up to the Senate like a naughty child, leaving a possible double dissolution trigger three months down the track. The Greens senator Sarah Hanson-Young succeeded in passing a motion to force immigration minister Scott Morrison to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. Labor attempted a suspension of standing orders on the basis that the government wasn't what it said it would be. Furthermore, Labor said Scott Morrison could not shed any light on asylum seeker boats for the Parliament or the public. Labor lost. Liberal senator Bill Heffernan, who is implaccably opposed to the sale of GrainCorp to Archer Daniels Midland, managed to kickstart a Senate inquiry into the takeover bid. The lower house will not sit until next next Monday, November 18. Senate estimates start up simultaneously. To all those public servants who will attend, rest up for the weekend. This blog will return on that day. Last but not least, we love your comments but due to my L plates, I failed to turn on the right button. Apologies. Until Monday, adieu. The House has disagreed with Labor's Senate amendment to the debt ceiling on a vote by 82 - 48. So now, the government's original bill which increases the ceiling to $500bn, goes back to the Senate. We are now having a division on the question of Labor's amendment to the debt ceiling. The debate, which has continued over the past 24 hours, has had some pearlers. Joe Hockey on Chris Bowen: Last night I couldn't sleep too well and I wondered who introduced the $75bn debt limit? It was old Bugalugs over here. Labor's Ed Husic on the Coalition: Who are these people who, before the election, were talking about budget emergencies and railing against debt... It is like they have been to one of these RSL hypnosis shows and they have been told, 'when you hear the clap, you will no longer be vandals and you will no longer rail against debt and deficit, and you will say, "we need to increase the debt limit".' This is magic! The Greens Adam Bandt supporting Labor's amendment: The Greens take the approach that most orthodox economists take to debt... Liberals Alex Hawke: If the member for Melbourne represents economic orthodoxy, then I confess to being a radical evangelical—praise God!—because I do not believe the Greens could be associated with economic orthodoxy. After Labor and the Greens amended the the debt ceiling bill in the Senate to $400bn instead of $500bn, it has now come back to the House. Treasurer Hockey is speaking against the amendment. Ipso facto, government is telling Labor and the Greens to shove it. From Mike Bowers on The Global Mail captured fun and games over the table. Katharine Murphy reports: The prime minister has gone in to bat for his friend, the leader of the Catholic church in Australia, George Pell, a man who was “not perfect” but was nonetheless “a fine human being, a great churchman”. Tony Abbott was asked to comment on a report that followed an inquiry in Victoria, which was highly critical of Pell, his attitudes to the problems evidenced in comments before the inquiry and the institutional failures of the Catholic church in stopping child abuse. From his interview with the ABC's Neil Mitchell: Mitchell: As probably Australia’s best known Catholic and a former trainee priest do you accept the church did cover up and move paedophiles around? Abbott: Well, again, I was a seminarian for a couple of years. I wasn’t involved in matters of policy and I wasn’t involved in administrative decisions. So, I just don’t know for a personal fact what was done. I know that it wasn’t handled well. I absolutely know that it wasn’t handled well and I understand that these things probably did happen but I suspect that it wasn’t just the church that didn’t handle these things well. I suspect a generation ago there was this general view in our community that certain things just didn’t happen. We all know now that they did happen. It was hideous, it was gruesome, it cost some people their lives, it cost some people their sanity, it has rightly damaged the reputations of institutions that otherwise deserve our respect and I deeply regret that as I think all decent people do. Labor loses its suspension of standing orders: 53 - 85. House divides for Labor's suspension of standing orders. Morrison accuses Shorten of asking him to undermine the decisions of the joint agency task force, overseeing Operation Sovereign Borders. It is the resolve of this government that puts the steel in the measures that makes them work. Those opposite ... thought the answer was to flip and flop with the wind. They sent a message to people smugglers that their heart wasn't in it ... that they would never have the resolve to carry it off. The policy will always be safer in the hands of the Coalition. Morrison says the mistake the Labor government made in the Malaysian people swap is that they announced it before the deal was done. The Coalition would not have made that mistake and will not telegraph information to people smugglers. My memory was the Coalition thought the Malaysian people swap deal was a mistake because Malaysia was not a signatory to the UN Refugee Convention. Speaking to his suspension of standing orders, Bill Shorten is charging that "this is not the government it promised to be". He is ripping into Morrison about his refusal to answer questions on asylum seekers. A choice selection of Shorten quotes: I love those people opposite...no I don't. The military should not be used to do the job of ministers. It's not satisfactory to say a matter relating to boats is on the water operations ...Thank you Decartes. Philosophy at work in your Parliament. Shorten is moving a suspension of standing orders on the Prime Minister's "failure to lead an open and transparent government" as he promised and the failure of Scott Morrison to answer questions regarding asylum seeker arrivals. This is a government that loves to hide. Scott Morrison is up again on boats, "operational matters on water" etcetera. If the minister doesn't know the answer he should be sat down, says Burke. Speaker Bishop says that is not a point of order, and then comes fairly close to cracking up with laughter. She is clearly enjoying herself. There is so much silliness your correspondent misses the next question but essentially a dixer from Fiona Scott to Malcolm Turnbull on the NBN. He's talking about the former communications minister Stephen Conroy's linguistics - Conrovian, a bizarre Orwellian language. Marles tries a question to Morrison about an "on-land" policy. How many Indonesian boats have you bought? Again it's an operational matter. Trust us, says Morrison essentially. The campaign to spread the word of the evils of people smuggling is progressing well. Pyne - self-described "workers' friend" is continuing on the building and construction industry and its union thuggery. Morrison asked by Shorten whether there is any information he can share with the Parliament. Morrison says no, he will ensure the integrity of the Operation Sovereign Borders. Christopher Pyne is asked about the reinstatement of the Australian Building and Construction Commission. (The bill was before the House this morning.) Pyne says construction sites had become: Intimidation, thuggery, lawlessness and violence. Union leaders support Bill Shorten, says Pyne, so when they tell him to jump, he says how high. He won't support the Abcc. Scott Morrison asked by his own side about the reintroduction of temporary protection visas. Then asked by Labor's Richard Marles about incidents between Australia and Indonesia regarding asylum seeker boats. Andrew Wilkie asks Tony Abbott about live exports and penalties for breaches of Export Supply Chain Assurance Scheme. "This trade is important for Australia and it should be encouraged. The department will always investigate breaches. We are satisfied with the rules as they stand and we don't intend to toughen them further." Treasurer Joe Hockey asked by his own side about Coalition changes to tax policies - read carbon tax and mining tax. Hockey talking about failure of the mining tax. Marles asks how many boats have been turned back successfully since the election. Morrison says Operation Sovereign Borders is a very different way of doing things. "One of those differences is the way we handle information..so as not to use information to assist people smugglers." Dance of the Seven Army Surplus Blankets. Tanya Plibersek makes a point of order. "The minister for asked for a number. If the number is zero, just say it." Speaker asks her to withdraw. If it assists the House, says Plibersek. Kevin Rudd says reports on Jessica's candidature is untrue. Warren Truss is asked about the effect of the carbon tax on Mackay Council. Anthony Albanese then asks Truss about a grant under the regional development fund for $411,484 in community infrastructure to Mackay Council. Truss 910 projects regional development grants were not contracted. We have our own election commitments, says Truss. We don't have to honour commitments made by Labor. Shorten asks about Treasurer Hockey's pre-election statement: "If debt is the problem, more debt is not the answer". Shorten wants to know why not give a full and frank Budget update? Abbott: "Everyone knows which party in this house is the party of debt and deficit." Dixer to Abbott on the impact of carbon tax on the Australian economy. It's just like the old days. Bill Shorten asking about Abbott's misleading statements on the debt cap and the fact Abbott claimed he never opposed Labor's attempts to increase the debt ceiling. Shorten has to change misleading and chooses "imaginative" statements. Speaker Bishop warns Shorten he is sailing close to the wind. I'm not imagining, counters Abbott, the debt disaster is real. Question Time coming up... Labor succeeded in amending the debt ceiling in the Senate from the government's planned $500bn to $400bn in a vote 37 to 32. This and other salient facts about the Senate's business can be gleaned from the delightfully named Dynamic Red. Not Katharine Murphy, but the Senate's version of the Live Minutes. I hope no one got too excited about Jessica Rudd's possible candidature. Lenore Taylor reports that the most likely candidate is Di Farmer, a state MP who lost the seat of Bulimba - which covers some of the same suburbs of Brisbane - at the 2012 Queensland state election. Immigration minister Scott Morrison has been ordered to report on-water operations by midday November 18 - next Tuesday. From Katharine Murphy: The Abbott government has been ordered by the Senate to produce documentary material detailing the “on-water operations” it wants to keep secret, in an escalation of the political effort to force greater transparency into the Operation Sovereign Borders policy. The Senate has passed a Greens motion ordering the government to produce any documentation relating to “on-water operations” occurring between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013 – by 12pm on 18 November. The Australian government will throw in a further $20m and deploy additional Australian Defence Force logistic support to help the Philippines to respond to Typhoon Haiyan. This brings our total contribution to more than $30 million. Just when we thought we had slid into Post-Ruddernism, there is unsourced talk reported by Mark Kenny and Chris Johnson over at Fairfax about Jessica Rudd running for public office. Cue Twilight Zone music. Re those pesky "on-water operations" details, my colleague Lenore Taylor on journalists' responsibilities to keep asking those annoying questions. Tony Abbott is starting to go further than just refusing to answer questions about his government’s asylum policy. He now seems to be suggesting journalists have some kind of moral obligation not to ask them. Liberal Senator Bill Heffernan has garnered support to continue the inquiry into ownership arrangements of grain handling, examining the proposed $3.4bn takeover of GrainCorp by Archer Daniels Midland. Joe Hockey is expected to make a decision on the takeover on December 17. Heffernan has been pursuing ADM like a dog with a bone and in his second interim report, he concluded that more evidence was required in public hearings from Cargill, Glencore, Toepfer International and the relevant financial representatives from ADM. Heffernan also got support from the Greens to continue the inquiry. Senator Hanson-Young to the immigration minister: "What are you hiding?" Proof of Barnaby's fan club. Former Labor senator David Feeney, now in the seat of Batman vacated by Mar'n Ferguson, has just completed his maiden speech in the House. He was preceded by the National member for Hinkler, Keith Pitt and Labor's Pat Conroy, member for Charlton. Former Howard minister, Mal Brough, is now making his maiden speech on his re-entry into the Parliament. Brough replaced his mortal enemy, the former speaker Peter Slipper in the seat of Fisher . Like Sandra Bullock in the movie Gravity, Brough nearly burned out during his re-entry to the Australian political stratosphere. For good background on Brough, check out Nick Bryant's piece in The Monthly. He is talking about how all MPs should be committed to improving behaviour in the House. He thanked the prime minister Tony Abbott and the member for Leichhardt, Warren Entsch, who he said practically "stalked" him to urge him to rejoin the party. Just casting back to this morn, Treasurer Joe Hockey had a D&M with Fran Kelly on his friend, Kevin Rudd. I totally understand why he is going. He has had a tumultuous political career. He has seen the great highs and the great lows of Australian politics. The most brutal words about Kevin Rudd have come from his own side and the most brutal actions against Kevin Rudd have come from within his own party. I fully respect his decision to go. I understand it. I understand it particularly because I know Therese, his wife, he has got three magnificent children, hugely impressive children. He has got a very young grand-daughter, I know he was feeling the pain of being away from his grand-child. Joe says politics has changed, not in the chamber but on the streets. Politics has changed. It is more brutal. I do not think it is more brutal in the chamber. I think it is more brutal on the streets. Social media has changed things. People feel that they can give you a character reference by screaming across a car parking lot in front of your children which I have experienced. I do not think we should just be gentler in the Parliament, I think as a community we must be gentler to each other. Love a friendship across the chamber. Greens leader Christine Milne is accusing the Coalition of undergoing an "extreme makeover" when it comes to debt. She is reading all for quotes from Abbott in opposition when he argued that Labor should justify its attempt to increase the debt limit. Milne channelling Abbott: The government should be forced to specifically justify this...not sweep it under the carpet. The Senate is debating the government's plans to increase the debt ceiling - universally described as the nation's credit card limit - to half a trillion dollars. Labor has agreed to raise it from $300bn to $400bn but doesn't want to go to $500bn. Treasurer Joe Hockey says he wants to jack up the limit so he doesn't have to come back to the House, i.e. at a later date when it is harder to pin it on Labor. Shadow trade minister Penny Wong says in the Budget forecasts, the $370bn debt is not reached until 2016-17. We are saying before we give you more (than $400bn), we think you should tell Australians why." Threatening to shut down the government for political purposes is a lie." When you think of Parliament protecting things, you may be thinking of the Southern Corroboree Frog. But Communications minister Malcolm Turnbull, has just been on his feet speaking to a couple of bills relating to telecommunications. One reduces red tape on industry and enhances consumer safeguards. The other protects submarine cables. Pffft, you may say but submarine cables are those things that connect voice and data traffic with the world. As an island, we would - to use a technical term - be buggered without them. Even though it wasn't immediately clear from the look on his face, Turnbull was cock-a-hoop about protecting submarine cables and: embracing the excitement of global connectedness. The deputy prime minister and infrastructure minister Warren Truss has made a statement in the house, announcing a review of aviation safety regulation, with the bipartisan support of former infrastructure minister Anthony Albanese. Truss "suspended" the standing orders just so Albo could speak. Truss flagged the review after adverse findings on the Civil Aviation Safety Authority and the Australian Transport Safety Bureau by the Senate and Regional Affairs and Transport Committee. That's obviously what they were talking about when photographer Lukas Coch caught them having a quiet chat in the chamber on Wednesday. Immigration minister Scott Morrison and military head of the government's Operation Sovereign Borders, General, Angus Campbell will have to do the dance of the seven army surplus blankets at the next asylum seeker briefing after the Senate passed a motion to provide more information regarding "on-water operations". Labor and the Greens have just passed a motion to force the government to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. It has just passed and means Morrison and Campbell will be defying the Parliament if they don't comply. Watch this space. The man with the best voice in Parliament, industry minister Ian Macfarlane, has just introduced a bill to move the Anti-Dumping Commission from under the umbrella of Customs and Border Protection to the Industry portfolio. Macfarlane says the move will free up customs and border protection to concentrate on "other matters". Notably (though he did not mention it), those infamous "on-water operations". Macfarlane said it was but one move in a suite of measures to strengthen the manufacturing industry. SPC Ardmona will await further advice. Apparently I have started the Ruddy Great Word Game. Julia Gillard tweets Kevin Rudd. The war is over. Good morning everyone - Katharine Murphy breaking in briefly on Gabi's blog.With boats a major issue of this political week, we've been waiting on a couple of items out of the Senate. One of these is a Greens motion to try and force the government to produce documents relating to those "on water" incidents it doesn't like talking about. Labor has been mulling whether or not to support this motion. Labor has confirmed they will support it when it is debated later this morning. The motion seeks to force the government to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. If the intel is correct and Labor supports this motion, it will be interesting to see how the government responds given its intention to produce as little information about these events as possible. It has been very much a case of by all means ask, but we don't intend to tell. Legislation continues apace in Parliament. Ninety minutes into sitting and we have had five bills, three relating to setting up the Australian Grape and Wine Authority, an amendment to the Australian Research Council and a bill to set up the Australian Building and Construction Commission. The Abcc was introduced under the Howard government following the Cole Royal Commission into the building and construction industry. It was abolished under the Gillard government and in the lead up to the September 7 election, Tony Abbott promised to reinstate it. Christopher Pyne, representing employment minister, Eric Abetz, is on his feet saying the Abcc will stop "thuggery" in the building industry and: change the lawless culture in the building and construction industry. Treasurer Joe Hockey has been belting the table this morning on Radio National, making threats about cutting services if Labor and the Greens don't tick the increase in the debt ceiling by $200bn to $500bn. If they want to own the cuts to the budget they will own them. I will not put in place a budget that has a bigger debt than the debt limit and therefore the Greens and Labor will be responsible for the very significant cuts that will need to be made. Veterans Affairs, Michael Ronaldson, is announcing details of the ballot to attend the Gallipoli service for the Anzac Centenary in 2015. Only four members of the Australian Parliament will be attending the service to maximise the number of places for members of the services, direct descendants of veterans and the public. The four members include the Prime Minister Tony Abbott, the Opposition Leader Bill Shorten, Ronaldson and his shadow, who at the moment is Senator Don Farrell, though he loses his Senate seat in July next year. The ballot will open at 12.01am on Saturday morning, November 16, and will close at 12pm on January 31. Early lodgement makes no difference to securing one of the 8000 tickets for Australians. There will be specific places set aside for direct descendants, school children, the defence forces and 160 places widows of veterans. The minister says strict conditions will be placed on the tickets, which are not transferable. These will not be available on Ebay. Sharpen your pencils. Parliament has opened at 9am with the maiden (or is that bachelor) speech agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce. Like Kevin, Barnaby's first name is enough. He replaced independent Tony Windsor in the seat of New England when Windsor resigned after having a pivotal yet controversial role in the 43rd Parliament. Barnaby's speech drew mining magnate Gina Rinehart to join his family and friends in the public gallery. He started his speech with his first memory at the laminex table in his high chair. From there, we ranged through his life, Roman philosopher Cicero and his hopes for rural communities. He grew up in a farming community, comforted by the knowledge that his food and fibre-producing district supported the nation. His country childhood was in the 1970s, though he said it felt like the 1950s. This moulded the man. Like him or loathe him - and he does have a capacity to divide - Barnaby is Barnaby. And his entry into the lower house is sure to change the landscape for the National Party. He represents a more muscular rural political force than the softer versions represented by former leader Tim Fischer and current leader Warren Truss. But his conversion on the issue of foreign investment is the first sign he has softened his old-style National beliefs. His reaction to any decision to sell Australian wheat giant GrainCorp on December 17 will be his first real test. Here we are considering our lives as politics' relentless march continues into Post-Ruddernism. The Grate Man quit and his emotional speech evinced a whole lot of warm tributes, from his own side and from the opposite side. It was hard to listen to the speeches without remembering the comprehensive bollocking they had all bestowed on him during his two periods in the prime minister's office. Pure and simple, Kevin Rudd's speech was heartfelt and emotional. And perhaps it was, as Speaker Bronwyn Bishop said, the fact that he stood there stripped bare of his political armour that everyone else gave him the public acknowledgement that they never delivered in the previous decades. Perhaps in the deepest, darkest corner of the souls, there was a touch of 'there but for the grace of God go I'. However we think of Kevin, everyone agreed that his legacy was topped by the apology to the Stolen Generation. As we listened to Labor senator Nova Peris' moving maiden speech and the Liberal's Fiona Scott, who talked about the importance of Indigenous recognition in the Constitution, it is clear that Rudd's apology took the nation a step forward. But Parliament slows for no Chan, so though there were some notable speeches, this blog will try to highlight them later in the day. In the meantime, here is Katharine Murphy's round up and Lenore Taylor's analysis. You can read his full speech here and it even includes the video.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Toxic opinion polls and continuing fall-out from the NBN
fields.bodyText: Time to wrap the #politicslive conversation for this evening. Thanks to those who made contributions. Let's do the evening summary. Bad opinion polls for Labor, and concerns about potentially dangerous exposures to asbestos associated with the NBN roll-out, dominated the political day. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon ventured out early and cracked jokes on breakfast television about what an MP should say on a day where there was wall-to-wall bad opinion polls - making it clear that there were official talking points, he thought them silly, and he didn't intend to parrot them. Kevin Rudd made a couple of public appearances and remarked how tough things were for the ALP. "Let's just be honest about it, it's very, very tough. I think that's the conversation going on right now with all my parliamentary colleagues who, I've got to say, knowing most of them really well, work their guts out in their communities. So it's really tough going." The jittery Labor caucus expressed its concern to Julia Gillard about her political management of the asylum boats issue, and interogated the government's stance on toughening the temporary skilled migration program - the 457 visa. Backbencher Laurie Ferguson warned Labor would be "dead" in western Sydney if something didn't happen on the boats, preferably yesterday.  But Ferguson is of course correct on the substance. Immigration and boat arrivals is always a red hot political issue in western Sydney. Tony Abbott sailed above the fray and went out to visit a steel works in Queanbeyan. He shrugged off questions from reporters about whether the Coalition could actually implement its stated policy of turning back asylum boats - was there, as his foreign affairs spokeswoman Julie Bishop hinted to Guardian Australia, an understanding with Indonesia? Indonesia knew what the Coalition thought was essentially the response. Abbott also shrugged off questions about his record as Health Minister on issues related to asbestos. He was not getting into the fine-print of all that, but could point to policy advances he'd made. Question Time was economy, boats, security and Gonski. Outside the parliament, the Reserve Bank left the official cash rate on hold. After Question Time, Labor kept pressure on Julie Bishop over her comments on Indonesia, and how she represented them to the House. Bishop has now been referred to the privileges committee of the parliament. There were also scraps on the Gonski school funding deal, and on marine parks. The House is just now considering an attempt by the Coalition to disallow the management plans for the Gillard Government's marine parks.
