I'll bring you up to speed with how that vote panned out when we join each other in the morning. That's about the sum of the parts. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers for his stellar work behind the lens today. See you all on the morrow. The Fitzgibbon performance doesn't get any more comprehensible on reflection. Let's think of that whole sorry leadership episode in March. Caucus is worried by terrible opinion polls. They've been terrible for months, and it doesn't look like changing. Labor is divided about what to do about it. The Rudd camp rises up, but the candidate won't stand. Rudd tells all and sundry that he won't stand. The expectation is he won't stand. So if there's nothing to be done, why keep twisting and turning? How is this rational exactly? After a messy day for Labor, the Finance Minister Penny Wong is clearly not amused by the antics of Joel Fitzgibbon. Sky political editor David Speers updates. Blinded by the light. Laurie Ferguson emerges from the ABC bureau to find fifty of his closest camera wielding mates waiting for him. Labor backbencher Laurie Ferguson has bobbed up on ABC24. Ferguson told the Prime Minister in caucus today that Labor needed to fix the damaging politics around the issue of boat arrivals, or Labor would be "dead" in western Sydney at the September election. Now he's telling ABC24 political editor, Lyndal Curtis, the same thing. Ferguson says decisive action is required, pronto. His argument is Labor is not engaging on this issue and it needs to. He's asked if people are still listening to the Prime Minister. Ferguson says he hopes so. The flow of events was quite quick in Question Time. I want to track backwards for a moment to be clear on the specifics of the contradiction that the Shadow Minister for Border Protection, Michael Keenan, was pursuing during a series of questions to Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor concerning the Egyptian man held in the Inverbrackie detention facility. I've chased a copy of the letter mentioned in dispatches in the chamber. Keenan was told by O'Connor on May 10 the assessment of the man's asylum claim was being progressed by the department. In parliament today, O'Connor suggested the process of assessment hadn't yet commenced. That was the point of all that tooing and fro-ing. (Dotting i's and crossing t's.) Kevin Rudd has been highly visible today by recent standards. As well as backing Queensland in the State of Origin in response to a question about the opinion polls, earlier today Rudd recorded a segment for a TV program with News Limited journalist Joe Hilderbrand down on the forecourt of parliament. TV cameras jogged along side. Much jolly jokes with a bunch of student politicians. No doubt you'll see some of that footage in the TV news packages this evening. Call me Hammo. A transcript of a radio interview between the Opposition leader and Barry Hamilton of Radio 4MK has just hit my inbox. Ham, or Hammo. You be the judge. HAMMO: Well, we’re only 102 days to go until the federal election and the federal Opposition Leader Tony Abbott joins me on the program this morning. Hello Tony, welcome again. TONY ABBOTT: Hammo, it’s lovely to be with you. HAMMO: You must be pretty happy with the latest polls which have come out overnight. It’s not looking good at all for the Federal Labor Government. TONY ABBOTT: Look, Hammo, gee we have an abundance of polls ... Ah look. Just as Albanese predicted, here's Malcolm Turnbull, back in good time for the MPI debate. Another referral to the privileges committee. Manager of government business, Anthony Albanese wants the issue of a no-confidence motion examined by privileges. He's referring to a front page story in The Australian last week that detailed letters to the parliamentary independents testing their support for a no-confidence motion in the government. Albanese says no letters existed at the time of publication. Can this issue be examined please? It's all happening. Peter Slipper, the former speaker, wants the current Speaker to examine whether the parliamentary press gallery is acting as a closed shop - locking out new online entrants, blogs and digital sites, like Independent Australia. Could this be looked into please? Now the Trade Minister is attempting to refer the deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop to parliament's powerful privileges committee. (This follows Ms Bishop's interview yesterday with Guardian Australia's political editor, Lenore Taylor.) Emerson wants an inquiry into whether Bishop misled the House concerning a deal (or otherwise) between the Coalition and the Indonesian Government about turning back people smuggler's boats. Emerson says a transcript of Bishop's comments to Lenore are not consistent with her statements yesterday in the House of Representatives. This matter goes to Speaker Anna Burke. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor wants to make a personal explanation. He says his statements to parliament and to Michael Keenan are entirely consistent on the matter of the Egyptian held in low security detention. Inconsistency? I reject that assertion. With the clock duly run down, the Prime Minister places further questions on the notice paper. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten has further particulars about Mackay and the asbestos case raised in Question Time. He says the site was inspected under state laws and COMCARE was told about it after the fact. Sharp eyes over in the House of Representatives chamber report the Prime Minister has been hitting the Strepsils this Question Time. Winter of discontent. Ruins the throat. All that. Pass the lozenge. It should be noted that suspension and gag - the parliamentary game de jour - helps the government run down the clock during these procedural skirmishes. The question, for the record, was negated. Liberal Michael Keenan, therefore, resumes his attack. A national security debate should not be gagged. Albanese promptly moves another gag motion. He's arguing the Coalition is playing this procedural card because the Shadow Communications Minister, Malcolm Turnbull, was ejected by the Speaker during Question Time. Bit tricky that, because Turnbull is listed to lead the Matter of Public Importance Debate after this session. Albanese counsels Turnbull can resume the chamber in plenty of time. He's due back in at 3.28pm. The division bells ring out once again. Here they are, voting with the Coalition. Thanks Mike Bowers. The ABC's Sabra Lane notes the cross benchers are voting with the Coalition on this motion. (They generally don't support gag motions on principle.) I'm told Opposition whip, Warren Entsch, is sitting out in the advisers box for this vote. (If the former Labor MP Craig Thomson votes with a Coalition, they pull out a parliamentary number to nullify his vote. This goes back to the days when Tony Abbott declared Thomson a tainted vote.) The Immigration Minister gets another question on the Egyptian jihadist. Brendan O'Connor repeats, more or less, the answer he gave Michael Keenan earlier in Question Time. And he has a slap at the Coalition about the Howard Government's detention record - mistakes made in that period, including the detention of an Australian citizen. Manager of opposition business Christopher Pyne then moves a motion attempting to force Brendan O'Connor to explain inconsistencies in his statement to parliament, and a letter he sent Michael Keenan in May. That doesn't work. Pyne attempts to suspend the standing orders. Manager of government business, Anthony Albanese, then attempts to gag Pyne. Albanese argues there's been no lead up to this as a course of action. The division bells are ringing now. Assistant Treasurer David Bradbury takes a Dorothy Dixer allowing him to smack the Shadow Treasurer, Joe Hockey, about Coalition policy costings. Bradbury points to an article we linked to first thing this morning - an edict from Hockey that spending proposals come past him before being announced - an edict that was repeated again in today's Coalition party room. Bradbury chortles about the Opposition attempting to avoid scrutiny. The Prime Minister is sardonic. We can't have that. The Prime Minister is pointed to a statement by the head of COMCARE that the Commonwealth will be liable for NBN-related asbestos cases. The Prime Minister speaks about Telstra accepting responsibility. She's checked by Abbott who takes a point of order. You were pointed to the COMCARE statement, not Telstra, please be relevant. Gillard: I was asked about responsibility. The Prime Minister says she will deal with these issues respectfully, unlike those opposite. Local Government Minister Anthony Albanese (pictured here with Julia Gillard a moment ago) is at the dispatch box attempting to make the case for Constitutional recognition of local government. Good luck with that. Back to asbestos. The Member for Dawson points to a Telstra site in Mackay that lay uncovered in April despite the health and safety risks. Shorten says he will see particulars about the case from COMCARE. It isn't acceptable. Abbott interjects - hang on, this happened in April, weeks ago, why aren't you across this yet? Dorothy Dixer now to Environment Minister Tony Burke on oceans. This concerns the disallowance motion on the marine park management plans that we foreshadowed earlier. There is much chortling about the shadow spokesman Greg Hunt making an announcement about this on land, in a tinny. Much outrage about the lack of bipartisanship on this issue. Burke is asked about the impact of the disallowance. He points out that the Coalition's motion would get rid of the south east marine zone, which was set up by the Howard Government. Burke: Twenty years of science, all the consultation. If it's about protecting the ocean, this Opposition has no interest. The asbestos questions continue. The head of NBN Co is being quoted. Shorten: I do believe asbestos is a big issue in Australia. Treasurer Wayne Swan is telling the House the Reserve Bank has left official interest rates on hold. The Opposition leader has to pick up where Turnbull left off. Another question to Shorten. Given you took action in 2009 with Telstra about your concerns about asbestos, what advice or help did you give the Cabinet on this issue? Shorten shouts about luddites. He is warned by the Speaker, Anna Burke. Shorten points out he wasn't in cabinet in 2009. He was a parliamentary secretary. Now Shorten is shouting about Tony Abbott abusing abestos campaigner Bernie Banton. His time expires. Abbott is back. Can you confirm you took no further action even though you knew the NBN rollout would disturb all these pits and ducts? Shorten: Goodness me I love a lecture from this pack of hypocrites opposite. We are the first government to put in an asbestos agency. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull has a question on asbestos. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten opens with bluster - attack being the best form of defence presumably. Turnbull seeks a point of order. Could the Speaker please remind the minister he is not currently in a Melbourne pie shop? (Shorten had an unfortunate incident in a pie shop last year .. he got a bit testy with the sales assistant.) Turnbull is ejected from the chamber for his trouble. Shorten ploughs on. Asbestos should be a bipartisan issue. Bye bye Malcolm. Keenan has come back on the Egyptian man. Will the Immigration Minister guarantee that this person will be deported once he's had his assessment for a protection visa? Mr O'Connor says the assessment is yet to commence in this case, and this person will be the subject of normal practices, including a character test and a security assessment. Mr O'Connor then backs up with a Dorothy Dixer on changes to 457 visas. The Immigration Minister says these reforms are consistent with Labor values. Presumably, given the ructions in caucus today, as much for the backbench as for the Coalition, (which has also been critical.) That go well? Julie Bishop in Question Time. While the Treasurer Wayne Swan takes a question from Queensland's Bob Katter, and Trade Minister Craig Emerson implores the Coalition to "put the kids before the Libs" - sheesh - I've back tracked slightly. Presumably Julie Bishop - with the question to the Prime Minister earlier about representations to her by the Foreign Minister Bob Carr - refers to a column in today's Australian by Phillip Adams? Adams: Private polling (not that the figures will be revealed by the PM's praetorian guard) shows that Victorian voters have now deserted Julia Gillard and we're looking at swings similar to those that obliterated the party in NSW: more than 20 per cent in safe seats. September won't be so much an election as an exorcism. It wouldn't help if the Prime Minister guaranteed eternal life. No one's listening. Remember the last days of John Howard? Which is why a lengthening number of Labor heavies have told Gillard, publicly or privately, that she has to go. The heaviest won't admit it publicly, but we're talking the aristocracy as well as the apparatchiks. Bob Carr and Simon Crean have not been alone. But Gillard's not for moving. Having led Labor to the edge of the abyss at the previous election, she's now doing a Thelma, with Wayne Swan as Louise, and pressing on the accelerator. Liberal frontbencher Michael Keenan is now turning back to the case of the Egyptian jihadist - the man who was housed in a low security immigration detention facility in South Australia. Keenan asks what is the status of his claim for asylum? Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor responds. There has been no assessment in relation to this matter. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop wants to know the veracity of a report that Foreign Minister Bob Carr has approached the Prime Minister and asked her to stand down for the good of the party. Can she confirm a report in the media today that she's been asked to quit her job for the good of the party? The Prime Minister: That report is entirely untrue. The Prime Minister is thinking of Queensland. She'd like Premier Campbell Newman to give a Gonski. He would, if only Tony Abbott would let him. The leader of the Opposition is prevailing on Premier Newman to put the politics first and the kids last. Labor's Dorothy Dixers open today on education. Bet you didn't see that coming? Mike Bowers captures the Prime Minister entering the House for Question Time. At the conclusion of some elaborate wordsmithing by Liberal backbencher Scott Buchholtz in the workplace debate, here comes the invective hour. Tony Abbott opens today with a question to the Prime Minister. Can she confirm that a recent NATSEM report showed that most people, 87 per cent of households, will be better off under the Coalition? The Prime Minister: Families will be worse off under his plan to take away the school kids bonus. That's real money, worse off, right around the nation. The House of Representatives is debating changes to the Fair Work Act in the downhill run to Question Time. Get your cup of tea. Almost 2pm. I shouldn't be snippy about Clive Palmer, who is, of course, a political journalist's dream. As opinion polls have been a dominant issue in Canberra today, let me share an interesting snippet I heard recently from north of the border. There's a major poll in Queensland tracking voting intentions that has been in the field for some months. A person close to the data told me that survey had Palmer on 6 per cent Queensland-wide for the federal poll; 7.8 per cent on a state-wide metric, and more popular on the Sunshine Coast. The number in Maryborough was more than 13 per cent. Interesting. The case study that is Clive Palmer this election season: can a high wealth individual blast through the lock the major parties have on politics in this country? Independents Tony Windsor and Rob Oakeshott have strolled out to one of the parliamentary courtyards to tell reporters they don't intend to support any further changes to Australia's labour laws unless there is bipartisan agreement between the major parties. The government is attempting to secure amendments to its Fair Work Act. Talk to the hand is the message from the NSW Independents. This will be one in - all in. Tony Windsor: We are not supporting anything that is not supported by the major parties. Rob Oakeshott has a formulation about red lights and green lights. So where is national affairs this lunchtime? Let's pause and take stock. A new batch of toxic opinion polls has triggered another outbreak of Brown's Cows within the ALP. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon went on a high rating breakfast television program to effectively defy the Prime MInister's office by laughing at the talking points. Asked about the polls, Kevin Rudd declared somewhat Delphically that he was backing Queensland in the State of Origin. The Labor caucus roiled on boats and 457 visas. Tony Abbott warned his troops that 102 days to polling day was a lifetime, strewn with mountains, and slippery bits. He then took himself to a steel factory in Queanbeyan. Abbott faced questions about whether it was deal or no deal with Indonesia to turn back people smugglers boats. We all know what we all think was Abbott's effective reply. Mining magnate Clive Palmer meanwhile thieved some oxygen - apologies, outlined more of his platform for government - at the front of parliament. Asbestos and the NBN continued to roll through the news cycle. Now, the Labor caucus. Thanks to Lenore. The Prime Minister opened the meeting with a recap of recent events - she'd welcomed the babies in her electorate, and opened the administrative headquarters of DisabilityCare in Geelong. She said Abbott was trying to undermine economic confidence, and Queensland Premier Campbell Newman was playing politics with schools funding. There were statements from MPs. One caucus member reflected that Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop's comments about understandings with Indonesia on towing back people smugglers boats were "dangerous for an alternative foreign minister." Bishop appeared to be advocating a "new isolationism." There were questions on the government's proposed changes to 457 visas, including a question from Kevin Rudd: what was the basis for the claims that employers weren't testing the local market before resorting to temporary skilled migrants? There were also the negative remarks about boats which we have pointed you to in the update at 12.26pm. My colleague Lenore Taylor is now back from the briefings after today's partyroom meetings. The Coalition first. Abbott told his troops they may have felt cheery after today's news reports about poor polls for the government, but 102 days until the election was an eternity. MPs he said, needed to stick to the key messages of hope, reward and opportunity. He added everyone needed to conduct themeselves in a manner befitting a member of parliament. Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey told colleagues that the Parliamentary Budget Office would take account of all announcements being made between now and the election, so MPs must not make unauthorised announcements. These would be counted in the costings. Announcements must come through him. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull said of asbestos and the NBN, that if Workplace Minister Bill Shorten complained of problems in 2009, why didn't he follow this through in 2011 as the NBN rollout accelerated? Turnbull compared the NBN/asbestos issue with Labor's handling of pink batts - the stimulus scheme that went badly wrong several years ago. While I've been watching Tony Abbott, the first news reports after Labor's caucus meeting this morning are hitting the digital sites. The Australian Financial Review: Phillip Coorey, James Massola and Gemma Daley. A warning to the Prime MInister: stop the boats or we are dead in western Sydney. Labor MP Laurie Ferguson has told caucus that Labor was “dead” in Western Sydney and must do something to address widespread community concerns about boat people. Sources have told The Australian Financial Review that Mr Ferguson, clinging to the Western Sydney seat of Werriwa, told Prime Minister Julia Gillard that silence on the issue was no longer an option and she must go on the front foot and explain to people what was a complex issue. This included exposing the claims by the Opposition that it would stop the boats, claims that Mr Ferguson noted were being increasingly diluted by the Coalition. Several MPs said Mr Ferguson had “belled the cat’’ on the issue which had caused Labor the greatest damage since it relaxed the laws for asylum seekers in 2008 and there was widespread support for his contribution. Mr Ferguson said repeatedly “we are dead” unless Ms Gillard made some attempt to explain to the community what was going on. He said anger was now throughout all elements of the community, including ethnic communities. It is also understood that Mr Ferguson’s brother, Martin, and former leader, Kevin Rudd, were among those who called on Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor for more evidence that the 457 Visa program was being rorted. They wanted to know where they where being used, where they were needed and where they were being rorted. 102 steps to the summit. Abbott invokes Kevin Rudd's Everest analogy again - winning government is as hard as climbing Mt Everest. 102 days until the election, but who is counting? Tony Abbott. Any slip can be fatal. Perhaps that's why he's heading for the First Aid room? Abbott is asked about media reports this morning negative about his record on asbestos while Health Minister in the Howard Government. We pointed to these reports first up today. Abbott: I'm not going to comment on decisions the department made. Abbott says he can defend his record on asbestos. The government needs to get this problem with the NBN under control so it can do the same is his reasoning. This is a massive asbestos problem if its not handled correctly. Abbott is with Peter Hendy, the Liberal candidate for Eden-Monaro (currently a Labor-held marginal seat). The Opposition leader is here this morning with a message of hope and optimism for the familes and businesses of Eden Monaro. While an incoming Coalition government can't solve all the problems overnight .. It's interesting that Abbott has started to insert this qualification more frequently to his statements about the universe post September 14. Like that Pantene commercial - it won't happen overnight but it will happen. Dangerous in politics, over promising, as Abbott well knows. Abbott is pressed in questions over whether the Coalition has an agreement with the Indonesian Government to turn boats around. (This question is in response to Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor's interview with Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop yesterday.) Like Julie Bishop, Abbott is suggesting the Coalition has an understanding with Indonesia. Not an agreement, but an understanding. Our policy is to turn boats around where its safe to do so. We are confident that we can have a strong and constructive relationship with the Indonesian Government. We do have a lots of discussions with senior members of the Inodensian Government. It's impossible for an Opposition to negotiate with a sovereign government but we've had very frank discussions. Here he is. Man of steel. Opposition leader Tony Abbott press conference coming up shortly from Queanbeyan. Speaking of the economy, we'll find out later this afternoon whether the Reserve Bank of Australia will cut the official cash rate. Market expectation is no, most likely. While I'm drawing your attention to good pieces around, I'll point you to the latest blog post from Greg Jericho, AKA Grogs Gamut. Today's meaty Grogonomics outing is on debt: should we be worried about government debt? With charts, it's here. Some wonderful pieces around this morning remembering Yunupingu, singer, educator and Australian of the Year, who died yesterday at only 56. Schools Minister Peter Garrett has written for us at guardian.co.uk/australia Here's an extract: The Yolgnu people of north-east Arnhem Land have lived for millennia on the top end of Australia. It is a vast tropical region of striking landscapes, criss-crossed by streams and wetlands, bordered by numerous white sand beaches and offshore islands. Unlike other parts of our country, this was a place Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people never left. It has always been their home. The Yolgnu are strong defenders of their living culture, with campaigns going back as far as the 1960s for recognition of their rights to land. On Monday we lost, as is tragically so often the case, a 56-year-old Aboriginal leader to kidney disease. Yunupingu, from one of the clans of this region, was both an educator and a musician who proved both eloquent and adept at transmitting the strong cultural messages of his people. He had a long-held view about the need for genuine on-the-ground reconciliation between Indigenous and non-Indigenous Australians – a view from which he never wavered. He saw that healing and then building relations between Yolgnu and Balanda (Europeans) was a task that needed urgent effort and action. The Age's wonderful political editor Michael Gordon has also penned his tribute, a must read in my view. Here's a taste. We met at the funeral of his niece, who died too young from a treatable disease and suffered all the more because of her inadequate command of English, her second language. At an airport in north Arnhem Land, men with ochre-painted faces had danced while the women cried, as the girl's body was taken gently from the back of a station wagon to the small plane that would return her to her homeland. ''When they cry, they heal,'' Dr Yunupingu explained to me later, his soothing voice hushed and heavy with his own grief. The next day, he followed the girl by car to her homeland community, some 300 kilometres to the south, for a ceremony that would extend over several days. ''I'm going back to re-educate myself, to spiritually empower myself,'' he told me. BREAKING: Parliamentary press gallery swim team absolutely, comprehensively trounced by the Coalition. Coalition declares omen. This note from ABC political correspondent and swimming maestro, Greg Jennet, explains. Folks, Unfortunate news: our Gallery Swim Team has suffered a mid-winter meltdown.. After an unbroken run of at least five wins, our gallant swim team was swamped by a powerful Coalition side; (Laming, Simpkins, Jensen, Chester, Frydenberg, Hartsuyker, McKenzie.) Of course, exuberant Coalition team members are speaking of their triumph as “an omen”.. Labor (Livermore, Leigh, Bowen, Macklin, Hayes, Perrett, Dreyfus) ground out a determined third. Thanks to; Tom Iggulden, Joe Kelly, Nick Butterly, Jacqueline Maley, Melissa Clarke and Eliza Borrello. Our female representation was at record highs this year. Cheers, Greg Jennett. Opposition leader Tony Abbott is off to the marginal seat of Eden-Monaro for a visit to a steelworks, TV cameras in hot pursuit. Down in Melbourne meanwhile, influential radio talkback host Neil Mitchell is maintaining his rage. AFL and politics. Mitchell has editorialised thus: Two people today are hanging on when they shouldn’t be hanging on. It is painful. Mark Neeld, coach of Melbourne, is like a carcass swinging in the wind. He is finished, and they are delaying the inevitable. More important, but the same thing, is Julia Gillard and her government. They are finished too. The decent thing to do here would be to call an election. Now. And out the front of parliament, mining magnate Clive Palmer, is insisting he can and will be Prime Minister. Palmer: I could spend my time in Switzerland or somewhere like that. But I care deeply about this country. Just another day in national affairs. Everyone talking. All the time. No State of Origin jokes from the rubgy league-mad Climate Change and Industry Minister Greg Combet. ABC Radio National Breakfast host Fran Kelly asked Combet about the polls on her show this morning. Here's the relevant exchange. FRAN KELLY: And just finally Minister, a number of polls out today all suggest Labor is in an irreversible decline headed for a massive defeat. With 103 days left, what can the Government do, what can Labor do to close the gap? Anything? GREG COMBET: Yes. There are many things that we can do. There’s no doubt that we are under considerable pressure as we head towards the election. There’s no news in that, it’s been difficult for quite some period of time. But I do believe that as the election does get closer many of the issues will come into the frame. We’ve been discussing the automotive manufacturing industry for example. There’s a very stark choice to be made on that issue alone, particularly for people in Victoria and South Australia who depend upon it, the better part of a couple of hundred thousand people and their families. The Liberal Party policy will kill the auto manufacturing industry stone dead in this country. It will. It’s not a piece of political rhetoric or hyperbole. Their policy is to take $500 million out of the support for the industry to period to 2015 and place a further $1.5 billion in support after 2015 at risk. Now, no business can invest in those circumstances in the car industry and the Liberal Party policy will see the car industry end in this country in 2016 or so. So it’s a pretty clear choice there and there’s plenty of other clear choices on education, on superannuation, on the tax system, on many things, the SchoolKids Bonus, things that are really important to people. And we’ll be arguing our case I can assure you. Thanks to my colleagues from Network Ten for this audio. Kevin Rudd was stopped by reporters at the Hyatt Hotel this morning, and asked for his opinion on the ball polls - including the survey predicting he would be the last Labor man left standing in Queensland after September 14. Rudd made a joke about the State of Origin. He was backing Queensland. Have a listen. Make of that intervention what you will. MPs are hunkered down in their party room meetings as they do each Tuesday during a parliamentary sitting. Looking ahead, there will be an interesting parliamentary vote later today. The Coalition will try to overturn the Commonwealth's proclamation of a network of marine reserves. The Coalition wants to disallow the government's marine park management plans. The ABC's Alex Kirk broadcast a report previewing this vote on this on the AM program this morning. The Coalition says the management plans have been imposed on communities with inadequate consultation. Environment Minister Tony Burke begs to differ. Mr Burke told Alex Kirk: Their concerns are entirely political. They're playing a game just like they did with the super trawler. Whenever it's a game about the ocean they're not on the side of the ocean. The Opposition are claiming for example that there's been no consultation. There's been five rounds of consultation, something like three quarters of a million submissions in that process. Observers believe the numbers aren't there for the Coalition, but as they say in the classics, time will tell. Liberal Senator Cory Bernardi - described by host Tony Jones on last night's ABC TV Q&A program as a 'social media enthusiast' - wants folks to know he's overhauled his website. As this audio makes clear, he wants the new site to be a meeting place for conservatives. Those who caught Q&A last night will recall Senator Bernardi described himself not as a social media enthusiast (perhaps that was a given) but a healer in the Liberal Party. This contribution is by far my favourite caption for Mike Bowers' lovely opening still this morning. Thanks for playing, Lee Skelton. If you think you can do better, you can leave a comment here on the blog, or join the Twitter conversation using the hashtag #politicslive @murpharoo Mike is @mpbowers Greens deputy leader. Powered by Ladyhawke. (My Delirium not inappropriate theme music for this sitting period). "Leaked internal polling" generally points to an agenda of some kind. Let's stick with the Newspoll for now. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott has picked up three points in this morning's survey on the preferred prime minister measure - and the Prime Minister has lost four points. The Coalition's primary vote is 49 per cent, and Labor's has slipped to 30 per cent. Newswire AAP is calling that a 35 seat loss for Labor if the election was held today. The Abbott Government would take The Lodge with an 8 per cent swing. The poll confirms a trend evident for many months. Issues in play over the past couple of weeks include the botched public funding agreement which collapsed last week - and of course the reports of asbestos and the NBN rollout were prominent as Newspoll went into the field. Fitzgibbon, in continuation. DAVID KOCH: Is there a push to take the party in a new direction now after figures like these? JOEL FITZGIBBON: No, Kochie, that little battle has been done and dusted and the die has been cast and again, we just need to bind together as a party and get on with it. Bind together as a party? I'll leave that thought with you. You can see that vision of Fitzgibbon giving it to the official line if you click on this link. Or here is an audio clip if you want to listen. Not everyone it seems is as intent as the Prime Minister in changing the conversation this morning. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon (Fitzgibbon went to the backbench after his contribution to Labor's botched leadership coup in March) has appeared on a popular breakfast television program this morning. Helping. Actually, not so much. The host David Koch wants Fitzgibbon's reaction to the bad opinion polls today. Here's the relevant excerpt: DAVID KOCH: Joel, Joel, Joel, are you shattered by this? And can you, are you still going to go to the election with this Prime Minister? JOEL FITZGIBBON: Hang on Kochie, I just brought the manual with me. I’ll see what it says. It says I should say, “polls come and go, but the only poll that matters is on election day”. A bit of translation: the line Fitzgibbon used on TV this morning is the line that is generally issued to MPs by the central communications command in the event of bad polls. It's what they are told to say. Fair to say Fitzgibbon just gave that whole process the bird on national television. Faced with a forest of bad headlines this morning, the Prime Minister is intent early on a change of subject. Let's talk about school improvement. Overnight, Julia GIllard has written to Queensland Premier Campbell Newman in the following terms - c'mon Campbell, what's going on with Gonski? Surely you aren't putting Tony Abbott's political interests ahead of the kiddies of Queensland? There's even a PS. Here's the Prime Minister's letter: Dear Premier Newman, Your state is one of the biggest winners from the National Plan for School Improvement (NPSI). Queensland schools will gain $3.8 billion – around $2.2 million on average per school, over six years. As you know, $2.5 billion of that extra cash would come from the Australian Government and $1.3 billion from your state. A two-for-one deal that will bring huge benefits to Queensland schools. Parents, principals, teachers and the broader community will wonder why you persist in refusing this extra money for their schools. It will be extremely disappointing to Queenslanders if you do not put extra money for schools in tomorrow’s Budget. It cannot be ignorance since you know how much Queensland schools – government and non-government alike – will gain from the NPSI. Your officials have been provided with detailed modelling, and updates, on five separate occasions. Your officials have also had 56 bi-lateral and multi-lateral meetings with my government’s officials. Despite this, you have not responded properly to the offer, and you have not authorised your officials to give accurate information about how you will fund schools next year and beyond. Perhaps it is simply that you do not want to guarantee your schools real funding growth in your Budget. As you know, we’re asking all states to provide indexation of at least 3%. That guarantee is essential. It ensures that schools get the full benefit of new money and prevents your government from using the new federal money to cover up your spending cuts. Perhaps it is because the Leader of the Opposition or members of his team are demanding you put their political interests ahead of the interests of Queensland children. Whatever the reason for your refusal to agree to $3.8 billion extra for Queensland schools, there can be no excuse for your government’s wilful misrepresentation of the scale of investment that is available, and surely there will be no forgiveness for you turning your back on Queensland’s kids. Yours, Julia Gillard Prime Minister PS: Reform is essential. Queensland schools were outperformed by NSW in every NAPLAN measure across every year level in 2012. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from Canberra. A gorgeous frame from Mike Bowers to open on the blog this morning - a golden parliament. Sometimes a little less golden than this image suggests. Let's get cracking. Unsurprisingly, the NBN rollout and asbestos is still a dominant political story this morning, but it’s been joined overnight by a batch of new opinion polls portending a wipeout for the Gillard Government in September. The coverage of the asbestos story cuts both ways in the papers and digital sites today - there are negative stories for the government and negative stories for Tony Abbott harking back to his days as Health Minister in the Howard Government. The opinion polls lean just one way: goodbye Labor. On our selection. Let’s run through the main stories. Asbestos Labor is fighting off new safety fears over its National Broadband Network as more cases of asbestos exposure during its construction fuel a growing political brawl over the management and cost of the $37.4 billion project. The health risks spread to all mainland states yesterday after the Queensland safety regulator revealed three new incidents and federal authority Comcare warned of an "alarming" spate of asbestos safety breaches by contractors. The Australian (paywalled) Telstra has admitted that up to one in five of its suburban communications pits contains asbestos, fuelling fears the National Broadband Network rollout could expose a big number of workers and households to the deadly fibre. The danger led the Federal Government to call an emergency meeting yesterday with Telstra and NBN officials to discuss the threat. It also invited asbestos advocates, union leaders and Australia's chief health officer to help co-ordinate a national response to public anxiety. The West Australian The NBN asbestos affair is, apart from being a major concern in itself, particularly bad for the government because it triggers memories of the mishandled pink batts scheme. That’s why the opposition is going all out to attempt to pin some blame on Workplace Minister Bill Shorten, despite Telstra accepting general responsibility. The Conversation Telstra tried to fast-track compensation arrangements for its employees exposed to asbestos but was rebuffed by Tony Abbott's own department back in 2001. With the opposition on Monday leading a parliamentary attack on the government over its alleged lack of urgency in addressing asbestos discovered in Telstra junction pits being handed over to the NBN, Fairfax Media has learnt the giant telco wanted to create an independent body to accelerate compensation and sought approval from the Department of Workplace Relations. However, the department, then under the ministerial leadership of Mr Abbott, rejected the plan. Fairfax Media Back in October 2007, when Tony Abbott was Health Minister, he denied asbestos disease sufferers access to subsidised medicine for their illness. Naturally, the asbestos disease sufferers were not happy with the decision. And they organised a petition, imploring Abbott to place the costly drug, Alimta, on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme. Some 17,000 people signed it. It was arranged that it would be delivered to Abbott’s Sydney office by Bernie Banton, 61, who was suffering terminal mesothelioma. But when Banton showed up, in his wheelchair, with his oxygen tube, trailed by media, Abbott had done a bunk. Banton was devastated and told the media so. He said Abbott had known he was coming. He called the then-Health Minister, now-Opposition Leader and probable future Prime Minister a “gutless creep” and a “disgraceful human being”. The Global Mail Bad polls Julia Gillard's personal support has dropped to a three-month low and Tony Abbott has extended his lead as the nation's preferred prime minister after public outrage over plans to give the Labor and Liberal parties $10 million each in taxpayer funds for electioneering. Satisfaction with the way the Prime Minister is doing her job has fallen three points to 28 per cent in the past two weeks and dissatisfaction has risen from 59 per cent to 62 per cent, the highest since the end of March. And Mr Abbott has stretched his lead as preferred prime minister to eight points. Ms Gillard faced a revolt from her Labor MPs and a public outcry at attempts to quietly agree to public funding changes for political parties last week. The Australian (paywalled) Julia Gillard's home state has abandoned her with as many as six seats in Victoria now at risk of falling to the Coalition, including that of her Attorney General Mark Dreyfus. And Tony Abbott has surged ahead nationally as a better potential Prime Minister, with three separate polls now confirming a worsening electoral crisis looming for Labor as it battles to contain the latest NBN asbestos debacle and more boat arrivals. In what will be a shock for the Prime Minister, an exclusive JWS/Herald Sun poll reveals a 15.4 per cent swing against Mr Dreyfus in the once-safe seat of Isaacs, enough to remove him. If repeated in neighbouring seats, the PM's hopes of retaining the furniture in her home state could be dashed. (news.com) Deputy Prime Minister Wayne Swan looks set to lose his Brisbane seat of Lilley, with internal polling suggesting Labor will struggle to retain any Queensland seats at the September 14 federal election. In a result even worse than the 1996 ''baseball bats'' election, when Labor was reduced to two of the then 26 seats in Queensland, Labor may retain only one MP - former prime minister Kevin Rudd. The Queensland polling, taken in recent weeks in Mr Swan's seat of Lilley, is believed to show Mr Swan's primary vote has collapsed to just 28 per cent, compared with 41 per cent at the last election. Fairfax Media Don’t speak out of school Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey has issued a gag order on Coalition MPs and candidates talking to the Parliamentary Budget Office over fears that new laws will expose the Coalition to greater scrutiny. But in a bid to crack down on ill-discipline in Coalition ranks, he has banned any candidate or MP speaking about election policy without prior approval from the leader's office or the campaign head office. Fearing that changes to the powers of the PBO, expected before the September election, will put individual members under PBO scrutiny over the total cost of Coalition policies, Mr Hockey has written to all MPs and candidates warning them about straying from official policy line. He has demanded that if any candidate or MP is contacted by the PBO, then the contact must be immediately referred to Mr Hockey's office. (news.com) Asylum boats There was almost no warning. The boat had stopped about 10 minutes earlier. Since then it had rocked gently in the swell, settling lower in the water. Its Indonesian crew shouted to one another, increasingly agitated. On the roof of the open wooden outsize fishing boat, Mohammed Ishaq was shaken awake by another refugee. “Get up, the boat is sinking,” he was told. But even as he stood, the 31-year-old Afghan-born Pakistani felt the deck tilting sharply under his feet. He slid, fell and hit the water. It was 24 June 2012. The boat was 107 nautical miles from the nearest land. Of the 204 refugees aboard, almost all from Afghanistan or Pakistan, 94 would die. (Guardian Australia) You can follow live updates throughout the day here on the live blog, or join the conversation on Twitter using the hashtag #politicslive You can also follow me on @murpharoo

tags.description: NBN: latest news on the national broadband network from the Guardian

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 30 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 30 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Delhi's new chief rejects security on mission to clean up Indian politics

fields.headline: Delhi's new chief rejects security on mission to clean up Indian politics
fields.trailText: Arvind Kejriwal, whose party is made up of all first-time candidates, vows to fight corruption after shock results at polls
fields.bodyText: The former tax inspector and activist who is to be Delhi's new chief minister, the top elected official in the metropolis, has refused police protection, saying that his "biggest security" is God. Arvind Kejriwal, leader of a new populist political party "dedicated to improving the lot of the common man", announced on Monday that he would form a government to run the sprawling, troubled and increasingly wealthy city of 15 million people. The 45-year-old novice politician, who has vowed to end the "VIP culture" of the capital, also said that new ministers would refuse to accept the large government bungalows that are customary perks of such posts as well as cars that use flashing red beacons to force their way through traffic. Even minor dignitaries in the capital travel with a large police escort, adding to the already acute congestion and enraging ordinary citizens. Kejriwal's Aam Admi (Common Man) party stunned political analysts and established parties when it won 28 out of 70 seats in local assembly elections in Delhi earlier this month. The newcomer to the capital's cutthroat machinations, who launched his party a year ago, beat the former chief minster of the city, a veteran of the ruling Congress party who had dismissed his challenge as "not even on our radar". Congress suffered a catastrophic defeat, being reduced to eight seats. Almost all the candidates of the AAP were political debutants and included a rickshaw driver, a lawyer and a TV actor. Their key pledge was to clean up politics and the endemic corruption that has crippled the provision of public service for the many millions who cannot afford to pay for private healthcare, schooling or even basics such as water. The party's message and symbol – a broom – proved popular with urban voters also struggling with runaway inflation, chronic youth underemployment and slowing economic growth. Kerjiwal, who called the party's victory a historic win, had initially said it would not form a minority government. But after lengthy negotiations in recent days, Congress has now decided to support the AAP in the Delhi local assembly. "It's a great day, a very exciting time," said Aathishi Marlena, a senior AAP activist and policy adviser. Marlena said the challenges were daunting but that the party would meet them. "Tackling the rampant corruption, the lack of transparency and accountability, the problems faced by the common people is a matter of political will and that's what we bring to this." The main opposition Bharatiya Janata party won 32 seats but no other political group was willing to offer it support. Sachin Pilot, a Congress minister, described the emergence of a new political outfit – the AAP in Delhi – as "absolutely astonishing". "For my party of course it has been a setback but generally speaking the fact that indiviudals can come together in a very difficult space and find themselves relevant and meaningful has been a new phenomenon. In the last three or four decades it hasn't happened at all. That's something one has to recognise reconcile and then deal with," Pilot told the Guardian, speaking shortly after the poll earlier this month. Senior Congress officials told reporters that the party's support for the AAP in Delhi would not be unconditional and would depend on the party's performance. The election in Delhi was one of four state elections in the last month, which saw Congress lose heavily. The polls were dubbed a semi-final for the general election due next spring, by analysts. Though India's population remains largely rural, the proportion of seats in the national assembly determined by urban voters has steadily risen in recent years. Some observers see the new middle classes in cities and, particularly, tens of thousands of small towns, as determining what is likely to be a tough battle between the Congress party and the BJP next year. Also crucial are likely to be between 120 million – 150 million first time voters. The AAP has said it now wants to focus efforts on expanding into the huge northern state of Uttar Pradesh, which has a population fo 180 million, and Bihar, another poor northern state. It hopes to become a national party however. Kejriwal's announcement ended two weeks of speculation and fears of a hung assembly in Delhi, a city which has become much richer in recent years but faces huge problems of crime, sanitation, housing and pollution . Though the outgoing Congress government in Delhi had been praised for successfully building a metro system and holding the Commonwealth Games in the Indian capital in 2010, it had also been hit by graft charges and suffered from the general unpopularity of the Congress-led coalition government at a national level. The new government will be sworn in on Thursday. The BJP called the AAP's decision a betrayal. "The AAP accused the Congress of being the most corrupt. Today they have compromised on their principles. This is gross betrayal," said Dr Harsh Vardhan, the BJP's chief ministerial candidate.

results.webTitle: Oh Lord, can't we keep religion out of politics? | Teresa Wiltz

fields.headline: Oh Lord, can't we keep religion out of politics?
fields.trailText: Teresa Wiltz: America's founders insisted on church-state separation. Yet now we have senators and justices prattling about the devil. Save us
fields.bodyText: The devil is making a comeback. Or maybe, if we're to believe a certain group of politicos, he's been hiding in plain sight all along. First, we had Representative Michele Bachmann (Republican, Minneosota) asserting that President Obama's decision to help a segment of the Syrian rebels is proof positive that the four horsemen of the apocalypse would be showing up any day now. Because terrorists. "Rather than seeing this as a negative, we need to rejoice, Maranatha, come Lord Jesus, His day is at hand," she said in an interview with a Christian radio station. Senator Rand Paul (Republican, Kentucky), speaking at the Values Voter Summit over the weekend, declared that there was a "worldwide war against Christianity" – and the president, he said, isn't doing a thing to stop it. And then we had Senator Ted "Shutdown" Cruz (Republican, Texas), at the same conference, using a line from the movie The Usual Suspects – "the greatest trick the devil ever played was to convince the world he didn't exist" – to make a clumsy analogy to liberals. Because leftwingers have "convinced conservatives that we cannot win". Never mind those plummeting Republican approval ratings, senator, Satan made the pollsters do it. Sadly, I've come to expect such "us v them" histrionics from Bachmann et al. They are, after all, playing to their base. So I'd be tempted to dismiss this all as political theater – played-out political theater – if it weren't for that crazy times interview that US supreme court Justice Antonin Scalia did with New York magazine. In it, he talked about the devil. At great length. With no apparent irony: And then You know, it is curious. In the Gospels, the devil is doing all sorts of things. He's making pigs run off cliffs, he's possessing people and whatnot. And that doesn't happen very much anymore … It's because he's smart … What he's doing now is getting people not to believe in him or in God. He's much more successful that way. And then, he told the reporter that since she didn't hold the same belief system, she'd be going to hell, too. Let's hope he was kidding about that. Now, I'm all about freedom of religion. (My own beliefs tend to veer towards agnosticism, with a healthy side of Buddhism.) And I certainly respect the good justice's right to believe in whatever the hell he wants to believe in, even if that means a fervent belief in Beelzebub. But given that Scalia's about to weigh in on a case about public prayer in government meetings, I really wish he'd kept his lips zipped. Religion has no place in politics – and it certainly has no place in the US supreme court. Religion, or the lack thereof, is such a deeply, deeply personal thing. People who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Scratch that. People in power who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Really nervous. Because religion has been used in this country to justify all sorts of things – slavery being at the top of that list. It encourages revisionist thinking, like the Texas Board of Education's reshaping American history in its textbooks. And unless you've pulled some mystical magic trick, died, crossed over to the great beyond and come back again, how do you really know what God's thinking? If, that is, She exists at all. So, I don't like the role that religion – to be specific, Christianity – plays too often in American politics. It made me cringe during the 2008 presidential election, watching Barack Obama tie himself into knots trying to establish his Christian bonafides. Why should it matter? What if he were, let's say, a devout adherent to the Baha'i faith? Or if he were a committed atheist? No, we weren't created to be a Christian nation, even though the Founding Fathers had Christians in their ranks. When he was president, Thomas Jefferson, a fervent theist, told a group of Baptist ministers government had no business meddling in religion. Moreover, he said, there needed to be a "wall of separation between church and state". What happened to the wall? I like the wall. We need the wall. In fact, let's toss a few more bricks on top of the wall while we're at it – the better to keep the devil from meddling in our political affairs.

results.webTitle: Labour conference - Ed Miliband's speech: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Labour conference - Ed Miliband's speech: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Miliband's speech
fields.bodyText: Here is a summary of that fringe event. • The lights could go out if Labour introduces its 20-month freeze on energy prices, Ian Peters of British Gas said. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices suddenly go up within a single year “that will threaten energy security,” he said. Asked if that meant the lights would go out, he replied: “I think that is a risk.” • Tom Greatrex, the junior shadow energy minister, rejected this. He said: “I do not accept that that [the price freeze] is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline.” • Greatrex was asked if Labour would cut the companies some slack if there was a sudden increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex said they would not, and pointed out that when wholesale prices have come down in the past that had not been passed on to consumers. That’s it from us for today. Join Andrew Sparrow tomorrow morning for the final day of the Labour conference. Reg Platt of the IPPR says he does not believe there will be a supply problem brought on by the Labour policy and he says that if the energy companies threaten that, they will deserve a harsh response from the public. Tom Greatrex is asked about this idea that the lights might go out. Ian Peters interrupts to say he was talking about “in year market volatility” rather than long-term increases. Greatrex says: I don’t accept that the process of ensuring you have transparency and clarity in the market is going to lead to blackouts … In terms of the fixed period, the price freeze, that is because we would need that period to get that framework in place. I do not accept that that is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline. Gary Smith of the GMB says he does not agree that the lights will go out because of Labour’s announcement. British Gas’s Ian Peters is asked what he thinks of the Labour announcement. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices go up “that will threaten energy security,” Peters says. Does he mean the lights will go out? “I think that is a risk,” he says. Nick Pearce of the IPPR asks what happens if there is a big increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex says from 2015 we would have to put a short piece of legislation through to establish the cap, and then until 2017 the costs will be frozen. When wholesale costs have come down in the past, Greatrex says, this has not been reflected in consumers’ bills. “During that period [2015-17] it is right to be able to have that freeze,” he says. He says “it’s not about a short-term fix”. Tom Greatrex, the shadow junior energy minister, is up next. He says all the big six energy companies know they have "lost trust with their customers". It’s “vital” that trust is rebuilt, he says. There are significant levels of profit from companies in the generation part of the energy sector rather than the retail part – and these are often the same companies, he points out. Greatrex responds to criticisms that Labour’s policy would threaten investment. Investment recently has “partly fallen away” because of “mixed messages” and “false debates” from the government on shale gas, renewables and nuclear energy, he says. Reg Platt of the IPPR thinktank speaks first, saying wholesale gas costs are largely responsible for high energy prices. Platt says: “It’s very difficult to know what those profits are that are being made, and what you might deem appropriate in the sector.” He predicted Labour’s cap would be “a very popular policy with the public”. Ian Peters, managing director of residential energy at British Gas, speaks next. He says the vast majority of a gas or electricity bill is made up of things his company cannot control – such as wholesale costs, delivery to the home, and taxes. “Energy prices reflect the cost of energy,” he says. Profits are a small proportion, he says. We’re a strong British country creating jobs and investing in Britain’s future, Peters says. Gary Smith of the GMB union goes next. He says energy bills could double over the next few years and “that is a crisis that is facing people in how they’re going to meet that”. Smith says large chunks of the UK’s gas and electricity infrastructure are owned by Chinese companies, and he doesn’t believe they have British interests at heart. He backs Peters on his contention that the main drivers of increased prices are out of the hands of energy companies. Hi, Paul Owen here to take the reins for the next hour or so. I came here to this a conference room on the top floor – more or less on the roof – of the Metropole hotel to hear Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, hopefully talk about Labour’s most eye-catching policy for years: Ed Miliband’s promise to impose a 20-month price freeze on all energy companies. Unfortunately Flint has pulled out and will be replaced by her junior shadow minister Tom Greatrex. But I think it will probably be worth hearing what he has to say. Miliband announced the new policy in his conference speech today, saying: “The companies won't like it because it will cost them money. But they have been overcharging people for so long because the market does not work. And we need to press the reset button." To my mind this policy has the potential to have real “cut-through” with voters and may even have some impact on the outcome of the 2015 election. Labour's promise to increase the number of hours of free childcare is the same, in my opinion. • Ed Miliband has delighted his party with a well-delivered conference speech that contained a promise to freeze gas and electricity bills for almost two years if Labour wins the general election. It was the one announcement in the speech not trailed in advance, and it is the probably most eye-catching example of his desire to tame "irresponsible" capitalism through robust regulation. For Labour activists who have been looking for populist policies to sell on the doorstep, it is a godsend. But it's a risky move too. Energy companies claim that it is unfair, and that it will curb investment. They may, of course, be exaggerating. (The plan will cost the industry around £4bn. Utility companies complained bitterly about the £5bn windfall tax proposed by Labour before the 1997 election, but in the event it was imposed with little fuss). But there are other dangers too. What happens if the Tories respond with fuel payments targeted at those on low and middle-incomes (which would allow David Cameron to criticise a price freeze benefiting the rich)? And what happens if energy prices start to fall, undermining Labour's key election offer? • Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has suggested that Miliband's policy could lead to "widespread blackouts". Energy UK, the trade body for the industry, has also suggested that energy companies could stop investing in Britain in response to Miliband's threat. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe later. Here are the best quotes from Ed Miliband's speech. • Miliband said his main theme was that Britain could do better. I want to start today with the simplest of thoughts. An idea that has inspired change for generations. The belief that helped drive us out of the Second World War and into that great reforming government of 1945. An ambition that is more important now than it has been for decades. An emotion that is felt across our country at kitchen tables every night. A feeling that is so threatening to those who want to keep things as they are. Words that are so basic and yet so powerful, so modest and yet so hard to believe. Six simple words that say: Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this; we are Britain, we are better than this. • He said he believed that listening to others, and understanding their problems, was the most important thing a leader could do. [Miliband's mother] is the most patient, generous person I have met in my whole life. And she taught me never to be contemptuous of others, never to be dismissive of their struggle. Now she was teaching me a lesson of life. And some people will say, ah yeah but you have to leave decency behind when it comes to politics. Well I say they are wrong, because only if you reach out and listen can you do the most important thing a leader can do, the most important qualification in my view for being Prime Minister. Only then will you have the ability to walk in the shoes of others and know who to fight for, whoever your opponent, however powerful they are, guided by the only thing that matters: your sense of what is right. This is what I believe, this is where I stand, this is the leadership Britain needs. • He said the key problem for Britain was that ordinary people were no longer benefiting from growth. Now what I’m about to tell you is the most important thing I’m going to say today about what needs to change about our country. For generations in Britain when the economy grew the majority got better off. And then somewhere along the way that vital link between the growing wealth of the country and your family finances was broken. This is, this goes beyond one party or one government. It is more important to you than which party is in power, even more important than that ... They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats, now the rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. Now I say this to the people of Britain. If I were you I wouldn’t even take a second look at a political party unless they make this their central defining purpose because your future depends on it. Your children’s future depends on it. Britain’s future depends on it. I say we are Britain we can do better than this. • He said this problem was linked to David Cameron's approach to what Cameron calls "the global race". The cost of living crisis isn’t an accident of David Cameron’s economic policy it is in his economic policy. Let me explain why. You see he believes in this thing called the global race, but what he doesn’t tell you is that he thinks for Britain to win the global race you have to lose, lower wages, worse terms and conditions, fewer rights at work. But Britain can’t win a race for the lowest wages against countries where wages rates are pennies an hour and the more we try the worse things will get for you. Britain can’t win a race for the fewest rights at work against the sweat shops of the world and the more we try the worse things will get for you. And Britain can’t win a race for the lowest skilled jobs against countries where kids leave school at the age of 11. And the more we try the worse things will get for you. It is a race to the bottom. Britain cannot and should not win that race. • He said the Tories were only interested in those at the top. So when they tell you the economy is healing, that everything is fixed, just remember, they are not talking about your life, they are talking about their friends at the top. That is who they are talking about; it is high hopes for them. And every so often you know the mask slips doesn’t it. The other day a man they call Lord Howell, he was I think their advisor on fracking at one point… There is nothing funny about that. He said it was wrong to frack in some areas but it was ok in others, it was ok in the North East of England because he said, and I quote ‘it was full of desolate and uninhabited areas.’ In one casual aside dismissing one who le region of the country. Let’s tell these Tories about the North East of England and every other part of Britain. People go out to work. They love their kids. They bring up their families. They care for their neighbours. They look out for each other. They are proud of their communities. They are proud of their communities. They hope for the future. The Tories call them inhabitants of desolate areas. We call them our friends, our neighbours, the heroes of our country. They are fed up of a government that doesn’t understand their lives and a Prime Minister who cannot walk in their shoes. We are Britain, we are better than this. • He said Labour would freeze gas bills. Take the gas and electricity companies. We need successful energy companies, in Britain. We need them to invest for the future. But you need to get a fair deal and frankly, there will never be public consent for that investment unless you do get a fair deal. And the system is broken and we are going to fix it. If we win the election 2015 the next Labour government will freeze gas and electricity prices until the start of 2017. Your bills will not rise. It will benefit millions of families and millions of businesses. That’s what I mean by a government that fights for you. That’s what I mean when I say Britain can do better than this. • He said Labour would get house building up to 200,000 a year by 2020. There are 9 million people in this country renting a home, many of whom who would want to buy. 9 million people - we don’t just have a cost of living crisis, we have a housing crisis too. In 2010 when we left office there was a problem. There were one million too few homes in Britain. If we carry on as we are, by 2020 there will be two million too few homes in Britain. That is the equivalent of two cities the size of Birmingham. Wave got to do something about it and the next Labour government will. So we’ll say to private developers, you can’t just sit on land and refuse to build. We will give them a very clear message - either use the land or lose the land, that is what the next Labour government will do. We’ll say to local authorities that they have a right to grow, and neighbouring authorities can’t just stop them. We’ll identify new towns and garden cities and we’ll have a clear aim that by the end of the parliament Britain will be building 200,000 homes a year, more than at any time in a generation. That’s how we make Britain better than this. • He said Labour did rescue the NHS. When we came to office there were waiting time targets of 18 months that were not being met, when we left office there were waiting time targets of 18 weeks that were being met. When we came to office there was an annual winter A&E crisis, when we left office the people had A&E services they could rely on. When we came to office there were fewer doctors and nurses, we when left office more doctors and nurses than ever before. And when we came to office people said well the health service, it was a good idea in previous generations but I don’t really believe it will be there in the next, and we left office with the highest public satisfaction in the history of the health services. Yes friends, we did rescue the National Health Service. • He said he would happily fight David Cameron on the question of leadership and character. But the next election isn’t just going to be about policy. It is going to be about how we lead and the character we show. I have got a message for the Tories today: if they want to have a debate about leadership and character, be my guest. And if you want to know the difference between me and David Cameron, here’s an easy way to remember it. When it was Murdoch versus the McCanns, he took the side of Murdoch. When it was the tobacco lobby versus the cancer charities, he took the side of the tobacco lobby. When it was the millionaires who wanted a tax cut versus people paying the bedroom tax, he took the side of the millionaires. Come to think of it, here is an even easier way to remember it: David Cameron was the Pr ime Minister who introduced the bedroom tax, I’ll be the Prime Minister who repeals the bedroom tax. You see here is the thing about David Cameron. He may be strong at standing up to the weak, but he is always weak when it comes to standing up against the strong. That is the difference between me and David Cameron, so let’s have that debate about leadership and character, and I relish that debate. Here's the full text of Ed Miliband's speech. I'll post the best quotes in a moment. Angela Knight, chief executive of Energy UK, the trade association for the energy body, told Sky News a few minutes ago that Ed Miliband's proposal could lead to energy companies pulling investment out of the UK. Possible the first thing that might happen is a bit more serious in some respects. Much of the money that is required to invest in energy in the UK comes from overseas. They will be looking at these sorts of statements and thinking do I go and invest this money in a country where I don't know if I'm going to get a return because there are price freezes around, or do I go and invest it in a country which perhaps thinks in a rather different manner. Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, has responded to Ed Miliband's speech. (He didn't like it.) The Labour leader’s speech was an extraordinary confection promising Britain a return to the divided, bankrupt country of the late seventies. Economically, environmentally and constitutionally inept and destructive, at over an hour, it may the longest political valedictory speech in our history .... [His plans] to freeze gas prices until 2017, without saying how he could possibly achieve this in a free market while adhering to the carbon cutting craze that is forcing energy prices up by channelling vast amounts of money into unreliable, inefficient green technologies such as wind. Labour’s idea to create an Energy Security Board does little more than bring back memories of candles after dark and burning braziers at street corners. Here's Guardian Comment is free panel verdict, with contributions from Diane Abbott, Jackie Ashley, John McDonnell, Melissa Kite, Hopi Sen and David Clark. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's policy and campaigns director Freezing energy bills will certainly be welcomed by cash-strapped households, who face rocketing prices due to the soaring cost of gas and bumper energy company profits. The only way to deliver an energy system we can all afford is by urgently investing in an ambitious and comprehensive energy efficiency programme and weaning the nation off increasingly costly fossil fuels - and this must be a priority for an incoming Labour Government. Ed Miliband was right to put the environment at the heart of his conference speech - he must now put it firmly at the centre of Labour's policy-making to realise his goal of one million new green jobs. Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has put out a statement about Ed Miliband's plan to freeze energy bills. He suggests that it could lead to power cuts. Everyone wants to help with the cost of rising bills, which is why Liberal Democrats have cut income tax by £700 for working people. But Labour’s plan is a promise that won’t work. When they tried to fix prices in California it resulted in an electricity crisis and widespread blackouts. We can’t risk the lights going out here too. Fixing prices in this way risks blackouts, jeopardises jobs and puts investment in clean, green technology in doubt. The Institute of Directors does not like the speech. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general, on the plan to penalise developers if they do not build on land with planning permission. The obstacle to house building is invariably the bureaucratic nightmare of trying to get a project off the ground. Taxing developers who are not building is no substitute for simply making it easier to build. This approach risks making it even less attractive for developers to build, whilst ignoring the urgent need for planning liberalisation. And this is what he says about the proposal to freeze energy bills. We should think very, very carefully before piling more distortion on an already grossly distorted energy market. Price controls only add greater uncertainty to companies who we need to take the financial risks of energy investment. It was also a great shame not to hear anything about the potential benefits of domestic shale gas exploration, which must feature as part of the UK’s long-term energy strategy. From Frances O'Grady, general secretary of the TUC This was a defining speech that rightly focussed on the living standards crisis and went on to offer hope. At its heart was a clear break from the view that Britain succeeds by reducing the rights, pay and prospects of people at work. More importantly the clear pledges made today and during the rest of this week showed that there will be a real choice at the next election – no-one can now say that the parties are all the same. The right were never going to like this speech. Here is reaction from two groups on the libertarian fringe of politics. From Matthew Sinclair, chief executive of the TaxPayers' Alliance When the Government fixes prices it always ends in a disaster for consumers. Ed Miliband is sticking by the green taxes and expensive subsidies that drive up the price of energy, so at best this new policy would just store up massive price hikes for another day. At worst it could create a crisis and force the Government to bail out the sector. If he really wants to help families struggling with their bills, he should promise that the next Labour Government would scrap the taxes and regulations that will otherwise keep making energy much more expensive for British consumers. From Philip Booth, editorial director at the Institute for Economic Affairs Ed Miliband clearly does not understand the root cause of rising living costs. His speech set out an array of government interventions. It is precisely these kind of interventions which have seen the price of food, energy and housing reach such burdensome levels. His tinkering policies will do little for families struggling to make ends meet. Unfortunately the Labour Party has not recognised that the most effective way to reduce living costs and unleash economic growth is to reduce taxes and ease regulation. In his speech Ed Milband defended England's union with Scotland with a story about a Labour delegate from Glasgow having to receive emergency hospital treatment in Liverpool. The SNP's Angus Robertson thinks trying to use the NHS to make a unionist point will backfire. For Ed Miliband to use the NHS as a reason to attack independence shows an astonishing lack of knowledge both about the National Health Service and about Scotland. The reality is that Scotland is already independent in terms of health policy, and it is precisely because NHS Scotland comes under the remit of the Scottish Parliament, rather than Westminster, that we are able to protect and develop the founding ethos of our health service, in stark contrast to the privatisation and fragmentation happening south of the border. This was a real blunder by Mr Miliband which plays right into the hands of the case for Yes, because it demonstrates that the best decisions for Scotland are decisions made in Scotland. And as experts have confirmed, exactly the same cross-border healthcare arrangements that NHS Scotland is obliged to have now with counterparts in the rest of the UK and overseas would apply in an independent Scotland. And here's some more union reaction. From Paul Kenny, leader of the GMB It was a very polished performance, with the welcome theme that Britain can do better. Millions of people know that life can be better. That was a completely different Labour leader to the one we saw three years ago. No prompts, no plastic performance - he has grown into being a prime minister. From Steve Murphy, general secretary of construction union Ucatt Our current housing crisis is a result of a failure to build a sufficient number of homes especially council housing for decades. This is the first time that a political party is seriously addressing this crisis which is affecting Britain socially and economically.
