From Lord Kinnock, the former Labour leader A brilliant speech, wonderfully delivered and, most importantly, I thought it was fundamental, practical patriotism that will resonate in the country and it's really what Ed means. From Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary [Miliband] showed he understands what people are going through. He offered firm commitments to help hard-pressed families. He gave a vision for the many, not the few. From Joss Garman, deputy political director of Greenpeace As energy secretary, it was Ed Miliband who introduced new pollution rules for power stations and pushed for more ambition at the global climate talks in Copenhagen, so it was good to see him once again putting climate change back at the heart of his pitch to the country. His promise that his government would completely remove carbon pollution from the energy system and freeze energy bills will mean he has to face down the powerful energy companies, but public opinion is on his side. Together with his pledge to create a million new green jobs, he has set a new bar for the coalition parties to meet. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Miliband is right to say that Britain should be in a 'race to the top'. While it was refreshing to hear a leader speak about major economic issues, he did not set out a clear vision to move Britain's economy from good to great. What's more, his speech contained more sticks than carrots for business. The way to have a high-wage, high-value economy is to encourage business investment and to build an enterprise-friendly environment. While some of his proposals pass this test, many others do not. What's more, he has made significant spending commitments, but it is not clear how the country will create the wealth required to pay for them. From Peter Hain, the Labour former cabinet minister It was a barnstorming speech, a virtuoso performance. I don't know any other politician in Britain who can deliver without a note that kind of rousing speech of that length of time and tell a story about what Ed Miliband's Britain will be like - a fairer Britain and more prosperous Britain for everybody. From curbing electricity and gas prices to cutting business rates to small businesses to abolishing the reviled 'bedroom tax', all of these policies - building hundreds of thousands of more houses - will resonate with every one of the concerns in every household in Britain. From John Cridland, the CBI director general Businesses will view the proposals on tax and energy as a setback for Labour's pro-enterprise credentials. Rising energy bills are tough on families and businesses, but the proposed energy price freeze will deter much-needed investment and is at odds with Labour's pledge to decarbonise the economy and create a million green jobs. The main reasons that bills are going up is the combination of rising wholesale prices, the cost of policies needed to keep the lights on and the move to a low-carbon economy. Here's Len McCluskey, the Unite leader, on the speech. People at home urgently wanting to know what a Labour government will do for them will certainly have a better idea today. They will know that this is a leader and a party that is on their side. Ed Miliband should never be afraid to articulate a courageous vision for this nation because that is what these extraordinary, fearful times urgently need. Voters are hungry for something better for Britain than foodbanks, tattered wages and Wonga-nomics. They want to hear about homes, that their children will have a future in this country, about a fair day's wage for a fair day's work. They need Labour to stand for a better life for the people of this country than the worry and insecurity this miserable government offers. It was good to hear Ed mention the role of unions positively in delivering the minimum wage, challenging in-work poverty. This is what we do day-in, day-out. Here are the key points from the speech. • Miliband says Labour would freeze gas and electricity bills for 20 months after 2015 general election. • 16 and 17-year-olds would be given the vote. • By 2020 200,000 new homes would be built a year. • Business rates would be cut for small businesses. • All primary schools would have breakfast clubs or after-school clubs. • Minimum wage would be strengthened. Here is some Twitter reaction from journalists and commentators. The energy companies have been reacting to the speech already. Snap Verdict: Labour delegates wanted a biggie and they got one. Many policies announcements have little "cut through" with the public, but the energy price freeze surprise at the heart of the speech will probably do the trick. Even if the Conservatives retaliate somehow (as they surely will), it will appeal to voters, and it will signal a determination to use the power of the state to strong-arm corporate Britain in a way the coalition (or New Labour) could not or would not match. Miliband was not using notes, and the speech was more conversational than a conventional leader's conference. But that worked, and may impress people who have doubts about Miliband's power as a communicator. The speech was also rich in hope and optimism (unlike much of what has been heard from the conference platform this week), and his character challenge to David Cameron was the most effective he has delivered. More later. But first I will round up some other reaction .... Our sentiment tracker showed a steady climb over the course of Ed Miliband's speech, with just one major dip into negative territory, at around 2.45pm, possibly around the time Miliband said Labour would not be able to spend money it does not have. PO We had a few internet problems here, and I may have missed some, but I think Ed Miliband only said three of our Milibingo terms: one nation, cuts and unions. I didn't hear any of the others: infighting, recovery, credibility, unemployment, macho or coalition. Let me know in the comments if I'm wrong. PO Miliband says that when people said at the dawn of the industrial revolution that Britain could be better than this, they were right. Those that said at the darkest hour, during the war, that Britain could be better than this, and they did. And the same applied when the battle for equality was being fought at the end of the last century. Britain can be better than this, he says. He will lead a government for Britain. Miliband says there is going to be a big fight in the run up to the general election. Think about the people, he says. I don't want to win this fight for Labour. I want to win it for Britain. Miliband condemns the Home Office for sending the "go home" vans to areas where immigrant families have lived for years. We are better than that, Miliband says. Miliband has a message for the Tories. If they want a debate about leadership and character, "be my guest". When there was a choice between Murdoch and the McCanns, Cameron sided with Murdoch. He sided with the tobacco industry. And he cut taxes for millionaires. And he was the PM who introduced the bedroom tax. Miliband will be the PM who abolishes it. Cameron may be strong at standing up to the weak. But he is weak at standing up to the strong. Miliband has a story about Scotland. Cathy Murphy lives in Glasgow. She was ill. But she came to the Labour conference in Liverpool in 2011. She fell ill, and had to have a serious operation. She had it at a world-leading hospital in Liverpool. She has to return every six months for a check-up. The doctors do not worry that she is from Scotland. They care for her anyway. He invites the audience to applaud. And he says he wants the Liverpool hospital to carry on treating patients like Cathy. Miliband turns to devolution. Devolution works, he says. Carwyn Jones is a brilliant first minister of Wales. And Johann Lamont is doing a brilliant job fighting the SNP. Miliband says he would like to hear more from young people. It was gay and lesbian young people who led the fight for gay marriage. And young women are saying the battle for equality is not won. They are not satisfied that 30% of Labour MPs are women. They want it to be 50%. And they are right. And they are not satisfied that women are still subject to violence and harassment. And they are right. Let's give the vote to 16 and 17-year-olds, he says. Miliband says he wants ordinary workers to be at the heart of the party. Leadership matters. But in the end change comes from the bottom. He wants to make Labour a mass membership party. Let's make ourselves truly the people's party again. Unions: Labour’s best frenemies. PO Miliband says he has explained how Labour can rescue the economy and society. Now he wants to talk about rescuing politics. It is the bit they have all been looking forward to - party reform. Change is difficult, he says. But Labour cannot just be a party of 200,000. It should be a party of 500,000, or 600,000. It is possible because of the unique link with the unions. Miliband says he does not want to end that link. He wants to mend that link. Miliband says Labour really did rescue the NHS. The government is responsible for what is wrong with it now. And Labour has to rescue the NHS all over again. (This gets what seems to be the largest round of applause of the speech.) Miliband says Labour does not believe in a race to the bottom. It believes in a race to the top. (Another glitch. I've missed a bit.) Miliband says the next Labour government should deal with the scourge of low pay. The minimum wage has been falling in values. Labour must do something about this. Some sectors, like banking, pay the living wage. But other sectors need to pay more. That's why government will strengthen the minimum wage. Miliband says firms will have to provide apprenticeships if they want to hire skilled foreign workers. And companies that train their workers will be able to "call time" on their competitors who do not do this. Miliband says to win the race to the top Britain has to support small businesses. New jobs will come from small firms. In the future most of the new jobs will come from firms that don't yet exist. The government has cut taxes for big businesses, but put them up for big ones. He says that's not right. Labour would take money from big business to cut taxes for small businesses. It will cut business rates for them in 2015, and freeze them for the next year. One nation Labour stands for small businesses. Cuts: Ed will cut small business rates, he says. PO Miliband turns to the environment. In 20 years' time his children will be asking if his was the last generation not to get climate change, or the first to get it. George Osborne says it's a matter of what you can afford. He's right. Britain cannot afford not to take climate change seriously. Miliband says Britain should be in a race to the top. But Labour is not going to be able to do this easily. You think opposition is tough? Government is harder. Labour will not be able to spend money it does not have. And, if he said it would, people would not believe him anyway. Miliband says it is not the low achievements of the Tories that annoy him. It is their low aspirations for Britons. The Tories are only interested in their friends at the top. Think of Lord Howell, the Tory adviser on fracking. He said it was wrong to frack in some places, but okay in others, like the north east, which is "desolate". Let's tell people about the north east. People love their kids there, they go out to work, they are proud of their communities, they look after their neighbours. The Tories call them desolate. We call them friends, and the heroes of our country. Miliband says he would advise the British public not to even take a second look at a party unless they acknowledge this. We can do better than this. Do the Tories get it? He invites the audience to respond. They shout no. Miliband says when he was growing up living standards were rising. People could afford to go on holiday for the first time. They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats. Now a rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. We need to understand how we got here. This bit is the most important thing he will say today, he says. For generations, as the economy grew, everyone benefited. But then the link between growth and the majority broke. This is more important to people even than who is in power, he says. (I've missed a bit, because my computer crashed, but I'm up now.) Miliband has just told stories of meeting people who like Britain, but who believe Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this, he says (twice). And Britain will be better than this, he says (again, twice). Mililband is now on to the political challenge. Britain can do better than this ... We're Britain, we're better than this. Are you satisfied with a country where people are working harder for less? People listen only to the powerful. Miliband is not satisfied. We're Britain.We're better than this. One nation: he stole it from a Tory, and he’s never giving it back. It’s Miliband’s catchphrase: one nation. PO She said Ed was attractive. He was not geeky, she said. And she said he was dressed casually "and had style". Miliband was pleased with this. But then he found out she was concussed. Anyway, he says to her: "You've made my year." Ed Miliband starts by thanking someone from the bottom of his heart. Not Justine - though he does want to thank her too. And not his mum. But a woman called Ella Phillips. She rode past him on his bike. She fell off. He helped her. And she said he was "an action hero." Ed Miliband has arrived in the hall. On the BBC Andrew Neil asks Nick Robinson asks if Ed Miliband will announce a 20-month energy freeze. Robinson says he has been told various things on an embargoed basis. But there will be an announcement about energy, he says. A random selection of Labour tweets from the hall ... Lord Ashcroft, the former Tory deputy chairman, is in the hall to watch the speech. (He has been speaking at a fringe meeting.) He's been tweeting. You can watch the speech live at the top of this page - and register what you think of it by pressing plus and minus on our "sentiment tracker". Just refresh the page to see them both. Ed Miliband will be speaking shortly. He is expected on stage at around 2.15pm. He is speaking without notes, and the party is not releasing a text in advance. That means that I will have to blog it in reported speech. Overnight Labour briefed two announcements from the speech, the house building plans and the business rate cuts for small firms. But we're expecting something more. The BBC's Andrew Neil has just posted this on Twitter. • Lord Adonis, the Labour former transport secretary who is leading Ed Miliband's growth review, has urged the party not to abandon the HS2 high-speed rail project. This is what he said at a Progress fringe event. We cannot as a party preach long-termism and not practice it ourselves. We have got to be very clear about that. We are the party that started HS2. I published the plan three and a half years ago. We set the whole thing out, we set out the rationale, including capacity. You have got to in politics, you cannot say that your principles and all that apply to other people but they don't apply to you when short term political advantage might rear its head. We have got to stick with this. It's very important. • Organisations representing large businesses have criticised Miliband's plan to cut business rates for small firms by abandoning a planned corporation tax cut for large firms. The CBI said the proposal undermined Labour's pro-enterprise credentials and the Institute for Directors said it was "a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment". But the Federation of Small Businesses and the British Retail Consortium welcomed the move. • Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, has said Labour would put the interests of rail passengers before profit to "end the racket on our railways". As the Press Association reports, Eagle highlighted a range of proposals to stop customers feeling like the system is "always trying to rip you off" and said Labour would introduce a "new deal for passengers". Among the changes Labour would bring in is a drive to end the confusion around peak and off-peak tickets, changing the law to make sure passengers are offered the cheapest ticket no matter where or how the buy it, and ending the inflexibility on advance tickets which means passengers cannot get the following train even if it is empty. • Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, has said energy companies would be forced to do their business in an open market under Labour. As the Press Association reports, she said if Labour won power in 2015, the party would prevent power companies doing their trades behind closed doors. She said there is currently no incentive for energy companies to keep prices down, telling the Labour Party conference in Brighton that power stations would no longer be run by the companies that supply gas and electricity directly to consumers. • Diana Holland, Labour's treasurer, has suggested that Miliband's reform to the union link could damage the party's finances. She delivered the warning in her speech to the conference. There are also important discussions about reforming membership and affiliation to the party. In all the discussions going forward, and about finding the best way, as your party treasurer I hope that whatever your point of view, everyone bears in mind the need to stick to the proven success of our party's finance strategy ... We cannot and must not jeopardise the hard-won financial stability of this party. Let us never forget as we develop the next steps for our party two very important truths - one, that the principles and values that unite us are far greater than the tactical and organisational differences that can divide us. • ITV have released a ComRes poll showing that 61% of people say Miliband does not come across as an election winner. Some 17% said he did, and 22% were not sure. • Gordon Brown was initially unimpressed by Barack Obama, Damian McBride has claimed in his memoirs. As Paul Owen reports, the book by the controversial former political adviser was published on Tuesday after a high-profile serialisation in the Daily Mail, with McBride's revelations about how he smeared Labour ministers gaining the most attention. But Power Trip also contains revelations about Brown's views of Obama, David Cameron, Rupert Murdoch – and the Mail's own editor, Paul Dacre. • Iain Dale, McBride's publisher, has been involved in a scuffle with a protester distracting attention from an interview that McBride was conducting. Paul Owen will be moonlighting as a bingo caller throughout Ed Miliband’s speech. He writes: The Guardian’s ruthless literary satirist John Crace has identified nine key words and phrases Ed Miliband is likely to weave deftly into his speech this afternoon. Click here for your interactive bingo card and you can tick them off when he does so. Here’s the full list: • One nation • Infighting • Recovery • Credibility • Cuts • Unemployment • Unions • Macho • Coalition I'll be following the speech and calling the words in this blog, as well as @gdnpolitics. Click here to get your bingo card. Good luck. Here's some more reaction to the plans being announced by Ed Miliband today. Cutting business rates for small firms From Helen Dickinson, director general of the British Retail Consortium The UK business rates are the highest property taxes of any EU country and lead directly to vacant shops. A consensus is emerging that the system is no longer fit for purpose and requires total reform. We therefore welcome this focus on supporting small businesses and high streets and the recognition that the cost of business rates has become unsustainable for retailers. With more than one in ten shops currently empty across the country, a complete reform of the system is required. We are keen to discuss these proposals in detail with Labour to understand how they might form part of this more fundamental root and branch review. From Alexander Jackman, head of policy at the Forum of Private Business There has been a lot of talk on the cost of living at this conference so we are pleased that Ed Miliband will use some of his speech today to highlight some of the rising costs of doing business. Taking money from a proposed business tax cut to fund a different business tax cut isn't an ideal way to fund the policy but importantly it recognises a significant rising cost facing many British small businesses. Building 200,000 new homes a year From Mike Quinton, chief executive of the National House Building Council, a body that sets standards for new homes. We welcome Ed Miliband's commitment to support the UK house building industry. Despite encouraging signs this year of a broad based recovery, there remains a critical shortage of new homes being built. Labour is right to focus on policies that will support the building of more new high quality homes and NHBC will work with all Parties to develop good housing policy. However, there is still some way to go before house building returns to pre-recession levels. As part of the Labour plan to promote more house building, developers would be penalised if they did not build homes on land granted planning permission. (See 11.49am.) At a fringe meeting at the conference, Sir Steve Bullock, London Councils' executive member of housing, backed the call for a crackdown on "landbanking". It’s frankly unacceptable that developers are hoarding land which could be used to build much needed homes for Londoners. With London’s population set to top nine million by 2021, we need to think radically about policy options to boost supply. Cracking down on landbanking could add 30,000 homes to London’s housing stock. London Councils say that in July 2013 there were 124,247 homes in London with agreed planning permission that had not been built. And the final shadow cabinet speaker this morning was Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. The text of her speech is on the Labour website. Here are the main points. • Flint said Labour would introduce the "most radical, comprehensive reforms since energy privatisation" into the energy market. The big six energy firms would be broken up, she said. The days where a company generates energy, sells it to themselves … and then sells it to us … Those days will end ... Just as the banks will have to separate their investment and trading arms from the high street branches, so we will make the energy companies separate their production from the companies that supply your home. • She said Labour would make energy bills clearer. And let me say one more thing about the bills you will receive… Under Labour, on every bill you will see one standing charge and one unit price. Simple. Straightforward. Easy to compare. Easy to switch. • She said Labour would set up an energy security board "to identify our energy needs, secure investment for the future and keep the lights on". Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, gave what may have been the best received speech of the morning. On policy, it was relatively meaty. The full text is on the Labour website, and here are the main points. • Eagle confirmed that Labour would impose a "proper cap" on rail fares. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to end what she described as "the racket on our railways". But we need a new deal for passengers too. No more talk of Super Peak fares, meaning your season ticket wouldn’t even be valid on every train. No more stretching peak time, when it’s actually about stretching profits. No more confusing tickets, but the exact time you can use it printed on the ticket. No more inflexibility when you book in advance, so you can’t get the next train – even when it’s empty. And if you do have the wrong ticket on the train, take off the price you’ve already paid from the cost of a new one. No more single and return journeys costing the same. Not just in one pilot area after 2015, as the government plans, but across the network. No more charging more at the ticket office than online, just to provide another excuse to close them. No more rip offs at ticket machines, but a new legal right to be offered the cheapest fare regardless of how or where you buy a ticket. No more inflation-busting increases in the cost of leaving your car at the station, when it’s just another way to clobber commuters. No more ripping people off with internet charges, just because you can’t afford to travel First Class. And isn’t it time that all trains had wifi in the 21st century? So let’s require it in franchises. • She said that Labour in principle supported HS2. This is what was in the text. So, as Ed Balls rightly says: we support the idea of a new north-south rail line but, if costs continue to rise – and the value for money cannot be demonstrated, we will have to ask if this is the right priority for £50billion pounds. But, as Rob Merrick points out, she added a line. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to boost cycling. And she said that she supported what Norman Baker, the Lib Dem transport minister, was trying to do on this. But Baker was being blocked by the Tories, she said. [Baker's] tried to get his Tory bosses to take cycling seriously. But while they’ve set out a plan to spend £28 billion on roads, he’s secured just £38million a year to support cycling. And conveniently forgotten the three wasted years that followed his decision to axe Cycling England and its £60million a year budget. Come off it, Norman: On ya bike. So, here’s what we need to do:clear goals to increase cycling. Separated routes. Redesigned junctions. Phased traffic lights. Cycling Safety Assessments for all new transport schemes. Restored targets to cut road deaths and serious injuries. Duties to support Active Travel, as Labour introduced in Wales. 20mph zones, the default in residential areas. Long term support for teaching safe cycling. Space on trains. Secure facilities at stations – required in rail contracts. Sentencing guidelines reviewed. Tough new rules on HGVs. Supporting cycling. Increasing numbers. Improving safety. • She said she wanted all InterCity trains to look the same. (Presumably this means that Virgin Trains' red carriages would get a repaint under Labour.) So, instead of all this waste, let’s reduce costs in our railway. Save money by bringing a fragmented industry together. With responsibilities currently spread across the Transport Department and multiple separate bodies, brought within a reformed and more accountable Network Rail. Save money by ending wasteful repainting and rebranding of trains and stations with every new contract. Restore a coherent InterCity identity to national train services, regardless of public or private operator. In his speech to the conference earlier Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, set out more details of Labour's plans to build more homes. The text is on the Labour website. Here's an extract. First, we must admit that we can’t carry on saying on the one hand “where are the homes for the next generation?” and on the other “please don’t build them near me”. Nor will we get more homes by top-down targets. Councils and communities must take that responsibility but they need more power to be able to do so. Communities should know where land is available. That’s why we will ensure developers register the land they own or have options on. And where land is not brought forward for homes, communities should be able to do something about it. And when communities have given planning permission they should be able to say to developers: we’ve given you the go ahead so please get on and build the homes you said you would. And if you don’t then we’ll charge you and, if you still don’t, we’ll sell the land on to someone else who will. Secondly, there are areas in the country where councils and communities see the need for more homes but there just isn’t the land to build them on. So the next Labour government will give those communities a new ‘Right to Grow’, allowing them – if they want – to expand and ensuring that neighbouring areas work with them to do so. Thirdly, conference, it’s time to build new communities – new towns and new garden cities. That’s what the great Attlee Government did as they started to rebuild Britain and we need that same spirit again. So we will invite local authorities to come forward, and in return, we will make sure that they get the powers and the incentives they need to acquire land, put in the infrastructure and build. Build those new communities. You can read all today's Guardian Labour party conference coverage here. As for the rest of the papers, here are some articles I found particularly interesting. • Janan Ganesh in the Financial Times (subscription) explains the difference between Ed Balls and Ed Miliband. Mr Balls is a conventional social democrat. He believes the market is the least bad way of making economic decisions. He wants to tax and spend its proceeds for egalitarian ends – lavishly, for sure – but does not aspire to interfere with its workings. He does not fancy himself wiser than the price mechanism. He is a leftwinger who has read his Adam Smith. Mr Miliband is more corporatist. He thinks the left over-relies on redistribution. He wants to intervene earlier – to “predistribute” – by making businesses narrow pay differentials, train workers and empower unions. He is happier to second-guess the market. This intellectual difference between the two men manifests in a thousand ways. Mr Balls values the City as a financial centre and source of revenue; Mr Miliband seems to regard it as a barely necessary evil. Mr Balls would expand London’s airport capacity to meet demand; Mr Miliband prefers the environmental case against. Mr Balls tends not to sermonise about what the economy “should” look like; Mr Miliband scolds betting shops, payday lenders and other “predatory” businesses. Even in their political styles, Mr Miliband is abstract and Mr Balls remorselessly practical. In the arcane typology of the British left, Mr Miliband is “pamphlet Labour”, all discursive academia and moral certitude, whereas Mr Balls brings the campaigning grit of “leaflet Labour”. Only by grasping these differences can anyone appreciate Mr Balls’ usefulness to his party. • Rachel Sylvester in the Times (paywall) thinks Ed Miliband is still bound by loyalty to his father. It is no coincidence that Ralph Miliband’s gravestone in Highgate Cemetery, which stands just a few yards from Karl Marx’s tomb, is engraved with the words: “Writer Teacher Socialist”. Indeed, according to a family friend, one reason Ed decided to stand against his brother David for the Labour leadership was because he thought he would protect his father’s political legacy more faithfully than his more Blairite sibling. Just as Ralph emphasised the importance of the Left working outside Parliament to create a “new social order in which democracy, egalitarianism and co-operation” took hold, so Ed wants a “new politics” based in communities rather than the Commons to bring about his One Nation ideal. The influence of the older Miliband — who opposed regime change through military intervention, even the overthrow of the Pol Pot regime in Cambodia — may also be evident in his son’s decision to block action in Syria. Although his head knows that he must stay in the centre, Mr Miliband’s heart beats to the left. Socialism, he has said, is a “response to injustice” and “a tale that never ends”. He fears betraying his father, whom he describes as his “lodestar”, a man whose approval both he and his brother have always craved. • Benedict Brogan in the Telegraph says Labour figures are worried that the Tories are beating them at spin. Every conversation in Brighton, it seems, includes an admission that the Conservatives are winning the propaganda war hands down. If Craig Oliver and Lynton Crosby could hear what the shadow cabinet are saying in the bars, they would blush: “Their attack unit is ruthless. Their messaging is consistent and disciplined. They are beating us.” This is not something said often in Westminster – but for the past six months or so, the Labour high command has been noticing that a widespread perception of Tory incompetence lags behind the reality. The Conservatives may complain about their own machine, but the Opposition is arguably the better judge. And they are definitely hurting. There is no argument that Labour has had a terrible summer. Doubts about its leader are, if anything, strengthening. The party rages that it has the better arguments on a wealth of issues – but that the Tories are more adept at distorting events to suit their purposes. There is even talk of creating a rapid-rebuttal unit, modelled on the one that helped Bill Clinton to his first win in 1992. • Owen Jones in the Independent writes the speech he thinks Miliband should deliver. • Laura Pitel in the Times (paywall) says Unite is boycotting the Labour gala dinner. Ed Miliband’s biggest trade union backer is to boycott his gala dinner in the latest rebuff to the Labour leader. For the first time in years, Unite will not pay for a table at tonight’s event, where Mr Miliband is guest of honour. Nor is it holding its usual conference party. A source at the union said that Unite was taking a low-key approach in Brighton, adding: “We’re not sulking, just watching.” It is partly a protest at the growing corporatisation of the conference, which has become an important money-spinner for Labour. The symbolism is clear, however, with tension between Mr Miliband and Len McCluskey, the Unite General Secretary, over the Falkirk row. • Oliver Wright in the Independent explains how party conferences have become glorified trade fairs. Electoral Commission records show why, exposing just how lucrative this source of income has becomes for our political parties. In their most recent accounts the Conservatives reveal that they raised just £747,000 in membership fees last year – but £4.2m from conferences. Labour raked in £3.37m from so called “commercial activities”, including conferences, while even the Liberal Democrats raised £1.4m from events. One senior party official admitted that what was once a forum to debate and decide party policy has now become little more than a “glorified trade fair” with almost as many lobbyists as delegates. The truth is that while big donations to political parties have been cleaned up and made more transparent, the same cannot be said for the money made at party conferences. These companies and lobbyists do not pay for passes or fund events out of the goodness of their hearts: they are buying access and the possibility of influence. In the conference, in the living standards debate, a delegate has just given a speech in her dressing gown. She was talking about a protest against the "bedroom tax" involving people wearing their dressing gowns. David Sparks, leader of the Labour group on the Local Government Association and leader of Dudley council, spoke in the conference hall earlier. He said it was only a matter of time before some councils in the UK went bust. I've served 37 years [in local government] ... I can honestly say that the situation that faces local government now has never been more precarious in all that time. Under this government and under this secretary of state, if things carry on as they are, a conservative estimate, with a small c, will show that local councils have had a reduction, on average, overall, over the last four years, of 42% in their grants. The financial black hole facing local authorities is widening each year at the astronomical rate of £2.1bn. It is only a matter of time in this country before some councils cease to be viable. And if anyone thinks that's impossible, just look across the Atlantic to Detroit and see what can happen when a large council runs out of money. One section of Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip confirms that “Gordon considered the Queen more important than [Rupert] Murdoch” – which is reassuring, sort of, writes Paul Owen. When a Treasury civil servant accidentally sent a “daft joke about the Chinese” to the entire Treasury press list, Brown was worried about “relations with the Chinese – not least Rupert Murdoch’s then-wife Wendi Deng” and felt “the Treasury should show no mercy”. But after McBride reminded him that this civil servant wrote Brown’s annual note on horse-racing for his audience with the Queen, Brown’s position shifted, “definitive proof that Gordon considered the Queen more important than Murdoch”. Murdoch gets one other mention: in the wake of the 7/7 attacks in London, “there were concerns at the highest level in the company – ie Uncle Rupert – about whether Gordon sufficiently grasped the seriousness of the security situation, and whether he could be trusted to maintain the strength of Blair’s relationship with the Bush administration”. Partly in response to this, Brown became “a whirlwind of activity” after the failed 21/7 attacks two weeks later, McBride writes. Damian McBride's publisher, Iain Dale, has been involved in a scuffle at the Labour conference. The Press Association has the full (and rather absurd) story. Verbal battles over Damian McBride's memoirs at the Labour conference escalated into actual fisticuffs today, as the ex-spin doctor's publisher stepped in to stop a protester disrupting the publicity drive for his controversial memoirs. Iain Dale, of Biteback Publishing, was involved in a scuffle with the man on the Brighton seafront in an effort to stop him appearing on screen behind McBride during a series of media interviews. The protester - a familiar face outside party conferences, where he regularly appears with banners opposing smoking or nuclear energy - managed to get himself into shot as McBride spoke to ITV1's Daybreak. But Dale decided to take action, grabbing the man's rucksack and physically hauling him out of the way of the cameras, and the pair grappled on the pavement as Mr McBride's interview continued. The barking of the protester's dog - which eagerly joined in the commotion - could be heard by TV viewers as the struggle continued. But the terrier - carrying placards reading "No Nukes" on its back - failed to live up to the loyalty expected from dogs, jumping up and biting its owner on the rear. The protester doggedly attempted to make the best of the situation, holding up his banner reading "No Nukes - Radio Active Dust Cancer Epidemic" to photographers who were busy recording the scrap. After a few moments, the pair separated and dusted themselves down, and the protester went back to trying to edge his way into view of the cameras. Writing on his blog, Dale - also a broadcaster on LBC 97.3 radio - joked: "I knew I shouldn't have had three Weetabix this morning." Dale has written more on his blog. Labour used to advocate a temporary cut in VAT as a means of stimulating the economy. But Ed Balls did not mention that in his speech yesterday, and at a fringe meeting last night he confirmed that the idea had been dropped. This wasn't a surprise. In a speech earlier this year he said that Labour proposed the idea when the economy was in recession and that, as the economy recovered, the balance of advantage would shift, and the case for other measures would grow stronger. The Lib Dems have claimed that this is "rank hypocrisy". This is from Stephen Williams, a Lib Dem Treasury spokesman. Labour have spent the last three years aggressively attacking the Coalition for raising VAT. Now they admit they won’t reduce it. This is rank hypocrisy from a party that has no answers to the big questions of the day – how to fix the economy, create jobs and help people with the cost of living. Paul Owen is reading Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he has found an anecdote about David Cameron that rather plays into his “chillaxing” image as a keen holidaymaker. McBride paints Cameron as rather callow after both Brown and Cameron had had to cancel their holidays because of the 2007 foot and mouth outbreak. During a phone call “Cameron asked Gordon almost pleadingly when he thought things would stabilise sufficiently so Gordon could go back to Dorset and Cameron could go back to Brittany. His exact words were: ‘I can’t go away until you do, and we won’t get a holiday at this rate. But you really have to go away first.’” Brown apparently responded to this call by telling McBride: “That was personal, that was private. You don’t say a word about that to anyone,” which was very saintly of him. Cameron betrayed this trust by giving a series of TV interviews criticising Brown based on information from the phone call, McBride claims. McBride says that from the moment Cameron emerged from the 2005 Tory leadership contest, he and Brown were worried. “If they [Cameron and George Osborne] were serious about modernising the Tory party; if they could succeed in neutralising the economy and public services as dividing line issues by agreeing with us on our tax and spending plans; and if – as was evident – they had the image and freshness to go with it, then they represented a real threat to Gordon’s chances of succeeding Tony, and then of winning any subsequent election.” Interestingly McBride says he rejected “the main Labour attack lines” of focusing on Cameron and Osborne’s poshness, preferring to try to paint them as “incompetent and weak”, and as “kids” who weren’t up to the job. That approach had a double value: they were “at the same time sending a not-so-subtle message to those on our own side who were giving David Miliband the glad eye”. (That approach eventually culminated in Brown’s “no time for a novice” jibe at both Cameron and Miliband.) But he praises Cameron for his response to the expenses scandal, saying the Tory leader “seemed remarkably well-prepared for the emergence of the story, and calm and decisive in his response”. Brown, by contrast, was “not only taken completely by surprise by the revelations, but also thrown into a total tailspin” by the Daily Telegraph’s decision to focus on him in its initial coverage. He attacks Cameron’s decision to block Brown as head of the IMF once he’d left Downing Street on interesting grounds, calling it not just partisan but “also tremendously short-sighted”. Why? Because “having Gordon run the IMF would have effectively neutralised the organisation’s influence on the 2015 election. Any criticism in the IMF’s reports on the UK economy could simply have been dismissed by Osborne with a ‘What do you expect?’ shrug.” The Federation of Small Businesses also likes the Labour business tax proposal. Here's the news release (pdf). And this is from John Allan, the federation's national chairman. The Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) welcomes the focus by the Labour leadership on this critical issue for small businesses. It affects thousands of our members across the UK and is an issue we've lobbied on for some time. Whilst we welcome today's announcement, the long-term solution is one that requires root and branch reform, including of the Valuation Office Agency. The system is clearly no longer fit for purpose. We know that many firms are struggling to pay business rates and FSB research suggests seven per cent of members pay more in rates than rent - and that cannot be right. In addition many small businesses are being forced by local authorities to pay the rates when it is beyond their means. But the Association of Convenience Stores likes Labour's business tax plans. This is from James Lowman, the ACS chief executive. Reducing business rates for smaller shops on high streets and in neighbourhoods would benefit tens of thousands of convenience stores. We will work with opposition and the Government to make proposals for tackling the rates burden workable. Immediate action is vital, but it must be accompanied by a commitment to a fundamental review of the business rates system. Paul Owen has got his copy of former Gordon Brown adviser Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he is busy speed-reading his way through it. Strangely the Daily Mail’s serialisation did not include McBride’s comments about that paper and its editor Paul Dacre. Indeed a Mail piece on Brown and the royal family misses out a few lines about McBride feeding a splash to the Mail about Brown wanting to fund a statue of the Queen Mother for the Mall. But disappointingly what McBride has to say about Dacre is quite tame. McBride compares Brown’s “very strong” relationship with Dacre with his relationship with Tony Blair – “bar all the shouting” – in that “because all their meetings took place behind closed doors … no one was ever quite sure how they interacted with each other in private, or how open, warm or personal they were with each other.” McBride says he never heard of any Mail journalist being instructed to “go soft” on Brown because of this relationship, and “Gordon never told me we that we were to put any particular scoops in the Mail’s direction”. He tells the story of Brown being invited into the Mail offices to tell its senior journalists about his ideas about Britishness, and McBride “couldn’t help but admire the way that Paul Dacre rocked back in his chair and let” his columnists attack Brown’s ideas, “as if thinking: ‘You want intellectual debate; here it is. You want to know what the Daily Mail thinks; we’re going to tell you.’” McBride does say Dacre “effectively scuppered one of my greatest plans”: a plan to cut the 30-year rule for releasing government papers to 15 years, and then rush out papers for all the years up to 1995 so that if Labour won the 2010 election promising to be “the only government ever to publish the secrets of our own time in office while still in power”. Brown asked Dacre to lead a review into the subject, but the eventual plan the Daily Mail chief came up with was slower and exempted the royal family, “which defeated some of the long-term news value”. And now the Institute of Directors has also criticised Labour's business tax announcement. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general. This is better politics than it is economics, and it creates a false distinction between small and larger businesses when in reality, all companies benefit from a more competitive environment. Whilst the IoD welcomes any commitment to reduce the burden of business rates, Labour’s plan to finance this by abolishing the proposed cut in the main corporation tax rate is not a price worth paying. The main corporation tax rate is paid not only by multinational corporations and FTSE100 companies but by medium sized companies and smaller firms. The government has spent three years telling the world that we are open for business, and reductions in corporation tax have been a key part of that strategy. It’s a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment in this way, at the same time as announcing a new bank levy. Globally, corporation tax rates are falling and it is imperative that the UK has an attractive ‘headline’ corporation tax rate low enough to attract much needed foreign direct investment into the UK. This will reduce unemployment, increase economic growth and, as a consequence, increase tax revenues. Labour is going to eliminate the debt by June 2016. That's the party's debt, not the country's. Diana Holland, the party treasurer, is presenting a report to the conference now, and she has just said the party is scheduled to pay off its debt within three years. Another business leader has criticised the business tax announcement in Ed Miliband's speech. This is from John Longworth, the director general of the British Chambers of Commerce. Labour must realise that you can't rob Peter to pay Paul. Business rates are an iniquitous tax and a drag on business – hitting companies of all sizes long before they make profits. Ed Miliband is right to look for ways to ease the business rates burden, as the BCC has urged the Treasury to do for years now. Yet we question why a freeze or cut in business rates for smaller firms should be offset by a delayed reduction in corporation tax. To create an environment where companies can thrive, both business rates and corporation tax rates have to be contained, and the broken business rates system fundamentally reformed. The notion that you can offset cuts in one tax with changes to another doesn't deal with the real problem – the fact that the business rates system is bust and in need of reform. Ultimately, companies of all sizes need to be clear on taxes and rates bills, so that they can generate jobs and wealth with certainty. Sir John Armitt, who chaired the Olympic Delivery Authority and who conducted a review of long-term infrastructure planning for Labour, told the Today programme that he would be disappointed if Labour cancelled HS2. I would be very disappointed if that was happening in this case. I think we’ve had political consensus around HS2. It’s a very large project. It requires a political consensus frankly to be delivered at the end of the day and I think the value of HS2 to the country long-term is still absolutely certain and I think is understood by senior politicians in all the major parties. However, he also said that he did not think that what Ed Balls said about HS2 in his speech yesterday indicated that Labour was planning to scrap the scheme. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. For the record, here are today's YouGov GB polling figures. Labour: 40% (up 3 points from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 32% (down 1) Ukip: 12% (no change) Lib Dems: 10% (down 1) Labour lead: 8 points (up 4) Government approval: -27 (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, these figures would give Labour a majority of 100. If the Labour party thought that announcing a tax cut for small business would win the approval of the business community, it's in for a disappointment. John Cridland, the director general of the CBI, has just been on the Today programme, and he said he was not impressed. He complained that the cut in business rates for small firms was being funded by an increase in corporation tax for large firms (or, more accurately, the reversal of a planned cut). This sent out the wrong message, he said. Some of the tax changes that we hear that Mr Miliband will propose are not particularly pro-business. Reducing business rates for small companies is a good thing to do, but I would not do it by increasing taxes on investment, on research, on job creation, which is what will happen if you put up corporation tax ... I just think it's divisive to take from one part of the business community to give to another. The key point, though, is what it says about the Labour party's pro-enterprise credentials. It is giving a signal that taxes are going up for some businesses. Actually, we are fighting for our share of global markets. We are getting back to the notion that we earn our corn around the world by getting on planes to far-flung countries to export. Whether you are small, medium or large, you need to invest as a business and grow as a business, and higher taxes don't do that. Overnight the Conservative business minister, Matthew Hancock, put out a response to the stories about what Ed Miliband would be saying in his speech. Here's his statement. This is yet more economic incompetence from the same Labour politicians who got us into a mess. Ed Miliband is calling for a tax rise on business that would cost jobs. And because he would reverse our planning reforms, the only way Ed Miliband could deliver his promise to build more houses is to spend more and borrow more. Tax rises on businesses and the same old Labour policy of more borrowing and more debt would undermine the recovery. And it's hardworking people who would pay the price with their jobs, higher taxes and higher mortgage rates. Harriet Harman, Labour's deputy leader, has been giving interviews this morning. Here are the key points. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome and the Press Association. • Harman said Labour had been making good progress since Ed Miliband became leader. If you look at the actual polls where people are voting, since 2010, since we lost the general election, actually the momentum with Ed Miliband’s leadership has been with the Labour party. We have won 1,950 new councils seat and that’s all around the country, and if you look at actual party membership, which has been on a long trajectory of falling across all the political parties, since Ed Miliband became leader we’ve increased our membership by 14%, whereas with David Cameron his party membership has fallen by 40%. • She said Labour was announcing plans to help small firms because they were the "engine of the economy". It's a bit of a cliche but it's absolutely true that small business is the engine of the economy. And they have really struggled after the austerity and the credit crisis and they haven't been able to borrow money from banks, and people haven't had enough money in their pockets to keep small businesses going. A lot of small businesses say they are paying as much in their business rates as they pay in rent. They have suffered really more than big businesses and one of the problems with the cost of living for businesses is that business rates have gone up. So what we're doing is, instead of giving a cut for big companies on corporation tax, we're using that money instead to take that money off the cost of small businesses rates. • She said that Damian McBride could not be believed because he was a self-confessed liar. The one person who is not worth believing because he’s admitted he’s a liar is Damian McBride, so I don’t think we need to be told by him who did or didn’t know what’s going on. According to Nick Robinson on the BBC, delegates at the Labour conference are going to be given a "One Nation Economy" book at the end of Ed Miliband's speech summarising the party's growth policies. This week (as the end of Ed Balls' speech yesterday showed) Labour is very keen refute the claim that it does not have any ideas. Leaders of modern political parties need a suite of skills - being able to communicate well on TV, leadership, team management, authority at PMQs etc - and being able to deliver a powerful, set-piece speech is probably no longer an essential part of the job requirement. Very few people actually watch these speeches in full. But a powerful speech can still shape the political debate for months, or even longer, and, for all the sniping about Ed Miliband's leadership, conference speech-making has been one of his strengths. He served his political apprenticeship under Gordon Brown (a great believer in the importance of politics by speech-making) and his last two Labour conferences speeches have been unusually good. Most party conference speeches are quickly forgotten, but Miliband's 2011 speech, which included an attack on "irresponsible" capitalism, generated a real debate, and last year's speech launched "one nation Labour", a branding exercise that lifted party morale. Miliband is speaking this afternoon. Can he pull off another biggie? We've had some briefing on the speech already. Here's the start of Patrick Wintour's story in today's Guardian. Ed Miliband will propose on Tuesday to reform Britain's dysfunctional housing market by building 200,000 new houses a year by 2020, the end of the next parliament. The Labour leader's move is a response to a collapse in UK housebuilding, which has stoked house-price inflation, and will see him set up a commission designed to curtail the planning logjams preventing houses being built in sufficient numbers. Miliband will use his set-piece address to the Labour conference in Brighton to announce that Sir Michael Lyons, the respected local government finance expert and former BBC Trust chairman, will lead a review designed to ensure that an incoming government he leads will be able to swiftly draw up housing reform legislation. The Labour leader also intends to use his speech to ask voters to recognise that Britain can be better – and to ask themselves in 2015 whether they are better off than five years ago, an echo of the famous question asked by the Republican Ronald Reagan in the 1990 presidential election. I'll be covering the speech in detail, and, of course, all the reaction to it. 9.30am: Conference opens. Conference arrangements committee report. 9.40am: Diana Holland presents the party treasurer's report. 10.15am: Debate on stronger, safer communities, and living standards and sustainability, with speeches from Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary and Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. 11.15am: Debate on Britain's global role. From 12.30pm: Fringe meetings, including Hilary Benn on Labour and the north, Arnie Graf, who is advising Labour on community organising, on winning votes, Maria Eagle and Ken Livingstone on transport, and Lord Glasman on Britishness. 2pm: Ed Miliband delivers his conference speech. I'll be covering all the news from the Labour conference, including looking at the papers and bringing you the best comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

results.webTitle: Politics Weekly podcast: coalition Britain - a great place to live?

fields.headline: Politics Weekly podcast: coalition Britain - a great place to live?
fields.trailText: Heather Stewart, Andrew Gimson and Toby Helm join Tom Clark to discuss an OECD report which ranked Britain as among the world's best places to live and work. Plus: Ed Miliband's living wage pledge; and Lynton Crosby gears up for a no-nonsense Tory election campaign
fields.bodyText: Ed Miliband this week set out his latest policy to combat what he calls a 'cost of living crisis' - tax rebates for companies that choose to pay workers the living wage. It was siezed upon by his critics as a meddlesome measure that could complicate the tax system and contribute to higher unemployment. Joining Tom Clark in the studio this week are the Observer's political editor Toby Helm, its economics editor Heather Stewart and the contributing editor of ConservativeHome Andrew Gimson. Also this week: a new report into wellbeing by the OECD found that the UK is among the best places in the world to live and work. Meanwhile, the Office for National Statistics predicted that Britain's population is set to grow by nearly 10 million by 2037. Plus: as the Conservatives hire the Australian election guru Lynton Crosby on a full-time contract worth £500,000 per year, we discuss how a no-nonsense approach to winning in 2015 will compare with 2010's "big society" campaign. Leave your thoughts below.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Wednesday 28 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Wednesday 28 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd says yes to Gonski - politics live blog

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd says yes to Gonski - politics live blog
fields.trailText: The new Prime Minister moves forward as the 43rd parliament completes its business
fields.bodyText: As the senators run for their planes and the departing ministers and staff back up their boxes downstairs, let's part ways for now. Today in politics: Kevin Rudd reconfirmed his support for the policy formerly known as the Gonski reforms and extended the deadline for the premiers to sign up by a fortnight. He promised his colleagues he would consult more, and things would be different than they were last time he was resident in The Lodge. Rudd set down some political markers on boat arrivals and reserved his right to change some of the Gillard Government's policies. He indicated his reshuffle ministry would be confirmed on Sunday or Monday. Tony Abbott gave his troops a pep talk and said he would not allow Kevin Rudd to whitewash his record. The senate adjourned - so as far as we know right at this moment, that's the 43rd parliament. Thanks for the strong support and engagement from readers for the live blog, particularly this week. You are gems, and I'm grateful. Be safe until we gather again for more conversation. If you want to look back over the day's business in the red place you can check the "red" here. There it is. The Senate stands adjourned. The 457 visa bill is now law. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor. Most Australians would expect that employers look local first before hiring workers from overseas, and these legislative changes will ensure that occurs where it’s not already happening. On the new requirements for employers to test the market before looking to foreign workers: Market testing will be straight forward, requiring employers only to place a job advertisement and show evidence they have done so. Employers facing genuine skill shortages already take steps to ensure there are no locals available to fill these positions. Labour market testing will also be extended to those cases where there was a redundancy or retrenchment and will have to be undertaken across all skill shortage areas, with the exception of nursing and engineering, and in other areas specified by the Minister. By ensuring employers provide proof that they are all looking locally first, they will be placed on a level-playing field, ensuring that one employer is not getting an unfair advantage over another. Couple of readers, couple of contrasting thoughts. Interested in thoughts from readers on Kevin Rudd's first press conference. My quick thoughts: There was an internal message - I've changed, it won't be like last time, I will restore Cabinet government, I won't go off half-cocked. This of course relates to Rudd's previous time as prime minister when he did not consult, and went off half cocked, as many of his colleagues have said. The was a message to Tony Abbott - I will try and disrupt your conversation with the voters. The konfrontasi comment concerning the Coalition's policy to tow back the boats upped the political ante. The barometer of that was Julie Bishop's rapid reponse, and demand for a retraction. Rudd was also (in effect) trying to slow down the media cycle, there was a specific plea for time. If you do need to consult your colleagues, to think through the ramifications of your public statements, then you do need to set some parameters in your conversation with journalists - like I won't be able to give you all every little detail in five minutes. (I'd say to the Prime Minster - good luck with that one.) My colleague Lenore Taylor has been thinking about the carbon price and Kevin Rudd's options. Tweaking the clean energy package is actually much harder to do than it seems (in terms of the practical and legal implications.) Then there's the political impact of the decision. History tells us that's not straightforward. At one level it seems simple - the policy isn't popular, so jettison the policy. Except of course Rudd has been down that road before, and it didn't end well for him. The Prime Minister was making it clear in his press conference before that he would be consulting his colleagues before making any changes. Here's an excerpt from Lenore: Dumping the carbon tax – the fixed price period of Australia's carbon pricing scheme – makes for a handy headline. But it would be much harder for a Rudd government to actually do. That's why the Gillard government didn't do it some time back when cabinet examined this option in detail. To start with, it would require legislation, which can't happen before the election since the parliament has risen and which the Greens leader, Senator Christine Milne – alive for an opportunity for pre-election product differentiation – was quick to say she would not support. That would leave a Rudd government, if re-elected, seeking support from the Coalition to change the carbon pricing legislation Tony Abbott has built his entire political persona on promising to repeal. Also, the current legislation requires that, before the shift from a fixed price to a floating price can occur as planned in July 2015, the new Climate Change Authority must set an annual cap on pollution based on its assessment of what Australia's emissions reduction target should be. The authority releases a draft report in October and a final report in February 2014 before announcing the first five years of annual caps in May. That means if a Rudd government really hurried, the earliest it could feasibly start a floating price under the current arrangements would be July 2014 – one year before the price is set to float anyway. Not happy Julie. I think it's fair to say that the konfrontasi that is Australian politics has just entered a new phase. Today - Kevin Rudd and Julie Bishop. Cold. Yesterday. The warmth. We'll take this as a comment from NSW Labor secretary Sam Dastyari. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop has trotted up to the Sky News studios to be outraged about konfrontasi. Bishop: A massive over reach on Kevin Rudd's part. He must retract this absurd and alarmist claim. This is a major gaffe. As The Australian's James Jeffrey points out, Rudd did not say I've got to zip. He just zipped. Rudd says he will not tolerate anyone trashing Julia Gillard’s reputation. He says he's been on the wrong side of reputation trashing, and it's not pleasant. I don't think the public like it either. He's zipped. Do none of you have daggy dads? Kevin Rudd, on his tendency to say things such as cooking with gas. Rudd consults his communications director and then the room before working out whether he'll take more questions. He resolves to go on for another five minutes. Rudd warns that Tony Abbott will plunge the country into recession if he goes ahead with a major austerity drive. Laura Tingle from the Financial Review asks whether any looming policy change will be revenue neutral? That has generally been my conservative approach, but I add the caveat, I have not yet been briefed by Treasury officals. Did you leak against Julia Gillard during the 2010 election? (Nope) What will you do on gay marriage? Rudd says his first preference is to convince Tony Abbott to allow his MPs a conscience vote. If that doesn't work, then he'll have to look at other mechanisms including the recourse to plebiscite or referendum. The Australians's David Crowe asks Rudd will he offer the premiers more money to sign on to the policy formerly known as Gonski. David I'm just a reasonable guy. But the parameters for variation are very very narrow. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop on konfrontasi. Have you really changed? Rudd is emphasising he will consult colleagues. (Over.) I will consult my colleagues. (Unless he has to act on the spot, then he won't. But mainly he will.) Will you save Trish Crossin - the Northern Territory senator who Julia Gillard moved on? Rudd says he hasn't considered any specific preselections. What is your attitude to women and the party? I have a good record on this, Rudd says. Konfrontasi Rudd is asked whether he's foreshadowing conflict with Indonesia. Serious conflict. Over Tony Abbott's plan to tow back the boats. He invokes konfrontasi. (Indonesia's armed opposition to the creation of Malaysia.) Rudd says he hopes it doesn't come to that. He's just pointing out that the Indonesians have said Abbott's tow back policy is not something they are interested in. I think of farms, I think of animals. Kevin Rudd is responding to a question from Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor about his new approach to regionalism. A picture from Mike Bowers crying out for a caption. Do feel free. I will be speaking with my Cabinet colleagues on that. This is adjusting the clean energy package. Rudd says the government needs to consider the budget implications of any change to the carbon price. He's asked does he accept the Budget parameters and the proposed welfare cut for single Mums. I'll get back to you is his answer essentially. Then party reform? Do you support it? I'll get back to you. But: I am revolted by what happened in New South Wales. Rudd says he'll be having a deep discussion with Cabinet colleagues about future directions for NSW. Now for questions. Boats. Are you going to Indonesia? Will you stop the boats? We in this country support orderly migration, Rudd says. He says he's going to be briefed this afternoon about the Indonesia trip, and will speak to the Indonesian President after this press conference. He says he'll announce his decision about whether to go or not once he's made it. Rudd says if he goes, he will seek a broader discussion than the one envisaged by Julia Gillard. Now he'd like to talk about Gonski. He knows David Gonski, in fact he's the chairman of the board of his wife's company. (Full disclosure Rudd says - before you people all go off and write your exposes.) Rudd suggests David Gonski doesn't want the Gonski reforms to be called the Gonski reforms anymore. Rudd's announcement appears to be that he supports the Gonski policy (or perhaps the policy formerly known as Gonski). Under my prime ministership we will support the national improvement plan for Australian schools. Under my prime ministership this policy direction will continue. Rudd is extending the deadline for the states to sign on by two weeks. Then more on the new politics. I think it's time we all came together. Rudd says he wants to make policy changes and Cabinet will consider those in due course. He says his feedback from voters was people were thinking about voting for Tony Abbott because they felt they lacked a clear choice. Rudd says his offering them a choice. He's speaking now about decision-making. Rudd says one thing he's learned from his previous stint as prime minister is colleagues need to be consulted. He says he will consult colleagues, so the media needs to calm down. Policy announcements will come, but they may not come quickly. Just chill for a while, in fact chill for quite a bit. Rudd says he'd like to talk about why he sought the leadership. The government was on track for a catastrophic defeat at the upcoming elections. He says there would have been a massive majority for Tony Abbott in the House and a majority in the senate. If that happened, Rudd contends, Labor's policy legacy would have disappeared. Some of these reforms, good reforms, have been Julia's. Some of the reforms date back to the period where I was prime minister after 2007. The first shock of Kevin Rudd's press conference is he's on time. Rudd says discussions about his new ministry remain ongoing. The new ministry will be sworn in on Monday. He says he's getting briefings from officials. We've had a few things on. Nine has posted some video of an interview Tim Mathieson did this morning outside The Lodge while out walking the dog. How is the Prime Minister, Nine cameraman Mark Jessop asked the former first bloke? She's really good, a little sad. We are all sad. Mathieson noted it had been an amazing few years for me. Guardian Australia's data guru Nick Evershed has been crunching the numbers, as he does, and has produced one measure of effectiveness in government. I'll let him explain: How do we measure the effectiveness of a government? There are polls, both of opinion and at the ballot box, but these don't really offer us any measure of effectiveness. You can look at the economy and measure the health of the populace - and these are both good indicators - but are not wholly under the influence of the government of the day. One way might be to look at the ability of a government to pass legislation. Admittedly this is a quantity over quality approach, but it does offer us a quantitative measure of a government, political party or prime minister. Someone that gets a lot of legislation passed might be considered to be good at getting things done. I took all of the Commonwealth of Australia Numbered Acts and assigned them to a prime minister, political party, and parliament based on the date of assent of the act. This isn't entirely exact, as some legislation may be introduced under one PM and passed under another, though I believe it is a good proxy. From this dataset, I counted the total acts for each PM, party, and parliament. Then, I determined the number of days in office for each PM, and the number of days each parliament and party governed. Using these figures you can calculate a rate of acts per day, which accounts for different lengths of prime ministers' or governments' terms. The results? Julia Gillard had the highest rate of passing legislation with a rate of 0.495, followed by Bob Hawke at 0.491: If you want to read Nick's blog in full, have a look here. Childcare Minister Kate Ellis. Resolute in her voting intention. The ABC's Annabel Crabb has written a column today on Julia Gillard and gender - was it a factor in her undoing - and in terms of progress, what does this all mean. Here's a taste: To attribute the demise of Julia Gillard to sexism does exactly the reverse of honouring her role as the first female prime minister of Australia. To disallow the possibility that she might have lost her job because a majority of the people who work most closely with her just didn't think she was good enough at it is to consign Julia Gillard permanently to an air-locked specimen-jar in the Ponds Institute For Gender Research. It's unhelpful. To her and to us. Julia Gillard was our first female prime minister. She was also a prime minister who lost her job. In the field of former prime ministers, that doesn't make her unique. Hell, on recent form it doesn't even make her unusual. I've just asked readers on Twitter to give me some suggestions about Kevin Rudd's fantasy ministry. Karen Lyon - quick as a flash. The path of regime change - it never runs smooth. We know why Kevin Rudd wants to talk about his future, because he can't talk about his record. Abbott riffs a bit more on leadership instability. Campaign pitch for the coda - vote one Coalition. The colleagues spring to their feet with appluase. Note to readers while we continue listening to Abbott. The guidance on Rudd's 12.30pm press conference has now shifted slightly - it may be a policy announcement rather than the ministry. In any case, Kevin Rudd will address the media at 12.30pm. Now, back to Abbott. I'm not being negative, I'm being factual when I say Kevin Rudd is the best friend people smugglers have ever had. And a newsflash. Newsflash Kevin. It is not all about you. I want to see cranes over our cities. I want to see bulldozers on the ground. Tony Abbott: solidly pro-bulldozer. Abbott pays tribute to his communications spokesman Malcolm Turnbull - a man who practically invented the internet in Australia. Abbott says the election is a contest of ideas and values. It's Labor's big government versus the Coalition, which is in favour of strong citizens. Opposition leader Tony Abbott is taking his turn now at the lectern. For the second time in three years, Labor has torn down a leader. An extraodinarily grave rent in the fabric of our national life. The Australian people are seeking certainty and stability, Abbott says, and the only place they will find it is with the Coalition. Julie Bishop on Kevin Rudd. Leopards do not change their spots. Only a Coalition can deliver a grown up government. we demonstrate our capacity to work cooperatively together. Truss is throwing now to the deputy Liberal Leader Julie Bishop. There's a civil war going on inside Labor and it will not end, there is no end in sight. Will Kevin Rudd be the second Labor leader to say there will be no carbon tax under the government I lead? Warren Truss, in continuation. Hurling the zingers. Well .. another campaign. Nationals leader Warren Truss is now addressing a special Coalition party room meeting in Canberra today. Yes sir. Another campaign. That much is certain. Meanwhile .. more of the law making. The red place is just belting through today. Interesting thought isn't it? If Kevin Rudd does dismantle elements of the clean energy package - it could boost the Greens politically with progressive voters. It would give the Greens some sharpness around product differentiation between themselves and Labor. It's now official. Kevin Rudd will announce his ministry at 12.30pm. Greens leader Christine Milne is looking very chirpy this morning. Milne is listing the Greens' achievements in this minority parliament. She says she hopes voters understand how the Greens cooperation with the Gillard Labor government delivered all sorts of things - the clean energy package, Gonski, the Parliamentary Budget Office. Of course there is a but. I really warn the Labor Party against going down the track of creating uncertainty around carbon pricing. We have an exemplary package in the clean energy package. Milne is referring to Rudd's billboard sized hints that he's moving to a floating carbon price rather than the fixed price. She points out that Labor's record on raising revenue is rather poor. If you move to flexible pricing earlier there would be a huge hit on the budget. A political strategy from Rudd won't save the planet, she says, clearly derisive about Rudd's big pitch to the big polluters over the past 24 hours. Our later start today - yes I did need one more coffee, it's true - means I haven't done my normal summary of the political news. I'm assuming readers are up to speed by now, more or less, on Rudd's first day back in the big chair. But there are some terrific pieces being written about the meaning of recent events. One I'd particularly recommend is one by one of my favourite columnists, Shaun Carney. Here's an excerpt of his essay for The Conversation, the political tragedy of Julia Gillard. The truth is that the Australian Labor Party nationally has in the past three years experienced its most rancorous divisions since the split of the 1950s. Unlike the period of the split, which occurred in opposition and guaranteed many more years of it, the party has endured these divisions while holding office, and the enmities have, for the most part, grown from ego rather than ideology. If the events of the past few days are to have any meaning, they need to be seen in the context of what has happened to the Government since early 2010. First things first: Julia Gillard’s downfall as prime minister is one of the greatest personal tragedies in Australian politics. It is a tragedy because Gillard’s ambition ultimately exceeded her political talent, and to the very end she would not see it.
