Her speech delivered after her caucus defeat on Wednesday night attributed her removal as leader to only two causes: a loss of fortitude among past supporters who buckled under external pressure and sexism directed at her as the nation’s first female prime minister. Good morning and welcome to the final day of the 43rd parliament. The House of Representatives has already folded its tent and gone home. The Senate grinds on with the last legislative business. I think it's more than likely we'll see the new Prime Minister Kevin Rudd announce his ministry later on today, but there's no official confirmation of that just yet. There's also broad hints that Rudd will seek to adjust Julia Gillard's clean energy package over the coming days.

results.webTitle: Iranians preoccupied with politics, UN survey shows

fields.headline: Iranians preoccupied with politics, United Nations survey shows
fields.trailText: Preliminary results suggest an emphasis on 'honest government', 'political freedoms' and 'gender equality'
fields.bodyText: In 2015, the UN's Millennium Development Goals, the organisation's principal framework for eliminating poverty worldwide, will draw to a close. In the unprecedented global survey to determine what follows, MY World, citizens from all countries are being asked to vote directly – online, by SMS, and by paper ballot – for their priorities in a new development agenda. Both across the world and in the Middle East, socioeconomic concerns predominate. Iranians, however, are stressing political concerns to a greater degree than elsewhere. And moves by the new government suggest this is well understood. Respondents to the survey select six of 16 given development priorities to define a new agenda. This week at the UN general assembly, the survey hosts an event to celebrate its millionth voter – as of this writing, 1.15 million people from 194 countries have participated. Results can be viewed in real time on a dedicated website, with priorities ranked top to bottom in order of popularity. Data can be disaggregated by sex, age, education, country, region, and UN Human Development Index (HDI) cohort. The priorities range over four broad categories. Seven of the 16 options concern socioeconomic management, including issues such as access to "education", "sanitation" and "personal safety". Three priorities, "transportation", "reliable energy", and "phone/Internet access", deal with infrastructure management. The third category involves environmental management: "protecting the environment", and "dealing with climate change". Questions of political management are represented by four priorities: "honest government", "freedom from discrimination", "gender equality", and "political freedoms". As results stand right now, socioeconomic concerns occupy five out of the top six priorities around the world and in most regions and countries. In Iran, however, socioeconomic concerns account for only four of the top six priorities, with the other two both being issues of political management. Take-up in Iran as of 22 September is relatively small, at 4,201 out of 27,877 for all of western Asia. Sample selection is inevitably an issue, as respondents often come from civil society organisations already working with UN agencies. But the sample continues to grow. The survey is available in English online, and it has also been translated into Farsi for distribution in paper ballot form. Translated ballots account for over half of the Iranian total so far. The majority of these were part of a global drive by UNAIDS, the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/Aids, to promote the survey, while encouraging participants to identify HIV/Aids as an additional suggested priority in the ballot's "write-in" field. Only 36% of Iranian respondents have more than a secondary education, so this is not simply an exercise for a liberal elite. Men dominate but not dramatically, with about three men responding for every two women. More than half the sample is under 30, but then so is more than half the country. Honest government is a top-six concern across all age groups. "Honest government" – a global favourite, ranking fourth internationally – is the second-most popular response in Iran, and a top-six concern across all age groups. As elsewhere, "healthcare", "education", and "jobs" also feature in the top six. But then so does "gender equality", which ranks only ninth internationally. And this is not a purely female-driven result: while 40% of women voted it a concern, so have 36% of men. At the beginning of September, with a little less than 2,000 votes cast in Iran, the focus on political concerns was even greater, with "honest government" and "political freedoms" as the two top responses. Now, with more than 4,000 votes cast, "political freedoms" has dropped out of the top six. The role of UNAIDS, in collecting about half of the total vote through outreach to individuals more dependent on social provision measures, may go some way to explaining the shift toward social management issues. Both in the survey's defined western Asia region and among Iran's "high" HDI cohort, four priorities continue to dominate: "education", "honest government", "jobs", and "health". Only one issue of political management features in the top six, for both groupings. In high-HDI countries overall, the only other issue of political management in even the top ten is "freedom from discrimination". What about country-level comparisons? Considering countries with broadly similar characteristics – comparable per capita wealth, population size, location, and economic make-up – Iraq, though confronted with its own particular issues, probably comes closest. Turkey is an interesting case: besides obvious political differences, it is something like an Iran without the petroleum. Finally, a broadly similar country from outside the region is Venezuela, oil exporting and of comparable wealth. In all three countries, only one political management issue features in the top six priorities, that of "honest government". In both Iraq and Turkey, the other three political issues all line up just behind the top six – in other words, political questions remain pertinent, just not predominant. In Venezuela, issues of "discrimination" and "political freedom" rank only ninth and tenth. Finally, to look at how the other half lives, we can select three considerably richer countries: the United Arab Emirates, the United Kingdom, and the United States. The UAE has the big four – "education", "jobs", "health", and "honest government" – and the regional concern with personal safety. Also paralleling Iraq and Turkey, political questions are just outside the top six: "discrimination" and "gender equality" are ranked eighth and ninth respectively. In all these comparisons so far, only the US and UK have two political management issues in the top six. A clue to understanding this comes from the other priorities chosen. All three countries list "water" and in the US and UK, "food" as well. These are not countries known for food-security problems. Rather what appears to happen is that in richer countries the survey becomes a hypothetical: "What would I most need were I to wake up in poverty?" But even hypothetically, none of these countries are as concerned with political issues as Iranian respondents are. In addition to questions about the statistical validity of such a small sample, there are questions regarding the priorities themselves. Freedom from discrimination is a broad theme that is open to interpretation. Social norms are also important. Though women in Saudi have fewer rights than elsewhere, gender equality is not a top-six concern even among women there. Another standout result is "support for people who can't work", at number three in Iran, whereas it is only 11th worldwide and 14th among high-HDI nations generally. Regionally, it is just outside the top six in Western Asia; at number six in Iraq, it is well down at 13th in Turkey. How to interpret Iranians' focus on this concern is not self-evident. The Islamic republic prides itself on the way it takes care of injured veterans: is that fuelling unusually high expectations, or does the vote in fact indicate a serious shortcoming? But overall, according to preliminary comparative results, Iranian respondents are thinking politically. Even taking into account the Arab uprisings and recent Turkish unrest, political issues do not emerge in these places in the same way as in Iran. And there is evidence that the administration of President Hassan Rouhani, now in his second month in office, recognises these concerns and is responding to them. The release last week of 11 political prisoners made headlines around the world. While this was seen by many as a signal to the international community, its domestic importance is arguably greater. Related efforts are under way to relax the conditions of house arrest imposed on Green Movement leaders Mir Hossein Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi. And the president has publicly stated that the (typically hardline) Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps should steer clear of domestic politics. Socioeconomic factors remain significant nonetheless. Iranians are still concerned about good education, quality healthcare, and decent jobs. For all the focus on the ideological shifts represented by Rouhani and his predecessors Mohammad Khatami and Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, their electoral victories rested on responding to socioeconomic concerns as well. This is what Rouhani intends to do in New York, by opening the path to a resolution of the nuclear and, by extension, sanctions issues. While he has a lot of open fronts to deal with, he is at least demonstrating a clear awareness of them.

tags.description: The latest news and comment on Hassan Rouhani

results.webTitle: Clegg accuses Boris Johnson of 'fairly unpleasant elitism': Politics live blog

fields.headline: Clegg accuses Boris Johnson of 'fairly unpleasant elitism': Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Nick Clegg hosting his Call Clegg LBC phone-in and reaction to the U-turn on plain packaging for cigarettes
fields.bodyText: • Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, has described Boris Johnson's speech defending elitism as representing "the worst of Thatcherism". (See 3.34pm.) Labour MPs also continued to denounce it. • The Fresh Start Project, a group of Tory MPs, has published a seven-page "Mandate for Reform" setting out the changes to the EU that it wants David Cameron to achieve. There are 22 measures on the list. They include member states having control of social and employment law, and the UK having a complete opt out from policing and criminal justice measures. There is a link to the report here. • William Hague, the foreign secretary, has said that a global summit on ending the use of sexual violence in conflict zones will be held in Britain next year. (See 2.26pm.) • Andrew Sells, a venture capitalist and major donor to the Conservative party has been chosen by ministers as the new chair of the statutory wildlife and nature watchdog, Natural England. • Stuart Agnew, a Ukip MEP, has said women often lack the ambition to achieve senior positions because their desire for a baby "gets in the way". As the Press Association reports, Agnew dismissed the impact of gender bias in education and the workplace, insisting the mothering instinct was what really held women back in their careers. If you look at the people who get degrees, more women get them and they are getting the jobs in the workplace, but for various reasons they don't have the ambition to go right to the top because something gets in the way. It's called a baby. I've never had a baby. But I understand if you do have a baby it can change your life - it changes your ambitions. So, the route is there. Those females who really want to get to the top do so. A Ukip spokeswoman said this was not the view of the party as a whole. • Lord Sugar, the Labour peer and star of the BBC's The Apprentice, has said that there are too many "jobsworths" at the corporation. As the Press Association reports, he said in a debate in the Lords that the BBC was "heavily overstaffed" and needed a more commercial approach to its work. "The BBC Trust is a complete and utter waste of time," he told the Lords. "It should be disbanded." And some management posts at the corporation should go. They are heavily overstaffed and there are too many jobsworths - and the organisation is not run in a manner that a commercial organisation should be. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. I won't be blogging tomorrow - we're put up a readers' edition, as usual - but I will be writing a live blog on Sunday morning, covering George Osborne's interview on the Andrew Marr show and all the other news relating to next week's autumn statement. Here's an afternoon reading list. • Andrew Gimson at ConservativeHome says a quarter of the female Tory MPs elected in 2010 could stand down in 2015. Why are so many Tory women finding life at Westminster so unpleasant that they wish to give it up after only one term? In 2010, the number of Conservative women MPs rose from 17 to 49, yet it is possible that over a quarter of the newcomers in this group will seize the first chance to leave. This will be seen as a rebuff to David Cameron, who at the start of his leadership made the recruitment of more women in winnable seats one of his most conspicuous goals. But it will also be a rebuff to Parliament, and perhaps also to the wider public for expecting so much of MPs, including a manic level of attention to events in the constituencies ... Women MPs get a particular kind of unpleasantness directed at them via social media by bullying men who indulge with vicious relish in every kind of obscenity. One woman told me that in her previous, high-level career, she at least felt when she went home she could switch off. She now finds she can never switch off. Nor does she feel that at Westminster she is able to make a meaningful contribution to policy. This is of course a sensation that can afflict male MPs too. But some at least of the women feel that real power is still in the hands of a chumocracy, and that they are excluded from the rooms where the meaningful discussions are taking place. They arrive at Westminster with knowledge of particular fields: knowledge to which no value whatever seems to be attached ... In the intractable debate about MPs’ pay, it seems to me a great pity that so little consideration is given to the idea of having part-time MPs. These could be paid less, in return for passing fewer laws and spending less time on local events and case work which ought to be the preserve of local councillors. It would be understood that part-time MPs might have other careers in order to make ends meet. But it would also be understood that they ought not to be working so hard that they never have time to think. • Daniel Hannan profiles Syed Kamall, the new leader of Conservative MEPs. Syed Kamall MEP is the last person Labour can have wanted to lead the Conservatives into the Euro-election. He ticks pretty much every box: he’s a souverainiste, a tax-cutter and a Thatcherite, but his main interest is in the relief of poverty. He doesn’t just talk about it, either: he has spent years working with charities and entrepreneurs in developing countries and in deprived parts of the UK. That first-hand-experience has convinced him that the way to tackle penury is not through subsidy, but through trade, property rights and education. • Cathy Newman at Channel 4 News says Sajid Javid could be the next Tory leader. • Rafael Behr at the New Statesman says the Conservatives deserve better than David Cameron. The Conservatives are marooned between being the party their leader once wanted them to be and the one they suppose they might have been without him. So Downing Street has settled for unification around a goal all can agree on – trashing Labour. With the help of Lynton Crosby, Cameron’s campaign strategist, the Tories are turning every debate into a warning against the perils of electing Miliband. If there is a problem with the NHS, it is the legacy of Labour negligence. Economic malaise? Labour spent all the money. Immigration? Labour opened the floodgates. The chairman of the Co-operative Bank is alleged to have taken hard drugs? Labour took money from the Co-op so, er, well, never mind the details, there’s something a bit whiffy over there on the left. Hold your nose and turn right ... The Tories would still be unwise to change leader before the election. (Only a tiny cabal of hardline Eurosceptics even consider this an option.) They could still win or, more likely, stay in power in another coalition with the Lib Dems. The economy is growing. Miliband looks ill-prepared for the total war that is coming his way. To be back in No 10 after the next election would be victory enough for Cameron. But to the outside observer, to anyone who is interested in Conservatism as a political creed, to anyone who values debate or who recognises that there are principled MPs on the Tory benches, the party looks diminished by its leader. It is being whittled into a crude instrument for the sole purpose of bludgeoning the opposition. You don’t have to be a Tory to believe it should be more than that. • James Maxwell at Total Politics says the future of the Scottish Labour party looks bleak. Scotland’s political landscape has changed radically over the last decade but Scottish Labour has remained practically inert. Its failure to adapt has come at a high price. Regardless of the outcome of the independence referendum, the SNP is likely to win a third term in office, meaning Labour faces another eight years in total on the opposition benches. Lamont’s role, then, is essentially that of a caretaker-leader. Her job is to stabilise the party for her successor, who – it is hoped – will take charge just as Scottish voters begin to tire of the nationalists. The problem, of course, is that waiting for your opponents to run out of steam does not amount to a strategy for renewal. Scottish Labour’s crisis is deeply-rooted. The party needs to be re-built from the bottom-up. One frustrating irony for Labour is that the devolved parliament it fought so hard to establish has systematically undermined its political dominance. At this rate, it will be a long time before that dominance is recovered, if it is recovered at all. • Dan Hodges at the Telegraph says PMQs should be scrapped. Perhaps it was the sight of Michael Fabricant sitting on the back benches wearing a giant fake moustache. Or the Prime Minister’s joke about Ed Miliband “loving Engels instead”, a reference to Miliband’s Desert Island discs choice of Robbie Williams song “Angels”, which contains the line “I’m loving angels instead”. Or the fact Labour’s leader didn’t have the wit or wisdom to inflict the mercy killing the pun so richly deserved, and instead spent his own time at the Dispatch Box rambling aimlessly from one issue to another. Anyway, whatever it was, as I sat there delivering my instant Twitter verdict – “Appalling joke aside, Cameron skated through this one. Ed's PMQ's strategy seems to be to meander from one issue to another. Weird” – I suddenly thought “what is the point?” Not just “what is the point of me sending this tweet?” which would have been a legitimate thought in itself, but: “what is the point of PMQs?” • Dominic Casciani at the BBC says there is very little evidence that "benefit tourism" attracts migrants to the UK. But what’s the evidence for benefit tourism? The answer is that there is very little - and it is an extremely complex picture. That does not mean that benefit tourism doesn’t exist - but what’s clear is that the evidence points strongly in the direction that people migrate to find work or for family reasons. They are less likely to up sticks to cross borders - or even continents - just for a weekly giro. And here is some more political reaction to the Boris Johnson speech. From Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president From Labour's Jon Trickett From Labour's Kevan Jones Here are some more blogs on the Boris Johnson speech. Chris Dillow's is particularly good. • Chris Dillow on his blog says Johnson's view of inequality is too simplistic. - The urge to keep up with the Joneses doesn't just spur useful work. It might also encourage people to get into debt to spend as much as the rich, and this can (perhaps) lead to over-gearing and a financial crisis. The experience of the 00s might not be the only data-point here. Rising inequality in the 1920s also ended in depression. - Inequality can reduce trust, and distrust can reduce growth. - Inequality might be a symptom of a dysfunctional economic and political system. If we have monopoly or other market failure and/or crony capitalism, we'll see some mega-rich people, but not a thriving economy. - Inequality might itself create dysfunctional politics. If the poor push for redistributive policies which weaken investment incentives, or if the rich use their wealth to buy political favours, growth will suffer. Now, I don't say this to claim that inequality is always and everywhere bad for growth. I do so merely to suggest that Mr J was expressing a very partial point of view, and was simplifying horribly. It's a good job he is merely a minor local politician who isn't in charge of anywhere important. • James Bloodworth at the Spectator says Johnson was right to say that equality is not achievable. Most of the speech (apart from the idea that ‘greed is good’, which I don’t accept) was a straightforward assertion of the blindingly obvious. Some people are cleverer and work harder than others. As a result they do better for themselves. Ability is a result of nature as well as nurture. Many are held back by their circumstances. People who work hard should be rewarded for their hard work. Fairly banal stuff, really. Personally I tend to err a bit more on the side of circumstance than ‘talent’ when trying to explain why some get so much more out of life than others; I’m baffled, however, by the near universal pretence that ‘economic equality’ is in any way an achievable goal. It isn’t. And for all his faults Boris was right to say it isn’t. • John McDermott at the Financial Times says Johnson came "empirically adrift on a sea of his own loquacity". It is not only the claim that Thatcher took on Neil Kinnock in 1983, or some odd statistics about IQ, or the idea that social mobility is declining, or that America is a particularly socially mobile society, or that his claim that the top 0.1 per cent of people pay 14 per cent of “all taxation” refers only to income tax, or the assertion that new overseas-backed developments constitute “hundreds of thousands of new homes” when they make up at best a few tens of thousands. Rather, in a peroration imagining what Thatcher would do today, it is Mr Johnson’s chutzpah to laud grammar schools’ supposed influence on social mobility without mentioning the current renaissance of London schools. (As mayor, Mr Johnson has little control over education but if that were a criterion for his interventions we would all be poorer.) Leaving aside whether grammar schools were indeed catapults of justice, what is happening now in London is a far more relevant and interesting trend. In the Commons earlier William Hague, the foreign secretary, announced that Britain will host a global summit on ending sexual violence in conflict next year. He described it as a "moral cause for our generation", on a par with the abolition of slavery two hundred years ago. It was only when the true horror of slavery came to light in the 18th century that our nation acted against it. In our time we have come to understand the true horror of warzone sexual violence in Bosnia, Rwanda, Colombia, Somalia and many other nations, including Syria. I will never forget meeting young women in a hospital in Goma so damaged by rape that they required surgery; or the woman in a refugee camp there who said they were being “raped like animals”; or male survivors in Sarajevo, who twenty years on still live lives shattered by trauma; or meeting women in refugee camps in Darfur who were raped collecting firewood. What they all had in common was that, unjustly, they bore the stigma and shame and loneliness, while their attackers walked free and unpunished. This is rape used as a tactic or weapon of war; to terrorise, to humiliate and to ethnically cleanse. It destroys lives, fuels conflict, creates refugees, and is often a tragic link in a chain of human rights abuses – from sexual slavery, to forced marriage and human trafficking. Sexual violence affects men and boys as well as women and girls. It undermines reconciliation, and traps survivors in conflict, poverty and insecurity. Preventing it is a moral cause for our generation. David Lammy, the Labour MP and potential candidate for London mayor, told the World at One that Boris Johnson's comments in his speech yesterday were "an insult" to the poor in the city. I don’t think that’s just careless. I think it’s an insult; it’s an insult to cleaners in London, to people who are home carers in London, people who are minimum wage, giving them the suggestion that they are sort of bottom of the cornflake packet. That’s not the sort of society that I thought we wanted to live in ... There is nothing wrong with aspiration, with enterprise and indeed with competition, but not when it’s at the expense of other people and that’s what we saw from the banking crisis. It’s extraordinary for a mayor, that should be for all of London, to think it’s alright to glorify greed, a greed that’s brought a banking collapse and caused misery and hardship to many Londoners, particularly to young people who can’t get on the housing ladder. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. • Boris Johnson has been strongly attacked for a speech he gave last night in which he argued that inequality was essential to fostering "the spirit of envy" and hailed greed as a "valuable spur to economic activity". He also suggested that IQ was a measure of worth. (See 9.51am.) Nick Clegg accused him of "unpleasant, careless elitism". And Labour said Johnson's comments were "absolutely disgraceful". (See 10.42am.) • The Office for National Statistics has published figures showing that net migration into the UK has risen year on year for the first time in two years. • Jane Ellison, the public health minister, has said that a review into plain packaging for cigarette advertising - described as a U-turn by Labour - will take the politics out of the issue. • Ministers believe they have found a way of cutting energy bills by as much as £50 in next week's autumn statement by putting one of the green levies onto general taxation and allowing energy companies to take longer to fulfil other energy efficiency duties, it has emerged. • The Bank of England has taken the first steps to ensure a housing bubble does not emerge by scaling back the Funding for Lending Scheme (FLS) to focus on business loans instead of mortgages.There are more details on Robert Peston's blog, and here, in a letter from George Osborne to the Bank of England. • Mark Carney, the governor of the Bank of England, has ducked an opportunity to criticise the SNP's plans for Scottish independence. At a news conference he said the Bank would present its "technocratic, boring" opinion on a range of issues related to Scottish independence at an appropriate time and venue in future. He also declined to say whether he was worried about becoming the lender of last resort to Scottish banks he did not regulate. • The National Audit Office has said that the government is not doing enough to get student loans repaid and that the total value of money owed continues to rise. And here's my colleague Michael White's take on the speech. Here's an extract. What Mayor Boris was trying to tell Big Cornflakes like himself was that London needs them in a globalised economy like the one now being created, but it needs them to behave better. On current evidence – subterranean basements the size of cathedrals being hewn beneath the capital's bigger homes and tax avoided on a massive scale by some corporations and business – the mayor is uttering a forlorn hope. That is what makes his populist appeal – it included a swing at east European migrants and a plug for his doomed, anachronistic estuary airport project – so badly judged. Those lucky enough to belong to the 2% elite - as judged by imperfect IQ tests, of course – are sensible when they lead lives of quiet discretion and taste, engaging in good works too, rather than flaunting their wealth and good fortune. Many did not earn it and many are not actually so clever; they have been lucky in their actions or their birth. The sensible ones - there are many, but they are out of fashion from here to New York and on to Beijing – know this. Successful idiots think it's just because they're so smart. Saps. The rest of us are unimpressed and may resent the mayor's avowedly elitist world view because – as Warren Buffett will confirm – it's not true. Buffett once said he'd given his children enough money to "allow them to do anything, but not to do nothing" with their lives. There speaks real wisdom. Here's the Guardian video with an excerpt from Boris Johnson's speech. Here's some more Twitter comment on Boris Johnson. From George Galloway, the Respect MP (and potential candidate for London mayor) From Ann Widdecombe, the former Conservative minister From Billy Bragg, the singer and activist From the financial journalist Paul Lewis From the Guardian's George Monbiot From the Labour MP Karen Buck From Jonathan Portes, director of the National Institute for Economic and Social Research From Richard Murphy, the tax reform campaigner From Pat Kane, the Scottish musician, journalist and pro-independence campaigner From Sonia Purnell, Johnson's biographer You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are three articles I found particularly interesting. • Laura Pitel in the Times (paywall) says the Labour councillor who exposed claims of vote-rigging in Falkirk has been eliminated from the race to become the town’s next MP. Linda Gow was told by party officials and members of the national executive that she had been knocked out of the race after an interview on Tuesday. Friends said that she had been barred from running purely because of her involvement in unearthing the scandal in Falkirk. “What does this say about the Labour Party’s attitude to whistleblowers?” said one. “She didn’t do anything wrong. She just made a complaint. We’ve got a local candidate who is being denied the right to go before local members and make her case.” • Andrew Grice in the Independent says Joseph Rowntree Foundation research has shown that spending cuts have hit the poorest parts of England and Scotland much harder than more affluent areas. The Joseph Rowntree Foundation (JRF), which analysed the 30 per cent real terms cut in local government spending between 2008 and 2015, said the North and Midlands are suffering more than the south, with deprived areas left about £100 per person worse off. Researchers warned that cash-strapped councils will end up serving only the neediest fraction of the population. "As a result, the willingness of those who do not rely on council services for the majority of their needs to pay for council services relied on almost exclusively by the poor could be undermined," the report added. • David Aaronovitch in the Times (paywall) says he does not like the tone of the current debate on immigration. If you examine the now extensive polling and surveying of British attitudes towards immigration, one thing that leaps out to the observer is that, indeed, the public don’t like it. Actually they never have. Although when the figures are interrogated some interesting nuances appear, it is true to say that a clear majority has always said it was for reducing immigration. And it is also the case that a substantial section of the public’s beliefs about immigration are plain wrong. People think most immigrants are asylum seekers but very few are. People think nearly a third of the country is composed of immigrants, in fact it’s 13-14 per cent (allowing for illegal immigrants). People object least to foreign students (the largest number) and believe that all immigrants stay permanently in this country (they most certainly don’t). They think immigrants create unemployment or “take jobs” (they don’t), depress wages (which on average they don’t), disproportionately claim benefits, commit more crime and depress standards in schools (none of which are true). In fact in many of these situations the reverse is the case ... I don’t like the tone or the lack of reality of the current discussion. But I don’t actually object to benefit restrictions for new migrants, because unlike many of my compatriots I know the evidence shows that migrants come here to work and as a result are net contributors. What I am worried about, however — more than worried, in fact — is the one-way ratchet of this “debate”. I am worried that the main parties are helping to summon up the very monster of which they should be most terrified: the Russell Brand-type impulse with added xenophobia. Voters hear “too many migrants” but don’t trust the conventional politicians to do anything about it. Nick Clegg was happy to criticise Boris Johnson, but Number 10 is not going down that route. Len Duvall, leader of the Labour group on the London assembly, has expanded his comments on Boris Johnson's speech (see 10.42am) in a post for LabourList. My colleague Mona Chalabi has been using a Reality Check blog to look at what IQ measurements really do, and do not, tell us. She points out that Boris Johnson has got into trouble on this issue before. While he was editor of the Spectator magazine, one columnist wrote: "Orientals ... have larger brains and higher IQ scores. Blacks are at the other pole". Johnson subsequently apologised for the publication. Len Duvall, the leader of the Labour group on the London assembly, has put out this statement about Boris Johnson's speech. Boris' comments are absolutely disgraceful. He seems to have forgotten one of the causes of the financial crash - greed. If the mayor really thinks the answer to spurring economic growth and getting our young people into high quality jobs is greed then it shows just how clueless he really is. He should be supporting practical policies like the educational maintenance allowance, lower tuition fees and wrap around childcare. He also seems to think that accumulating vast amounts of wealth is the only motivator in life. It might be for him but many people are motivated by public service - like our nurses, police officers, firefighters and teachers. Boris spoke about those who are less able as if they were some kind of sub species. This kind of rhetoric is deeply offensive and does nothing except create division. This is further evidence of the 'Crosbyisation' of politics. The mayor should wake up and use his position to help all Londoners and not just pander to the right. Boris has confirmed he is the mayor for the super-rich. Rather than putting in place assistance to help everyone achieve their full potential he has written off large numbers of people in his own city. We don’t want to write people off and not give them a chance, we want all schools to be good schools and give all children the best start in life possible. This sums him up, a mayor for the 1%. His comments sound like economic eugenics and are typical of his and his government's approach - causing further polarisation and dividing our country. My colleague Nicholas Watt wrote up the Boris Johnson speech for the Guardian. He was there when Johnson delivered it, and he points out that, in an off-the-cuff remark not in the script of the speech, Johnson appeared to mock people with a low IQ. This is from the version of the story Nick filed for later editions of the paper. In highly provocative remarks, Johnson mocked the 16% "of our species" with an IQ below 85 as he called for more to be done to help the 2% of the population who have an IQ above 130. "Whatever you may think of the value of IQ tests it is surely relevant to a conversation about equality that as many as 16% of our species have an IQ below 85 while about 2% …" he said as he departed from the text of his speech to ask whether anyone in his City audience had a low IQ. To muted laughter he asked: "Over 16% anyone? Put up your hands." He then resumed his speech to talk about the 2% who have an IQ above 130. And here is some web comment on the Boris Johnson speech. • Benedict Brogan at the Telegraph praises it. Thank goodness for Boris, who has spoken in praise of inequality. Hizzoner gave the third Margaret Thatcher lecture at the CPS yesterday, and didn't disappoint. Greed is good, some people are too stupid to get on, and – notably – bring back grammars were among the livelier messages packed into his address. The Mail liked his speech so much that they've run a panel of the best extracts. Once again Boris demonstrates his knack for the arresting phrase, and for confronting his party with the kind of plain truths that too many politicians avoid expressing. His line about a measure of inequality being necessary to encourage the "spirit of envy" which, like greed, "is a valuable spur to economic activity", is particularly telling: it is hard to imagine Dave saying the same. • George Eaton at the New Statesman says that Johnson was wrong about social mobility ... His presentation of social mobility as a form of compensation for inequality was almost comically inappropriate. As anyone with the most cursory grasp of the subject knows, reduced opportunity is the inevitable result of greater inequality: it's harder to climb the ladder when the rungs are further apart. As the empirical masterpiece The Spirit Level showed (see graph), it is the most unequal countries, such as the UK and the US, that have the lowest levels of social mobility, while the most equal, such as Sweden, Canada and Japan, that have the highest. ... and wrong about tax too. [Johnson] told his audience: "Today, when taxes have been cut substantially, the top one per cent contributes almost 30 per cent of income tax [one might note that he is among them]; and indeed the top 0.1 per cent - just 29,000 people - contribute fully 14 per cent of all taxation." Yet this statistic tells us less about what has happened to the tax system than it does about what has happened to the income system. Over the period in question, the earnings of the rich have soared to hitherto unimaginable levels. As a recent OECD study showed, the share of income taken by the top 1% of UK earners increased from 7.1% in 1970 to 14.3% in 2005, while the top 0.1% took 5%. Quite simply, the rich are paying more because they're earning more. • John Rentoul at the Independent says it was a brilliant speech, but that Johnson was wrong about inequality. • Sebastian Payne at Coffee House says the speech was a guide to what Johnson might do if he were in Downing Street. Here is some Twitter comment on Boris Johnson's speech. From Nick Faith, from the centre-right thinktank Policy Exchange From Labour MP (and potential candidate for London mayor) David Lammy From the Labour MP Nia Griffith From Sam Freedman, a former adviser to Michael Gove From Fiona Millar, the Labour education campaigner From Adam Bienkov, a Politics.co.uk reporter From the Lib Dem blogger Stephen Tall From Harry Phibbs, a Conservative blogger and councillor Here's the full quote from Nick Clegg about Boris Johnson's comment about the value of the elite. I don’t agree with Boris Johnson on this and I have to say to you much as he is a funny and engaging guy, I have to say these comments reveal a fairly unpleasant, careless elitism that somehow suggests that we should give up on a whole swath of our fellow citizens. I think the danger is if you start taking such a deterministic view of people and start saying they’ve got a number attached to them, in this case an IQ number, somehow they’re not really going to rise to the top of the cornflake packet, that is complete anathema to everything I’ve always stood for in politics, which is, yes of course, you shouldn't aspire - and as an old-fashioned Liberal I don’t aspire to a perfectly homogenous society where everyone has the same kind of outcomes but you’ve got to try and do more to instil greater opportunity in society. And there are people, certainly young people, who develop at different paces, who might discover talents they didn't know about before, and our job, surely, in politics is not to simply say , look, we're going to hive off one bunch of people and put them in one kind of category and, say they're parked and there's not much we can do about them. Instead what we should be doing is saying we should be instilling an opportunity culture of aspiration of hard work where people can get ahead for everybody. Clegg also said that there were some "very funny, colourful phrases" in the speech - he particularly liked Johnson's line about the "deep Freudian terror that every man has for the inner recesses of a handbag" - but he said the overall message was "very dispiriting". It seems to me what we should be about in politics is encouraging people to get ahead in life, not basically saying to people 'well, if you're not up to scratch you should basically give up. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. And here is the passage from Boris Johnson's lecture on elitism that particularly offended Nick Clegg. Like it or not, the free market economy is the only show in town. Britain is competing in an increasingly impatient and globalised economy, in which the competition is getting ever stiffer. No one can ignore the harshness of that competition, or the inequality that it inevitably accentuates; and I am afraid that violent economic centrifuge is operating on human beings who are already very far from equal in raw ability, if not spiritual worth. Whatever you may think of the value of IQ tests, it is surely relevant to a conversation about equality that as many as 16 per cent of our species have an IQ below 85, while about 2 per cent have an IQ above 130. The harder you shake the pack, the easier it will be for some cornflakes to get to the top. And for one reason or another – boardroom greed or, as I am assured, the natural and god-given talent of boardroom inhabitants - the income gap between the top cornflakes and the bottom cornflakes is getting wider than ever. I stress: I don’t believe that economic equality is possible; indeed, some measure of inequality is essential for the spirit of envy and keeping up with the Joneses that is, like greed, a valuable spur to economic activity. Here's a link to the full text of Boris Johnson's lecture. Well, that was better than normal. Nick Clegg was hitting out all over the place - at Boris Johnson, at Laszlo Andor, and event at the Indie's Owen Jones. Here are the key points. • Clegg accused Boris Johnson of "fairly unpleasant ... elitism". He as referring to the speech Johnson gave yesterday. Clegg said: I don’t agree with Boris Johnson on this and I have to say to you much as he is a funny and engaging guy, I have to say these comments reveal a fairly unpleasant, careless elitism. I think the danger is if you start taking such a deter view of people and start saying they’ve got a number attached to them , in this case an IQ number, somehow they’re not going to rise. • Clegg said the EU employment commissioner Laszlo Andor was wrong to suggest Britain's new curbs on migrants claiming benefits could make Britain look like a "nasty" country. I think the commissioner is just plain wrong. The new German government in its coalition agreement actually highlighted that this is an area that they themselves wanted to take action in it. Member states are having a perfectly rational debate that says, as the EU grows and grow, we must, yes, protect the freedom for people to move, but that is not the same as saying you can claim benefits from day one, no strings attached. I don’t think it’s right for the commissioner to turn around and say this is just a British issue. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. I will post more from what Clegg said about Johnson, and what other people have said about the Johnson speech, shortly. Q: How concerned are you about today's football match-fixing allegations? Clegg says this needs to be looked at. It is "very worrying". He hopes this is the sum total of it, and that it is not wider. Q: Why should mothers returning to work be given priority? Clegg says he strongly believes that the "Edwardian rules" that expect mothers to take all the time off when a new baby is born make it difficult for women to have a successful career. This is not taking jobs away from other people. There is a "chronic waste of talent" at the moment because of the barriers making it hard for mothers to return to work. Q: When will the NHS ban smoking on hospital property? Clegg says this is a recommendation that has gone out to NHS trusts. Q: It is not very liberal is it? Clegg says this is not out of keeping with what happens in other work places. Clegg says he has not completely given up smoking. Talking about the review announced today, he says the government has an open mind on this. He hopes the review will argue in favour of plain packaging. If it does, the government will legislate. Q: Would plain packaging have made a difference to you when you bought your first packet of cigarette? Clegg says he cannot remember. He thinks he bought his first packet of cigarettes in his early teens. Peer pressure was a big factor, he says. Q: Is Johnson turning into a Thatcher figure? Clegg says we are in 2013. We are not in the 1980s. What he "really objects to" in Johnson's speech is the "careless elitism" at the heart of it. "It is a very dispiriting message" to parents who want their children to get ahead in life. Q: What do you think of Boris Johnson's speech. He said some people were too stupid to get one. Clegg says he does not agree with these comments. Johnson revealed a "fairly unpleasant, careless elitism". He was talking about people like dogs. This is against everything he believes, Clegg says. People develop at different paces. Politicians should not prioritise one group of people. You should not treat society like a great big cake, he says. Q: Are you on a zero-hours contract? No, says Clegg. Some people find the flexibility in these contracts suit them. Other people are not happy about being on these contracts. The government is looking at what it can do to address this, he says. It is "absolutely not ruling out taking action". Q: How will you get more people to participate in the "befriend a lonely old person" scheme? Clegg says he hopes the scheme goes well. And he praises Esther Rantzen for setting up Silverline. Q: David Cameron reportedly called you an "idle fucker" [Nick Ferrari actually says f-er]. What do you call him? Clegg says he is feeling too lazy to answer. Cameron must have got him confused with Slacker Johnson. Q: Cameron regularly beats you at tennis. Clegg says he is a generous guy. Q: What was the score at the last game? It was very close, Clegg says. But Cameron pipped him at the post. Asked about Dominic Grieve's comments about corruption in the Pakistani community, Clegg says it is wrong to suggest this is problem with a particular community. Nick Clegg is hosting his LBC phone-in. Nick Ferrari starts with a question from an IQ test (a reference to Boris Johnson's speech.) Clegg gets it right. First question. Q: Is your support for the new curbs for benefits for EU migrants conditional on their legality? Clegg says he thinks the proposals are legal. We don't put forward things that are not legal, he says. The government obeys the law. It would be "entirely wrong", to do anything else. He says he is a passionate defender of the right of people to move within the EU. But freedom to move is not the same as freedom to claim benefits on arrival. Q: If these measures are found to be illegal, will you still support them? Clegg says no government acts illegally. Unless or until it is shown they are illegal, the government will still go ahead with them. He does not believe these measures are a "nasty" thing to do, (as the EU employment commissioner Laszlo Andor suggested yesterday). Q: What do you think of Andor's comments? Clegg says he is just "plain wrong". Other European governments restrict benefits for new arrivals. And yesterday Germany and France proposed new measures along these lines. This is not just a British issue. Q: In the Independent Owen Jones says scapegoating immigrants is your most unforgivable collaboration with the Tories. Clegg says that is a ridiculous thing to say. He says he does not know Owen Jones, and suggests he does not know who he is. It is important to remember that the right to move is not the same as the right to claim, he says. It's U-turn week. On Monday the Treasury announced that it was going to impose a cap on the cost of payday lending, something that it had previously resisted. And today we learn that the government is going to legislate to give ministers the power to force cigarettes to be sold in plain packets, even though earlier this year it signalled that nothing was going to happen until after 2015. On the Today programme Andy Burnham, the shadow health minister, said this was just a ploy to avoid defeat in the Lords. He criticised the government for not acting now. If the government were serious it would be taking action now to cut the harm from tobacco to children. Instead, we’ve got another review and why have we got this announcement now? It’s because the government was on the verge of losing a vote in the House of Lords in the next couple of weeks. So, I don’t believe this is a serious move that they’ve now converted to the cause and I’m afraid it’s just a tactic to avoid humiliation in the House of Lords. What else explains the timing? You know, we had a clear steer in the summer that this was now off the agenda and let’s remember Andrew Lansley had said at the beginning to his government that he was very committed to it and I supported him in saying that and today we’re no further forward than we were when he said that, we’ve got another review. In response, Jane Ellison, the public health minister, rejected claims that this was a U-turn. Ministers always said they were keeping this under review, she said. Andy Burnham’s quite wrong to say that it was ruled out in the summer. What the government said in July was that it would look at the emerging evidence from Australia and elsewhere, we’ve listened hard to what both Houses of Parliament have said over recent weeks where it’s been very clear that members in both Houses feel that there’s not just emerging evidence in Australia but other studies and I think the time is right to look at this. People do feel strongly and quite rightly. This is fundamentally about children’s health – we know that two thirds of people start smoking when they’re children. I don’t blame parliament about feeling strongly about this issue, it’s one of the most important public health issues we face in this country. (Ellison is right to say that ministers said in the summer that they would keep this matter under review, but at the time government sources were making it plain that they did not expect to review the matter any time soon.) As more reaction to this comes in during the day, I will be covering it here. Otherwise, it's quietish. Here's the agenda. 9am: Nick Clegg hosts his Call Clegg LBC phone in. 9.30am: The Office for National Statistics publishes migration statistics. 10.15am: The Conservative Fresh Start Project publish their EU Negotiating Mandate report. 10.30am: Mark Carney, the governor of the Bank of England, publishes the Bank's financial stability report. Around 11.30am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, makes a Commons statement about his campaign against the use of sexual violence in conflict zones. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Tuesday 27 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Tuesday 27 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Revolt by MPs torpedos donations and public funding bill
fields.bodyText: Time to part ways for this evening. Let's take a snapshot of the political day. Opposition leader Tony Abbott finally put the kybosh on the election funding and political donations package after a rebellion from his backbench, and strong disquiet from within his leadership group. Gillard Labor wasn't going to be left holding a smelly baby alone. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus released a letter from Mr Abbott from May 24 in which he clearly agreed to the package. Labor then executed a pivot to make the issue one of Mr Abbott's character - whether he could be trusted to live up to his undertakings, whether he was a man of his word. Mr Abbott's reply to this change in the play was the people had spoken (negatively, sometimes at length on talkback radio), and in killing the deal, he was simply a leader who listened to the voters. The Prime Minister announced the ACT had signed onto the the Gonski education reforms. She also copped another sandwich from a teenager during her visit to Lyneham High School. This 'hurl the white-sliced' trend is clearly catching on. Question Time was the economy, boat arrivals, and a bit of ASIO security assessments. Thanks very much to Mike Bowers for lovely pictures, and to Mary and Madhvi - and to our office mate Brian, who at one point had to kick our internet when it stalled during a hill start. Live publishing. What can possibly go wrong? Have a great evening. Steve Lewis, national political reporter for News Limited, has filed this story. Former Labor Prime Minister Kevin Rudd is on the move. Has Kevin Rudd had a pre-election premonition - or is he just waiting to reclaim his prime ministerial throne in the Lodge? Canberra tongues are wagging furiously following news Mr Rudd and his wife Therese Rein have listed their "sophisticated and luxurious" Yarralumla residence, for a budget-fixing $2.25 million. The former PM moved out of the five-bedroom property almost 18 months ago - when his youngest son finished high school in the national capital - but the decision to sell has some musing about whether Mr Rudd is planning to draw the curtains on his political career. The split-level home - which features "formal Italianate gardens" and "exceptional quality finishes" - has played host to visiting diplomats and heads of state, and is a mere 10 minute drive from the federal parliament. It went on the market yesterday and its internet listing is already doing a brisk trade. I'm keeping an eye on the ASIO head Mr Irvine and his evidence in senate estimates. Labor senator Mark Furner has been trying to persuade Mr Irvine to get cranky at the Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis for sharing details of a briefing he got the other day after a Four Corners report which alleged the ASIO building had been the subject of a hack. Mr Irvine looks a bit thin lipped. If I was a betting person I'd venture that he's not entirely happy. But he declines to rise to the bait. He says only: The government follows a policy of not commenting on operational matters. I don't want to get into a discussion (of recent events). It's not appropriate for a public servant. One of my colleagues was downstairs a little while ago and caught one of those only in Canberra moments. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott was jogging past Aussies Cafe to go to the gym, as he sometimes does in the afternoon after Question Time. At one of the tables was Bob Ellis - the author whom Mr Abbott sued for defamation several years ago concerning some allegations in the Ellis book, Goodbye Jerusalem. There was, my informant noted, a slightly uncomfortable moment as the two crossed paths. Mr Ellis was having afternoon tea with a couple of ALP folks. He was heard to remark to his tea companions that today, "we'd reached a tipping point" concerning the man on his way to the gym. Presumably this was a reference to Mr Abbott's difficulties today on the political funding deal. Mr Ellis is a bit of a fixture around parliament on big political days - how he materialises at such moments is part of the Ellis mystery, or perhaps, mythology. Senator Brandis has now concluded his line of questioning. As we begin to wind down, here's a fun GIF of the Prime Minister's reaction to the sandwich toss at Lyneham High from earlier today - captured by Mike Bowers. Thanks to The Global Mail. There's a bit of stereo going on now. The Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is, with colleagues, having a press conference to raise this case that we've referenced on the live blog this week of an Egyptian man held in low security detention in South Australia despite being the subject of an Interpol alert. The case has featured in Mr Abbott's Question Time attacks this week on Labor's national security credentials. Senator Brandis is also pursuing Mr Irvine on the same matter in the estimates committee. Mr Irvine says at no stage was this person let out of detention. Light touch assessment. Senator Brandis has picked up Mr Irvine on his description a moment ago of the nature of ASIO's security assessments. Mr Irvine says anyone who goes into community detention gets an ASIO assessment. They go through the triage process when they are on the track to get a protection visa. My figures say in 2010-11 about 11,000 people have received the full assessment, he says. Senator Brandis: We used to assess everyone who came in. Now he says the number of boat arrivals has gone up, and the number of threat assessments have gone down. That must create a greater risk that something is going to be missed. Mr Irvine says in reducing the number of people to be assessed ASIO uses rigorous criteria to determine who should get the additional assessment. Senator Brandis: But there are probalaistic judgments. Mr Irvine: Yes. Senator Brandis: There's a risk somebody is going to slip through. Mr Irvine: It is always a matter of risks and probabilities. But he says the triage system works to put intelligence resources in the right place. Mr Irvine is detailing current procedures for security assessments for would-be asylum seekers following the influx of boat arrivals over the past few years. He speaks of a triage system. The ASIO boss says 10 or 15 per cent of people heading towards a visa proceed to full threat assessment. It varies. That figure can go up and down. Mr Irvine says when there were less arrivals, there were more comprehensive threat assessments. He says thus far this financial year there have been about 3,000 assessments. Let's look in on a senate estimates hearing. ABC correspondent Naomi Woodley has been watching proceedings. Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis is asking questions of the ASIO head, David Irvine. The current topic is security assessments of asylum seekers. Not often stuck for words. This, however ... Apparently, all these fresh faced youngsters are in. The Prime Minister has placed further questions on the notice paper. We have a batch of personal explanations. Mr Abbott is contesting the Prime Minister's assertion during Question Time that he hasn't attended security briefings while Opposition leader. He lists all the dates of his security briefings. The Nationals leader Warren Truss is raising a matter of privilege. Mr Truss is objecting to a claim from Independent Tony Windsor that his opponent in the seat of New England, Senator Barnaby Joyce, has taken a donation from mining magnate Gina Rinehart. This isn't true, Mr Truss says. Surely this untruth is a breach of privilege? Mr Truss says Mr Windsor's statement was made with malicious intent. Speaker Anna Burke allows Mr Truss to table his supporting documents. Manager of government business Anthony Albanese, objects. What's the matter of privilege exactly? He says Mr Truss has enaged in the breach of process that he just complained about. Here's Mr Windsor's reaction, captured by Mike Bowers. Mr Pyne isn't sold on Mr Garrett's response. He moves a procedural motion requiring Mr Garrett to speak for three minutes in order to complete his answer. All those in favour, Speaker Anna Burke asks? (Loud ayes.) Against? (One, lone, no rings out in the chamber.) Everyone laughs. Mr Garrett shouts on for several minutes. Are we there yet? Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey. Gracious. A portfolio question from Christopher Pyne. Normally Mr Pyne is more intent on his role as Manager of Opposition Business. Mr Pyne is asking about funding for a particular school in 2014. How much will MacKillop College get next year? Schools Minister Peter Garrett is pointing again. Jabbing with his finger. The indexation commitments included in the plan for school improvement ... will see additional investments flow from 2014. Now Ms Bishop has been tossed out of the chamber by Speaker Anna Burke. Ms Bishop is back with security checks and asylum seekers. How can a security check be adequate if a suspected terrorist is allowed to be held at a low security detention facility? The Prime Minister is going through the criteria for security assessments. Ms Gillard says a report from the intelligence committee that Mr Abbott has quoted from several times this week found the security system was adequate. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison, getting the early plane home. The Member for Wentworth does raise a very serious issue. The shadow communications spokesman Malcolm Turnbull is asking about the exposure of NBN workers to asbestos - a story we pointed blog readers to earlier today. The Prime Minister is responding. We take all of this very seriously. There are no shortcuts in dealing with asbestos. Ms Gillard says COMCARE is investigating to ensure safety standards are being met. She says the asbestos problem may cause delays to the roll out of the NBN in some sites. The NBN rollout wil continue in other places. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop is back with boats and security checks. Is the Prime Minister confident that Australia's light touch security checks for "illegal" boat arrivals are adequate? The Prime Minister: The government is satisfied by present arrangements. Ms Gillard has a blast at Mr Abbott on the way through for not asking the security question today. She refers to a media report this morning that suggested Mr Abbott had failed to avail himself of security briefings whilst Opposition leader (until his interest in them this week.) Woah. Schools Minister Peter Garrett. Scaring the children. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has a question for the Prime Minister. Why are you making life hard for police who want information about asylum seekers? Mr Morrison has been pursuing this issue for the past couple of days. The Prime Minister tells Mr Morrison that work is underway and ongoing between federal immigration officials and NSW police on the matter to which he refers. Mr Morrison is having a shout, and the House of Representatives Speaker Anna Burke asks him to leave the chamber. Transport Minister Anthony Albanese is pursuing variations on a theme. The Coalition don't like public transport. Public broadcasters. Anything with the word public in it. They don't like the public. Off the economy. The asylum boats are back. Liberal Michael Keenan wishes to know why there's not enough police in Indonesia. The Prime Minister hints there might be an announcement on that subject soon. Speaker Anna Burke has, meanwhile, asked the Shadow Health Minister Peter Dutton to leave the chamber. Mr Abbott, thinking of the children. Another Gonski question. Will no-one (apart from Labor) think of the children? Education Minister Peter Garrett is on his feet, thinking of the children. The Canberra children. ACT chief minister Katy Gallagher is being congratulated for her vision. Labor's Dorothy Dixers continue on the theme of education and Gonski. Mr Swan says the Coalition, by opposing the reforms, is stealing from the future. He says Mr Abbott has been trying the thug the premiers, pressure them against signing on to the Gonski package. Mr Swan: We know he likes the biff. We know he's been going the biff on the Liberal premiers. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne takes a point of order. I know I'm the only person left in the country left listening to the Treasuer .. Mr Pyne is counselled not to abuse points of order. Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey is continuing the economy theme. Mr Hockey raises the new report from the OECD we mentioned in our wrap of the news first thing this morning. Mr Hockey cites new taxes and regulations. Things are going badly aren't they? The question is to Treasurer Wayne Swan, who says: Those opposite come in here day after day, talking down the economy. They want to generate a sense of uncertainty. We've got an economy which is good nick, but it is an economy in transition. Meanwhile, out on the internet, Queensland's Bob Katter is hosting his own Q&A. Mr Abbott has opened on the economy. A change of topic from the national security theme seen for most of this week. Labor's first Dorothy Dixer is about the ACT saying yes to the Gonski agreement. It being 2pm, Question Time is underway. I'm with cartoonist Cathy Wilcox on this one. With character tests and truth telling the order of the day in national politics, have a read of Canberra blogger Paula Matthewson's thoughts on whether truth matters in politics anymore. Paula, who is part of our Comment is Free network, (and blogs as Drag0nista), has responded to a column Lenore Taylor wrote this week about the post truth election. Truth? Well, it's not so important Paula reasons. The sad truth is that we expect politicians to lie: it is simply part of what they do. While we denounce the lies of politicians we’d never vote for, we forgive the untruths of those we support. Trust is the make-or break issue. Paula argues that competency is the issue voters care most about. Guardian Australia’s political editor Lenore Taylor wrote that we’re heading to a post-truth election. This might have come as a surprise to the many political observers who consider truth to be a quaint artefact from a golden age of politics that may not have actually existed. Truth, or its absence, has not been a deciding factor in politics for a very long time. Nor will it play a definitive role in the 2013 federal election. That's a sad thought for Factinistas. No currency in truth. Interested in thoughts about this, here, or on Twitter. You can follow me @murpharoo and join in the daily conversation using the hastag #politicslive Here is Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor's lunchtime news wrap of the row over public funding. The Coalition has forced its leader Tony Abbott to back down on a written agreement he gave to Labor that his party would vote for a deal to increase public funding to political parties. The party’s leadership group made the final decision to overturn the deal – which Abbott promised to support in a letter to Labor just six days ago – on Wednesday morning after a revolt by the backbench, senior members of the shadow ministry and state divisions of the Liberal party organisation. Brisk old day in national affiars. Let's take stock with the lunchtime summary. A rebellion from the Coalition backbench has killed a package designed to boost public funding for the political parties ahead of the September election; and make political donations more transparent. Mr Abbott's leadership group in effect told the Opposition leader that support for the package would be untenable, given the strong public backlash against the package. The bill, expected to be introduced to parliament today, has been withdrawn. Labor was determined to make sure that it didn't end up holding the baby. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus released a letter showing Mr Abbott had agreed to the package on May 24. The government then flipped the whole imbroglio to being a character test for Mr Abbott: can you believe anything this man says? Manager of government business Anthony Albanese was tasked with landing this message. The parliamentary crossbenchers chimed in with some reinforcement - all the while arguing that the Gillard Government needed to press on with real disclosure reform. Labor is leaving its options open on that score. In other news, the ACT Government has signed up to the Gonski changes. And a young man has thrown a sandwich at Ms Gillard during a visit to Lyneham High School, copying another sandwich toss in Queensland a couple of weeks back. Getting Canberra over the line on the Gonski agreement is actually more difficult than it sounds. The ACT public school system is one of the strongest in the country. The tussle was over whether or not the Gonski changes would make Canberra schools worse off. Different story in some other jurisdictions. In any case, big Canberra and little Canberra have come to terms. While Twitter goes nuts on the sandwich, let's stick to some nuts and bolts on the Gonski announcement. Here's an excerpt of Ms Gillard's statement: Building on the already strong public investment in the ACT, (the Gonski agreement) will bring the total public investment in ACT schools to around $5 billion over the six years from 2014 – with around $190 million extra for ACT schools over this period. This extra resourcing will mean that all ACT schools are funded to at least the level of the new Schooling Resource Standard (SRS) by 2019, as well as ensuring that schools across all sectors benefit from new more stable indexation arrangements over this period. In simple terms, these indexation arrangements mean all schools in the ACT – Government, Independent and Catholic – will see fair funding growth each year. Schools already funded at, or above, the new SRS will see their funding grow in real terms. In addition, the Gillard Government has committed to grow its school education spending by 4.7 per cent per year from 2014 into 2015 and throughout the agreement. In return, ACT has agreed to grow its own school budget by 3 per cent per year from 2015 onwards. The results of ACT students are among the best in the country – and that’s why the Gillard Government is also announcing today that we will provide $26 million over six years to establish a Centre for Quality Teaching and Learning at the University of Canberra. This will help all education authorities in the ACT to continue to be at the forefront of school reform in this country. Just to confirm gravity works. What goes up must come down. Was it salami? Jam? Hard to tell. Concerning reports this morning about workers on the NBN rollout being exposed to asbestos (we linked to this story first up), Ms Gillard says she has received assurances from Telstra that action is being taken to ensure the safety of contractors. She's then asked whether Senator David Feeney should replace the retiring Martin Ferguson in the seat of Batman. The preselection process isn't even underway. I haven't got any comment. The PM is asked about the collapse of the election funding agreement. Ms Gillard rounds on Tony Abbott: There's a huge gulf between what Mr Abbott says and what Mr Abbott does. If Mr Abbott says he won't cut to the bone, you can't believe him. Prime Minister are you disappointed that you had another sandwich thrown at you? A question from a reporter. Ms Gillard: They must have thought I was hungry. Nice riposte. The Prime Minister has left the kids and is now speaking to reporters. Ms Gillard welcomes the ACT signing on to the Gonski reforms. Canberra is the second jurisdiction after NSW to agree to the school funding package. Ms Gillard: I'm delighted we've been able to reach agreement with the ACT. The ACT will get $26 million for a new centre of teaching excellence at the University of Canberra. Ms Gallagher says today's deal will ensure that no Canberra schools are worse off. Clever, that Mike Bowers. See the flying sandwich in the right hand side of the frame, near the Prime MInister's elbow? What's your view on social media at school? Is this a trick question, Ms Gillard wonders. The Prime Minister is asked to reflect on being the first female leader of Australia. She doesn't think about it exhaustively. It strikes her as inconvenient when she stumbles in her heels. Ms Gillard says frequent school visits have given her feedback that young girls are interested in the pathway she's created. If doing this job has that effect, then however good or bad the day is, then that's a good thing. I'm hearing a whisper out of the pack at Lyneham that the PM copped another sandwich on the way in to the school. Remember this happened a few weeks ago in Queensland, and there was a media frenzy for about 24 hours? Oops, the bell has just rung in the library. But Ms Gillard is continuing with her Q&A. Hello Lyneham. The Prime Minister arrives at the campus in north Canberra. Little reflection on resilience. If you are an insecure person, you would be buffeted by incoming negativity, the Prime Minister says. If you are confident, it doesn't worry you as much. Digital natives .. Ms Gillard is speaking of iPads. What is the successor to the iPad? Er .. dunno. But there will be one. Ms Gillard is unfurling an anecdote from when fax machines were a newfangled invention in offices. I wonder if these kids have ever seen a fax machine? Here's the Prime Minister over at Lyneham High School. Ms Gillard is taking some questions from the students. She looks slightly worried about the possibilities. This visit is to announce that the ACT has signed on the the government's Gonski reforms. Ms Gillard is with ACT Chief Minister Katy Gallagher. Speaking of data blogs, I'm so delighted that Simon Jackman will be bringing his somewhat mind bending number crunching expertise to our election coverage at Guardian Australia for 2013. (Can you bend minds and crunch numbers at the same time? Probably not .. but you know what I mean.) Simon was a contibutor to the Huffington Post for last year's presidential election, and won plaudits among his fellow political stats wonks for making predictions as accurate as the New York Times pundit, Nate Silver. Silver, as political tragics know, has a rock star following. Simon has done some number crunching for his first blog this morning. Can Labor win the September election? Hard to see how on current poll trends is his broad conclusion. You can read Simon's blog here. If you are interested in the topic of political donations in Australia, who gives what to whom, let me just remind you we have this informative datablog which will give you an insight. I'm a supporter of public funding for political parties. I think more public funding and less private donations is a recipe for a cleaner system less vulnerable to influence by a small group of wealthy individuals or corporates. More public funding also allows more professionalism in party organisations that in this country at least, largely run on the smell of an oil rag. I'm also a fan of a low disclosure threshold for political donations, and frequent public disclosure. Thus ends my sermon. Greens leader Christine Milne is out on the gospel truth theme. If you can't take Mr Abbott's word from written agreements, what else will he rip up? The crossbenchers are now flipping this into a trust and character issue for Mr Abbott. Bit deft that, given they didn't support the deal in the first place. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie says he's glad the proposal is dead. But he says there is an urgent need to bring down the disclosure thresholds for political donations. Independent Tony Windsor says parliament needs to get stuck into regulating associated entities. It's possible for donor to make big injections to entities which then flow through to candidates, but this largesse is never declared. The origins of the money remain obscured. I see some some folks on social media are going back over Mr Abbott's famous interview with the 7.30 Report's Kerry O'Brien on the subject of the gospel truth. Mr Abbott: The statements taken as gospel truth are carefully scripted remarks .. Mr Albanese has stepped into the place recently vacated by Mr Abbott. He says the government will now examine alternatives to the package it proposed to the Liberals. We'll consider our options, and we'll have discussions with other representatives in the parliament. I'm of the view, and have been for some time, that transparnecy is important in the political process. The Manager of Government Business is going where he would go in such circumstances. This goes to (Mr Abbott's) character. It is very hard to have discussions with someone who even when (an agreement is) in writing, walks away. We were prepared to negotiate an outcome in good faith. He says the agreement was in black and white. I think there should be public funding of political parties. The ideal would be removing the ability of people or companies to donate to political parties. I think public funding is needed. Transparency is needed. Mr Albanese says without public funding, millionaires own politics, as they do in the United States. He says the principle of one vote one value, and equal participation in democracry, is undermined by large donations, particularly large anonymous donations. It's not clear whether Mr Abbott was rolled by his leadership group. It's not clear what's happened here. I was told last night they were in a difficult position. Here's some audio from Mr Abbott's press conference for readers interested to have a listen. Lively, that press conference. Mr Abbott has arrived for his press conferece.
