Paul Owen reports: The international community is now in a “better place” on Syria due to the government’s losing its parliamentary vote on taking military action last month, international development minister Alan Duncan has said. Speaking at a fringe event at the Conservative conference in Manchester, Duncan said the UK would have been “perfectly justified to take action after an international treaty had been breached by the use of chemical weapons” apparently by the Assad, but “I think we are perhaps now in a better place than we might have expected to be for a number of reasons”. He said that as a result of the loss of the vote Russian “involvement in this chemical disarmament process should to a large extent guarantee their no further use. Secondly, there’s a chance that because the Russians are there actively engaged we might actually improve humanitarian access [to Syria, and] the third element is that there might just be a better chance now than there would have been of getting people around the table for a second round of Geneva talks, because the only solution ultimately to the Syria conflict is a political one, with everyone sitting around the table trying to piece something together and at the very least getting a ceasefire.” Asked by interviewer Peter Oborne if he was saying he was glad the government had been defeated on this issue, Duncan – who said he was “given leave to remain on holiday” instead of taking part in the vote – said: “I never like to see my government defeated but I think that the consequences that have unfolded since have been serendipitous … I think that the Russian initiative has opened up new alternatives that would not have been there.” Asked by a Tory delegate whether the whole episode had damaged the UK’s standing in the world, Duncan said: “I don’t think this has dented our reputation. I think there was a very short-term hiccup; I think the world said: ‘Hello, what’s happened here?’ and far from losing respect I think we gained it.” Referring to the UK’s reputation at the UN, he said: “If you want to compare the engagement and respect of the UK with that of France, there’s no contest.” After the UK’s vote, the US eventually changed its policy on military action, and Duncan said: “I’m pleased [Barack Obama] has not been forced into a corner where he’s lost face.” Would he vote for military action in Syria in future? “It’s an immaterial question,” he said. He would not be drawn on whether the removal of Bashar al-Assad as Syrian president was government policy, but he said: “I think it’s difficult to see a stable future for Syria with Assad at the top. I think it’s very unlikely you’re going to see a future Syria with Assad running it. But he should not kid ourselves that this is just a straightforward, easy, ‘do you like the guy – yes or no?’” Duncan was also asked if Iran would have a full place at the table at the forthcoming Geneva peace conference on Syria. “Well, not necessarily at the table, in that way,” Duncan said. But he added that Iran was “entering or opening a new chapter” in its relations with the west” and “the initiatives that took place in New York last week are commendable”. The government lost the vote on possible military action against Syria on 30 August by 285-272, with 30 Tories and nine Liberal Democrats joining Labour to reject the move. Here is some Twitter comment on the Osborne announcement. From the Guardian's Patrick Wintour From Labour's Michael Dugher From Channel 4 News' Faisal Islam From Sky's Ed Conway From the FT's Chris Giles The other new announcement in the speech was the one about fuel duty. This is what Osborne said. We’ve cut fuel duty. Abolished Labour’s escalator. And I can tell you today that provided we can find the savings to pay for it, I want to freeze fuel duty for the rest of this parliament. Osborne has already frozen fuel duty for two and a half years. The Tories say that this means fuel is 13p a litre cheaper than it would have been if Labour's fuel duty escalator had been implemented. The new pledge will cost £750m a year, they say. If fuel duty is frozen to the end of this parliament, motorists will have gained £22.6bn over the parliament compared to what they would have paid under Labour, the Tories say. More details have emerged about the budget surplus law that George Osborne is proposing. This is based on what we're being told by party officials. • Osborne is going to draw up a new "fiscal mandate" with two new rules: - The budget should be in surplus in the good years (ie, when the economy is growing) - Capital spending would increase at least in line with GDP. • Osborne is aiming for an absolute surplus, not a cyclically-adjusted surplus. Here's an extract from the briefing note. We will only aim to achieve an absolute surplus during periods of economic growth - the fiscal mandate that will underpin this aim will not override the automatic stabilisers if the economy slows ... Relying solely on cyclically-adjusted measures of the deficit requires estimates of the output gap, which cannot be observed and must be estimated. As the last decade shows, this can lead to excessive government spending if buoyant tax receipts are viewed as permanent and structural, but are in fact temporary and cyclical. • More details will be published before the election. • A future Conservative government would set out these principles in its charter for budget responsibility. The Budget Responsibility and National Audit Act passed by the coalition says governments have to set out their fiscal mandates in a charter at the start of each parliament • Osborne's new rules would force governments to control day-to-day government spending, particularly on welfare. • The Tories will challenge Labour to say whether it would accept these rules. • Osborne's plan is modelled on rules that apply in other countries. Sweden set a surplus target in 1997, saying there should be a budget surplus over the economic cycle, and Canada adopted a "balanced budget or better" surplus objective in 1995. Both countries are now seen as having strong public finances, the Tories say. • The "automatic stabilisers" (ie, higher spending on unemployment benefits) would continue to operate in a recession. • Osborne believes Britain has a historic deficit problem. The government has only run a surplus in seven of the last 50 years, and in three of the last 20 years. (That was in the early years of New Labour. The Tories say that was because Tony Blair was initially sticking to Tory spending plans.) Osborne also believes that if Labour had run a surplus from 2002-03 to 2007-08, the deficit would have peaked at 8%, not 11% Here is what Osborne said about committing himself to running a budget surplus It should be obvious to anyone that in the years running up to the crash this country should have been running a budget surplus. That’s what we mean when we say they didn’t fix the roof when the sun was shining. Let us never make that same mistake again. Never again should anyone doing my job be so foolish, so deluded, as to believe that they have abolished the age-old cycle of boom and bust. So I can tell you today that when we’ve dealt with Labour’s deficit, we will have a surplus in good times as insurance against difficult times ahead. Provided the recovery is sustained, our goal is to achieve that surplus in the next Parliament. That will bear down on our debts and prepare us for the next rainy day. That is going to require discipline and spending control. For if we want to protect those things we care about, like generous pensions and decent healthcare, and buy the best equipment for the brave men and women who fight in our armed forces, all of us are going to have confront the costs of modern government – and cap working age welfare bills. And only if we properly control public expenditure will we be able to keep lowering taxes for hardworking people in a way that lasts. I’ve never been for tax cuts that are borrowed. I want low taxes that are paid for. We also want to go on investing in the essential infrastructure of our country - the roads and railways and science and communications that are the backbone of the future economy. So we should commit, alongside running a surplus and capping welfare, to grow our capital spending at least in line with our national income. These principles will form the foundation of our public finance policy and I will set out the details next year. Is Osborne going to legislate for this? It sounds as if he might. In which case we might get another of those bills making commitments on behalf of a future government, such as the Child Poverty Act or the deficit reduction bill passed by Labour. UPDATE: Earlier I talked about "another of those bills committing a future government to spending limits". That was not very well phrased. Imran Hussain from the Child Poverty Action Group points out that the Child Poverty Act does not set spending limits. It sets a target to end child poverty by 2020 and requires ministers to produce a national strategy every three years. And Labour's deficit reduction bill (actually, the Fiscal Responsibility Act) set a deficit reduction target, not a spending limit. I've reworded that sentence. There were two new announcements in the speech. • Osborne is committing himself to running a budget surplus by the middle of the next parliament. And he says he is going to codify this in some way. We have not got the details yet but it sounds like a some version of Gordon Brown's "golden rule" and raises the prospect of further spending cuts. • Osborne wants to freeze fuel duty until 2015. This will go down well with voters and with the conservative press (particularly with the Sun, that has campaigned aggressively on this), but it is not unprecedented. Osborne has frozen fuel duty in his last few budgets because putting it up has become so unpalatable. Fuel duty is becoming one of those taxes, like council tax, that is now at the limit of usefulness to government. Osborne says he wants people to remember this as the time when the foundations for a better future were laid. The future looks better than the past, he says. And that's it. The speech is finished. Osborne recalls Lady Thatcher's funeral. As he was there, he wondered what he would be remembered for. But for him what really mattered was her optimism. She believed Britain could have a better future. We are at our best when we are optimists. We are at our best when we have faith that our country's better days are not behind us, but ahead. Osborne says HS2 is in the best traditions of engineering in the UK. Britain should not let others lead in scientific endeavour, he says. Not for us the comfort of the past. Ours is the Britain of the future. Osborne says he recently met people making a car that will travel 1,000 miles an hour. That was in Bristol, he says. UPDATE: When I wrote this post I said Osborne mentioned a car doing "1,000 miles (either a gallon or a tank, I did not hear)". Actually it was a car travelling 1,000 miles an hour. I misheard. Osborne says he is going to China at the end of next week. The ambition of China is astonishing, he says. He says he does not want to see other nations pushing the frontiers of science, and to have to tell his children: that used to be us. Osborne says no one will get something for nothing. A fair welfare system is fair to those who need it, and fair to those who pay for it too, he says. Osborne says there is still more to do. He is part of the generation of Conservatives that came after the great struggles of the 1980s. Worklessness took hold. The Conservatives did not do enough to stop that. The party has to work hard in some parts of the country to overcome the opposition this aroused. Labour left 5m people on out-of-work benefits. Now the government has capped benefits, and cut immigration. It will not abandon the long-term unemployed. Called Help to Work, it will make the long-term unemployed do something in return for their benefits. Osborne says the recovery should be for all. Lectures from the left on fairness stick in the throat, he says. The rich paid less tax than their cleaners under Labour. The Conservatives are backing fairness. The pupil premium was Michael Gove's idea. Meeting the 0.7% aid target was Andrew Mitchell's idea, he says. And they are the party of home ownership too, he says. He has given the Bank of England powers to check a housing bubble. But Help to Buy will be extended next week. Those who are opposed should take their arguments to those areas where house prices are falling. They should tell people in those areas they are not allowed to buy their homes. Those people would say: it is alright for you, you have a home. The Conservatives are the party of Macmillan, and Thatcher's right to buy, he says. Osborne says his parents took a risk and set up their own business. He grew up with the experiences of running a business. He is hugely proud of his parents, who are in the hall today. He says the Tories are nothing if they are not the party of small business. That's the way it is going to stay. Osborne says that, provided he can pay for it, he wants to freeze fuel duty for the rest of this parliament. Osborne says the Tories stand beside hard-working people who are better off because of tax cuts. He mentions other tax cuts. And there will be a marriage tax allowance too. A Conservative promise made and a Conservative promise more than delivered. The Lib Dems like to point out that David Cameron said in 2010 that increasing the tax allowance would be expensive. Cameron was right. Increasing the allowance is easy. Funding that increase is hard, he says. Osborne says this will help hard-working people. This time the government will fix the roof while the sun is shining, he says. The government will go on investing in capital, he says. Capital spending will grow at least in line with inflation, he says. These will be the principle of his new approach to finance. Details will be set out next year. Osborne says he wants low taxes that are paid for. Labour should have been running a budget surplus before the crash. Let us never make the same mistake again, he says. No one doing Osborne's job should be so foolish as to think they can abolish boom and bust. So he can announce he will run a surplus when the economy has recovered. Osborne says no responsible chancellor rules out tax changes. But he thinks the deficit can by brought down by spending cuts. Look at how Ed Miliband's brother has responded. 1. Leave parliament. 2. Leave politics. 3. Leave the country. 4. Dedicate your live to international rescue. The Miliband brothers have the greatest sibling rivalry since the Bible. Cain and not very Able. Osborne says you should ask the people of Greece what happens to living standards when borrowing goes up. Higher borrowing and higher taxes are not the solution to lower living standards. They are the cause of lower living standards. Osborne says the Tories made unworkable promises when they were in opposition, on things like tuition fees. They lost the following election. David Cameron made them credible. More debt remains Labour's policy. Yet they won't talk to the public about that. Osborne says Labour's plan amounts to declaring war on enterprise, a tax rise, and an apprenticeship policy that was illegal. And the energy plan unravelled. Everyone wants cheaper energy, he says. But when you draw up policy on the back of a fag packet, companies will jack up prices before and afterwards. There will be a phony freeze on prices in between. Britain can do better than that, he says, quoting Miliband. Yet there is no serious plan from the other parties. Today Nick Clegg has said he wants to form another coalition. He wants to form a coalition with Vince Cable. Osborne says he is offering a serious plan for a grown-up country. Without a credible economic plan, you do not have a living standards plan, he says. The events in Italy and in Washington are a stark reminder that the debt crisis is not over. There is no feeling of the crisis being over, he says. Until the unemployed are helped, "it's not over." He repeats "it's not over" several times. It is not even close to being over, he says. Osborne says the government is forging a new attitude in this country, saying it is not afraid of the future because it intends to shape it. Osborne says he does not share the pessimism of Ed Miliband. Miliband talked about a race to the bottom. That is what Karl Marx predicted. Yet socialist policies create these problems, he says. The people who suffer when the economy is ruined are working people. Osborne says he is an optimist. I'm a believer, in free markets. Now people are asking new question. Can we make the recovery last? And will I feel it? Osborne says he likes to be straight with people. The answers are yes, and yes. But family finances will not be transformed overnight. Because Britain was made much poorer by the crash. That is what happens when you have a catastrophic failure of economic policy. George Osborne starts by thanking Karren Brady. "You're hired," he says. At every conference since 2010 the question has been is the economic plan working. They are not asking that question down. The deficit is down, 1.4m new jobs have been announced, 1,000 jobs have been announced today. Our plan is working. We held our nerve in the face of huge pressure. Now Britain is turning a corner. We owe the British people thanks, he says. Brady is introducing George Osborne now. He is the only man she would happily be an apprentice for, she says. (Sounds like a job application.) Brady praised the government's economic record. And she ends her speech with an announcement. She's becoming a small business ambassador. Which is why I have agreed to be The Conservative Party’s small business ambassador. I promise I will do all I can to help, promote and listen to the people who don’t always have a voice and I know how vital it is that the Government supports business wherever and whenever it can, by giving us. (The last Labour government liked appointing "tsars". This one seems more keen on "ambassadors".) ITV's Tom Brady is impressed by Brady. Brady says promoting women and equality are issues close to her heart. A door was held open for me when I went to Birmingham, and I see it as a personal responsibility to keep that door held open as wide as possible for as long as possible to get as many other women through it as possible. This government support my ambition. I am now the vice-chairman of West ham United. I came to West Ham when its future was threatened by debt and have spent the last three years working to turn it around. To do this successfully you need a business plan. You need to set out what you want to do and how you are going to get there. Karren Brady starts by describing how she got into football management. My football career started in 1993 when I saw an advert in the Financial Times newspaper, that said ‘football club for sale’. I phoned up and got the details and went to my chairman at the time and said there is this football club for sale in Birmingham why don’t we buy it and I’ll run it. “Football’? He said. ‘Very male dominated. You’ll have to be twice as good as the men to be thought of as only half as good”. Luckily. That’s not difficult! I replied. That was on the Friday and on the Monday we owned a football club. I went to my first press conference and 200 press were crammed into a room. I was 23 years old; desperate to look at least 25 so I had big hair, big shoulder pads and gave a serious presentation of all these things I was going to do with this football club now I was the CEO. When I had finished my very serious presentation I asked the press if there were any questions. I little hand went up. ‘Yes sir’ I asked. He said ‘what are you vital statistics?’ That was 20 years ago, and on that day I was the first woman to break into a very male dominated industry. But I had the last laugh. I made a trading profit in my first year in charge for the first time in the clubs modern history; I floated my business on the stock market in 1998, making me the youngest CEO of a PLC in the country and when I sold the business in 2009, 75% of the senior management team running the business were women! Karren Brady, the businesswoman and the Apprentice star, is speaking at the conference now. She is introducing George Osborne. Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, spoke in the hall earlier. My colleague Gwyn Topham has sent me this assessment. Patrick McLoughlin's speech was a fairly robust and unequivocal defence of HS2, with fewer concessions to the fears of opponents than he has made in previous outings. But he certainly didn't get much love from the hall for doing so - even a fairly bland commitment to faster wifi for most rail passengers by the end of the decade got a better reaction than the faltering HS2 oratory. He played the northern and regional card by attacking the hypocrisy of “moaning London commentators” who oppose HS2 while welcoming infrastructure schemes that benefit the capital. “We need a new north-south line to make the country stronger." McLoughlin twice employed that phrase that Labour has been trying to popularise – the “north-south line” - that emphasises HS2 simply as an additional and crucial part of the railway rather than talking about time savings or speed. He stressed that the investment would not affect funding for other transport schemes. But he told the Conservative conference that the paucity of fast rail links was “bad for our nation and it has got to change.” The reaction however suggests grassroots Tory opposition to HS2 is strong - at fringe meetings Conservative members have raised the spectre of Labour stealing an electoral march by ramping up opposition to the scheme, after Ed Balls' recent comments. Labour remains officially supportive of a scheme that promises to benefit its heartlands. But there is of course recent precedent for a party deciding to oppose major infrastructure plans ahead of an election: the Conservatives eventually scrapping Heathrow's third runway in 2010. In the conference hall Michael Fallon, the small business minister, has just finished hosting a discussion with some young entrepreneurs. Like some of the other Conservatives who have already spoken, he had a go at the business secretary. We've had enough red tape and enough yellow Cable, he said. On Twitter Andrew Fisher has sent me this about the TaxPayers' Alliance comment about the "work for benefits" plan. (See 11.11am.) Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, told BBC News that George Osborne's "work for benefits" plan was "unravelling already" because it would only affect 2% of claimants. This scheme that we've heard today will only actually affect 2% of people on the dole. So our perspective on this is very, very simple, we don't think it's big enough or bold enough. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. Byrne has also written a blog for the New Statesman setting out his case in more detail. David Willetts, the science minister, spoke to the conference earlier. He said the government wanted to double the proportion of women studying engineering. We are not going to win in the global race if we waste the talents of half the British people. The proportion of engineers who are women is one of the lowest in Europe and we’ve got to raise our game. That is why we support the ambition to double the proportion of engineering degrees taken by women. Today I can announce two initiatives to help us achieve that. We will extend fee loans to part time students of engineering, technology, and computer science who already have a degree in a different discipline. And we will invest £200 million in new teaching facilities for science and engineering in our universities. Universities will have to match it with private money. So that makes £400 million of investment so that students can be taught on the latest equipment ready for the world of work. That is our commitment to working with universities and business to help win the global race. As you would expect there was also a huge amount in his speech about the achievements of British science. My favourite line was this one. Of course we must always leave room for our scientists to pursue their own ideas, like the scientist in Newcastle University wondering how locusts manage to fly in such dense swarms without colliding. So she analysed locust brains to see how they worked. Now she is going to sell her anti-collision software to the car industry. On the "work for benefits" plan, it is also worth reading what the centre-right thinktank Policy Exchange said about workfare in a report published at the end of last week. Here's the news release and here's the report (pdf). And here's an extract from the report. Rolling out workfare for large numbers of benefit claimants would be prohibitively costly (with recent suggestions costing upwards of £1 billion in the first year alone) and would ignore the very deep barriers to employment facing many jobseekers. It is vital for these to be addressed with personalised and intensive support and coaching. In short, workfare is not suitable for all, or even a large proportion of, benefit claimants. However, for some groups of benefit claimants, the experience of work that workfare can provide and the diversionary impact of increasing conditions could both be effective in helping them into work. Workfare schemes are also popular with the public. Previous polling commissioned by Policy Exchange showed that 80% of the public supported community work in return for benefits for those who had been unemployed for over 12 months. New YouGov polling commissioned for this report found that, by a margin of over three to one, the public would rather reform labour market policy to introduce a workfare scheme than one that guaranteed long-term benefit claimants a job at the National Minimum Wage using taxpayers’ money. However, importantly, it also found that general support for the principle of workfare does not apply to all groups of claimants; while just 8% of the public say that young people under 25 without children should not be subject to workfare, that figure rises to over two thirds (67%) for mothers with pre-school children and more than a half (52%) for people with medical conditions affecting their ability to work. Here's some reaction to George Osborne's "work for benefits" plan. • Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, said Labour's proposed jobs guarantee would be more effective. It's taken three wasted years of rising long-term unemployment and a failed Work Programme to come up with this new scheme. But this policy is not as ambitious as Labour's compulsory jobs guarantee, which would ensure there is a paid job for every young person out of work for over 12 months and every adult unemployed for more than 2 years. With Labour's plans we would work with employers to ensure there are jobs for young people and the long term unemployed - which they would have to take up or lose benefits. Under the Tory scheme people would still be allowed to languish on the dole for years on end without having a proper job. • Graeme Cooke, research director at the IPPR thinktank, says relatively few unemployed people will be affected. George Osborne’s welfare announcement today is not a revolution. It will probably end up affecting less than one in twenty JSA claims, which accounts for 2.5 per cent of all benefit spend. People have ‘had to do something in return for their benefits’ since 1911. There is nothing wrong in principle with expecting the long term unemployed to undertake work experience or a more intensive job search. The key issue is how such schemes are designed. If they give people real experience of work and the practical employability habits that go with it, they can help people be more attractive to prospective employers. But if it is pitched as a punishment where people do menial tasks, it risks acting as a signal to employers that these are people not to employ. The government’s mandatory work activity pilot, which mirrors today’s announcement, was found to have no impact on future employment, only a short term impact on JSA claims and it actually increased claims for disability benefit. By contrast, job guarantee schemes like the Future Jobs Fund – which created real work placements, paid at the minimum wage, and compulsory for the long-term unemployed – have much better results. We should be guaranteeing everyone a job paid at the minimum wage after they have been out of work for more than a year, but also insisting that they work or risk losing their benefits – limiting the amount of time society is prepared for anyone to be unemployed. • Boycott Wokfare says the proposal as the "criminalisation of unemployed people". Here's an extract from their news release. Unemployed people and campaigners have condemned George Osborne’s announcement that long-term unemployed people will be forced to work unpaid or face losing their social security as a criminalisation of unemployed people. The maximum community sentence that a judge can hand out is for 300 hours, but claimants on six-month workfare schemes are already being forced to work without pay for 780 hours. The four-week Mandatory Work Activity scheme is already the equivalent of a medium level community service order that a person might receive if they were found guilty of drink driving or assault. When a similar scheme was introduced in the US, thousands of jobs in the Parks Department were lost in New York alone - to be replaced with forced unpaid workers. Similar case studies have emerged in the UK, where workfare placements are already taking place in hospitals, council offices, charities and businesses. • The TaxPayers' Alliance has welcome Osborne's proposal. The TaxPayers' Alliance has given a warm welcome to George Osborne's announcement that the long-term unemployed will be required to do unpaid full-time work or job seeking or lose their benefits. It comes just three weeks after the TaxPayers' Alliance published Work for the Dole: A proposal to fix welfare dependency, which called for mandatory participation in community work and training in return for the continued payment of benefits payments. The TaxPayers' Alliance report, written by award-winning entrepreneur Chris Philp and published on 4 September, provides the most comprehensive analysis of how a Work for the Dole scheme along the lines of those adopted in Australia and the US could be successfully introduced here in the UK. • Fiona Weir, chief executive of Gingerbread, the single parents' charity, says the plan will stigmatise the unemployed. This announcement seems to be a smokescreen for a failing system: acknowledging that the Work Programme isn’t delivering the ‘revolutionary approach’ promised to support people into work, and ignoring the weak labour market. Single parents are highly motivated to work but face practical barriers such as childcare and a shortage of family-friendly jobs – none of which are addressed by Help to Work. Workfare has been shown to be ineffective in helping those with multiple barriers to work, and ineffective in a weak labour market (2). This announcement will only deepen the stigma that unemployed people face, and does nothing to offer new, constructive solutions to help them back into work. Jonathan Portes has responded to George Osborne's comment about him. (See 10.31am.) You can read all the Guardian's Conservative party conference coverage here. As for the rest of the papers, I've already mentioned the Nigel Farage article in the Times. (See 8.27am.) Here are some other articles I found interesting. • James Slack and James Chapman in the Daily Mail say Theresa May will announce that she will change the rules so that people who want to appeal against deportation have to do so from abroad. Foreign criminals, terrorists and illegal immigrants will be kicked out of Britain before they get the chance to claim their human rights are being breached. In a massive shake-up of immigration law, Theresa May today tells the Daily Mail the Government plans to ‘deport first, and hear the appeal later’ – after they have been put on a plane home. The Home Secretary will also slash the number of grounds on which migrants can lodge an appeal from the current 17 to just four after the fiasco of the deportation of Abu Qatada, who finally returned home to Jordan earlier this year after a 12-year legal battle. • Boris Johnson in his Daily Telegraph column that Ed Miliband's promise to freeze energy prices is a con. [Miliband] says he will imitate the catastrophic policies of the emperor Diocletian, by imposing a price freeze on energy bills for the 20 months succeeding the election. And, er, then what? Well, then the energy companies will of course recoup their losses by whacking the prices jaggedly upwards again. In the meantime, the Labour government would have achieved all sorts of undesirable outcomes. By their meddling jiggery-pokery, they will send out the worst possible message to anyone thinking of investing in this country, or buying shares in British businesses. Worse still, perhaps, he will trigger all sorts of perverse behaviour by the companies – none of which is likely to be in the interests of the consumer. The energy companies will sullenly cut costs by laying off staff – so that you spend even longer waiting for a human being to answer the phone, and have to wait in all day for a repair man to come. • David Cameron tells the Sun in an interview (paywall) that his wife Samantha wants him to serve a full second-term if he wins the next election. (There have been rumours that she wants him to stand down before 2020.) It sounds as if Conservative MPs have all been reading Jesse Norman's biography of Edmund Burke over the summer. William Hague cited Burke in his speech yesterday. And Owen Paterson, the environment secretary, did the same in his speech to the conference a few minutes ago. Here's what Paterson said. It was after all Edmund Burke who wrote that we are the “temporary possessors and life-renters” of the earth who must live in a way which doesn’t “leave to those who come after… a ruin instead of a habitation.” If we do, history will quite rightly judge us harshly. Here's the full text of the Paterson speech. And here are some other key points. • Paterson said that the broadband rollout would have more impact on the rural economy than anything else. I believe that nothing will have a more spectacular impact on the rural economy than the roll out of superfast broadband. For the first time, we have a technology that can truly bridge the gap between urban and rural. • He said that biodegradable bags could be exempt from the new 5p tax on plastic bags. (I suppose you could see this as amounting to a tax cut announcement.) In tandem with Nick Clegg’s announcement of a 5 pence charge on plastic carrier bags, I want to work with industry on setting a standard for biodegradable bags. Any bags complying with this new standard could be given out by retailers for free or at a lower cost, reducing their environmental impact and incentivising a whole new industry. • He said the government was exploring biodiversity offsetting. Biodiversity offsetting resolves the long-running conundrum of how to grow the economy at the same time as improving the environment. It could, for example, see the extension of a dairy, which will result in the loss of a pond, get the go ahead. This would, however, only happen after every other avenue of mitigation had been explored and would be on the basis of a long-term agreement to enhance an existing pond or create a new one elsewhere. Any such offset would need to deliver measurable and sustainable biodiversity gain. The concept has been around for some time now and on a small island such as ours, with some of the most delicate ecosystems on earth, we must give it serious consideration. That’s why I launched a consultation at the beginning of September. I’ve seen it working well in Australia. And we can learn from other countries such as Germany, India and the United States. We already have six pilots underway in England but the time has come for us to broaden the discussion, to unleash our ambition. • He criticised Labour for politicising the badger cull. Labour's approach to bovine TB amounted to "doing nothing", he said. George Osborne's Today programme interview was just one of several that he gave this morning. Overall, he covered a broad range of topics. Here are the main points. I've taken some of the quotes from PoliticsHome and the Press Association. • Osborne said the "work for benefits" plan being announced today was designed to help the long-term unemployed. Previous governments of all colours have abandoned those people and just allowed them to recycle in and out of jobcentres, collecting the dole, and people didn’t really do anything about them. I think that’s wrong. I think it’s something we’ve got to learn from what went wrong in the past. We’ve got to help these people, but in return expect them to put something back into their community. On the basis of this comment, and other similar ones, you might conclude that Osborne is investing in better welfare provision. But this message does not seem to have got through to the papers who are presenting it as a punitive move, such as a "crackdown" (in the Daily Mail's headline) or an attack on "workshy Brits" (in the Sun's story). It is hard to believe they can both be right. • He rejected suggestions that his proposal was harsh. I’ll tell you what I think is heartless, it is leaving the long-term unemployed to just recycle through jobcentres and never get a proper chance of getting a job … I think it is a very compassionate approach to people who previous governments just ignored. • He rejected claims from a leading economist that the plan would not work. On the Today programme Sarah Montague asked him about comments from Jonathan Portes, head of the National Institute for Economic and Social Research and a former government economist, who has been speaking out about the plan, on his Twitter feed and elsewhere. Yesterday Portes used Twitter to highlight a blog post about government research showing that a similar programme has not worked. Here's how it starts. The Department for Work and Pensions today published an impact assessment of the Mandatory Work Activity (MWA) programme. The analysis compares participants on the programme with "comparable" (as determined by sophisticated statistical techniques) non-participants. Briefly, what the analysis shows is that the programme as currently structured is not working. It has no impact on employment; it leads to a small and transitory reduction in benefit receipt; and worst of all, it may even lead to those on the programme moving from Jobseekers' Allowance to Employment and Support Allowance. Sadly, the DWP's press release doesn't mention any of these key points. Osborne said he did not agree with Portes' analysis. Portes was "comparing apples with pears", he said. • Osborne said he did not accept that today's announcement meant the work programme (the government's current scheme to get the unemployed into work) was failing. "The work programme is working," he said. • He said his "work for benefits" plan would save money in the long run. I think it will make savings to the welfare bill, because of course if you get people off welfare, that’s a huge saving to the taxpayer and they themselves become contributors through the taxes they pay in work so there’s a huge benefit in that. • He rejected claims that he regarded Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, as "thick". Montague was paraphrasing a comment in the Sunday Telegraph yesterday, which was serialising Matthew d'Ancona's new book about the coalition, In It Together. Here's the Telegraph extract. Nor was [Osborne] confident that IDS had the IQ. “You see Iain giving presentations,” he confided in allies, “and realise he’s just not clever enough.” Duncan Smith believed that Osborne had been captured by the Treasury and its institutional arrogance. “I can see his whole sense of government changing,” he told colleagues. “He’s not a gambler at all.” Asked if he had described Osborne as "thick, or not very clever", Osborne said he had not. No, I did not. And I, by the way, work incredibly closely with Iain Duncan Smith. I think he is a remarkable individual who has transformed, almost single-handedly, the entire debate about welfare in this country and is deeply compassionate ... and clever .... Just because it’s in a book doesn’t make it true. I have huge, huge respect and time for Iain Duncan Smith. Osborne also said he and Duncan Smith worked well together. And, by the way, here’s a staggering truth about this government: the fact that the chancellor of the exchequer and the welfare secretary can work together and deliver programmes like this and talk to each other is itself a sign that we have a functional government that is working for hardworking people. • He said the Conservatives would not allow their candidates to do stand as joint Conservative/Ukip candidates at the election. In an article in the Times today, Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, said he would let local Ukip parties strike deals of this kind. But Osborne said it would not be allowed. There aren’t going to be any deals with UKIP and there are not going to be Conservative-Ukip candidates locally. The only candidates who will stand for the Conservative party at the election are Conservative candidates ... There’s a very clear rule in our country, which is that candidates have to be approved by the party and the Conservative party will be fielding Conservative-only candidates. • He rejected a claim from Chris Huhne, the Lib Dem former energy secretary, that the Treasury was blocking government moves to get people to insulate their homes. This is what Huhne told the Today programme earlier. I think the government has made a lot of progress on green issues. It does depress me that there are still some very easy hits, for example on energy saving we could be saving a lot of energy and reducing our bills if there were greater incentives for the green deal. Our homes are incredibly inefficient. They leak energy, and that costs an incredibly amount. It has been the Treasury that has really been the obstacle to that. Osborne said the Treasury was funding these schemes. Chris would know, because I worked with him on designing this, we do have a programme funded by the Treasury or funded by taxpayers more to the point, or indeed energy bill payers, that does help insulate the homes of those who are the poorest or in fuel poverty. It’s not just the Green Deal. We also have something called the ECO, which is a scheme that energy companies use to go into some of the poorest homes or the homes with the highest energy bills relative to their income and the size of their home to make sure they’ve got all the energy efficiency that they can possibly have. • Osborne said the Conservative party was not moving to the right. The Conservative party is appealing to all parts of our country – we’re not moving to the left or the right; we are on the centre ground of British politics. And I think policies on immigration, welfare and Europe which are sometimes called ‘right-wing policies’ or policies that appeal to people who might be tempted to Ukip, I think they have a very broad appeal. I find people of all political colours worried about immigration, worried about welfare. And which party is tackling those problems? The Conservative party. • He said the global debt crisis was not over. And anyone who thinks the debt crisis is over, they only have to watch your news bulletins and they’ll see problems in Italy, problems this week in Washington DC. • He said governments could not tackle the living standards crisis without fixing the economy. We have to go on delivering an economic plan because that is the only credible plan for living standards. If you don’t fix the economy, people don’t get jobs, mortgages go up in cost because borrowing gets out of control, you’re not able to take people out of tax – all those things would be a total disaster for this country’s economy and a disaster for living standards. • He said raising the tax allowance was worth as much to low-earners as a 10% increase in the national minimum wage. • He said Labour's' plan to freeze energy bills was "just a gimmick". He went on: "It’s not a serious economic policy and I think the country understands that. • He said he accepted that man-made climate change was happening. I do accept that climate change is happening and I accept the overwhelming evidence that it is man-made and I want us to be part of the international solution to that. My argument has always been that saving the planet should not cost the earth – what good does it do Britain to have a set of policies that closes, let’s say, an aluminium smelter in the north of England and then that same company opens an aluminium smelter in China? • He welcomed the announcement that the delivery firm TNT is creating 1,000 new jobs in Manchester. • He said he might have a pint of Our Maggie beer, which is on sale at the conference, after his speech. Now we get the Nick Robinson post-match analysis. Robinson says Osborne "put to bed" the idea of a Tory/Ukip pact. In the Times today Nigel Farage is saying Ukip and the Conservatives could do local deals at the election. Here's an extract from the Times story (paywall). Writing in The Times, the UKIP leader says that he is despised by David Cameron, and a formal electoral pact with the Conservatives would be nonsensical. However, Mr Farage indicates that he would not stop agreements in individual constituencies, an intervention that threatens to split the Conservatives during the party’s annual conference in Manchester. Although he claims that UKIP is diametrically opposed to Mr Cameron, he says that there are many MPs who take a different view from the Tory leadership, naming Peter Bone and Jacob Rees-Mogg. “If either they, or others like them, even Labour MPs, with their local associations, chose to propose running on a joint ticket then I would leave the local UKIP association to have those negotiations,” he writes. “If after discussions they feel that it would be a better way to serve their constituents, then I and the National Executive Committee would be happy to hear reasoning. After all we are a party that believes in real localism and doesn’t think that the centre is the repository of all wisdom.” I'll post a summary of Osborne's interview on Today, and the others he has been doing this morning, shortly. Q: Would you mind Conservative candidates doing deals with Ukip? Osborne says that will not happen. The only candidates standing for the Conservatives will be Conservative ones. Q: So if a Conservative MP puts something on a leaflet saying they are supported by Ukip, you will stop that? Osborne says Conservative candidates have to be endorsed by the party. The Conservatives are moving to the centre ground. Policies on immigration and welfare have a broad appeal, he says. Q: Chris Huhne this morning said the Treasury was blocking tax breaks for energy savings. These measures would cut bills, he says. Osborne says he worked with Chris on the government's scheme. The government is doing various things. But the government needs the right mix. Q: People think you do not believe in these measures. Do you accept the IPPC conclusions on climate change? Osborne says he does. Climate change is happening. But he thinks there is no point closing an aluminium smelting plant in the UK only for it to open in China. That does not help the environment. The argument he has been making has now come to the forefront of debate: how do you have an energy policy that also cuts bills. Osborne says, without an economic plan, you cannot have a cost of living plan. Growth is needed to improve living standards. Q: But for all? Labour says a rising tide now just lifts the yachts, and that you are just for the rich? Osborne says he does not accept this Labour criticism. Under Labour, the rich payed lower tax than their cleaners. Q: Are you satisfied everyone will benefit from the government's policies? Osborne says he wants everyone to benefit from government policies. The minimum wage is going up tomorrow. And the increase in the tax allowance is worth a 10% rise in the minimum wage. Increasing the personal allowance was easy. The hard thing was paying for it, he says. Q: Why are you announcing this, not Iain Duncan Smith? The Matthew d'Ancona book says you think he's "thick" Osborne says that's not true. He never said that. Just because it's in a book, that does not make it true. He says he has huge respect for Duncan Smith because he has transformed the welfare debate in this country. Q: Are you serious about taking people's benefits away? Osborne says he is serious about helping people into work. Q: But there will be sanctions. Osborne says the sanctions are important. They will help people into work. There needs to be a bit of tough love. Sarah Montague is interviewing George Osborne. Q: This will affect 200,000 people. It suggests the work programme has not worked. Osborne says he does not accept that. The work programme has got 350,000 into work. More than 150,000 of them have got jobs lasting more than six months. The plan announced today will help people into work. Q: It will cost £350m. Jonathan Portes, who used to be a Cabinet Office economist, says it won't work. Osborne says Portes is comparing apples and pears. There is a broader point. In the past cities like Manchester accepted some people would be workless. But he does not think we should accept that. The government should fix the problem of "endemic worklessness". It will help those on welfare. But it will also be fair to people who pay for it. George Osborne, the chancellor, is speaking at the Conservative conference today and he is already dominating the headline. Overnight he released details of his plan for tougher conditions for the long-term unemployed receiving benefits. Here's the Guardian story about it, and here's how it starts. George Osborne will continue the government's push to reduce welfare spending by announcing a nationwide scheme on Monday to force 200,000 long-term unemployed benefit claimants to either undertake community work, attend a jobcentre every day or go on a full-time intensive programme to tackle the underlying reasons for their failure to find work. In his set-piece speech to the Conservative party conference in Manchester, the chancellor will also repeatedly warn that the battle to secure Britain's recovery is not yet "even close to being over" – a message designed to remind voters of the risk of returning to Labour. The £300m jobs programme, appealing to the electorate's demand for stronger welfare measures, will start in April and will be aimed at 200,000 jobseekers allowance claimants. Polling suggests there is continued public support for ever tougher welfare crackdowns and with the Tories trailing in the polls and in need of a strong response to Ed Miliband's populist conference speech last week, strategists have returned to one of their strongest and most familiar policy areas to push the message that they are on the side of hard-working people. Osborne will be talking about this on the Today programme in a moment. I will be covering the interview in detail, as well as the speech and all the reaction to it. Here's the agenda for the day. 8.10am: George Osborne is interviewed on the Today programme. 9.15am: Session on the environment and climate change, with Owen Paterson, the environment secretary. 10.30am: Session on the economy, with speeches from Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, and George Osborne, the chancellor. 12.30pm: Fringe meetings, including Nigel Farage on Europe, Liam Fox on conservatism and Jeremy Hunt on the general election. 2.30pm: Session on home affairs and justice, with speeches from Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, and Theresa May, the home secretary. I'll be covering all the breaking news from the conference, as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best conference-related comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

results.webTitle: Michael Gove: defying EU on new migrants is just practical politics

fields.headline: Michael Gove: defying EU on new migrants is just practical politics
fields.trailText: Education secretary backs extending period Bulgarians and Romanians must reside in UK before claiming benefits
fields.bodyText: The education secretary, Michael Gove, has said it is practical politics to defy the EU by extending the period migrants from Bulgaria and Romania have to be in Britain before claiming benefits. There had been reports on Saturday and Sunday that David Cameron was looking at increasing the length of time from three months to a year, but No 10 sources said such a long extended period was unlikely. Ministers feel under pressure because of the January deadline that allows Bulgarians and Romanians to enter the country freely, so lifting the remaining transitional controls. Both the Daily Mail and the Times published opinion polls last week showing overwhelming negativity towards further immigration from the two countries. Cameron is expected to set out his plans as early as this week, but a decision is fraught with legal and diplomatic arguments. Cameron will be nervous of acting unilaterally without EU diplomatic support. There is growing EU backing for measures to limit the free movement of workers within member states, a measure introduced at a time the EU was much smaller and culturally more homogenous than now. Cameron is also aware that Ukip's unique selling point in next year's European elections is its offer to quit the EU and so regain control of Britain's borders. Theresa May, the home secretary, has also published an immigration bill to restrict access to benefits including housing welfare and healthcare. On The Andrew Marr Show on BBC1, Gove said: "I absolutely agree with [Cameron] and I do think it's practical politics. The prime minister has struck exactly the right note on migration, which is to celebrate the achievements of people who have come here, to recognise that migration has to work for those who are already here." He added: "When it comes to new migrants from accession countries in the EU, we need to look properly at the benefits system here to make sure people are coming here to work and contribute, not to take advantage of what is rightly a generous welfare system." A cabinet minister told the Sunday Times that other EU member states had "also had enough" of the European commission's stance on the issue. But the Scottish secretary, Liberal Democrat Alistair Carmichael, said the plans were still under consideration in government and said any approach should be discussed in Europe. He told BBC Radio 5 Live's Pienaar's Politics show he could "see the need for robust transitional arrangements" but added: "I would need to be persuaded of that but I am prepared to look at the whole situation in the round." He warned: "Potentially it might put us on the wrong side of the rest of the European Union. As a member state I don't want to find myself in that territory. It's something that we should be discussing with them, certainly." The shadow home secretary, Yvette Cooper, acknowledged that the "pace of immigration was too fast, the level of immigration was too high" under the previous Labour government. She told The Andrew Marr Show: "We already said last year there were changes the government could make already within the existing rules and changes that they should argue for across Europe as well to make sure the system is fairer. "I do think when people are coming to this country they should be contributing and so we have already said there are changes you could make to jobseeker's allowance so people can't come and claim jobseeker's allowance straight away. "If the government had done that nine months ago when we suggested it, we could have had more progress made on this right now. It's important to recognise that most people who come to this country do come to work and to contribute." In a speech last March to the Instiutute of Public Policy Research thinktank, Cooper said "The habitual residence test works in the vast majority of cases but one practical change within existing European rules would be to add a "presence test" to the habitual residence test to make it clear and to clarify for everyone that jobseeker's allowance cannot be claimed within a few days or weeks, and that people will be expected to be in the country for some time or to contribute before they get something back. That could be done swiftly." She added: "Requiring countries to treat new migrants exactly the same as long-standing residents creates a risk that member states simply cut family support, housing or services for all citizens in order to avoid attracting too many migrant workers. That can't be good for anyone. So Europe should look again at the benefit rules and residence requirements that are in place."
