My colleague Henry McDonald was in the Waterfront Hall listening to President Obama. He said Obama's comments about "peace walls" were particularly interesting. One of the most interesting aspects of the speech was his appeal for the infrastructure of sectarian divisions, particularly the glorious misnomers "the peace walls" to be dismantled. Despite nearly 20 years of peace and 15 years since the Good Friday agreement, Belfast remains a divided city with more than 30 barriers separating Catholic and Protestant communities from each other. One of the barriers is only a short walk away from the Waterfront Hall where the president spoke this morning. While I was struggling with the blogging tools, Henry was listening to Michelle Obama's speech introducing her husband. Michelle Obama was introduced on to the platform by 16-year-old Methodist College Belfast student Hannah Nelson. In her speech, the first lady said that in summits and international meetings, “wherever we go, no matter what's on our plate, we do our best to meet young people like you … You are the most important people we talk to on our visits because in just a couple of decades you will be the ones in charge.” She added: "When I look around this room I just don't see a bunch of teenagers but future world leaders." Henry also sent me a line on the jokes at the start of Obama's speech. Obama also joked in speech that he hoped to get some golf practice in while attending the G8 summit at the Lough Erne hotel resort which was designed by Nick Faldo. He said he has been talking to Ulster golf star Rory McIlroy about improving his golf. "Rory offered to get my swing sorted, which was a polite way for saying: 'Mr President you need help!'" And he spoke to some people in the audience. Pupils from St Rose's secondary school off the Falls Road in west Belfast were visibly excited at the prospect of being in the same room as President Obama. Katie McCorry, Catherine Shaw and Celine Maxwell have just completed their GCSEs and are enjoying the treat of being selected for the Obama speech. One of their teachers Angela Grant said: "We are more excited than the pupils about being here today. You know what teenagers are like, you can hardly get a word out of them. I was in Washington DC for the President's first inauguration speech and so to be here today is a double privilege. For the girls it's an experience of a lifetime!" Obama ended with a final appeal to the young. All of you here today – you possess something the generation before yours did not: an example to follow. When they took a chance on peace, they didn’t have a successful model to emulate. They didn’t know if it would work. But they took a chance. So far, it has succeeded. The first steps are the bravest. The rest, now, are up to you. “Peace is indeed harder than war,” the Irish author Colum McCann recently wrote. “And its constant fragility is part of its beauty. A bullet need happen only once, but for peace to work we need to be reminded of its existence again and again and again.” You must remind us of its existence again and again and again. And to those who choose the path of peace, I promise you, the United States of America will support you every step of the way. We will always be a wind at your back. And like I said when I visited two years ago, I am convinced that this little island, that inspires the biggest things – its best days are yet ahead. Obama said the "peace walls" in Northern Ireland had to come down. Sylvia Gordon is the director of an organization called “Groundwork Northern Ireland,” which aims to bring about change from the ground up. As someone who got my start as a community organizer, this is something I appreciate. A few years ago, Sylvia visited the U.S. to learn more about how Americans organize to improve their communities. After she came home, Sylvia rolled up her sleeves here in Belfast – and decided to do something about Alexandra Park. Some of you may know this park. For years, it was thought to be the only park in Europe still divided by a wall. Sylvia and her colleagues knew how hard it would be to do anything about a peace wall, but they reached out to the police and the Department of Justice anyway. They brought together people from across the community anyway. Together, they all decided build a gate to open that wall. And now, people can walk freely through the park and enjoy the sun – when it comes out – just like people do every day in other parts of the world. As long as more walls still stand, we will need more Sylvias. We will need more of you – young people who imagine the world as it should be, and bring a community together to make it happen – who make even the small impossibilities a shining example of what’s possible. That, more than anything, will shape what Northern Ireland looks like 15 years from now and beyond. But there was more to do, he said. But as all of you know well, for all the strides you’ve made, there’s still much work to do. There are still people who haven’t reaped the rewards of peace; who aren’t convinced that the effort is worth it. There are still wounds that haven’t healed, and communities where tension and mistrust hangs in the air. There are walls that still stand; there are still miles to go. From the start, though, no one was naïve enough to believe that peace would be anything but a long journey. “Peace,” as Yeats once wrote, “comes dropping slow.” This was a lesson that applied in America too, he said. America, too, has worked hard over the decades, slowly, gradually, to keep perfecting our union. 150 years ago, we were torn open by a terrible conflict. Our Civil War was far shorter than the Troubles, but it killed hundreds of thousands of our people, and the legacy of slavery endured for generations. Even a century after we achieved our own just and lasting peace, we were not fully united. When I was a boy, many cities still had separate drinking fountains and lunch counters and washrooms for blacks and whites. My own parents’ marriage would have been illegal in certain states. And someone who looked like me often had a hard time casting a ballot, let alone being on one. Still, over time, laws changed, and so did hearts and minds, driven sometimes by courageous lawmakers, but more often by committed citizens. And while we have work to do in many ways, we have surely become more tolerant, more accepting, more willing to see our diversity not as something to fear, but as a source of our national strength. Ultimately, individuals had to make a contribution, he said. As your leaders step forward to address your challenges, through talks by all parties, they will need you to do the same. Because ultimately, whether your communities deal with the past and face the future united, together, isn’t something you have to wait for someone else to do – that’s a choice you have the power to make. Whether you are a good neighbor to someone from the other side of past battles – that’s up to you. Whether you let your kids play with kids who attend a different church – that’s your decision. Whether you take a stand against violence and hatred, and tell extremists on both sides that no matter how many times they attack the peace, they will not succeed – that’s in your hands. And whether you reach your own outstretched hand across dividing lines, across peace walls, to build trust in a spirit of respect – that’s up to you. Obame paid tribute to the way peace has been established in Northern Ireland. It has been 15 years since the Good Friday Agreement; since clenched fists gave way to outstretched hands; since the people of this island voted in overwhelming numbers to see past the scars of violence and mistrust, and choose to wage peace. And, over the years, other breakthroughs and agreements have followed. Understand how extraordinary that is. For years, few conflicts in the world seemed more intractable. And the world rejoiced in your achievement. Especially in America. Pubs from Chicago to Boston were scenes of revelry, folks celebrating the hard work of Hume, Trimble, Adams, Paisley, and so many others. In fact, in America, you transcend our differences. If there’s one thing on which Democrats and Republicans in America wholeheartedly agree, it’s that we strongly support a peaceful and prosperous Northern Ireland. Belfast was cool, Obama told his audience. And he even included the Irish word "craic" (although he did not manage the accent). Belfast is a different city too. Once-abandoned factories are rebuilt. Former industrial sites are reborn. Visitors come from all over to see an exhibit at the MAC, a play at the Lyric, or a concert here at Waterfront Hall. Families crowd into pubs in the Cathedral Quarter to hear “trad.” Students lounge at cafés, asking each other, “What’s the craic?” To paraphrase Seamus Heaney, it’s the manifestation of sheer, bloody genius – this island is now chic. Obama is speaking to an audience of mostly young people. He told them they represented hope. See, your generation has come of age in a world with fewer walls. You’ve been educated in an era of instant information. You’ve been tempered by turmoil, too. And from what I’ve seen of young people like you around the world, these currents have conspired to make you a generation possessed by both clear-eyed realism and optimistic idealism; a generation keenly aware of the world as it is, and eager to forge the world as it should be. And when it comes to the future we share, that fills me with hope. You give me hope. Here, in Northern Ireland, you have known even more rapid change. And while you have unique challenges of your own, you also have unique reasons to be hopeful. For you are the first generation in this land to inherit more than just hardened attitudes, but a just and hard-earned peace. You now live in a thoroughly modern Northern Ireland. The recessions that spread through nearly every country in recent years have inflicted hardship here, too, and there are communities enduring real pain. Still, day by day, life is changing throughout the North. There was a time people couldn’t have imagined Northern Ireland hosting a gathering of world leaders, as you are this week. Obama started his speech by saying how important Ireland was to Americans. In particular, we have wanted to come here, to Northern Ireland, a place of remarkable beauty and extraordinary history; part of an island with which tens of millions of Americans share an eternal relationship. America’s story, in part, began right outside the doors of this gleaming hall. 325 years ago, a ship set sail from the River Lagan for the Chesapeake Bay, filled with men and women who dreamt of building a new life in a new land. They, followed by hundreds of thousands more, helped us write those early chapters. They helped us win our independence. They helped us draft our Constitution. And soon after, America returned to Belfast, opening one of our very first consulates here in 1796, when George Washington was still the President. Today, names familiar to many of you are etched on schools and courthouses and solemn memorials of war across the United States – names like Wilson and Kelly, Campbell and O’Neill. So many of the qualities that we Americans hold dear we imported from this land – perseverance and faith, an unbending belief that we make our own destiny, and an unshakable dream that if we work hard and live responsibly, something better lies just around the bend. So our histories are bound by blood and belief; by culture and commerce. And our futures are equally, inextricably bound together as well. That’s why I’ve come to Belfast today – to talk about the future we can build together. I'm sorry I haven't been able to post for the last hour and a half. My blogging tools crashed. But I seem to be okay now. Barack Obama is speaking in Belfast. I'll summarise his speech soon. My colleague Henry McDonald is at Belfast's Waterfront Hall waiting for Barack Obama to deliver his speech. He says Michelle Obama is expected to introduce her husband to the audience, which includes hundreds of local schoolchildren. Martin McGuinness, Northern Ireland's deputy first minister, has just arrived. The Hague interview is over. It was a "holding the line" job. He did not say anything that told us anything new about whether Britiain, or the West, will or will not send arms to the opposition in Syria. But there were a couple of striking lines in it. • Hague said that the Syrian crisis was "the worst human tragedy of our times" - and that it was getting worse. It is the worst human tragedy of our times. It is on a trajectory to get worse. I don't want to understate in any way the severity and bleakness of this crisis. • He said all options were unpalatable. There are no palatable options. I want to be clear with the whole country about that. There are no easy options at all. Of course it is not easy to take any decision to send arms into a conflict. It's also not easy to take a decsion to allow people to be killed who are faced by much superior arms, and who may be driven to radicalism and extremism by being placed in that situation. Hague says it is only possible to get a political solution if the opposition cannot be destroyed. Q: The opposition is being destroyed at the moment, isn't it? But if we give them arms, we will get sucked in. Hague says the opposition has had important setbacks. Giving the opposition help can mean help in many different ways. The UK's overriding concern is to save lives. Hague says there are no easy anwers. This is not a problem the government can ignore. Britain's main effort at the moment is humanitarian. Britain is getting water and food to refugees. It is also trying to set up a peace conference, he says. Q: Boris Johnson says arming the rebels would be insane, "total madness". Hague says the government should not rule any option out. There are no palatable options, he says. It is not easy to take any decision to send arms to a conflct. But it is not easy to allow people to be killed. Q: But there are extremists, "truly awful people", on both sides. Hague says there are more than two sides. He has had many meetings with opposition leaders committed to a democracy in Syria. Their commitment to that is sincere. Q: But how can you give machine guns to one group of opposition fighters without them ending up in the hands of the al-Qaida thugs? Hague says Britain is currently sending equipment that saves lives to the moderates. Q: But you can't stop arms falling into the hands of terrorists. Hague says the government has not taken any decision on this. Q: But you would not have pressed for the arms embargo to be lifted if you had not wanted to send arms. Hague says he wanted options. Coming back to his earlier point, he says there is no evidence that the non-lethal equipment already sent to the rebels has fallen into the wrong hands. William Hague is being interviewed by John Humphrys on the Today programme. Q: It is insane to think of pumping more arms into Syria. Hague says Britain has not taken any decision about this. This is the "worst human tragedy of our times". And it is going to get worse. There can only be a political solution. A military solution would lead to the collapse of the state. And Britain thinks there can only be a political solution if the moderate opposition is protected. It is not just Vladimir who is opposed to arming the Syrian rebels; Boris has come out against the idea too. In a column in the Daily Telegraph, Boris Johnson, the mayor of London (and one of David Cameron's leading rivals in the Conservative party) has declared that sending arms to the Syrian opposition would be mistake. Here's an extract. Odious, twisted, hate-filled thugs; arrogant and inadequate creeps, intoxicated by the pathetic illusion of power that comes with guns; poisoned by a perversion of religion into a contempt for all norms of civilised behaviour. They are fighting not for freedom but for a terrifying Islamic state in which they would have the whip hand — and yet there is no dodging or fudging the matter: these are among the Syrian rebels who are hoping now to benefit from the flow of Western arms ... This is not the moment to send more arms. This is the moment for a total ceasefire, an end to the madness. It is time for the US, Russia, the EU, Turkey, Iran, Saudi and all the players to convene an intergovernmental conference to try to halt the carnage. We can’t use Syria as an arena for geopolitical point-scoring or muscle-flexing, and we won’t get a ceasefire by pressing weapons into the hands of maniacs. As I said earlier, some of the reaction to our G20 spying splash seems a tad snooty. Here's a flavour. From the Labour MP Jonathan Reynolds From Iain Martin, the political commentator From Paul Richards, the Labour former special adviser From Mark Ferguson, the editor of LabourList Personally, I think they are all being a bit churlish. It's true that, as the story acknowledges, there has been talk before about Britain spying on its allies. The Alastair Campbell diaries include occasional references to Tony Blair receiving intelligence about the thinking of his EU partners (although where the intelligence came from is never spelled out) and, in a documentary on the Foreign Office, Michael Cockerell once asked Robin Cook if it was true that Britain did this sort of thing. Cook, normally the most articulate of men, was almost rendered speechless as he tried to avoid answering the question. But no one has ever reported on this in as much detail as the Guardian does today. It's a great story. Do read it. If you want to read more about the press conference yesterday that David Cameron held with Vladimir Putin at Number 10, the Russian embassy have put out a full transcript. David Cameron and seven other leaders from some of the most powerful countries in the world are meeting in a rainy, remote but rather beautiful resort in Northern Ireland for the annual meeting of the G8, which the UK is chairing this year. I'm in the press centre and I'll be blogging from the summit for the next two days. Cameron wants the event to be about the three Ts – trade, tax and transparency. But this morning it looks instead as if the summit could dominated by two Ss – Syria, perhaps spying. The leaders are expected to discuss Syria at their working dinner tonight but, if Cameron's meeting with the Russian president Vladimir Putin yesterday is anything to go by, then the prospects of an agreement seem slim. Here's an excerpt from Patrick Wintour's story about the Cameron/Putin talks. A diplomatic breakthrough on the Syrian civil war at the G8 summit in Northern Ireland appeared unlikely when the Russian president, Vladimir Putin, chastised the west for considering arming Syrian rebels, saying they ate human organs. He said Russia by contrast was arming the legitimate government of Syria. Speaking after a difficult meeting with Putin in Downing Street, David Cameron claimed both men were in agreement on the need to end the human catastrophe of the civil war. But there was little to suggest the two men made progress on how to convene a fresh Syrian peace conference in Geneva, let alone who should attend, or its agenda. In icy exchanges at a press conference, Putin said: "You will not deny that one does not really need to support the people who not only kill their enemies, but open up their bodies, eat their intestines in front of the public and cameras. Are these the people you want to support? Is it them who you want to supply with weapons? Then this probably has little relation to humanitarian values that have been preached in Europe for hundreds of years." In today's Guardian the Syria story has been overshadowed by our revelation that in 2009 Britain spied on foreign politicians and officials attending two G20 summits in London. Some commentators seem a bit snooty about the story (more on this later), but it's a terrific scoop and a fascinating read and it will be interesting to hear what the G8 leaders have to say about it. Here's the agenda for the day. Early morning: David Cameron does a brief media interview. 8.10am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, is interviewed on the Today programme. 8.30am: Supporters of the Enough Food for Everyone IF campaign hand in a petition to G8 leaders. Around 9.30am: Barack Obama, the US president, is delivering a speech to an audience of mainly young people in Belfast. According to the Irish Times, he will talk about his admiration for what the people have accomplished during the peace process and his deep belief in their capacity to finish the job and to take the necessary but often difficult steps. 10.45am: Eddie Rich, deputy head of the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative international secretariat, holds a briefing. 11am: Herman Van Rompuy, the EU president, and Jose Manuel Barroso, the president of the EU commission, hold a press conference. Morning: Cameron holds a meeting with his Japanese counterpart, Shinzo Abe. 2.30pm: The US and the EU hold a trade meeting. 3.45pm: Official arrival for leaders at the Lough Erne resort. 4.45pm: G8 leaders start their first session, on the global economy. 8pm: G8 leaders begin a working dinner. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow.

tags.description: Latest news from Syria as covered by the Guardian. News and stories about the country, people and politics

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Thursday 8 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Thursday 8 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Monday including some in today's paper, are here.
